<a id="t-698742e310"></a>
## Thread: Determining free-form or fixed-form reference format. — 29 this month / 36 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

_Thread started [1999-12](1999-12.md#t-698742e310)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Determining free-form or fixed-form reference format.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000101195738.07851.00000563@ng-fc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<84iiqo$2tn$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

Overall where do we stand with the special ciphers *> and >> entering the COBOL
language?

Has any approved standard put the right angle bracket ( > ) into the language
yet?   

What stage of approval are any proposed or evolving language standard that
permit this cipher.

This ill chosen character will make it very difficult to get COBOL to work well
with XML as source code or literals in a common source file.

In XML, the star (asterisk, *) has meta characteristics as well.

 This means that COBOL lexical tools need to be able to see two different kind
of comments, and disambiguate geater than from .*>. and .>>'. To merge that
into a single tool set that might hope to be able to see XML in stream is going
to be very difficult.

This seems like a serious problem for the future of COBOL. To your knowledge,
has this issue been considered in the standards effort?

Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Determining free-form or fixed-form reference format.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84ma9r$tcl$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<84iiqo$2tn$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <20000101195738.07851.00000563@ng-fc1.aol.com>`

```
To the best of my knowledge the interaction of
   "*>" and ">>"
with XML has not yet been considered by the Standards committee.  I do
believe, however, that Micro Focus (aka MERANT) already has support for both
character strings *and* XML.

I cannot think of any place that a COBOL XML program with have "problems"
with confusing these.  I would think (but this may show my ignorance) that
the XML "symbols" would ONLY appear in a COBOL program - in an alphanumeric
literal.  Such literals are required to support ALL characters and
combinations of characters.  (Actually this is really only true of fixed-form
reference format.  The new free-form reference format allows an implementor
to restrict "special" characters from non-hex-alphanumeric literals.)

Therefore, I don't see that there could ever be ANY set of characters that
couldn't be confused from "meaningful" characters when outside a literal and
a string inside a literal.

Obviously, ">" is already a part of the COBOL language so there is nothing
that can/should be done that would in any way impact its use in COBOL
programs.  Furthermore, it is already used in the "double glyph"  ">=".   And
the JOD (and possibly/probably the Standard after this one) includes "<>" to
mean "not equal to".  (Again, a number of vendors already include this as an
extension.)

As far as the actual characters chosen, I am *so* used to "*>" for inline
comments, that it is hard for me to picture other possibilities. (Micro Focus
has supported these for about a decade now.)  The only "technical" issue that
was discussed at J4 (and WG4) that I still have some "quandary" about is
whether there should be a "terminator" character string so you could have
"inline comments" in the middle of a COBOL line, for example

  Move ABC *> sending field <* to XYZ

where "sending field" is a comment and "to XYZ" is back to COBOL source.
However, I don't think that this "enhancement" is worth adding at this
stage - and it would mean that you would have to "parse" the comment to find
its terminator.

As far as ">>" goes, I was a little happier when an earlier draft required
you to code
    >>DIR
at the beginning of directives (rather than having the "DIR" be optional).
But that led to some really UGLY code for
    >>EVALUATE and >>IF
not to mention
   >>D (debugging indicator)

Bottom-line:
  If you can come up with some examples for J4 or WG4 to think about where
problems may occur with likely XML code, then please let them know.
Otherwise, I don't see this as a serious "open" issue - but that's my opinion
only.

NOTE:
   When OO was just starting, we spent almost a year changing the "inline OO
indicator" each meeting.  We ended up with "::" (double colons) but even that
has some problems that have only surfaced in the last year or so.  (The
problems have to do with the fact that the single colon is *ALWAYS* a
text-separator in COPY/REPLACING.) These types of "aesthetic" issues, really
drive the Standard committee into more "confusion" and circular reasoning
than usual.
```

```

when using free-form.  When did this idiocy come in to proposed
standard?  

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restraictions on character set in literals was Re: Determining free-form or fixed-form reference format.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84okg3$2n6$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<84iiqo$2tn$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <20000101195738.07851.00000563@ng-fc1.aol.com> <84ma9r$tcl$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <386FC45B.7ADB7E16@istar.ca>`

```
Although I am not "thrilled" by this rule, there is a (semi-)good reason for
it.  The problem arises when the "CR" and "LF" (carriage-return and
line-feed) characters MIGHT appear in an alphanumeric literal.  In fixed-form
reference format, these can "happily" occur in alphanumeric literals -
because the literal *must* continue to the R-margin.  HOWEVER, when you start
having free-form reference format, it is a "tad difficult" to figure out if a
CR/LF (or whatever your "line sequential type file system uses") that appears
somewhere after an opening quotation mark is coded is

   A) a real line-feed (and you have a coding error with no ending quotation
mark)
   B) "data" to be included in the alphanumeric literal.

FYI,
  The exact wording from CD 1.7 are,

"The implementor shall specify any control characters that terminate a
free-form line, and whether such control characters may be specified in
comments and the content of alphanumeric and national literals."

(although I have a VAGUE feeling that those words may be under revision "even
as we speak")
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Determining free-form or fixed-form reference format.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84qjlg$ck0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<84iiqo$2tn$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <20000101195738.07851.00000563@ng-fc1.aol.com> <84ma9r$tcl$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <84qhq9$1vp$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
You are correct that those characters are not in COBOL (yet).  I see two
problems with them (but this isn't to say you can't/shouldn't submit this to
J4 or WG4 for consideration)

Problem 1:
  Are those part of "Standard ASCII" (i.e. the 7-bit, not the 8-bit variety)?
I am not positive about this.

Problem 2:
  The GOAL in the revision was to NOT introduce many (initially any) new
characters to the COBOL Character Set.  The reason is that these cause
"funny" things to happen with COPY/REPLACING rules - for any program that
happened to use them before. The exact problems vary depending on what the
rules are for the new characters.

Again, I view this as an "aesthetic" issue and not a technical issue - but
that's my opinion only.
```

news:85111l$rgc$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com...
>     I also like the /* */ group, but the */ MUST be optional!!!!
> Ie, a multi line comment would need a new /* at the beginning of each
line.
>
>
<<SNIP>>

It sounds like we're back to expecting a compiler to read the programmer's
mind:

Is the GO a comment, or is it a command?


 INFINITE-LOOP.
        /* Some comment
                GO INFINITE-LOOP.


You cannot expect a multi-line comment delimiter to work without and end
delimiter.

P.S. The WANG VS COBOL 74 compiler accepted the '%' sign for comments in the
'A' margin. Of course, % symbols within quotes where not interpreted as the
comment delimiter.

Karl Wagner
```

- **References:** `<84iiqo$2tn$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <20000101195738.07851.00000563@ng-fc1.aol.com> <84ma9r$tcl$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <84qhq9$1vp$1@news.cerf.net> <3870E7B1.5E120188@home.com> <84qucm$69o$1@news.cerf.net> <38711CBA.3C901F43@home.com> <38714F3E.831555AC@worldnet.att.net> <38716464.A57CD55D@home.com> <85111l$rgc$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <wr2d4.9848$g12.334743@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

"Oscar T. Grouch" wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> P.S. The WANG VS COBOL 74 compiler accepted the '%' sign for comments in the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Karl Wagner

I always liked this feature.  If I remember correctly the Wang compiler
flagged a '%' comment as a warning/reminder in the listing.  It was very
handy to use for messages to myself while writing a program. i.e.,
"don't forget to come back here and do some more work."  After the
compile you could easily jump directly to that spot and continue.

As for the */ and /* pair, I have to say I *strongly object* to them
even if they are used extensively in other languages.  I've always found
them difficult for some reason--I have to mentally pause and decipher
"on" or "off".  A little dyslexia maybe?  My $.02.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ ST Re: Determining free-form or fixed-form reference format.

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000106192455.14080.00000216@ng-ca1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<2B00116E4D86C632.CB1831A08C8198E5.806DF87BA8648E48@lp.airnews.net>`

```
I really dislike "implied" commands, things that we should expect compiler
authors to know and account for - primarily because I spend so much time
teaching rookies what the "implied" rules are, and even more time trying not to
forget to mention any of them.  If we do accept /* ...*/, fine, but lets keep
both ends explicit, just to reduce the elegance and increase the simplicity.

"A thing either is, or is not.  Any other approach leads to mental rot, tooth
decay, and voting the liberal ticket"  From the Notebooks of Lazarus Long.
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Determining free-form or fixed-form reference format.

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>

```
    I personally have never liked the idea of an open ended comment.
The programmer is the entity that needs to be considered, not the compiler.
As noted below, a multi line comment can cause confusion.

    The existance of legacy code that uses continued literals WITHOUT a
trailing quote
(ie extend to col 72, "-" in col 7 of next Cobol line)
would seem to make a comment indicator that comes on the same line
impossible

    Perhaps both kinds would be helpful.  Pascal used the curly brackets as
well as a multi character
comment (/* */   ???).

    C uses { and } for it's own use.  Since these 2 characters have to exist
anyway
on most keyboards, why can't Cobol use them? Not to mention C uses the
square brackets.
Those characters are alreay out there

    Sorry about the capital letters (The work 'MUST').  I really don't feel
that strongly about comments.



Oscar T. Grouch <dustbin@home.com> wrote in message
news:wr2d4.9848$g12.334743@news2.rdc1.on.home.com...

the
> 'A' margin. Of course, % symbols within quotes where not interpreted as
the
> comment delimiter.

> Karl Wagner

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Determining free-form or fixed-form reference format.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3875F9B7.3B9E475F@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<84iiqo$2tn$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <20000101195738.07851.00000563@ng-fc1.aol.com> <84ma9r$tcl$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <84qhq9$1vp$1@news.cerf.net> <3870E7B1.5E120188@home.com> <84qucm$69o$1@news.cerf.net> <38711CBA.3C901F43@home.com> <38714F3E.831555AC@worldnet.att.net> <84t8je$hbo$1@news.cerf.net> <85111o$rgc$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3874A3D5.44F48286@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8538i3$g2f$1@news.igs.net>`

```
The programmer I know who explains his one handed typing because of the
mouse (he programmed that way before mouses (bm?) as well), does in
fact, mouse left handed, and type right handed.

Most left handed people I know do NOT re-program their mouses.  Some did
when Windows only used one button, but gave it up when two buttons are
in wide use.  It is easier to be standard.

For the same reason, I long ago stopped programming most of my FKEYS.  I
do reset a few TSO keys.  SPLIT & START are now START & SWAP NEXT, but
that won't bother anybody using my keyboard.  I don't have RETURN, which
won't bother them, but the key I really change is pretty standard, F12
is RETRIEVE.  That command doesn't make any sense to be anywhere except
an F-key.  (non TSO people, RETRIEVE recalls previous commands from a
buffer)

A coworker loves making keyboard macros.  I would rather know the way of
doing commands which will work on the next machine I use.  With Windows
terminals, I also learn CTL-V, CTL-X, and CTL-C, etc. to avoid having to
use the mouse and menus.  

donald tees wrote:
> 
> Learn to mouse left handed, and you can double your editing speed.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >handed to keep the other hand on the mouse.
```

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85699i$950$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<84iiqo$2tn$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <20000101195738.07851.00000563@ng-fc1.aol.com> <84ma9r$tcl$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <84qhq9$1vp$1@news.cerf.net> <3870E7B1.5E120188@home.com> <84qucm$69o$1@news.cerf.net> <38711CBA.3C901F43@home.com> <38714F3E.831555AC@worldnet.att.net> <84t8je$hbo$1@news.cerf.net> <85111o$rgc$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3874A3D5.44F48286@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8538i3$g2f$1@news.igs.net> <3875F9B7.3B9E475F@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
It is funny how the brain works.  I cannot touch type, even though I have
been "keyboarding" now for thirty years.  However, as long as I do not think
about it, I am quite capable of typing 50-60 words per minute one handed, in
total darkness.  As soon as I realize what I am doing, I cannot do it
anymore.

I use my left hand for shift, ctrl, alt, and trackball.  I type with my
right.

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3877BFB0.9B6E0E55@home.com>`
- **References:** `<84iiqo$2tn$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <20000101195738.07851.00000563@ng-fc1.aol.com> <84ma9r$tcl$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <84qhq9$1vp$1@news.cerf.net> <3870E7B1.5E120188@home.com> <84qucm$69o$1@news.cerf.net> <38711CBA.3C901F43@home.com> <38714F3E.831555AC@worldnet.att.net> <84t8je$hbo$1@news.cerf.net> <85111o$rgc$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3874A3D5.44F48286@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8538i3$g2f$1@news.igs.net> <3875F9B7.3B9E475F@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <85699i$950$1@news.igs.net>`

```


donald tees wrote:
> 
> It is funny how the brain works.  I cannot touch type, even though I have
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I am always intrigued how non typist computer keyboarders picked up
their typing skills, because they are so very often as efficient as
mine. Trained in the RAF, this was the pattern, and our typewriters were
leftover clunkers from W.W.II :-

- (1) laboriously follow basic examples in a book, a,s,d,f,g,h space...
- (2) then with a cloth over our hands on the keyboard, looking at a
      grainy black and white film projected on a wall, following the
      operator's keystrokes.
- (3) Step 2 speeded up - now we went through the pattern with a music
      background. One tune used was 'D'ye ken John Peel'.
- (4) Now look at draft letters in a book and start typing, and of
      course, eventually use your own shorthand notes to type from.

Just like you I am not consciously aware where each character is on the
keyboard but just type it. In the case of the "a,s,d,f,g, h, space"
example above - yes, I did have to look at the keyboard to get that
right.

Don - that 'independent' Mouse that you referred to previously - what
was the trade name ?

Jimmy
```

think
> > about it, I am quite capable of typing 50-60 words per minute one
handed, in
> > total darkness.  As soon as I realize what I am doing, I cannot do it

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> right.

Interesting topic.  I have a rather weird typing style.  I'm right handed.
I took touch typing for a semester in high school, but I don't use it.  When
typing I generally use the three main fingers on my left hand for the keys
on the left side of they keyboard.  For the right side of the keyboard I use
my middle finger only for probably 90% of the keys.  For the other 10% I use
the ring finger.  For the space bar I use my right thumb.

Dunno where I picked it up.  The only characters I generally have to look at
the keyboard for are the punctuation symbols on the numbers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Making Cobol Programs Y2k compliant

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DTbb4.968$_S6.24607@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<s6q11rek5k248@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Herb wrote in message ...
>I am looking for some sort of program that makes all, or some cobol
>programs y2k compliant.  Could somebody help me out?
>


There are some automated remediation programs available which can locate
potential problems, and even do a tentative repair. I think they start at
around $10K, and you can probably find someone open on Tuesday. With luck
you could begin testing next week Friday or Saturday or so.

Note that Y2K bugs in COBOL are unlike werewolves: there are no silver
bullets.

(Werewolves are EASIER to handle!)

MCM
```

> 

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84n69c$sa0$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <386e7932@zoey_server.midtechcorp.com>`

```
    When using the rebuild utility, KEEP the old IDX.  It is used by rebuild
to
determine the structure of the indexed file before being overwritten.

    The IDX need not be "correct", it need only be of
the correct file type.  Ie, substitute an IDX from another file
of the same sort, then rebuild.

    Note that the newer rebuilds allow the /D option to
rebuild both data and index.

    Assuming dos or windows product (workbench or net express).


Tom Francen <tom_francen@midtechcorp.com> wrote in message
news:386e7932@zoey_server.midtechcorp.com...
> try fixing the error.  that usually resolves the problem.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** John MacAulay <john.macaulay@virgin.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38753CFC.DA58BF16@virgin.net>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <386e7932@zoey_server.midtechcorp.com> <84n69c$sa0$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:

>     When using the rebuild utility, KEEP the old IDX.  It is used by rebuild
> to
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Hi, George/Neil & Russell -

Russell is perfectly correct to assert that, if you have a second IS file with
an absolutely identical file structure, you could use that index to re-create
your corrupt idex, but it misses the point, really; the whole idea of having a
rebuild utility is to deal with exactly your situation, where the index is
corrupted.  The MF rebuild utility includes a facility where you can include
within a text file a definition of the IS file's record and key structure,
including key start point, length & type [alpha , numeric, comp etc.]; this
definition is then used to re-create the IS file (preserving the data, of
course).  I'm sitting at home typing this and don't have access to the MF COBOL
manuals, but I believe you'll find the necessary details in one or t'other of
the "System Reference" manuals - they're reasonably detailed.  Or is it in the
"Additional Topics" manual...?

You will certainly be able to avoid going back to earlier backups.  I'm afraid I
don't have a sample text file to include here, but I've used this facility
before.  And, believe me, if I can do it, you can ;-)  If push comes to shove
and you can't get your head 'round how to create the record structure text file,
post a copy of your FD here (or, in extremis, e-mail it to me at
john.it.macaulay@cityedin.demon.co.uk).

No promises, but I'd be surprised if we couldn't give this one a quick
turnaround.

Regards,

John
```

---

<a id="t-04ab4d4441"></a>
## Thread: A "trial ballon" — 7 this month / 8 total

_Thread started [1999-12](1999-12.md#t-04ab4d4441)._

#### ↳ Re: A "trial ballon"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386D6FFA.EEE75753@home.com>`
- **References:** `<84jhqm$oqs$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> As someone who voted AGAINST having a separate newsgroup for
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Let me throw in my pennyworth. The concept isn't bad at all, but as a
group we don't want a 'split camp' - we are all interested in the
general standards and techniques of 'OUR' language. How I SORT or SEARCH
is relevant whether or not I'm using a mainframe or PC. What we have to
watch is that we don't lose touch with one another as a community using
our COMMON-BOL.

Certainly, I have found some of the issues raised regarding mainframes,
such as handling multi-users, coping effectively with large volumes of
data of extreme interest. To use a WMK phrase, 'On the other hand', I
really have no interest in those technical issues which relate to using
mainframes tied in with JCL or their OS features which support file
handling, and topics like "LE". (And obviously, I don't give a damn
whether you use NEXT SENTENCE, or not, in an 'old' compiler which
doesn't have END-IF). It often means I have to peruse the first message
in a thread and then 'Mark Thread read' - Don't misread me; these are
IMPORTANT issues to mainframers.

I'll try and tread carefully here - should it be :-

(a) Mainframes
(b) Unix/Desktops/OO - this latter category suggested because, (I'm
treading gently again), the emphasis on using OO seems to be of more
immediate interest in this group.

I think it is problematic to try and split 'desktops' between OO and
non-OO.

Third choice, as Bill suggests - a separate OO NG - but obviously open
to all who wish to participate. It doesn't happen too often, but there
are the occasional 'guilty-parties' apart from moi, who admit to using
OO. Perhaps an NG focused to OO would draw them out more - that could be 
good for the language as a whole, with the hope that techniques and
ideas get swapped.

Something important to remember in 'splitting' - where does a Newbie go
for, "How do I do this....?".

Can't offer the ideal solution - but 'them's me thoughts' ????

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A "trial ballon"

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386E3834.C585D758@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<84jhqm$oqs$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <386D6FFA.EEE75753@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > having a:

I think there is way too much specialization in our industry.  General
purpose groups such as the AITP (formerly DPMA) are hurting as people
spend their extra time in more specialized groups such as Oracle or NT. 
The world and especially our industry are NOT static.  We need to have a
broader view of our jobs.

Keep the barely different messages in our news group, even if we give up
without understanding them.  Even awareness helps.
```

#### ↳ Re: A "trial ballon"

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3Gpb4.561$W41.5943@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<84jhqm$oqs$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote in message <84jhqm$oqs$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>...
>As someone who voted AGAINST having a separate newsgroup for
>acucorp/acucobol, I was wondering what comp.lang.cobol would think about
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>for Object Oriented discussions.....


I am very much sympathetic to Mr Gavan's views that there is much in "the
world of COBOL" which is platform and/or dialect independent.

About two years ago (well, sometime in the previous millenium) one of my
compiler vendors (PowerBASIC Inc Carmel CA USA), started a web-based BBS for
peer-peer discussions. They originally set up the sections based on their
products: one for their Windows Console Compiler, on for their Windows
DLL/EXE compilers, one for the MS-DOS compiler.

The first change they made in response to comments was to add a section
simply titled, "Programming" - for discussion of algorithms, techniques and
tips which were not dependent on "which specific compiler was being used"
and,  "which compiler should I be thinking about for this application and
why?"  There is, in BASIC programming, simply so much more in common across
compilers than there is different.

So... while not opposed to the idea of niche forums,  I would just as soon
get "all the news" in one place, and filter out that in which I am not
interested. ( Confession:  I also subscribe to the "whole newspaper" even
though I never read it all).

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A "trial ballon"

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386FF706.59819461@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<84jhqm$oqs$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <3Gpb4.561$W41.5943@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> So... while not opposed to the idea of niche forums,  I would just as soon
> get "all the news" in one place, and filter out that in which I am not
> interested. ( Confession:  I also subscribe to the "whole newspaper" even
> though I never read it all).

I would vote for it.  There are many people that do not want to read
the business section so they just put it to one side.  It is delivered
with the rest of the paper though.

I would recommend that we do have an OO group and that everyone
subscribe to it.  It will allow the few to filter out what they do not
want.

Another suggestion is to prefix messages with OO:, MVS:, etc.  This
quasi grouping will allow those not interested to filter it out
easily.  This does not work very well but it does not require a major
change and argument.  (We do not want another standards body :-})

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A "trial ballon"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387026DE.27DFEE74@home.com>`
- **References:** `<84jhqm$oqs$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <3Gpb4.561$W41.5943@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <386FF706.59819461@zip.com.au>`

```


Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Michael Mattias wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> change and argument.  (We do not want another standards body :-})

Good idea Ken ! Won't always work - but if we were all to prefix as you
suggest, it does allow us to filter more easily. Newcomers might be a
bit 'curious' at first - but I think they would latch on fairly quickly.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A "trial ballon"

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000103213242.00414.00000109@nso-bd.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<386FF706.59819461@zip.com.au>`

```
In article <386FF706.59819461@zip.com.au>, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
writes:

>
>I would recommend that we do have an OO group and that everyone
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I would prefer to see something along the lines of Mr Foskey's suggestion
of prefixing the message subject with OO:, MVS:, etc. and allowing the
newsgroup filtering to handle sifting the messages.
I have found a great deal more benefit from viewing the diversity available
in this newsgroup.   The comments from an earlier response about
OO being more PC specific may have some merit, but I believe that the 
overall concept has been implemented on hte mainframe via called modules
(or the PC DLL equivalent).    Therefore trying to separate the PC / Mainframe
newsgroups might further separate the two environments when it would 
seem that this forum has provided much in the way of alternative approaches
to the solution of mainframe and PC related coding opportunities.
```

```
*YES*YES*YES
Prefix is the way. Some of us are doing MVS for money and OO  for fun. A
prefix will show the flexibility of Cobol and underline the quality of the
language.

Regards
Ib

to work and played with my systems.  All looks to be fine with the on-lines.

run the first schedule of the year.

side of town (and it wasn't that cold either), there were some minor power
outages in the suburbs after the bars closed and the drunks hit the road and
power poles, and my next door neighbor is trying to figure out how to get all
the left over guests out of her livingroom :)

Happy New Year all :)

- **References:** `<83eq1m0119c@enews2.newsguy.com> <386D8184.B4ECD3F3@zip.com.au> <386EBB68.3FE17F74@home.com> <oGSb4.359$jG2.5263@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```
I had a problem, but it wasn't COBOL, but was ADS/O.

Three dialogs had weirdly formatted dates.  We were lucky to find one,
as it was a delete transaction for the first - only by looking at the
data after performing the transaction did we see a delete date which was
messed up.  

I picked the first program:
I checked the code and could not find a problem, and then decided to
generate (compile) the code with an option to allow me to use a
debugger.  It worked fine.  So I generated it without that option, it
worked fine.  I migrated all three to production, they failed.  I had
the DBA generate the dialogs in production and they worked fine.
```

<a id="t-e59b722c2c"></a>
## Thread: Grace Murray Hopper — 4 this month / 13 total

_Thread started [1999-12](1999-12.md#t-e59b722c2c)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Grace Murray Hopper

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000101163443.18674.00000545@ng-cu1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<385E9060.D3D3B3DF@melbpc.org.au>`

```

In an important way GMH did pioneering work in compiler bootstrapping. She
insisted that the UNIVAC COBOL compiler be written in FLOW-MATIC, with
initially no mods to FLOW-MATIC and later with minimum mods (instruction image
emits: analogous to BASIC POKEs, from what I can tell). Newspaper editors and
biographers might not have the background to perceive the importance of that.

I do not know what her motivation was.  It could have been pride in FLOW-MATIC,
or comprehension that it (FLOW-MATIC) was slightly cross-platform, so a
compiler bootstrapped from there had potentially wider availability. One can
guess that she understood that if the building blocks were a restricted set of
functions then they could reach other platforms, and be positioned well as
their own company's hardware product line evolved.

Does anyone know if IBM used the earlier translators much to develop their
early COBOL? (Was this called COMP-TRAN?)

I do not know if anyone has ever made a claim that Grace Murray Hopper did the
first bootstrap compiler; but she atleast possessed one of the earliest tools
that could have used that way.  


There is an interesting note about Grace Murray Hopper at
http://www.cs.yale.edu/~tap/Files/hopper-story.html

Does anyone know if any image of the first COBOL program survives? This is the
one that compiled on RCA and on UNIVAC. Is there a machine readable version of
this on the net?


-Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com




Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Grace Murray Hopper

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84m459$26h$1@bgtnsc01.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<385E9060.D3D3B3DF@melbpc.org.au> <20000101163443.18674.00000545@ng-cu1.aol.com>`

```
I don't remember that many details, Bob, but Flow-Matic had been used to
bootstrap to Amico (?) a FLOW-MATIC VERSION that generated object code for
Air Force Logistics Command 1100 systems, and that group along with my
employer and others worked on an extension to use on the IBM 705.  This
design work was one of the inputs to the COBOL short range committee.

Regarding copies of the first Univac COBOL compilers, at the demo to the
Executive Committee a tape containing this compiler was presented to each
member.  My boss, a member, gave me his copy.   Beyond the nice paint job
that they did to the real of tape, I can not swear that it still or ever
had a copy of the first compiler on it.  Many times I had wished that I
could read that tape. However, finding a Univac I that could process the
code is beyond hope, and so I have it on a book shelf where I can look at
it several times a day.

Another thing I know about it vs the FLOW-MATIC compiler is that:
    Flow-matic was one program that read into memory overlays when extra
memory was needed and used just 1000 words of 12 characters each.
    COBOL '60 for the Univac was a string of programs (over 100 as I
recall) that processed the input, created output, and loaded other programs
until all was processed.  Compared to FLOW-MATIC it was much faster.

Of course, those are my reflections only, and aside from actually having
the real of tape and pictures of the demo I can't say what is fact or what
is fiction.

Warren Simmons
w.g.simmons@worldnet.att.net

news:20000101163443.18674.00000545@ng-cu1.aol.com...

## Thread: COBOL binaries for Bit Wizards — 5 msgs

### COBOL binaries for Bit Wizards

- **From:** "Tony Tooley" <tony@infosearch.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<73wb4.2723$r6.390961@news.flash.net>`

```
I got a lot of help with my COBOL dates problem from the bit-wizards.
Many thanks to all who helped.
I have one last problem - decoding what looks like a 4-byte binary number.
I am 90% sure that the following byte dumps are some kind of COBOL comp
field representing the number to the right of the equal (=) sign.  Since I
don't have the source or file layouts, I have had to figure this out by
trial and error (and screen shots).
This one has me stumped but that's prob bc I don't know how COBOL stores its
binaries.
Can anyone see a pattern here?

 <008> <049> <100> <044> = 831642
 <008> <121> <130> <124> = 879827
 <007> <081> <134> <060> = 751863
 <016> <057> <007> <076> = 1039074
 <008> <052> <001> <076> = 834014
```

<a id="t-bac61dd223"></a>
## Thread: Nested/Stacked programs — 3 msgs

### Nested/Stacked programs

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4Gpb4.562$W41.5943@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I woudl like some opinions. (Can I get those here?)

Environment: IBM mainframe, mostly batch-sequential processing, latest
COBOL/390 (COBOL for VM) compiler, exclusively QSAM/VSAM (i.e., no DB2) .

I have been using multiple programs per COBOL source file to, essentially,
accomplish user-defined functions. I have been doing this using what I will
call "stacked" construction.

ID DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG1
...
END PROGRAM PROG1.
ID DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG2.
...
END PROGRAM PROG2.
ID DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG3.
...
END PROGRAM PROG3.

However, I see that an option is "nested" construction:

ID DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG1
...
ID DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG2.
...
END PROGRAM PROG2.
ID DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. PROG3.
...
END PROGRAM PROG3.
END PROGRAM PROG1.

My question is this: why would I choose "stacked" versus "nested"
architecture? The only differences I see are in the way data are shared
among stacked v nested programs; but I tend to use multiple programs per
source file specifically to avoid (inadvertent) data sharing.

All ideas/comments welcome.

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested/Stacked programs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84lpoq$d9n$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<4Gpb4.562$W41.5943@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
IMHO,
  The *only* reason to use nested programs if you want (or are used to - or
think you SHOULD want) "data encapsulation" via GLOBAL vs local data.  With
IBM's compilers (which is what the question referenced) there is some pretty
reliable information that CALLing a nested program "performs better" than a
CALL to a "separately compiled program" (which would include those in a
sequence of programs such as you show - in other words, any program that has
a separate "load/object" module).  This last does point out something that
you may not know (it isn't ALL that clear in the IBM documentation), nested
programs do NOT get separate load/object modules - while a series of programs
in the same compilation run do.

The other "issue" is whether you have "common" programs that are called by
multiple other programs.  Such programs should NOT (IMHO) ever be handled as
nested programs.  It *is* possible to do so, but using a COPY statement just
before the END PROGRAM header of you "main" program - but that means that you
need to recompile every program that CALLs them, whenever anything changes.
(For that matter, I would never include such "common subroutines" in a series
of compiles - for the same reason.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested/Stacked programs

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84po2p$rsq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<4Gpb4.562$W41.5943@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
In article <4Gpb4.562$W41.5943@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
  "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
> I woudl like some opinions. (Can I get those here?)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> using what I will call "stacked" construction.

<snip>

> My question is this: why would I choose "stacked" versus "nested"
> architecture? The only differences I see are in the way data are
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> michael.mattias@gte.net

The use of 'nested' programs is a matter of your personal programming
style. If you are a PL/I or C programmer and have to design a new COBOL
program, you may use nested programs instead of procedures and functions
with the same advantages and disadvantages:
- loss of performance (very few)
- advantage of 'local' varibles
- disadvantage of global varables
I see only one real sense for the need of a nested program: To build an
address of a field definded in the working storage section.

The use of what you called 'stacked' sources may or may not be usefull,
it depends on how you manage the version control and the maintenance. As
long as you are the only person, it's OK.
The use of multiple little 'functions' is supported with the new 'DLL
linkage'. You might find it usefull to use this kind of calls (including
long names).
```

---

<a id="t-acfec38c12"></a>
## Thread: Y2K — 2 this month / 18 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

_Thread started [1999-12](1999-12.md#t-acfec38c12)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K

- **From:** pknudsen@gw.total-web.net (Paul Knudsen)
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386e5031.10957295@news.gw.total-web.net>`
- **References:** `<83b3av$mdd$1@news.igs.net> <19991229002213.22112.00001849@ng-cp1.aol.com> <8wya4.248$_j1.4802@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

donald tees wrote:

> Howard Brazee wrote in message <386CBACB.C5E60FD4@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, that is true.  But what do you mean by "Mobile capacity"?

I was referring to switching from hard wired communication to mobile.
 
> I do see a place for web retailing, particularly in small towns that
> traditionally have lacked "a decent bookstore", for example. What I do not
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> transferred over wire.

This is probably cheaper, but it some people wouldn't be able to do it. 
On the other hand, I find it easier to pick up a product when I am free
than to be at home whenever the UPS man arrives.
 
> Net commerce is only suitable for very specific things.  Books are about the
> largest thing that ship cheaply enough to make it practical.

In large quantities.   Ordering a rare part can be very good over the
net.  Nobody wants to warehouse them near your house, but you know
exactly what you want, find out whether it is in stock in Timbuktu, and
then have them send it to you.

## Thread: IBM "C" problem (pass on to those "other" programming language people) — 1 msgs

### IBM "C" problem (pass on to those "other" programming language people)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84lp17$a3f$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI,
  From the IBM database (impacting C programs - but some of us COBOL-types
still speak to them)

APAR Identifier ...... PQ28710      Last Changed ........ 99/12/31
  UNREADABALE RESULTS FROM #PRAGMA COMMENT(TIMESTAMP)

  Symptom ...... IN INCORROUT         Status ........... CLOSED  PER
  Severity ................... 2      Date Closed ......... 99/11/16
  Component .......... 568821601      Duplicate of ........
  Reported Release ......... 211      Fixed Release ............ 999
  Component Name SAA AD/CYCLE C/      Special Notice       ATTENTION
  Current Target Date ..99/11/17      Flags
  SCP ................... MVS
  Platform ............ MVS                          YR2000


  Status Detail: SHIPMENT - Packaged solution is available for
                            shipment.

  PE PTF List:

  PTF List:
  Release 201   : UQ37349 available 99/12/31 (1000 )
  Release 211   : UQ37350 available 99/12/30 (1000 )

   Parent APAR:    PQ26728
  Child APAR list:

  ERROR DESCRIPTION:
  When using #pragma comment(timestamp) unreadable output results:
  "RLD      ""    """"""""""""""""""""""""""""""" """"""""
  "END                            15688187   210000003
  "END                             OPTIMIZE(0) START RENT TARGET()
  "END                             5688187 V2 R1 M00 IBM C/370
  "END                             Mon Jan  3 21:45:08 2000"
  "END           PROBLEM >>>>>>>>  " ""b"  " ;\b"9;"":{"""""
  "ESD      ""  ""@@XINIT@""""""""@@INIT@ """""""
  #pragma comment(date) gives correct ouput.

  LOCAL FIX:

  PROBLEM SUMMARY:
  ****************************************************************
  * USERS AFFECTED: Users who compiles source codes that         *
  *                 are created beyond year 2000.                *
  ****************************************************************
  * PROBLEM DESCRIPTION: The compiler can not extract the        *
  *                      timestamp of a file that is created     *
  *                      after year 2000.                        *
  *                                                              *
  *                      This causes problem in compiling codes  *
  *                      involving file's timestamp              *
  *                                                              *
  *                      For example:                            *
  *                      #pragma comment (timestamp)             *
  ****************************************************************
  * RECOMMENDATION:                                              *
  ****************************************************************
  The routine that retrieves VM file's timestamp must be
  able to recognize a timestamp that is beyond year 2000.

  The cause of this problem is due to an effort in making the
  CMS system Y2K compliant.  In order to allow the system to
  recognize Y2K date, a century bit was turned on when the year
  is beyond 2000.  The compiler needs to make a corresponding
  change to recognize this century bit.


  PROBLEM CONCLUSION:
  The compiler routine that retrieve the VM file's timestamp
  has been modified to recognize the century bit."
```

<a id="t-a3720bb4ca"></a>
## Thread: COBOL "Internationalists" — 1 msgs

### COBOL "Internationalists"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84m1h7$ft5$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Would those of you outside "standard roman alphabet" counties (and yes,
Canadians who need to support French - this means you too), please consider
looking at the following PDF:

    http://anubis.dkuug.dk/JTC1/SC22/WG20/docs/n699.pdf

This is a (draft) Standard for including "extended" letters in identifiers in
programming languages. (In other words, COBOL words in the next Standard will
need to know about "this stuff").

Please let me know (via private and/or public) email what you think about
this - and how it will fit in with COBOL.
```

## Thread: Windows - OR is it me ? — 31 this month / 56 total

_Thread started [1999-12](1999-12.md#t-b4599bb00c)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386E9E71.283F3E91@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <84829k$7ce$1@news.cerf.net>`

```


Cheesle wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> from C++, Delphi and Acucobol etc...

One closing thought that developed from this thread - there is no one
perfect mouse-trap although at this stage Flexus SP2 does seem to be a
winner with AcuCobol a close second.

- Thane's progression through GUI handling was illuminating. I was a
little quizzical when Jerry said "user can exit right in the middle of
editing a record...." - I haven't found that a problem - I just quit
doing anything with that record.

It's interesting what some private messages have revealed. Just as there
are here, strong supporters of SP2 etc. with all their advantages,
private messages from others derisively dismiss SP2 or Dialog system in
favour of APIs, VB or Activex.

Then of course there are the M/F GUI classes that I'm using - they are a
sod to understand, but I believe them to be really powerful. As WMK has
said, the N/E IDE is written using OO/GUI. Just curious Thane, have
Fujitsu indicated how their IDE is written, their own in-house GUIs or
did they 'cheat' using C++/VB/???? - superficially at least it looks
very close to N/E. And if it is in-house, are samples/code available to
you developers.

As regards GUIs I guess it's like your compiler - for whatever reason,
you come down in favour of one particular 'flavour'. An overriding
factor has to be productivity - how quickly can you switch without
getting embroiled in non-COBOL technology. (In my case with limited
bucks and time - I had to stick with OO/GUI). You guys have several
decades left to explore and play - me, that's it; I'm not quite there
yet but I am decidedly walking down the hill rather than running up.
(Note : I said 'walking' down the hill - not 'falling'). <G>

And Bob, your modesty becomes you - well done !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4ESb4.358$jG2.5346@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <84829k$7ce$1@news.cerf.net> <386E9E71.283F3E91@home.com>`

```

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:386E9E71.283F3E91@home.com...
>
>
>
> - Thane's progression through GUI handling was illuminating. I was a
> little quizzical when Jerry said "user can exit right in the middle
of
> editing a record...." - I haven't found that a problem - I just quit
> doing anything with that record.

Actually, Jerry (me) said we were having problems when the user
exits in the middle of a FIELD. Leaving a field kicks in the data
validation routines, which, in turn, may return the user to the
beginning
of the field containing the bad data. But returning to the field with
bad
data simultaneously means leaving the field to which the
user moved and may, in its turn, activate another data validation
routine
which requires a reset to the beginning of field #2.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387025D3.83634DCC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <84829k$7ce$1@news.cerf.net> <386E9E71.283F3E91@home.com> <4ESb4.358$jG2.5346@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```


Jerry P wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> which requires a reset to the beginning of field #2.

Sorry Jerry, I misread you. That would be a problem or so I thought - so
I checked out what I'm doing (one of those lousy programs you don't like
Ken <G>).

Now it might have something to do with the GUI features that you are
using, like setfocus, losefocus etc. Briefly, I ignore the TAB key - the
user likes his Numberpad too much with its ENTER key. So the sequence is

- create entryfields and on ENTER key event checks is this the Default
button (OK-pushbutton) or an entryfield - jumps back to business logic
with the result. The Dialog box code set-up :-

       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "z10-entryFields".
       *>-------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 n                             pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-id                         pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-event                      pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-length                     pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division.

       perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > MaxEntryField

	*> I'm using a table of Resource file IDs and field-numbers,
	*> similar to what Ken Mullins mentioned in an earlier message
	*> but Ken is using APIs

          move ws-EntryID(n)     to ls-id
          move ws-EntryField(n)  to FieldNumber
          move ws-EntryLength(n) to ls-length

          invoke self "getObjectFromId"
                 using ls-ID returning os-Control(FieldNumber)

          if  ls-length <> zeroes
              invoke os-Control(FieldNumber) "setLength" using ls-length
              invoke os-Control(FieldNumber) "accessSystemEvents"
              move Button1Down to ls-event
                   invoke os-Control(FieldNumber)
                        "setEventto" using
                        ls-event, self, "set-nextfocus ", FieldNumber

               *> Note space after "set-nextfocus "

               map event p2ce-functionKey upon
                     os-Control(FieldNumber)
                     to Method z"check-character-code" upon self

          else invoke os-Control(FieldNumber) "readonly"
          End-if
       End-perform

       Exit Method
       End Method "z10-entryFields".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Method-id. "z20-pushButtons".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------
       Local-storage section.
       01 n                                 pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 n2                                pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-event                          pic x(4) comp-5.
       01 ls-id                             pic x(4) comp-5.

       Procedure Division.

          move clicked to ls-event
          perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 5

            move ws-PushID(n)       to ls-id
            move ws-Pushfield(n)    to FieldNumber

            invoke self "getObjectFromId"
                   using ls-id returning os-Control(FieldNumber)

            if   not field-PB-OK
                 invoke os-Control(FieldNumber) "setEventto" using
                  ls-event, os-Caller, "return-pushbutton " FieldNumber

            *> Note space after "return-pushbutton "

            else set os-DefaultButton to os-Control(FieldNumber)
                 invoke os-Control(FieldNumber) "default"
                 map event clicked upon os-Control(FieldNumber)
                     to method z"default-button" upon self
            End-if
          End-perform

       *> Dialog Editor allows me to set OK-button as 'Default" - but
       *> this is one of my 'belt and braces' approaches

*> I hope by now los Americanos don't need me to to spell out what 'belt
*> and braces' means <G>

       Exit Method
       End Method "z20-pushButtons".
       *>---------------------------------------------------------------

That's the code - so to prove it, I brought up a record clicked on a
field which takes me to "set-nextfocus" above, empties the field and
then prompts for data. With two tests, Half-way through entry, I clicked
on :-

-  Cancel-button (cancel this record) which returns me to 'business'
logic which invokes "display-emptyscreen"

- and Exit-button/or the "X" top-right (quit program) - which quits the
program(class) by going to my 'business' logic for method "exit-program"
, which 'hides' the dialog, finalizes objects and returns to my Main
Menu.

Both worked just fine.

I think your Exit is only problematical depending upon how your events
are set up. From memory, I think you said you were using SP2. Perhaps
Bob would like to comment - no you're not pushing your product, just
giving technical background.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** KHansen@screenio.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38753bb8.29600312@gateway>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <11f733ec.5d8778af@usw-ex0107-042.remarq.com> <VOba4.435$631.3513@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <853ads$fio$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Thu, 6 Jan 2000 18:56:07 -0500, "Ken Mullins"
<KenMullins@mindspring.com> wrote:

>once you understand windows and GUI, it's no more difficult in cobol than it
>is in C/C++ or even visual basic...and is probably faster if your using
>native windows API's...

Too true.  In fact, we're just putting the finishing touches on
version 1.0 of a general purpose GUI screen manager for COBOL known
quite imaginatively as "GUI ScreenIO" - a followup to our character
mode ScreenIO product.  (Contact us directly if interested in the
beta, we think it's pretty cool).

It's written entirely in CA-Realia COBOL but works with any 32-bit
compiler; we've tested it with CA-Realia, Fujitsu, and Micro
Focus/Merant (although our older MF compiler exhibits some odd
behavior at times).

The tricky part is that one about "understanding Windows" - which can
easily take a couple of years.  Once you start to understand it,
though, you really have to admire it, although the argument passing
stuff (pointers to structures passing more pointers ad infinitum) is
certainly tortuous at times.  

Petzold's book covers only the very basic stuff - and suggests certain
techniques that, in retrospect, are not the best way of doing some
things.  The Microsoft SDK leaves lots of stuff unsaid, there are a
couple of poorly documented (and undocumented) bugs in certain
elements,  and it can take quite a bit of digging to work out the
behavior of some APIs and controls.

Kevin
Kevin Hansen
NORCOM - Developers of COBOL Programming Tools
http://www.screenio.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI : Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** ThaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-YZ1NPCPCgcvg@localhost>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <11f733ec.5d8778af@usw-ex0107-042.remarq.com> <VOba4.435$631.3513@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <853ads$fio$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <38753bb8.29600312@gateway> <3875615F.C3A0C816@home.com>`

```
On Sun, 7 Jan 3900 03:50:07, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> 
wrote:

> 
> But I am very interested in your approach - PARTICULARLY as it is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I imagine the Screenio product is very similar to sp2, if they kept to
their screenio roots.  I used Screenio for many years, and converting 
from Screenio to Sp2 was very very easy.

ActiveX is NOT a replacement for your GUI.  It's a componant driven 
model.  It's black box.  You don't care what happens inside, you give 
it input and something comes out.  All ActiveX I have seen (these can 
be OCX, or even DLL's) require a hosting window at the very least.  
They do not replace the API.  ActiveX uses a specific Interface 
(COM/DCOM are similar but not quite the same).  ActiveX is the 
combination of the black box object and the interface, where COM and 
DCOM are the interface to DLL's.  Clear as mud?  

Sp2 supports ActiveX btw.
-------------------------

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI : Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** KHansen@screenio.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38761f51.87865624@gateway>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <11f733ec.5d8778af@usw-ex0107-042.remarq.com> <VOba4.435$631.3513@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <853ads$fio$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <38753bb8.29600312@gateway> <3875615F.C3A0C816@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,

Since you asked...

GUI ScreenIO is basically a screen-at-a-time interface, with options
that allow you to get control at the field or character level if you
really want to.  You call our routine to display and manage the
window(s) and it returns when an event of interest to you takes place;
a button press, tabbing to a different field, menu selection, and do
on.  It is a true Windows product and supports many of the popular
Windows features; you can't tell by looking at it that it's a COBOL
application.

SP2 is, of course, our primary competition (look out, Bob) and I would
be inclined to say that our product is both more powerful and far, far
easier to use.  Unlike SP2, however, GUI ScreenIO is strictly a
Windows product - but shucks, that's a pretty big market.

In general terms, you design your windows in our WYSIWYG editor which
produces a panel copybook that contains all of the runtime info needed
to operate the panel.  They are quite compact.  

One of the more interesting things about our GUI ScreenIO, is that all
types of windows are treated (from the application programmer's point
of view) identically. 

Property sheets, popup dialogs, wizards, and regular dialog boxes are
just another panel to your program.  If you find that a dialog becomes
too complicated, you can convert it to a property sheet and use the
property sheet with perhaps one line of changed code in your program.

If you require multiple language support, you can translate all of
your strings using the panel editor, and the runtime will (soon) allow
an end user to switch languages on-the-fly.  Again, all of this is
stored in the panel copybook so you don't need any external files.

We presently support a good handfull of controls, and have tons of
features in the design.  You can use edit controls, listboxes,
comboboxes, treeviews, pushbuttons, and - soon - listview controls.
We provide tooltip support.   We're also providing a simple interface
to the Windows Common Dialogs so you can use stuff like the standard
File/Open dialog.  The list of features is quite extensive.

We expect to release a stable and very functional (though not
complete) product later this month.   

I'll email you the beta information.  We would be most interested in
your impression of the product.

As for OO, I'm not terribly impressed with "strict"  OO re
inheritance, polymorphism, etc. but if you mean you just want to call
other languages from your program, that's something else entirely.
GUI ScreenIO's runtime dll is CA-Realia COBOL, which is callable from
any 32-bit language.  Older versions of Micro Focus runtimes were
somewhat problematic - they did strange things - but I believe that
NetExpress works fine.  We have called Micro Focus dlls from
CA-Realia, and I suppose that it would be possible to do the same with
Fujitsu, but our expertise lies with CA-Realia so I can't give any
substantive advice re the other products.

Kevin

On Fri, 07 Jan 2000 03:50:07 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>You'll appreciate having started this thread and performed
>self-flagellation getting a handle on N/E OO/GUIS, I'm in no mood to
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB

Kevin Hansen
NORCOM - Developers of COBOL Programming Tools
http://www.screenio.com
```

Tony (and other interested parties),

Your interest is appreciated, believe me.

I hope you'll understand that we prefer to keep the beta under fairly
tight wraps (e.g., people we more-or-less know) to avoid
misconceptions caused by public discussion of a beta product's bugs or
shortcomings.  Betas tend to have both.  

We're presently wrapping up our implementation of listview controls,
the last piece of the initial development, which will make the product
complete enough to develop real applications.

We've got a pretty good bunch of testers now, but we're planning a
production release (with an available to everyone evaluation copy) in
a couple of weeks.  

We'll post a quiet product announcement then.

Kevin

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI : Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<854u2k$v7i$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <11f733ec.5d8778af@usw-ex0107-042.remarq.com> <VOba4.435$631.3513@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <853ads$fio$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <38755CFD.511229CA@home.com> <854n46$psd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com> wrote:
>Isn't activex only implemented into the NE dialog system...

Strictly speaking, ActiveX covers everything from simple automation to COM 
controls and ASP, all of which are possible with NE COBOL.

ActiveX controls can be both used by, and created using, NE COBOL. There 
are several demos in the product, both DS and non-DS.

DS is written using NE COBOL, and provides extra help with the programming 
and GUI side of ActiveX controls over and above plain COBOL, which is just 
what it does with general GUI.

Steve.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI : Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38769759.8B5E794@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <11f733ec.5d8778af@usw-ex0107-042.remarq.com> <VOba4.435$631.3513@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <853ads$fio$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <38755CFD.511229CA@home.com> <854n46$psd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <387632E7.FD48BD88@home.com> <855rg3$tvq$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


Ken Mullins wrote:
> 
> I am not sure I understand the question? what is a subpane?
> 
Sorry Ken, assumptions, assumptions. I had 'assumed' because it was GUI
class terminology it was also a 'Windows' term. Visualize a Dialog as
follows :-

(A)	Dates 		 Jan	Jul	Mar
			  98	 99	 00
	Temperatures	 125	125	150	
	Instrument #s	   1	  2	  2

(B)	Line Ids      (C)    Inspections values (e.g. 1.023 ins)

	Top	1	 0.323  0.321   0.319
	...	2	 0.322  0.321   0.318
	Bottom 12	 0.322  0.320   0.317
	.......23
	.......24


I can create the outline, static stuff with the Dialog Editor, text
labels etc. Then I need to get a 'frame box' for A, B and C - so specify
the size (x, y, w, h) for each of these and create the frame or SUBPANE.

 (A)  4 x 10
 (B)      24
 (C) 24 x 10

I want right-jusitified entry-fields. So now within in each subpane I
now dynamically create entry-fields to contain the maximums shown above. 
Then to use that other new buzz word, I 'populate' the tables with
existing data or user enters new data.

Hope that explains it.

---------------

Seems like we might have some communication problems. Anybody else
having problems ? My last message posted to Thane about "as clear as
mud" which appears in CLC got returned to me with "Warning not delivered
in last 4 hours".

A certain gentleman in NZ doesn't appear to be getting his messages
through. So asked me to check. He reacted to something I posted, and I
hadn't even mentioned the bugger by name <G>. So here's the message he
says didn't get posted :-

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

-----Original Message-----
From: Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
Newsgroups: comp.lang.cobol
Date: Saturday, 8 January 2000 03:11
Subject: Re: GUI : Re: Windows - OR is it me ?
>
>James J. Gavan wrote in message <3875615F.C3A0C816@home.com>...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>As for sliced bread, I really couldn't care less whether you or anybody
else
>use ActiveX. I find it useful, I've tried to explain to you at length why I
>find it useful. This was intended to be helpful, not to be a contest. This
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>has a minimum of complexity. It runs efficiently even though I didn't
define
>the Event Classes and the properties and methods for every single control.
>This was already done by the vendor, transparently to me. I am quite happy
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>not suggesting it is the only way, or that you and everybody else should
use
>it,...only that it works for me. As you are aware, I am planning some major
>developments in the near future and I see a platform independent,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Yes, I'd like to see other people doing it too (I could use some help) but
I
>am not getting into a "contest" over it. At the end of the day, the
>like-minded souls will work together and , hopefully, achieve something;
the
>academics will still be arguing about it when their arguments are overtaken
>by events...
>
>Pete.
------------------------------------------------------------------------

Howzat Mr. Dashwood - Happy ?

Pete, briefly I'm not getting into a contest either, but indicating that
others are probably pursuing another path, as in your case, (though I
specifically didn't mention you by name). Then I see somebody producing
a new product, and ask for information/their philosophy, bearing in mind
the comments I have picked up from others, (like you), to look for
comparisons and make my own judgements. OK - so in your case I may have
misconstrued what you said, and that comes from not knowing the products
- but can teacher lay-off a little bit, slapping wrists. 

Don't want to flog this one to death - and I know you have enough savvy
to be on the right track, but within the NetExpress 'stable' alone,
there are those who would fiercely support their chosen path - Ken
Mullins for instance has been very successful using just Win APIs, Ray
Leech very successful using Dialog System, though he might qualify that;
and others have taken a 'grab bag' of the N/E features to achieve what
they want. OO/GUI - ignore me, but Simon is very 'pro'. Outside of
NetExpress there are other independents here who too have been
successful even using what you and I consider to be 'old hat' the Screen
Section - not sure they're going to show you their Profit & Loss - but
Judson and Donald Tees can demonstrate success using Screen Section.
Like you, at the end of the day they are concerned with something that
works and gives them monetary reward.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GUI : Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3877941A.B8FE7E7B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <11f733ec.5d8778af@usw-ex0107-042.remarq.com> <VOba4.435$631.3513@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <853ads$fio$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <38755CFD.511229CA@home.com> <854n46$psd$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <387632E7.FD48BD88@home.com> <855rg3$tvq$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <38769759.8B5E794@home.com> <857dak$i7l$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


Ken Mullins wrote:
> 
> Why not simply create all of the controls with the dialog editor?
> 
> kenmullins

If anybody is using the Microsoft Dialog Editor with C etc., or
NetExpress Dialog Editor, (the latter is parallel to the first), wishes
to step in and correct me, please do so. Anybody at Newbury want to chip
in ?

Until I gained familiarity with generating GUIs that's the initial
approach I took Ken. BUT - look at my sample Dialog; I had a 'screen'
giving me controls (4 x 10), (1 x 24), and (10 x 24 ) a total of 304
before you throw in static text etc. Guess what happened when I
displayed using the test option - over 25% of the controls didn't show !
There is obviously an upper limit of around 250/255 - not an
unreasonable restriction, even though the limitation doesn't appear to
be documented.  

Now even if you were to suggest the 'subpanes' could be produced as
separate dialogs to be displayed in the 'main dialog', unless I'm on the
wrong track, that still means each entry-field has to have its own ID,
eg. :-

	ID_VALUE1    ( is entryfield (1, 1) in COBOL )
	ID_VALUE2    ( ..............(1, 2) ......   )
	.........
	ID_VALUE240  ( ..............(24,10) ......  )

Now that has to be a lousy way of doing it - unless there is a design
alternative. My alternatives - drop in a grid, created in Activex,
(which I currently know nothing about), or as in my case do a perform
240 times against a method to generate the entryfield which also sets me
up with an object table :-

	01 os-values.
	   05 occurs 10.
	      10 os-value occurs 24 	object reference.

Using the COBOL table concept, I can zero-in on the specific control as
a result of the mouse click event.

Does that clarify it - or do you still contend it can all be created in
Dialog Editor ?

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8604s7$n79$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com>`

```
Sorry, I've been away for some time - work, you know.

I began with OS/2. Windoze 3.0 wasn't really worth to talk about, so I made
all the basic errors in OS/2. I managed to run the program h2cpy on a
computer with SDK installed. I can tell you, that I got some copybook. Later
on I was more selective.

It was the years of AD/Cycle - remember that? Dialog System with CICS/OS2
and ECI, Tandem on GUI and Client/Server with OS/2-Lan, LU6.2, AAI and
CICS - wild stuff.

Then IBM decided to listen to their stock-holders instead of their customers
and Billy Windows  won the game. Windoze 95 was introduced shortly before
'96 and we went there.

The platform learns us how to reboot - we did that several times during the
day. I think that we were working 5 hours a day - the last 3 hours were
spent with analyzing 'access violations' and rebooting.

From late 96 to early 97 the platform began to be reliable, and many GUI
aplications were introduced to customers.

I am very fond of WinNT as a developing platform. It is almost as good as
OS/2, and it has something OS/2 didn't - customers (read money).

Regards
Ib


James J. Gavan skrev i meddelelsen <38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com>...
>The most challenging thing I have found in switching to OOs/GUIs is the
>Windowing, not the OO.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<861v43$b89$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <8604s7$n79$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
"Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk> wrote in message
news:8604s7$n79$1@news.inet.tele.dk...
> I am very fond of WinNT as a developing platform. It is almost as good as
> OS/2, and it has something OS/2 didn't - customers (read money).

I share your opinion that the end of OS/2 was a great loss to personal
computing. And I also agree with you that WinNT has been almost as good as
OS/2. However, it is my opinion that this has changed. With the release of
Win2000 it finally feel that I have got something that is better than OS/2.
I have been playing around with Win2000 Server for a while now, and I am
nothing but impressed. The new functionality and the improved stability
really is a huge step ahead.

I encourage anyone to take a look at it, and in particular the opportunities
that resides within the new Windows Terminal Server API.

Yes I am aware that the WTS has been there since NT4 SP4 and before that as
a product from Citrix. However, the NT4 implementation was far from reliable
and a functionality so close to the OS itself cannot be served well by a
third party, no matter how excellent work they have done.

It is my belief that the Terminal Server functionality will open up huge
business opportunities for us Cobol developers, in the context of
downscaling.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

- **From:** "Gerald Moull" <gerald@moull.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<865kkj$7fo$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <8604s7$n79$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <861v43$b89$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
I share your belief that 'real thin client' computing is very well suited to
COBOL.  Server based is the industry's perception of COBOL and the world is
moving back that way.

I must admit to not having a lot of experience of Terminal Server, but that
which I have indicates to me that it is a 'fudge' in Microsoft's attempt put
as many Unix characteristics into NT/2000 as possible.  There are many
better products becoming available for the 'open systems' COBOL industry
that really do offer the real benefits of thin client computing to COBOL -
and these will not just be limited to Windows or Intel as the server.

Y2K is dead, well taking its last few pay cheques, so COBOL programmers will
need to learn to adapt to component interfaces, event driven programming and
other technologies if they have not earnt enought folding stuff to retire
on!  Luckily, the industry is moving to environments that suit COBOL and
COBOL integration.

Gerald Moull


Cheesle <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote in message

I second that Microsoft tends to steal from others, isn't that what they

Cheesle

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows - OR is it me ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86drkr010d2@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<38667ACE.3CD6181@home.com> <8604s7$n79$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <861v43$b89$1@news.cerf.net> <865kkj$7fo$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk> <86b97t$8a7$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
it all sucks.  I'm ready for an abacus.  To me, an operating system should
be transparent.  I remember back 15+ years ago to the days of Atari 8 bits.
Nobody ever gave a shit about the operating system.  Armed with a small
arsenal of commands you could do just about anything you needed to do in
about 16k of RAM.  I think it's funny how people get all pumped up about
operating systems.  Give me 2 Nintendo Game Boyz and a link cable and I'll
build ya a network!  I think it's also funny how 'certified' network people
get paid so much money (more than most of us programmers).  Many, many
people in this world are rich simply because Microsoft didn't make it
better.  That's kinda not fair to us.  It's like if COBOL was only 50% as
good as it is; it would probably still be around anyways and we'd all be
making 6 digit salaries right now.

Don't kid yourself, there's still a long way to go.

Jeff

----------
In article <86b97t$8a7$1@news.cerf.net>, "Cheesle"
<cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:


> "Gerald Moull" <gerald@moull.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
> news:865kkj$7fo$1@news5.svr.pol.co.uk...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
