## Thread: COBOL to Perl — 7 msgs

### COBOL to Perl

- **From:** Augustine Daimler <perl.news@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386E8DF6.683B@bigfoot.com>`

```
Has anyone heard of a (Perl-)program that converts COBOL source code to
Perl?
```

```
On Sun, 02 Jan 2000 15:10:39 -0500, G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>>Has anyone heard of a (Perl-)program that converts COBOL source code to
>>Perl?

>being the resident expert on practically anything computer, i can say
>that there *might* be a product out there, but if you bought it, don't

Highly unlikely :-)

>count on it working for anything with less than perfect code.

Agreed.

>and some constructs may not convert very well. the worst construct, i
>could see, would be perl's associative arrays and cobols indexed
>files. of course, it's been 1 year since i looked at perl, and maybe

Why would any Perl construct be an issue in converting _to_ perl?
IMO the biggest problems will be REDEFINES, RENAMES, and ALTER.
Followed by GOTO.  And all the "extensions" that many COBOLs have
will be in between somewhere.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL to Perl

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i4ga7sk6c88tbo1csa1fvmti9lt741seog@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<386E8DF6.683B@bigfoot.com> <ic9v6ssinoh3fnmj1t3ad4v5723p79tisj@4ax.com> <slrn8740fa.8mk.sitaram@japh.unidata.com>`

```
sitaram@dimensional.com (Sitaram Chamarty) wrote:

>>and some constructs may not convert very well. the worst construct, i
>>could see, would be perl's associative arrays and cobols indexed
>>files. of course, it's been 1 year since i looked at perl, and maybe

>Why would any Perl construct be an issue in converting _to_ perl?

because if you want to convert correctly with *any* chance of it
working at normal speed, it's bet to convert the indexed file
constructs to associative arrays (or, you could find a perl module
which works with indexed files, but then your perl programmers would
have to learn an extra module.). also, alternate keys with duplicates
would fail, so far as an associate array conversion.

it would be an interesting project, if anyone paid me to do it. but it
surely wouldn't make any money.

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL to Perl

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387876B9.62CE3621@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<386E8DF6.683B@bigfoot.com> <ic9v6ssinoh3fnmj1t3ad4v5723p79tisj@4ax.com> <slrn8740fa.8mk.sitaram@japh.unidata.com> <i4ga7sk6c88tbo1csa1fvmti9lt741seog@4ax.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
> sitaram@dimensional.com (Sitaram Chamarty) wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> it surely wouldn't make any money.

I reviewed an article in the Cobol issue of some magazine.  There was
an interesting article on code conversion from one language to
another.

I basically said it was impossible to do an adequate job.  There are
too many decisions to the process.  How do you map pic 9(4) to Perl? 
If you use binary your get something close but not the same.

Automated conversions from one language structure to another leads to
bad results in two languages (is this deja vu?).

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL to Perl

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** x@wins.uva.null (X)
- **Date:** 2000-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85blbq$1q3@adam.wins.uva.nl>`
- **References:** `<386E8DF6.683B@bigfoot.com> <ic9v6ssinoh3fnmj1t3ad4v5723p79tisj@4ax.com> <slrn8740fa.8mk.sitaram@japh.unidata.com> <i4ga7sk6c88tbo1csa1fvmti9lt741seog@4ax.com> <387876B9.62CE3621@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> writes:

>How do you map pic 9(4) to Perl? 

Good question.  So let us try, by way of counter example, to convert the
following COBOL program to perl:

DATA DIVISION.
 01 A PIC S9V9999 VALUE -1.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 ADD 1.005 TO A.
 DISPLAY A.

the first intuition seems to be:

#!/bin/perl
$A = -1;
$A = $A + 1.005;
print $A, "\n";

but this yields:

0.00499999999999989

>Automated conversions from one language structure to another leads to
>bad results in two languages (is this deja vu?).

indeed.  For in order to get the above correct, you have to emulate the
data type of S9V9999, and the behavior of this data type wrt operations.

Then you hvae to emulate the DISPLAY statement as well, since the above
COBOL program has 

00050+

as its output.

Have fun coming up with a more correct perl program.  

  --XX

for more info read http://adam.wins.uva.nl/~x/con/con.html
```

<a id="t-e62d6e8949"></a>
## Thread: If we knew then, ... (was Re: Yardeni Warns Y2K Problems Still Ahead — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### If we knew then, ... (was Re: Yardeni Warns Y2K Problems Still Ahead

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84n0le$f7i$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<EIyb4.1239$W41.22757@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <20000102011803.01491.00000521@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
JLBalvanz <jlbalvanz@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000102011803.01491.00000521@ng-fq1.aol.com...
<snip>
>
> I wonder...it was really, really expensive to store those century bytes 30,
20,
> even 10 years ago, so not including them was a significant cost savings.
Now
> -- maybe even 40 years later -- we've paid for it in the costs of
remediation.
> Do you suppose that even considering the huge amount of money "we" have
spent
> on fixes (and in at least some cases the money was spent by the same
companies
> who saved that money years and years ago) that a good financial analysis
would
> show that we've at least broken even, or maybe even shown a profit?  I
suspect
> you'd have to do the math on a company-by-company basis, and some of the
> information wouldn't even exist any more, so it might be tough to even
estimate
> the equivalent annual cash flow.  But it'd be interesting if you could...
>

This one is worth answering (I think).

If you check comp.lang.cobol, the "COBOL" programming language is my love and
the way I used to make my living and still enjoy myself.  Hopefully, those
(with at least a minimal bit of knowledge) in this (comp.software.year-2000)
NG know at the rumors of the WHOLE *Y2K* problem being the fault of that
"old - now dead" language COBOL were (and still are) false!!!

On the other hand, there are lots and LOTS of "mission critical" business
applications written in COBOL (many of these still-in-use programs were
originally written before 1995, many before 1990 - and a surprising number
before 1980).

Now, since about the mid-1980's (when the last official ANSI/ISO COBOL
Standard was approved) the fact that COBOL is a HIGHLY portable programming
language has been exploited by many companies.  UNFORTUNATELY, the native
COBOL language - as defined by ANSI/ISO did NOT include any way of getting a
4-digit year (for the current date) until it was ammended in 1989.
Furthermore, many compiler vendors did not introduce compilers that supported
this "4-digit year" enhancement (via Intrinsic Functions) until well into the
'90s (and even then their "new" compilers had other "inhibitors" for existing
programs and programmers).  A final enhancement was made to the draft of the
next COBOL Standard in the last few years which made it even easier to get a
4-digit year.  (ACCEPT from DATE/DAY enhancements).  When these were added to
the draft Standard there was every expectation that this draft would have
been made "official" long before Dec 31, 1999.  UNFORTUNATELY (for reasons
too many and too painful to go into here), it still isn't official.
Therefore, COBOL (the "new and improved variety" - for those of you who still
hear it is dead or dying) which includes this enhancement won't become
official until well into the 2000's.

The bottom-line is that programmers who wanted "portable" code (as defined by
the ANSI/ISO Standards) - but to stay in the COBOL programming language have
had REAL problems getting the 4-digit year for the current date.  This was a
FAULT in the language, not in the programmers.

It is remarkable to me how many programs written in COBOL have been fixed AND
FIXED CORRECTLY  by "sliding windows"; assuming that "today's date if it
began with a "9" must be in the 1900s"; and various other "programming logic"
solutions.

Again, don't blame those programmers who used a "flawed" tool (for whatever
business reason they continued to use that language - and there were and are
many such reasons). Just thank them for "getting around it".
```

#### ↳ Re: If we knew then, ... (was Re: Yardeni Warns Y2K Problems Still Ahead

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<386FCD5E.82F13100@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<EIyb4.1239$W41.22757@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <20000102011803.01491.00000521@ng-fq1.aol.com> <84n0le$f7i$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Most of the Y2K projects I have been aware of have actually spent
most of their time looking for problems and testing whether there
were any problems. The actual remediation was usually only a
minor part of the cost.

Given that you probably have to test anyway, even if the code is
supposedly Y2K compliant, the savings may have been quite minor. 

And in addition, most of the old code *has* been retired, though
not all of it. In some cases, too, you just may not have been
able to fit the program in memory if you used 4 digit dates. You
look at the old source files and they often have number of bytes
for each routine in the comments, which shows what an issue it
was. Try running a bank on a 64k machine.

Bill is right though; providing support only for 2 digit years in
COBOL 85 was not COBOL's finest hour!

Tim Josling

William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> JLBalvanz <jlbalvanz@aol.com> wrote in message
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > show that we've at least broken even, or maybe even shown a profit?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: If we knew then, ... (was Re: Yardeni Warns Y2K Problems Still Ahead

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000103235023.15110.00000303@ng-ce1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<386FCD5E.82F13100@melbpc.org.au>`

```
Actually, an analysis of this was done by a columnist in Computerworld (I
think) several years ago.  His numbers seemed to be top-of-head stats, and so
are somewhat suspect, but he came up with a a net savings of using 2-digit
years and remediating and testing the code (I agree that testing was much more
expensive, particularly building adequate test environments for non-trivial
systems!) of 1.5 times.  That is, the savings in storage cost was 1 and half
times the Y2K project costs.  That may be wishful thinking, and within the
limits of error of the numbers, but still, seems like a good deal, particularly
when we, the currently active programmers, had no choice in the matter!

And, I have to admit to several contradictory feelings.
1).  Relief that civilization did not end because of something I and my
colleagues did.
2).  Insulted pride at the chicken littles - what the h*** did they think we'd
been doing for the past three years!
3).  Injured feelings for thos who say, "a con job"! - again, what do they
think we've been doing the last 3 to 5 year!

IMHO.
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

---

<a id="t-6168f05ef2"></a>
## Thread: DYNAMIC-STORAGE SECTION and the NAVIGATE verb — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [1999-12](1999-12.md#t-6168f05ef2)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DYNAMIC-STORAGE SECTION and the NAVIGATE verb

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000101204223.07851.00000581@ng-fc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38615961.B7F02E60@spc-systems.com>`

```
John R. Piggott,Piggott@spc-systems.com 
Date: Wed, 22 Dec 1999 23:06:09 +0000

wrote
<<
You need to bear in mind that ALLOCATE and FREE are already in the draft
standard. 
>>

A nice thing would be to get managers to understand that it would be much
better to stay on the path of letting the compiler manage allocation and frees.
Much cheaper, and much more reliable.

When you need them, programming devices lilke ALLOCATE and FREE are great. But
there are vast domains that need none of that from the expensive programmer.


So a problem that I think Rick Smith's proposed  NAVIGATE verb, or a similar
verb capability would solve is the problem of paying programmers to allocate
and free, when that is mundane work suitable for an inexpensive machine.

Best Wishes
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: DYNAMIC-STORAGE SECTION and the NAVIGATE verb

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000101203502.07851.00000577@ng-fc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<83ocad$1n4i$1@news.hitter.net>`

```

Rick Smith, ricksmith@aiservices.com 
Date: Tue, 21 Dec 1999 15:54:16 -0500

Makes an interesting proposal for defining linked lists in COBOL, and then
navigating these with a prposed new verb such as this;

<<
    NAVIGATE dynamic-storage-name-1
                { FIRST | NEXT | PREVIOUS | LAST }
        [ INTO record-name-1 ]
    [ AT END imperative-statement ]
    [ NOT AT END imperative-statement ]
    [ END-NAVIGATE ]

>>

This is a very useful proposal.  It would be nice to make the memory allocation
and memory free into automatic wrapper functions that are implicit in the
compiler.

The linked list concept is exactly what got the ALGOL-60 and Simula language
genre off into programming domains like dynamically active simulation models.
Simula died because the common business model was for the vendors to own the
syntax of the language.  The dynamic internals were born again, eventually in
C++.

COBOL would be a good place of residence for this stuff, in some fashion short
of the nested program glop.

The linked lists you propose, could conceptually map web pages as well, not
just monotone data objects of one type.

I would encourage you to try to fold your syntax into the existing
READ/WRITE/DELETE verbs. Rather than be so distinct.

I would also propose that you try to avoid the proliferation of specialized
tokens such as is  LEFT-CHILD  RIGHT-CHILD.  Instead just add the single token
CHILD, and work up a scheme for FIRST / LAST that means left/right (where the
spatial aspects of left/right are a distraction anyway). Pure abstraction might
be better: so that  FIRST/LAST could as easily mean up/down as righ/left or
good/bad or ONERROR/NOTONERROR (should someone want to write a compiler with
you extended language).

Similarly, the ROOT/PARENT tokens might be folded into some way of referening
to FIRST LAST, or maybe some way of using the START verb.  

Generally, I think that it would be useful to make tree walking look as much
like file I/O as possible. Where the future really is just 'access methods'
with decreasing sense of whether the material is on disk, in core, or online. 

Nice proposal!

Best Wishes
Bob Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com






Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

---

<a id="t-228b9fbd6a"></a>
## Thread: Question and Answer from the GNU-COBOL project — 1 msgs

### Question and Answer from the GNU-COBOL project

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84n5rg$d5p$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I thought the following question(s) and my answers might be of interest to
all of c.l.c.

> Bill,
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> feel my questions are particularly dumb ;-)

   ****

Your questions are not dumb.  Furthermore, they are all issues that get
discussed and RE-discussed at J4 (ANSI) and WG4 (ISO) groups regularly.  I
can try to answer from my perspective but do want you to understand that I
*often* disagree with J4 - and this is why I have just recently rejoined it
after several years of being off it.

"OPTIONALITY"
  Getting rid of the '85 Standard concepts of "optional modules" and
different "levels" of support was a "design goal" early in the revision work.
It was felt that one of COBOLs biggest "selling points" is its portability
(which - pardon the expression - is considered MUCH better than that of C,
for example, where you can't even tell from ANSI/ISO C how big an "int" is.)

Report Writer was the "prime" example of how "optionality" was the "kiss of
death" to portability.  Back in the 60's when it was first introduced, it was
"state-of-the-art".  Yet, it was not universally implemented, so programmers
didn't rely on it, so it got less used, so less implementors implemented it,
etc, etc.  The decision was made that ANY feature that was worth defining was
worth requiring WHEN/WHERE the "processor" could support it.  (Therefore, it
isn't required to support Report-Writer for a compiler that isn't used on
machines with "printers" - nor is it required to support the new
ACCEPT/DISPLAY in environments without character-capable screens).

OK, lets look at some of the "specific" examples of required features.

Enhanced ACCEPT/DISPLAY (based on the existing X/Open definition and in
COMMON use among UNIX and Workstation compilers - as an extension - today).
   This is WITHOUT DOUBT the most "controversial" required new feature.  One
country (Germany) has done everything they can (via the ISO channels) to get
this removed.  It is interesting to me that when they first started
complaining about this "feature", they told us all that GUIs would be all
that ANYONE would be using come the "next millennium".  Anyone on this list
want to tell me what GUI *standard* interface is available YET for UNIX?  How
about the number of "green-screen" applications still running in IBM
mainframe environments, (and I believe several other proprietary systems).
   My personal opinion is that this feature is a useful feature and will be
used in the following cases:
  - To port X/Open conforming applications into the new Standard
  - As a "debugging tool" (similar to the "old" DISPLAY statement) when you
don't want/need a "full debugger"
  - for almost NO "new" development of "production code"

       ***

Report-Writer:
  Another controversial (still) decision.  It has been enhanced as well as
being made mandatory.  I can tell you that when IBM "dropped" it from their
mainframe compiler (between OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II), they THOUGHT that
it wasn't used very much.  Experience since then has caused a VERY lucrative
business for an "add-on" product that provides this support for any existing
or new IBM COBOL site.  Personally, I still think that there are more COBOL
programs being written and maintained with "report output" than there are
with "GUI" *OR* character interfaces.  It is true that such programs COULD be
written in COBOl with "output" to 4th GLs, but for those of us who "know and
love" RW, this really is an incredibly easy-to-learn and use and FLEXIBLE
tool. (And yes, I am a report writer "bigot" so this is one area that I
*hope* stays required.)

Note:
  You may want to look at the new VALIDATE facility for another case of where
a "new COBOL feature" seems - to some - to be adding support now for
technology of the '70s or '80s.  The interesting thing is that this feature
WAS first defined for COBOL in the '70s (I think, certainly by the '80s) but
is only now getting into the Standard.

OO:
  Well, you said it - look at C versus C++.  The WHOLE point that J4 and WG4
made was that they did NOT want the language divided into two languages.
Personally, (and I have some friends who HATE me for this) I wouldn't mind a
BIT if OO were made optional.  Given the lack of "truly" portable class
libraries,  I question the portability of applications that use this feature
(for the foreseeable future).  I used to follow "CORBA" - but have yet to be
convinced that "portable" stuff for the COBOL language to interface with is
there now.  If you gave me the choice of having ONE feature added to the
"optional" list, this would be it.  On the other hand, it is the MOST
"publicized" selling-point for the "new COBOL" - so I don't see any real
chance of
this happening.

   ***

Words "reserved in context"
  Actually, the intrinsic function module first introduced this (when
"FUNCTION" wasn't a reserved word - but acted like one MOST of the time). The
correction amendment ('91) reversed this, but it COULD have been "reserved in
context" all along.
  One of the complaints that COBOL has had from "outsiders" is the number of
reserved words that it has.  This isn't just a "theoretical" complaint but is
a complaint from existing programmers who have tried "upgrading" their source
code from one standard to the next - only to find that their data-names and
procedure-names have become "reserved".  This new feature is an attempt to
minimize this problem.  It is thought that it doesn't matter MUCH how much
work it takes for the compiler vendors - as long as life is "easier" for the
programmers.  (My words, not those of J4)

  ***

Publishing "rationales" is not something that the ANSI/ISO rules encourage
(EXCEPT in the case of incompatible changes).  If you think that it takes too
long now to get a new Standard (and almost all of us do think that) it would
be FOREVER if J4 and WG4 had to agree to the wording of their "reasoning".
On the other hand, all J4 documents and minutes "are public" and if you have
any question on the rationale of a specific feature, you can send a note to
the J4 chair (or NCITS - the former X3) asking to see the proposal that added
a feature.  These do include a "justification" section, but I think you would
be disappointed if you ever read them. OTOH, WG4 stuff is NOT publicly
available, "don't ask, don't tell" is pretty much their way of working. (A
reason that I usually do NOT join the US delegation to WG4 meetings.)
```

## Thread: DATE-problem — 1 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

_Thread started [1999-12](1999-12.md#t-c9f937e8cd)._

#### ↳ Re: DATE-problem

- **From:** "Julian Brett" <julian@rubiconcs.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<946835180.9417.0.nnrp-13.9e98ac08@news.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<386BD69F.DB9CD3F3@t-online.de>`

```
Hi.

I've just seen this problem occur elsewhere.  I believe that you meant
100010.  It seems that MF Cobol II returns 100 for the year value of the
call to DATE, and therefore you are really losing one of the characters
which makes up the day value (YYMMDD) as everything else is shifted.  A call
to DATE will only return a 6 digit field.

If you ACCEPT FIELD FROM DAY, you get 10000, instead of 00001, so this
doesn't help either.

===
Julian Brett.
===


Michael Stoll wrote in message <386BD69F.DB9CD3F3@t-online.de>...
>Hi,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ciao Michael
```

```
supports told me that my question is out of the support service (i can't  
agree with them).

Ok - here are the problems:
===========================
We have generate a DB2 Database with IBM UDB 5.2 in a NetExpress 3.0 Service  
Pack 1 and Windows NT Service Pack 3.0 environment.
We use a general USER.

But if i compile a cobol program with the direcitves NOACCESS,NOPRE,CTRACE i  
can see an error in the ctrace.logfile.

The precompiler want's to get access to a db2 object that should owned by  
myself. But we use a general user, so this access gets an error.

For example the general user is TEST and a view WORKER exists.
The precompiler wants to access to my own userid myid.worker instead of  
test.worker.

This isn't changed if i use the collection-id and user, password direktive.

I think the other problem is the same problem.
A compile with the directives access, pre isn't end successful.
```

<a id="t-9975dd04ad"></a>
## Thread: DB2 Primary KEy — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Re: DB2 Primary KEy

- **From:** Gary Joehlin <Gjoehlin@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s6vl6toe5k2129@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<s4n0h5tkqeg25@corp.supernews.com> <82hclt$2l2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Eddie,

If the DB2 you speak of is for OS/390 then, DB2 has only two types of 
KEYS.  Primary and Foreign.  It allows an index on any column 
combination.  But, if it is defined with a Primary key, it must be 
Unique.  The purpose is to prevent duplicates of the Prime key (SQLCODE -
803).  You can check in the DB2 catalog to determine if your table has 
such a prime key.  SELECT * FROM SYSIBM.SYSTABLES WHERE NAME = 'table-
name';  Then check, the KEYCOLUMNS column.  If it is greater than zero, 
you have that many columns in that table's primary KEY.  Then names of the 
column, that make up this Primary key are found in SYSIBM.SYSCOLUMNS under 
the KEYSEQ column; which identifies the order of the columns in the 
PRimary Key, then see the column called NAME for the name of the column.

=== nothing that I entered follows ===


Eddie White wrote:
> 
> 
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: DB2 Primary KEy

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d6Tb4.16220$y11.202310@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<s4n0h5tkqeg25@corp.supernews.com> <82hclt$2l2$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <s6vl6toe5k2129@corp.supernews.com>`

```


Partly Correct!

DB2 has more than two KEYS.
[Optional] Primary Key [must be CREATEd UNIQUE PRIMARY KEY ]
Foreign Key - indexed
Foreign Key NOT indexed
Index Keys (Not defined as PRIMARY KEY)

SO NO you can't 'dup' a 'PRIMARY KEY'
   BUT
    You can define a table (w/o dependents!) with Only one Index (a
'primary' index NOT
created as PRIMARY and UNIQUE)  AND it will allow DUPLICATE VALUES

Gary Joehlin <Gjoehlin@aol.com> wrote in message
news:s6vl6toe5k2129@corp.supernews.com...

…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: more y2k problems — 2 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

##### ↳ ↳ Re: more y2k problems

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84p9ii0au1@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<84em9t02d9@enews4.newsguy.com> <A8F81C2AD9018649.E4B467E77000F55E.5C40F80A5B4CEB83@lp.airnews.net>`

```
We have alot of programs, don't know how many.  Our group is responsible for
well over 2,000 programs, mostly batch.  The guy who 'tested' this system in
particular *retired* in 1998 so that probably explains it.  Plus, this
particular database runs on a different LPAR than our main one; I joined the
company after most Y2K testing was done so I really don't know how well they
tested this database.

Otherwise, very few Y2K related problems occured over the weekend to my
knowledge.  The worst one was our pharmacies were denying over 10 different
insurance plans on Saturday but it was attibuted to the third party systems,
not ours.

We also had a problem with a yearly job that was modified with some new
steps last year, but that would have happened for any year.  IDCAMS was
trying to delete/define/repro an open 'date' dataset.  A call to the systems
people shut the files down in a matter of seconds and the job was restarted.
Sounds like a need for a CMTBATCH close step.  Funny part is, I doubt
anybody's gonna fix it so it'll just happen again next year.  Hope I'm not
on call.

Jeff

----------
```

```
Nothing to do with COBOL but this amused me.

The systems where a friend of mine works were shut down over the new
year and were due to be brought back up on the Monday. He had to go in
and test the distribution systems on the 1st and they had been planning
this for months - fully scripted, the works. 2 days before Xmas the user
of the time and recording system rang him and asked 'while you are in
could you just test our system for us please?'. 

Three guesses what the answer was (and 1 guess as to which of the
systems in his place didn't work).
```

<a id="t-88f6ada26b"></a>
## Thread: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry! — 104 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Monday, 01/03/2000

If you were involved in Y2K remediation, you probably came in some time over
the weekend and did some final testing, checking, etc.  And this was probably
after many weeks, or months, or perhaps even years of tedious and boring 
work expanding files and pouring over source code.  After you found the source
code, that is.  I won't go in to all the problems with Y2K that have been
thrashed
in this ng for the last 2 years, suffice to say we all know what was involved.

So I've had three of four people today say *What's the big deal, nothing
happened
and I think it was all hype so you could make a bunch of overtime*.  BTW I'm
on salary and don't get overtime.   The final straw was when our postman said
his 486 rolled over just fine and he thought there should be a congressional
investigation of the whole pack of lies (his exact words).

I've finally started saying that I wish I hadn't done a darn thing and let it
all go
in the toilet.  When the roof fell in on our business system, maybe then they
would
have realized how much work went into *Nothing Happened!*.  Sheesh, didn't we
all bust our butts so nothing would happen???

My hat's off to all you other geeks out there that worked 50-60 (and more)
hours
a week getting it done.  We know what we did, and we know what would have
happened if we hadn't done it.  That *nothing happened* is a tribute to us.

rant mode disabled, thanks for letting me vent.

JC (not to be used as a flotation device) Jones
```

#### ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

- **From:** PAL3000 <pauldub*REMOVETHIS*@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IgRxOIBSnsvbMcnJ1LBYMlaLbXuA@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
I personally felt the same way after completing numerous Y2K upgrade
projects.  I put in all that work, and everything looked the same.  It
was a pretty hollow accomplishment for me, and that is why I hated all
the Y2K work I did.  

I mean, I like to see some payoff for my labor.   Yes, I know that
this work had to be done, and I know what the work did, but it is
nicer to create new software and have it work and also have the end
user use it and like it.  This produces a much better feeling of
accomplishment.

My hat is tipped to everyone that did a lot of Y2K work.  If you hear
a lot of comments like "What was it all for?" , and "Why did we have
to do it?", just rest assured that this proves how little the end user
really understands about computers, and how much we programs control
the world!  Muhahahahaha.


Paul
--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out 
*REMOVETHIS* from my email address
```

#### ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

- **From:** x@wins.uva.null (X)
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84r7lc$i1f@adam.wins.uva.nl>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347) writes:

[nothing happened = the best success, but also a marketing problem]

agreed.  a metaphor that helped me is as follows:

1) a lot of money was spend on Y2K
2) nothing happened at 01/01/2000

conclusion:

money on Y2K is waisted

If we replace Y2K with something else, people readily understand the
foolishness of their argument:

1) a lot of money was spend on anti-conception
2) nothing happened after nine months

conclusion:

money on anticonception is waisted.

If people are still not convinced, then it might help to explain them that
both anti-conception and Y2K are preventive innovations.  You apply them to
make sure that nothing happens.  For both inventions it is hard to convince
people to use them.  FOr instance, use of condoms is very hard to
understand for many people so that AIDS can spread freely.  If we had
nothing done about the Y2K problem it would have spread as freely and
deadly as AIDS.  

HTH

  --XX
```

#### ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W6bc4.1313$X14.21636@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
I contract at a MAJOR financial services company, and I can tell you that
because (primarily) my sharp eyes, a major disaster was averted (
and entire system that some IDIOT manager hired an offshore company to
convert would have had to be redone at the LAST minute - it would have been
all but impossible)  when I saw a snipet of their code in an email to
someone
else on a business rle change.  They wrote:

if 6digit-date < other-6-digit-date
   move 6digit-date to work1
   call date-routine
   move other-6-digit-date to work2
   call date-routine
   if work1 < work2
....

ALSO, Saturday I spent several hours researching why SOME
transactions came in with a local date of '001231'!
Then I had to put in quick patch in two batch reformat
modules to fix what the Front End group KNEW wasn't ready
for several months!  A 'Year-change' flaw.

JCJ0347 <jcj0347@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com...
> Monday, 01/03/2000
>
> If you were involved in Y2K remediation, you probably came in some time
over
> the weekend and did some final testing, checking, etc.  And this was
probably
> after many weeks, or months, or perhaps even years of tedious and boring
> work expanding files and pouring over source code.  After you found the
source
> code, that is.  I won't go in to all the problems with Y2K that have been
> thrashed
> in this ng for the last 2 years, suffice to say we all know what was
involved.
>
> So I've had three of four people today say *What's the big deal, nothing
> happened
> and I think it was all hype so you could make a bunch of overtime*.  BTW
I'm
> on salary and don't get overtime.   The final straw was when our postman
said
> his 486 rolled over just fine and he thought there should be a
congressional
> investigation of the whole pack of lies (his exact words).
>
> I've finally started saying that I wish I hadn't done a darn thing and let
it
> all go
> in the toilet.  When the roof fell in on our business system, maybe then t
hey
> would
> have realized how much work went into *Nothing Happened!*.  Sheesh, didn't
we
> all bust our butts so nothing would happen???
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> happened if we hadn't done it.  That *nothing happened* is a tribute to
us.
>
> rant mode disabled, thanks for letting me vent.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
JC:

I've heard the same and dismiss it as "ramblings from the uninformed."
Please read on...

jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347) wrote:

>Monday, 01/03/2000
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>investigation of the whole pack of lies (his exact words).

Ask your postman the following question...

1.  I will assume that he owns an automobile and that he routinely
takes the vehicle to a repair shop for an oil change.

2. Let's assume that when his car was manufactured, it was possible to
build an engine which never requires an oil change or perhaps one
which doesn't use oil at all, but each engine would cost $50,000.
Therefore, the manufacturers make a business decision to reduce the
cost in the short run knowing that at some point the oil must be
changed.

3.  When he *does* take the vehicle in for an oil change, in all
probability, the car is operating well, therefore the need to have
work on the vehicle is not visibly apparent.

4.  If the car is working, then why does he take it in for an oil
change?  Is there a conspiracy among the oil companies which are
simply a "pack of lies" (his words)?  Perhaps the oil companies simply
are telling people that it is necessary to change their engine oil
simply to sell more oil!  Perhaps the conspiracy extends to the engine
and automobile manufacturers who are all "in cahoots!"

5.  Therefore, because he cannot see any visible reason to change his
oil, he should never do so again.....and according to his logic, the
vehicle will continue to operate wonderfully for the next 200,000
miles (320,000 km for our metric friends).

In short, I believe that your postman suffers from an "occular-related
disease"...ergo..."I cannot see it, therefore it must never have
existed."

Apologies if my analogy stinks, but it was the only one my little
pea-brain could devise at 8:30 AM.

>I've finally started saying that I wish I hadn't done a darn thing and let it
>all go
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>all bust our butts so nothing would happen???

But if you would not have corrected the problems, then everything
would have crashed and burned and you would have been blamed that your
lack of action caused worldwide catastrophe.  Pardon the expression,
but I'm afraid that you are "damned if you do and you are damned if
you don't."

>My hat's off to all you other geeks out there that worked 50-60 (and more)
>hours
>a week getting it done.  We know what we did, and we know what would have
>happened if we hadn't done it.  That *nothing happened* is a tribute to us.

Absolute and total agreement.  Kudos to all who busted their rear ends
to prevent the massive system failure which would have most certainly
occurred.

>rant mode disabled, thanks for letting me vent.
>
>JC (not to be used as a flotation device) Jones


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net>`

```


Cheesle wrote:
> 
> "Bob Wolfe" <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> totally 01.01.2000. Now it is only you folks over there that is reactionay

It is funny how you 'Vikings' sometimes get it wrong <G> Yes UK did
switch to metric '73 I think for coinage; not sure of measurements.
I haven't been back since '75 but I understand you still buy gas/petrol
in gallons in the UK ?

Now as to metric - certainment au Canada ! We only buy in litres or
kilos - but of course if you want a chunk of wood for carpentry it is
still a 2 x 4 ! They cheat in the supermarkets and show us the
Avoirdupois pound equivalents for weight. And made in US or elsewhere
our autos show kms on the speedometer, though we also have mph on the
dial. Interestingly enough, (the system I work on is corrosion at oil
plants), whether or not you are in N. America or Europe, pipe size
diameters are expressed in inches ! And that's based on ASME tables
although there are the BS(British), DIN(German), UNI(Italian),
FR(French) etc. equivalents.

Come to think of it - if we could get the Irish and Brits to drive on
the 'right' side of the road - then we would all be in sync.

No bloody good you telling me how many kms Oslo is from Copenhagen - now
if you give it to me in miles, I'll understand. Remember, remember - it
was YOU Vikings who introduced this crap to Britannia, inches, feet,
yards...  :-)

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> (from U.S.A.)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> diagonally in inches.

People get paid money for giving advice <G>  :-

	1/4 (farthing) = quarter penny
	1/2 (halfpenny) 'hapney' 
	1d  (penny)
	1s  (one shilling - see the Viking connection) = 12 pence 						
(pennies)	
	20s (twenty shillings) = 1 pound sterling
	1G  (one guinea)       = 1 pound sterling plus 1 shilling

and just to add confusion to the above we had 3/4 (three farthing
coins), 6d (six pence), 2/6d (a half-crown - two shillings and
sixpence), and a long time ago I believe there was a crown (five
shillings).

Traditionally the silver three-farthing coin was put into Christmas puds
- the person getting it being the 'lucky' one. Then they swapped to a
bronze hexagonal coin.

As to the origin of the guinea I'm not sure, ( a wild guess, came in
during the Georgian/Hanover period) - but it used to have a 'snotty'
flavour to it - auctions for the fine arts being expressed in guineas
not pounds. Similarly racehorses were always sold in guineas.

Can you see why this particular rocket scientist was only too happy to
switch to decimal; the personal agonizing at school. You go figure -
calculate 3.456 % of 1 pound 17 shillings and threepence three-farthings
!!! 

Questions, questions. Paper sizes. Check an HP laser book - it quotes
the European, (also International (???)) paper sizes, A4, A5, A6 etc...
- forget which way round those numbers are but essentially each is a
'quarter' of the previous size. In UK we had foolscap, and from memory I
think that was slightly larger than our current N. American 8.5 x 11.

And the next question is ..........

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com>`

```
<jjgavan@home.com> writes
>People get paid money for giving advice <G>  :-
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You forgot:
   the thruppenny bit (threepence piece)
   the florin         (two shillings) - we share the Florin name with
                                        Holland.
   the ten bob        (ten shillings).

If you go further back we even had the Groat (dont ask me how much it
was). Note - the d (as in pounds shillings and pence) derived from a
Roman word for Denarius (I think) and the Shilling is also a unit of
currency in Austria.
```

```
<dlpolzin@coredcs.com> enlightened us:

>Or - How much does a "stone" weigh?
>Or - If my horse is  x  number of "hands" tall, how tall is that? / whose
>hands?
>
>

A stone = 14 pounds or 6.4 kilograms
A hand = 4 inches or 10.2 centimeters.
```

```
Is it true that the pound sterling is based upon silver, and the guinea
is based upon gold?  That would imply that the original coins differed
in size quite a bit.  And that their exchange rate would vary over the
centuries.

I also (as an outsider) have the impression that the guinea was
something the crown set up and the pound was something the banks set
up.  Is that correct?
```

```
My, I think I'm already going ga ga. Forgot the florin. Hold that cheque
Howard, I made a boo boo. It wasn't a three-farthing piece that went
into the Xmas puds but the three-penny (thrupenny) piece.

Alistair, unless I've really lost it the ten shillings was only ever a
note - rumbling back through my memory - can't think of a 10/- coin.
And yes the reference to shilling ties in with the Germanic schilling -
still in those days Angles-Saxons was another polite way of saying
Vikings. The buggers were still raping and pillaging, no matter which
way you cut the cookie <G>

four-letter word !

Jimmy
```

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<851mm1$ki7$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com> <tbs$AAAtl9c4EwNA@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:tbs$AAAtl9c4EwNA@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> In article <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com>, "James J. Gavan"
> <jjgavan@home.com> writes
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> you find out how strong they are.

The groat was fourpence I believe.   Also the s in LSD (pounds shillings
pence) was from the roman solidus coin not from shilling.

BTW Jimmy I don't think there was ever a three farthing coin.  I think
you're mixing it up with the thrupenny bit.  Also, one thing about the old
coinage was that everybody could do quite complex calculations in two
different number bases (20 and 12), including fractions.  If mathematics and
numeracy empowered a nation, we should have ruled the world.  We almost made
it.<G>

Rick
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sJ1d4.144$CJ1.4336@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com> <tbs$AAAtl9c4EwNA@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <851mm1$ki7$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
Rick Price wrote in message <851mm1$ki7$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>...
>
>BTW Jimmy I don't think there was ever a three farthing coin.  I think
>you're mixing it up with the thrupenny bit.  Also, one thing about the old
>coinage was that everybody could do quite complex calculations in two
>different number bases (20 and 12), including fractions.  If mathematics
and
>numeracy empowered a nation, we should have ruled the world.  We almost
made
>it.<G>
>


At one time didn't the British Empire control one-fifth the world's land
mass and population, never saw the sun set, and was undisputed master of the
seas? That sounds like ruling the world to me. This is not a bad thing:
Britain brought much civilisation(your spelling, my small form of homage) to
the globe, and in the disolution of its empire - willingly undertaken to
preserve the freedom of the Crown's subjects - has provided a model for
colonization without subjugation.

Just $0.02 (or is it twopence?) from the rebellious American provinces.

Michael Mattias
Racine WI USA

(Now, if you guys can just get a Tory government back! I got real nervous a
few weeks ago when I read of the little coffee klatsch held by Tony Blair,
Gerhard Schroeder and Lionel Jospin at which Bill Clinton was an invited
guest!)
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bQld4.139$5d4.11082@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com> <tbs$AAAtl9c4EwNA@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <851mm1$ki7$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <sJ1d4.144$CJ1.4336@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net>
>
> At one time didn't the British Empire control one-fifth the world's
land
> mass and population, never saw the sun set, and was undisputed
master of the
> seas? That sounds like ruling the world to me.

I heard it another way:

There was a time, not so very long ago, that every time Big Ben struck
the hour, the flag of the British Empire was being raised at sunrise
somewhere in the world. Today, every time the atomic clock at the
Bureau of Standards ticks a new second, the Microsoft Windows startup
logo appears on a screen somewhere.
```

- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com>`

```
And for anyone who still happens to have an (old) OS/VS COBOL compiler, try
using

   USAGE DISPLAY-ST

or either a "d" or "D" in your picture clause.

This is a truly useful feature <G>
   In case you get stuck in a pre-1970 English COBOL time-machine.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Unit conversions

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3874AA91.6C88F7D0@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 

> > When we buy a computer monitor, it is still is listed (inaccurately)
> > diagonally in inches.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> shillings).

I remember seeing a list of words showing every letter in the alphabet
as silent (one letter per word).  It had "halfpence", and a bunch of
other words with English pronunciations.  Since a majority of us Yanks
had ancestors literate before they spoke English, we tend to be more
phonetic than Brits.  If we see the letter, we pronounce it.

I read that the reason $2.00 bills are unpopular here is that the first
time they came out, they were worth about the same as the Spanish pieces
of 13 (an unlucky number).

> Can you see why this particular rocket scientist was only too happy to
> switch to decimal; the personal agonizing at school. You go figure -
> calculate 3.456 % of 1 pound 17 shillings and threepence three-farthings
> !!!

I have a clue.
 
Did you ever have to program such conversions?
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<38765839.911EE96@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com> <3874AA91.6C88F7D0@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <387556FB.8D08C313@worldnet.att.net> <387639AB.93D92B1C@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> I bet MOST Americans pronounce it as spelled, or at least much closer
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Bill Lynch

When in Rome do as the Romans do :-

	Worcester	= WOOSTER
	Worcestershire 	= WOOSTER-SHEER
	Birmingham	= BUR-MING-H'M
	Derby		= DAH-BEE

States : Birmingham	= BURR-MING-HAM
	 Derby		= DERR-BEE

But note bene - UK and New England :-

	Porstmouth	= PORTS-MT'H

Check Bill K's dialog/dialogue - he knows when a vacation is a holiday
<G>

Jimmy
```

```
William Lynch wrote:

Just so long as you are referring to Lea and Perrins - not the junk from
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unit conversions

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<852dc2$l3$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com> <3874AA91.6C88F7D0@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
We certainly did.  But it wasn't a problem.  For interest calculations etc
you just converted l.s.d to pence.  Then it just becomes as simple as
working in decimal currencies.

Also I seem to remember that some manufacturers supplied l.s.d calculation
sub routines to make it even simpler.  However this was the sixties and 'who
can remember the sixties' (WCRTS?).

Rick

Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:3874AA91.6C88F7D0@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
> James J. Gavan wrote:
> >
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> Did you ever have to program such conversions?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unit conversions

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3874F85B.29A6BD52@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com> <3874AA91.6C88F7D0@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:
> > Can you see why this particular rocket scientist was only too happy to
> > switch to decimal; the personal agonizing at school. You go figure -
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Did you ever have to program such conversions?

No, wasn't programming back then, just designing. But I think I can say
with certainty that any language compiler took that headache away and
did it for you. Similarly, switching to decimal coinage overnight was a
snip - regardless of corporate size, you just ran a manufacturer's
converter against your transaction and master files - no horror stories.

And of course before decimalisation our mainframe print barrels had the
characters for pound sterling, 1/2 1/4 and 3/4 the fractions obviously
being used for coinage - but selling things like cheese - that could
also finish as fractions of a pound - say a 56 lb Cheddar at 323/-
(shillings) per cwt - actual weight was 55 3/4 lbs, so price extension
on invoice had to reflect that.

If Rick is reading this - yep I picked up on my boo boo confusing three
farthings with the thrupenny bit (three pence piece). Info we had on
guinea was interesting - had no idea of its origin. Interesting TRIVIA
about coinage that's been gone over a quarter century ago, and to cap it
all, used by those people who lived in the last century <G>

Now pronunciations - I think you mentioned that topic Howard :- 

Sri Lankan friend's sister lives in Texas, I believe some 60 miles north
of Dallas. With a convent upbringing in Ceylon her English is as good as
mine. Anyway she works for a law office, their main business being
real-estate, property.

Phone rings with query, so to focus, she asks caller "Which county do
you live in ?". "Huh ?", comes reply. In the end she gave up. "I had to
go Texan" she said.

"Which COW'NEE do you live in ?". Ah! Now they understood. Aw shucks
pardner <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Jerry@zonko.co.uk (Jerry Ozaniec)
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<memo.20000106094817.105B@zonko.cix.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com>`

```
In article <3873D47E.7E6D961C@home.com>, jjgavan@home.com (James J. Gavan) 
wrote:

> As to the origin of the guinea I'm not sure, ( a wild guess, came in
> during the Georgian/Hanover period) - but it used to have a 'snotty'
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> not pounds. Similarly racehorses were always sold in guineas.

It represented the 5% buyer's premium paid to the auctioneer, so bidding 
could be done in whole numbers, the seller knowing how many pounds (s)he 
would receive, the seller knowing how many guineas (s)he had to pay and 
the auctioneer knowing how many shillings (s)he would get as commission.

Jerry
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84vst8$au0$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
> What standard paper sizes are common around the world?  In the U.S., the
> big one is 8�" x 11", but books vary more.  Do computer printers vary

In Europe, at least in northern Europe, the format A4 is most common for
laser / ink printers. It is slightly longer than your letter format, and the
letter format is a bit wider than A4. So, essentially I believe the total
expanse is the same of the two formats. When it comes to matrix printers, we
probably are more like you, using 8x12 or something like that.

As for printers, I guess it is pretty much the same, we use matrix, where
matrix serves the best, ink/laser/termo where those meet the need.

> much?  Is greenbar still used much - with what sizes?

What is a greenbar? (pardon me if this is a stupid question).

> When we buy a computer monitor, it is still is listed (inaccurately)
> diagonally in inches.

As it is here, TV's as well. Funny how measures for certain items totally
breaks the standard, and is accepted as it.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3873AABA.8060C4C9@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com> <84usvh$ljr$1@news.cerf.net> <38735370.5D3F780D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <84vst8$au0$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
Cheesle wrote:
> 

> What is a greenbar? (pardon me if this is a stupid question).
> 

Old mainframe computers printed on 11" long paper which was maybe 14"
wide with perforations on each side for the cogwheel.  Depending on your
setting, the paper alternated between 3 lines on green (or blue)
background, and three on white.  Usually the printer had a band with the
alphabet on it maybe 3 times.  The band went around with hammers over
each print position and printed very fast (for an impact printer).  It
was sort of a line at a time, since any character on that line would be
hammered whenever the character was at the right place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3873C763.344376B8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <84ug7201mtg@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```


Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> So then, maybe sometime in the year 2212, we will have to remediate all the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Excuse my ignorance but is there COBOL in languages other than English?

My guess is no. But there is a feature in NetExpress, associated with OO
classes where you can use a 'vocabulary' to 'swap' words - so you could
substitute SCHRIEBE for WRITE etc. (WMK - is this part of new Standards
?). As you'll appreciate, there's absolutely nothing to stop you
describing your record formats or variables in the 'local' language -
and probably they wouldn't hit very many RESERVED words.

But from a practical point-of-view, if you switch to Dutch or German,
you lose something in communication. You can discuss with your local
peers, but using the 'Net - you certainly wouldn't be able to seek/give
advice from/to us N.American 'Anglos'- not even with the help of
Babelfish !. (Reminds me - must ask Jose if he's yet written a
Portuguese version of PC_PRINT <G>).

Jimmy
```

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84vsf5$31$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <3872D7CA.14C9B56D@worldnet.att.net> <84usej$ldv$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
You notice that these places are all islands?  It doesn't matter what side
they drive on.  They haven't got problems with borders.   However saying
that, Sweden only moved from the left to the right in the last 30 or 40
years and I guess there weren't too many problems crossing the border to
Norway.

BTW I'm not sure these places copied from us.  I thought that everyone used
to pass on the left.  Something to do with having the sword arm nearest a
possible attacker.  I thought Napoleon changed the rules in mainland Europe.

So as you can see, we drive on the right side.  Everyone else is wrong <G>.

Rick
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 

> Now as to metric - certainment au Canada ! We only buy in litres or
> kilos - but of course if you want a chunk of wood for carpentry it is
> still a 2 x 4 ! 

And in the U.S. we buy milk in quarts, cola in liters, and whiskey in
fifths!!!  When people were talking about going metric there was a lot
of grumbling about having to translate.  But people never translated
between quarts (1/4 gallon) and fifths (1/5 gallon).  When I drive in a
metric country, I read the speed limits and look at the metric dial on
the speedometer.

The only place I care is when I keep records of my mileage to calculate
how many miles/gallon my car gets (this helps me tell when I need a tune
up).  If I am buying gallons, I don't want to switch to liters and visa
versa.

When I was a child I wondered how deep 20,000 leagues under the sea
was.  I had no idea how long a furlong was, nor even a fortnight.

I suppose the first country to come up with an accurate clock also set
the standard for how to measure days.  I don't know - history books
mention how useful it was for navigation, but I haven't heard that China
or Indonesia measured days in tight increments.

When Hirohito died, most of Japan gave up on any pretense of using a
calendar based upon the emperor's reign, and most of the world (even
using other calendars), celebrated the millennium.
```

```
Wow I was really surprised to learn it's cola in liters(res). Perhaps

Gallon. In addition to being El Supremo for Parkland Data Systems Ltd, I

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3873D066.8A9BB1E@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Wow I was really surprised to learn it's cola in liters(res). Perhaps
> more insiduous, which you didn't mention - is the Imperial and U.S.
> Gallon. 

Insidious if you don't know.  Sort of like comparing $ with $ or even
pesos with pesos.

> In addition to being El Supremo for Parkland Data Systems Ltd, I
> am also designated chief cook and bottle-washer. The confusion over
> pints, fluid ounces, US cup sizes etc. when you are trying to use an old
> English cookbook with everything in weight (Lbs) and Imperial pints !

I talked with a Russian how her cookbooks handled teaspoons vs
tablespoons, and she replied that she never used measuring spoons, it
was all guesswork and whatever spoons she found.
 
> PS: If you know how, let me know - how the hell do you get a cup of
> butter, when the cup is round and the bloody butter is square !

We have two different shapes of butter cubes here, neither are cubic. 
Out west they are shorter and fatter than they are in the Eastern part
of the U.S.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** georgekorz@cs.com (George K)
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000109000012.22011.00000249@nso-fn.news.cs.com>`
- **References:** `<$lDb$PAW0zd4EwFU@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
I can see there aren't  many cooks in this group! So here are two practical
ways:
1- you get a glass, two-cup measuring cup and fill it with one cup of ice
water. You then add enough globs (technical cooking term) of cold butter to
displace one cup. In other words, when you push the butter under the water with
a fork, the level rises to the two cup level.
2- You use soft, room temperature butter and simply pack it in till full.

Wouldn't it be great if fixing a corrupted index could be this easy?
George K

George K
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387A5CCF.53675ABA@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<$lDb$PAW0zd4EwFU@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <20000109000012.22011.00000249@nso-fn.news.cs.com> <ZmoiPJA3Tge4EwIK@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <c5lk7s0nrj8523ivmlrv9kjp1ao0is5l5s@4ax.com>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> and, btw, in Germany nobody ever refers to butter by 'cups', but by 'grams'  -  but that
> would be to sophisticated for my American friends  <beg>

Professional bakers use weight here.  Butter in the U.S. comes in pound
boxes with 4 "cubes", each surrounded by paper or foil which is marked
with tablespoon increments.  So whether your recipe is for weight or
volume, a simple knife is all that's needed.  Now shortening is another
story.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387AB27A.53D60C2C@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<$lDb$PAW0zd4EwFU@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <20000109000012.22011.00000249@nso-fn.news.cs.com> <ZmoiPJA3Tge4EwIK@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <c5lk7s0nrj8523ivmlrv9kjp1ao0is5l5s@4ax.com>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> Talk about topic drift.... And one thing all of you overlook:  What the h... is a cup in
> the first place?  I use to drink tea out of a cup which other people might call a
> bucket....., and the cups used for the espresso are rather smallish - so, what gives?

A "cup" is still a common standard measurement here in the States. It's
8 fluid oz., or a half-pint (I'm sure all these measurements sound
bizarre to you). Maybe we'll catch up with the rest of the world
someday? We inherited these measures from the English, then they went
and converted to metric:-(

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

```
it wouldn't help.

of the day <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<855e56$fop$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com> <850t3q$5t2$1@news.igs.net> <8513ic$q8j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3874AB9E.D518A00A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <853fnd$k0f$1@news.igs.net> <3875FE8D.5D2E9B8A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@nospamwebaccess.net> wrote:
:> Is not the American "fifth" of Liquor an Imperial Quart?

: desktop dictionary, which said 3.a. one fifth of a gallon (U.S.), a
: measure used for alcoholic beverages.  But that is an American
: dictionary - are whiskey bottles intended for export to the U.S.
: different sized than whiskey bottles consumed in the U.K.?

A US pint is 16 fluid ounces, a gallon is 8 pints or 128 fl.oz.
On fifth of this is 25.6 fl.oz, the 'normal' size of a bottle
of beer or whiskey.  

These bottles are actually a 'reputed quart'.  The 40 oz size
is an imperial quart.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8569fv$95d$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com> <850t3q$5t2$1@news.igs.net> <8513ic$q8j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3874AB9E.D518A00A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <853fnd$k0f$1@news.igs.net> <3875FE8D.5D2E9B8A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Liquor in Canada comes in 26 oz. bottles. An Imperial gallon is 160 oz, or
ten pounds of water.  The Imperial quart is 40 oz.  Damned if I know why for
any of it.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v8ed4.2375$R51.27743@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com> <850t3q$5t2$1@news.igs.net> <8513ic$q8j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3874AB9E.D518A00A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <853fnd$k0f$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Just a note from the token British Ex-pat.
Imperial gallon is about 1.2 US (junior) gallons.
Since moving to the US - 6 years ago - I have become something
of an expert in converting, whenever I phone my relatives in Blighty.
Would you believe that my Camaro (SS) only gets about 16 miles
to the (US) gallon, at an unsteady 55-155MPH?

Also, for those who care - it's LITRE - not LITER. This is another problem
with a country where the spoken word is king. But, for those, about to get
upset about "those damn foreigners", I'm about to apply for citizenship.
I feel like a citizen of the North Atlantic at the moment. I still feel a
bit out
of place in Arizona, but I feel VERY out of place, when I go back to "the
old country".

P.S. anyone who thinks - "there ain't no such thing as a free lunch", hasn't
spoken
to a consultant recently - Long Live Cobol - 370 !!!

"donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
news:853fnd$k0f$1@news.igs.net...
>
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <3874AB9E.D518A00A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38760020.8FB5CBCB@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com> <850t3q$5t2$1@news.igs.net> <8513ic$q8j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3874AB9E.D518A00A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <853fnd$k0f$1@news.igs.net> <v8ed4.2375$R51.27743@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
Dave Cawdell wrote:
> 
> Also, for those who care - it's LITRE - not LITER. This is another problem
> with a country where the spoken word is king. 

I don't think that's the problem.  The problem is that in the U.S. most
people were literate when they immigrated and learned the language.  So
we're far more phonetic than the Brits.  Although look at today's
http://www.randomhouse.com/wotd/ discussing pronunciation of lieutenant
and colonel.

I lived in Mexico for a couple of years as a child and want to pronounce
Mango their way, not ours.  The town of Nevada Iowa is not pronounced
like the state and neither are pronounced like the Spanish word for
snow.

I don't mind spelling liter, what I want to spell phonetically backwards
(or Brit style) is theatre.  Don't know why, I am gradually learning to
change - mainly by not putting theatre in my spell checker.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8569ft01uid@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com> <850t3q$5t2$1@news.igs.net> <8513ic$q8j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <3874AB9E.D518A00A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <853fnd$k0f$1@news.igs.net> <v8ed4.2375$R51.27743@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
> Would you believe that my Camaro (SS) only gets about 16 miles
> to the (US) gallon, at an unsteady 55-155MPH?

NIce car.  Does that have leather?  I've never driven an LS1 but I once
tested an LT1 Camaro 6 speed.  The gas pedal felt directly connected to the
engine.  Push down, more power.  Amazing that it didn't matter what gear it
was in and the handling was very precise and amazingly flat.  Reported top
speed is what 150+ stable mph with brakes that match a 911?  American value,
pure and simple.

>
> Also, for those who care - it's LITRE - not LITER. This is another problem
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> to a consultant recently - Long Live Cobol - 370 !!!

Amazing stuff, COBOL is.  I had a user visit me today, cute young woman.  We
were looking at her report distributions in InfoPac and one of the reports
didn't seem quite right.  I went into ROSCOE and looked at the PROC JCL.  In
a matter of seconds, I had the program nailed down.  OOPS, no logic in here
for the recent application expansion.  I jotted it down on some stickies.

The cute young woman says "You read that that fast?"  Uh, it really wasn't
that hard.  That's the beauty of JCL and COBOL.

Jeff

>
> "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com> wrote in message
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38742495.6790C9C8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com> <850t3q$5t2$1@news.igs.net> <8513ic$q8j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```


Russell Styles wrote:
> 
> 
>     Most US citizens have probably never heard of the Imperial gallon.
>  Is it really used somewhere?

Wont put money on it, but would suggest probably every English speaking
country with the exception of the U. S. of A. used the IMPERIAL gallon
until such time as they went metric, (and probably still do if they
aren't metric)  - UK, Republic of Ireland, Australia, Canada, New
Zealand, Malta, Gibraltar, South Africa and the former African English
speaking 'colonies', West Indies, Pakistan, India, Malaysia, Singapore,
Burma(?), Sri Lanka(Ceylon) etc...... 

It's a wild guess, but with the British 'presence' since WW I (1918),
could well be that the Imperial gallon is the de facto standard in
Iran(Persia), Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Egypt etc... But who knows, with the
power of the greenback they may have switched to US gallons.

Don't feel put down. In truth most other English speakers just knew they
had gallons; they probably weren't aware of the US gallon. I certainly
wasn't until I came to Canada. Any good dictionary mentions both in 
weights and measurement tables.

You had your tea party in Boston, Britain 'had' its empire.

Jimmy (PS: Did say I wouldn't put money on it)
```
