- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com> <850t3q$5t2$1@news.igs.net> <8513ic$q8j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38742495.6790C9C8@home.com>`

On Thu, 06 Jan 2000 05:18:28 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
enlightened us:

>
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy (PS: Did say I wouldn't put money on it)

The British system of weights and measures was at one time known as
the Imperial system, thus the Imperial gallon.

A gallon in America is a unit of volume in the U.S. Customary System,
used in liquid measure, equal to 4 quarts (3.785 liters). 

An Imperial gallon is a unit of volume in the British Imperial System,
used in liquid and dry measure, equal to 4 quarts (4.546 liters). 

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Should crematoriums give discounts for burn victims?


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Unit conversions

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3874E4BA.23365C10@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com> <850t3q$5t2$1@news.igs.net> <8513ic$q8j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38742495.6790C9C8@home.com> <1F66517B44F17393.86E58BBCC8F4AF9D.EE5EAA589D01337B@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> 

> A gallon in America is a unit of volume in the U.S. Customary System,
> used in liquid measure, equal to 4 quarts (3.785 liters).
> 
> An Imperial gallon is a unit of volume in the British Imperial System,
> used in liquid and dry measure, equal to 4 quarts (4.546 liters).

So in the U.S. a fifth of scotch is a fifth of a gallon.  I always
assumed that is a U.S. gallon, but scotch is a British product.  It has
to be the same size as a fifth of bourbon though.  I am not much of a
drinker, so I don't know these things very well.

A barrel is: "the amount contained in a barrel; especially : the amount
(as 31 gal. of fermented beverage or 42 gal. of petroleum) fixed for a
certain commodity used as a unit of measure"

Now that's confusing.  Want to get started on wines?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Pete Maddock <Frank@maddockp.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uZswSKAqjgd4EwVL@maddockp.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com> <850t3q$5t2$1@news.igs.net> <8513ic$q8j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38742495.6790C9C8@home.com> <1F66517B44F17393.86E58BBCC8F4AF9D.EE5EAA589D01337B@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Both Gallons = 8 pints.

1 pint (US) = 16 fluid ounces. 1 pint (British) = 20 fluid ounces.

Hence the difference. (1 Gallon British = 10 lb weight, which is handy.
Also, from an earlier entry in this thread, 1 stone = 14 pounds weight).

>
>A gallon in America is a unit of volume in the U.S. Customary System,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3874321F.E9860F89@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3871f575.1571551@news.enter.net> <84t7mm$h0s$1@news.cerf.net> <3872A1FD.86B95E7A@home.com> <387351EE.953EBD55@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3873CC40.73C81DB5@home.com> <38741EFD.DC421ADD@worldnet.att.net>`

```


William Lynch wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

You get an "E" for Effort too. In you wildest dreams, does your good
lady let you make a cake with WARMED butter ? I bet your wife doesn't
use Heloise as a reference. BUT ------ if you want to use warm butter,
try this :-

1.	approx 1/2 cup butter
	approx 1/2 cup Brown sugar
2.	melt and stir on gentle heat, don't let sugar crystallize
3.      The good part - now add 1/2 cup ( or a real good slurp) of 
	Dark rum.
4. 	Stir and pour mixture over two peeled bananas and broil until
	heated.

5.	D-E-E-L-I-S-H-O-U-S !

Gavan's @Home Catering Service
```

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00

Everything went so well, all of the reporters and news folks have to come up
with something to keep the Y2K thing going...Pretty pathetic if you ask
me...These people do not have the slightest bit of real knowledge about what
they are reporting...Then again, what's new about that...:-)

> Worse than that, I have heard people on the radio claim that it was all

To some extent the people on your radio has been right. Although, not
programmers, but advisors, has been profiting a lot on the Y2K problem.

Many advisor companies has in my opinion exaggerated the problem greatly
with no other goal than making a profit on it. But this is not something
that is unique to the Y2K business. Just take a look at the so called oracle
Mr. Negroponte in the US, it is amazing how that man is being paid thousands
of dollars to "predict" what everybody knows.

Understand me correctly, I agree that Y2K was an issue, I have worked with
it myself, where it was necessary. But it is also correct that there was all
this hype that the so called advisors made around it.

However, it is history now. The world keeps moving forward (where else
should it go?).

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Non-programmers think Y2K was hype... and I'm angry!

- **From:** Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85ihfq$d36$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20000103144807.02174.00000058@ng-fd1.aol.com>,
  jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347) wrote:
> Monday, 01/03/2000
>
> If you were involved in Y2K remediation, you probably came in some
time over
> the weekend and did some final testing, checking, etc.  And this was
probably
> after many weeks, or months, or perhaps even years of tedious and
boring
> work expanding files and pouring over source code.  After you found
the source
> code, that is.  I won't go in to all the problems with Y2K that have
been
> thrashed
> in this ng for the last 2 years, suffice to say we all know what was
involved.
>
> So I've had three of four people today say *What's the big deal,
nothing
> happened
> and I think it was all hype so you could make a bunch of overtime*.
BTW I'm
> on salary and don't get overtime.   The final straw was when our
postman said
> his 486 rolled over just fine and he thought there should be a
congressional
> investigation of the whole pack of lies (his exact words).

Of course it is out of the realm of possibilities because of all of the
OT put in.

Well those same people who hold this attitude are the same who read and
influenced by Art Bell & The Weekly World News.  Did you see the Weekly
World News, like the week b4 the rollover?  Frightening headlines
promising widespread power outages, cars & appliances with
microprocessors would not run, bank failures and so on.  Art was going
off on how the most technologically advanced organization in the world
(NASA) was afraid of this bug (i.e. from the Y2k driven decision to have
the shuttle back on the ground).

We are not out of the Y2k bug world yet.  The media hype surrounded all
of the real-time & controller computers crapping out.  By definition
these system are not especially sensitive to the format of the date,
they just want to know when a particular event happens,  oooh its been 1
minute (I don't care what minute) since I have checked the load on that
line etc.

Anyone who sees the news should see that there are real problems yet and
wonder what would be going on w/o Y2K remediation.  The first post y2k
paycheck cycle rapidly approaches for eveyone, that is the kind thing
that is touchy to date format.  Also why have we all been instructed to
carefully review our January credit card bills?  A real y2k problem
there.

The Arabs have a wonderful saying:  The dogs bark but the caravan moves
on.  Like you I salute all of those who kept the caravan rolling despite
all of the whoofing dogs then & now.
```

---

<a id="t-ebb22c4593"></a>
## Thread: MS-COBOL? — 20 msgs

### MS-COBOL?

- **From:** Torsten Allar <allar@allar.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3870784E.D655BDE9@allar.net>`

```
Hi all,
does anyone know, where MS-COBOL is going to?
Is it MICRO-FOCUS now?
I have a problem with MS-COBOL and Y2K. The
Runtime-Interpreter allways return 1999 as the actual
year. Is there an update for MS-COBOL? Maybe
an cross-update to MICRO-FOCUS? Or does
anyone know a bug-fix to the runtime-interpreter?
Thanks for any answer
Torsten
```

#### ↳ Re: MS-COBOL?

- **From:** riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84qrst$kdq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3870784E.D655BDE9@allar.net>`

```
In article <3870784E.D655BDE9@allar.net>,
  Torsten Allar <allar@allar.net> wrote:
> Hi all,
> does anyone know, where MS-COBOL is going to?
> Is it MICRO-FOCUS now?

MS-Cobol versions 1 & 2 were developed by MS.  Versions 4 & 5
were rebadged MicroFocus Cobol with an offset of 2 in the version
number.  So MF 2.5 -> MS 4.5.

The last MS was 5.0 which was MF 3.0 (though there were rumours
of a 5.1 I never saw it).  When MF opened its US office it went
back to direct selling and MS dropped the product.  This was
several years ago.

> I have a problem with MS-COBOL and Y2K. The

Which version ?

> Runtime-Interpreter allways return 1999 as the actual
> year.

That seems unlikey as ACCEPT FROM DATE will only give a
two digit year.  What exactly is returning '1999' (as
opposed to '99' ?

> Is there an update for MS-COBOL?

Which version do you have ?

> Maybe
> an cross-update to MICRO-FOCUS? Or does

There were upgrades and cross-grades available for several
years, but expired now.

> anyone know a bug-fix to the runtime-interpreter?

Which version ?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MS-COBOL?

- **From:** "michael vasmatics" <vasmati@snet.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84u0i6$6uq@news1.snet.net>`
- **References:** `<3870784E.D655BDE9@allar.net> <84qrst$kdq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>
> That seems unlikey as ACCEPT FROM DATE will only give a
> two digit year.  What exactly is returning '1999' (as
> opposed to '99' ?
>
Pleas keep in mind as you read, that I'm a student and this maybe all wet.
I would like to point out that in microfocus the FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE will
return a 4 digit year if your system is set right.
I had to change the system long date and short date to YYYY and then I got
my homework programs to recieve 4 digit year.
The system is windows 98
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MS-COBOL?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84u514$7ko$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3870784E.D655BDE9@allar.net> <84qrst$kdq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <84u0i6$6uq@news1.snet.net>`

```
I don't usually "blast" classes (or students) but if you (any reader) have a
class that is using "MS-COBOL", then something should be done about that
class.  That compiler is INCREDIBLY out of date (and not supported).  As
indicated, Micro Focus (MERANT) has an "academic" edition compiler that you
could be using.  You could be using the free (slightly out-of-date) Fujitsu
compiler - or any of a number of other compilers.

Please feel free to pass my email address on to ANY instructor having
students use "MS-COBOL" (and I will be MORE than happy to correspond directly
with him/her).

NOTE WELL:
   Any current "conforming compiler" (that supports the '89 Amendment to the
'85 Standard) will support a 4-digit year via the "FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE"
intrinsic function.  Many compilers ALSO include an extension (that will be
Standard with the next revision) of

    ACCEPT data-name FROM DATE YYYYMMDD

to get the 4-digit year.  HOWEVER, (as stated before) any CONFORMING compiler
will return ONLY a 2-digit year if you use the (currently standard)

   ACCEPT data-name FROM DATE

syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MS-COBOL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Well,
  One that was developed (and tested) on DOS 3.3 (as I recall - later some
testing was done with Windows 3.1 - but not much).  One that has LOTS of bugs
in it that the vendor has fixed in 6 or 7 releases since then.  One without
the "enhancements" from the Intrinsic Function Amendment (1989) MUCH less the
Correction Amendment (1991).

Do you want hear more? <G>
```

#### ↳ Re: MS-COBOL?

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38728C8F.4F429AD3@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<3870784E.D655BDE9@allar.net>`

```
Torsten Allar wrote:
> 
> Hi all,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Torsten

MS Cobol has been dead (unsupported) for years.
MS has dropped it some time ago and it is now supported 
by MF in their compiler.

If you do not have MF, then you can get Fujitsu compiler
and use our MF2FSC converter that also converts
MS Cobol to Fujitsu Cobol.

More on this at http://www.siber.com/sct/tools/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MS-COBOL?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00

- **References:** `<3870784E.D655BDE9@allar.net> <38728C8F.4F429AD3@siber.com> <387299DE.BDC57067@home.com> <j5Jc4.533$jC3.11478@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <38744680.9B361EE1@siber.com> <387525CA.BFFDB80C@home.com>`

```
MS-COBOL was MF-COBOL with a workbench built by MICROSOFT.  In fact, the
version of MF COBOL was always one release behind the current MF COBOL
compiler.  So how can MS compete with MF.  I do not think the MF would have
benefited from teaming up with MS.  That is why MS dropped it's version of
COBOL.

George McGinn
Systems Engineer
GTE-TSI

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-08T00:00:00

```
Just so it is clear (from "ancient" history), there were IBM and MS COBOL
compilers BEFORE they each started using the Micro Focus product (in the
mid-80s).  If you have a Micro Focus compiler with an IBM-MS (or is it
MS-IBM) directive, that is the compiler that it is "emulating".
```

- **Message-ID:** `<84r9ja$usf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have been altering some *.cob files and after overcoming some initial
problems, i am stuck on the following error message :

Cobol procedure error 204. Error loading main program

Can anybody give some hints/tips/solutions ?

I am not an experienced user so please don't get to technical on me !?

Thanks for your help!

kind regards,
m van huenen


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/

#### ↳ Re: Cobol error message

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38713635.86F79439@home.com>`
- **References:** `<84r9ja$usf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


m.van.huenen@chello.nl wrote:
> 
> I have been altering some *.cob files and after overcoming some initial
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Tell us which compiler you are using - then somebody can zero in for
you. "Error 204. Error loading main program..." doesn't look like a
Micro Focus(Merant) error - but just in case, as you've been changing
programs :-

M/F Error 204 - wrong level numbers for redefines :-

01 my-record.
   05 my-numberA	pic 9(08).
   10 my-numberB
      redefines my-numberA.
      15 my-cc		pic 9(02).
      15 my-yy		pic 9(02).
      15 my-mm	        pic 9(02).
      15 my-dd          pic 9(02).

The Level 10 against my-numberB should read "05 my-numberB"; you can
leave the Level 15s 'as is..'.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol error message

- **From:** "Anton Rusbach" <a.rusbach@coss.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84slrc$hjj$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`
- **References:** `<84r9ja$usf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
> Cobol procedure error 204. Error loading main program

looks like RMCobol.. you are trying to run a .cob file that doesn't excist
or the CobolOBject was corrupted

succes,
Anton
```

<a id="t-83143a4608"></a>
## Thread: I am NOT a lawyer, ... — 27 msgs

### I am NOT a lawyer, ...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I am NOT a lawyer - nor do I "play one" on the internet <G>

(For those outside of the US, this refers to an old series of TV ads that
start, "I'm not a doctor, but I play one on TV.)

HOWEVER, there are some legal issues that I thought I would "bring to your
attention" (and ask you to pass on to anyone else who might be interested).
And YES, this is "ON-topic" to COBOL.

Please see a paper (particularly the *SECOND* Issue) that I submitted to J4
(the ANSI-ish) COBOL group which can be viewed at:

    http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/00-0172.doc

If you think that either of these issues impact you (or your "vendor of
choice" - or any language reference manual, text-book, or other hard- or
soft-copy material) that you might have access to in the future, please do
NOT respond to me.  For a change, I do NOT recommend you contacting J4 itself
(which can really do nothing about this).  Instead, you need to express your
concerns and/or ask for clarification from the NCITS (formerly X3)
Secretariat.  For information on how to contact them, go to

    http://www.ncits.org/

This really only applies to US residents or those doing business in the US.
If you are in another country, then I recommend that you contact YOUR
"national Standards body" *now* (not AFTER the next COBOL Standard gets
published). For information about "your" national body, see

    http://www.iso.ch/members/index.html

****

NOTE WELL:
  This may seem to many to be an "obscure" topic that you don't need to worry
about - that someone else will "solve" for you.  However, I think this is
something that SIGNIFICANT "user-input" can stop before it starts.
```

#### ↳ Re: I am NOT a lawyer, ...

- **From:** "Steve Thompson" <sthompson2@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RVLc4.720$Zo.31781@sandstorm.neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I am not an attorney, solicitor, or lawyer.  However, I believe that you may
be over reacting here.

The standards group here is a quasi-governmental body.  And, publishers of
COBOL texts are members of each part of this standards group.  Therefore, I
think it highly unlikely that they [any part of the standards committee(s)]
would cause such a problem with copyrights as you are suggesting.  But, even
should they do this, would not the documents they publish/develop not be a
public domain item to start with?

Now, knowing that the "HAL" Corp. does make large use of this matter (or at
least has in the past) and has put it in their licensed material as well as
their general available matter (both of which are copyright protected as I
recall), I don't think they will stand by and allow such a thing to happen.

Regards,
Steve Thompson
OSP LLC
330/335-9907 office
330/334-2097 fax


William M. Klein wrote in message <84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>...
>I am NOT a lawyer - nor do I "play one" on the internet <G>
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>    http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/00-0172.doc
<snip>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: I am NOT a lawyer, ...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85092j$mlo$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <RVLc4.720$Zo.31781@sandstorm.neo.rr.com>`

```
Actually, both the ANSI and ISO Standards groups are getting VERY much into
the "copyrighted" material business - and selling "restricted" copies of
their documents.  So far, the charges they have made for copies have been
"semi-reasonable"  (although the current ANSI COBOL Standard costs $85 - the
last time I checked.)  Their BIG issue is that they are trying (for other
Standards) to PREVENT anyone from distributing them (in part or whole) on the
web.  For the "new" Standards, I think they can "get away with this" - but
for the COBOL Standard (because of the old rules), I do not think they can.

For the C "language standard" (for example) they went thru a long
"discussion" with Bell Labs (as I recall) and basically would NOT publish it
as an ANSI Standard until Bell "signed-over" the rights to them.

They have, for other languages, also REQUIRED all vendors to get permission
before they could quote from the Standard in their reference materials.

So, NO, I don't think I am over-reacting.  However, if you sent your "view"
as a question to NCITS, then they could give you their "official" position on
it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am NOT a lawyer, ...

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3873C343.F4B573D6@home.com>`
- **References:** `<84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <RVLc4.720$Zo.31781@sandstorm.neo.rr.com> <85092j$mlo$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
So you are not a lawyer - who are you kidding <G> Now put your Rumpole
wig on for a second.

So far as I can tell the 'Good Book' isn't copyrighted. We are a
multi-faceted family so I checked an American King James version plus an
English Catholic version - see, we are even 'bi-lingual' - neither
indicates a copyright.

Bearing in mind that caveat in COBOL literature, "COBOL is an industry
language and is not the property of any company or group of
companies.....", how can there be a suggestion of copyright. OK for HAL,
Fujitisu, Merant etc. to say that their specific material is copyrighted
- but surely not the 'Standards', a body set up to promulgate the
original COBOL concept. Under law it would be like a central/federal
government introducing legislation which attempts to abrogate common
law, and any higher court rulings. (?)

Arguably they could make a case for a modest cost for materials - and
that is no big deal to corporate purchasers. But I betcha if lil' old 
me, a one-man band, pleaded with the courts that their actions
prohibited me from effectively studying/using COBOL........?

Remember the incident, quoted in Jerry Garfunkel's 'COBOL 85' -
Travellers' Insurance putting the kybosh on the first revision to '74. 
The instigator, Brophy. His 'cause' generated 2,300 negative
communications to the Standards' people. ('Brophy' - Hmmm - another
bloody Irishman <G>).

Jimmy ( a former common law, contractual law student; and like Brophy,
with roots going back to the Auld Sod <G>)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am NOT a lawyer, ...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<850hn5$7l5$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <RVLc4.720$Zo.31781@sandstorm.neo.rr.com> <85092j$mlo$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <3873C343.F4B573D6@home.com>`

```
Just so it is clear, the '85 Standard, the '89 Intrinsic Functions Amendment,
and the '91 Corrections Amendment *all* include a "COPYRIGHT" statement.
*However* - they also include the wording giving everyone the right to copy
any or all of the document.  I have previously let my opinion of what that
does to the "copyright" be known (to ANSI).  HOWEVER, now that there is talk
of ISO *REMOVING* the "Acknowledgement" - I do think this needs a LOT of
public (international and US) comment ASAP.

Therefore, Jimmy -
  Please DO contact the Canadian National Body at:

    Canada (SCC)
    Address:
    Standards Council of Canada
    45 O'Connor Street, Suite 1200
    Ottawa, Ontario K1P 6N7

        Telephone:+1 613 238 32 22
        Telefax:+1 613 995 45 64
        E-mail:info@scc.ca
        Web:http://www.scc.ca/

and let them know what you think about this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am NOT a lawyer, ...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** stringer@es.co.nz (stringer)
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<947117251.744350@newsch.es.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <RVLc4.720$Zo.31781@sandstorm.neo.rr.com> <85092j$mlo$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <3873C343.F4B573D6@home.com> <850hn5$7l5$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <850hn5$7l5$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>, wmklein@nospam.netcom.com 
says...
>
>Just so it is clear, the '85 Standard, the '89 Intrinsic Functions Amendment,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Snipped....>--
>Bill Klein


WELL, I AM A LAWYER

And I say, write!

Its not what happens now that's so important as what some mealy minded 
beurocrat might do in the future when he is told to implement the 'user pays' 
principle.

In this country (NZ) we now have copyright included in our statutes! but we got 
an assurance from the Government at the time that it would not be used to 
restrict access in any way to the laws of the land.

If you want an example, our High Court Code is a good one.
In the early days of (last) century, these were compiled into a large and very 
expensive treatise on High Court procedure. As this was the ionly place where 
the code was written down, and the author claimed copyright, it effectively 
meant either having to buy the book or fly by the seat of the pants if as 
library copy was not available.

Back in 1985 the code was rewritten and included as a schedule at the back of 
the Judicature Amendment Act (No2) 1985. At long last we could acquire the Code 
for a mere $12.50 (NZ has been totally metric for many years now - wonder how 
long it'll be before the reast of the world sees sense?)

D J H Stringer
(stringer@es.co.nz)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am NOT a lawyer, ...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%YSc4.670$q5.29009@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <RVLc4.720$Zo.31781@sandstorm.neo.rr.com> <85092j$mlo$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <3873C343.F4B573D6@home.com> <850hn5$7l5$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The way I've always understood it (IANAL) to work with the ANSI X.12 EDI
standards (a fair parallel?) is that while the standards themselves - that
is, the ideas and concepts therein - are public domain, a specific "tangible
embodiement" (books, CDs, floppy disks, et al)  may be copyrighted, and
reproduction of those copyright materials restricted to uses permitted by
the copyright owner.

Seems fair to me. That is, you may *use* the standards all you want and
develop your own (copyrightable) tangible embodiements; however, you may not
copy, repackage and redistribute the creative work of others.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am NOT a lawyer, ...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<850v5r$8ff$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <RVLc4.720$Zo.31781@sandstorm.neo.rr.com> <85092j$mlo$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <3873C343.F4B573D6@home.com> <850hn5$7l5$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <%YSc4.670$q5.29009@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
You are probably correct about that Standard (and many others).  It is the
(infamous) "ACKNOWLEDGEMENT" that appears in the COBOL Standard (and has
since before ANSI took it over), that EXPLICITLY says that it may be copied
"in part or whole" and (re-)distributed in any form whatsoever.

If you haven't read that acknowledgement recently, then look at any ANSI
COBOL Standard - or any LRM and you'll see that I am *not* making this up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am NOT a lawyer, ...

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pKld4.138$5d4.10663@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <RVLc4.720$Zo.31781@sandstorm.neo.rr.com> <85092j$mlo$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <3873C343.F4B573D6@home.com> <850hn5$7l5$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <%YSc4.670$q5.29009@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:%YSc4.670$q5.29009@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
> The way I've always understood it (IANAL) to work with the ANSI X.12
EDI
> standards (a fair parallel?) is that while the standards
themselves - that
> is, the ideas and concepts therein - are public domain, a specific
"tangible
> embodiement" (books, CDs, floppy disks, et al)  may be copyrighted,
and
> reproduction of those copyright materials restricted to uses
permitted by
> the copyright owner.

Don't think so. The physical embodiment of the creative idea is
irrelevant to the copyright (although the work has to have some
physical form to copyright it in the first place). If the original
material is in the 'public domain,' all representations of that work
are non-copyrightable. If an author adds creative material to the the
public domain stuff - such as explaining how a COBOL verb works - that
part (the author-contributed part) is subject to copyright.

Of course there is no penalty (as far as I can determine) for
asserting a copyright where none exists.

>
> Seems fair to me. That is, you may *use* the standards all you want
and
> develop your own (copyrightable) tangible embodiements; however, you
may not
> copy, repackage and redistribute the creative work of others.
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am NOT a lawyer, ...

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000116143522.10160.00000177@ng-cn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<rTG+iCAKjFf4EwUi@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
As explained to me by a intellectual property attorney (I recommend all
software developers consult one - and pay for it themselves, not use the
company's!):
1).  Working program code fits the enabling legislation definition of the
"device embodying the idea", thus, allowing for software "patents."
2).  Ideas which are in common use before the patent request are not
patentable.
3).  The Patent Office is not the sole arbiter of whether or not a patent
"should" be given, and will occasionally grant a patent later determined to be
invalid.
4).  The holder of the patent has the responsibility to defend the patent,
usually as a civil action in response to a violation, after due process
(notification of patent rights, request for appropriate compensation, and so
forth).

Thus, while a patent on "windowing" may have been granted, since the concept
has been around for several years, it seems unlikely that the patent will be
upheld by a court.  To find out, the holder of the patent will have to file
suit against somebody who used it as a coding technique after the patent was
issued.  Hopefully, he or she will chose somebody with lots of good lawyers
(ok, give a rest, there is at least one GOOD lawyer!) on retainer, maybe EDS or
Arthur Anderson???

I'll wait for the legal news! 
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

> reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam

H!!!! 96 percent of everything is based on previous common ideas. 
Putting them together in new and useful ways is the essence of
(patentable) invention.

For illustration of how absurd this can become, take a look at the
IBM-MAIN list (& newsgroup?).  Someone in Europe is claiming patent
rights to coding    CLC =C'LITERAL',Something
instead of   CLC Something(Len),=C'LITERAL'

(For those not into Assembler, that's a COBOL string comparison to a
literal.

But, as was pointed out on this thread, what is important is the ability
to defend a patent.  Darn trouble is, that means everybody involved gets
to contribute to the Legal Vacations in Aruba fund.
```

>
> Then why does the patent office grant patents for software?

No one knows. It's a mystery. The law says the patent office may
REQUIRE a working model of the device seeking a patent. Therefore, one
cannot patent an idea, only a device that embodies the idea.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: I am NOT a lawyer, ...

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xsuf4.113$DM5.4305@nnrp2.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<84tmlk$uj2$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <RVLc4.720$Zo.31781@sandstorm.neo.rr.com> <85092j$mlo$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <3873C343.F4B573D6@home.com> <3873CF67.A027BD2B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <o30i7ssrnkeor1rhdij103c9hg6ihui6d1@4ax.com> <mVje4.109$Gp4.4571@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3879EDFB.5E1B67AA@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <r4ok7skd7l0ugfptodd95l5hhas3tivkt5@4ax.com> <387C926E.D522A316@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <u38f4.69$oG5.3799@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net> <xvmf4.947$bQ1.6662@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net
>
> Isn't the commissioner of the patent office a political appointee?
'nuff
> said...

Story:

Some time back I was reading my copy of the Federal Register (for
those who don't know, FR is the Congressional Record of the Executive
Branch). Anyway, a joint called "Jack's Fried Chicken," in Kentucky
has applied to the Patent Office for a "Service Mark" ('Coke's The
Real Thing,' 'Ford Has A Better Idea,' etc.) and an objection had been
lodged to the issuance of the service mark. So, the Commissioner of
the Office of Patents and Trademarks was announcing a hearing on the
merits of the proposed service mark and inviting those with any
insight, opinion, or objection to draw nigh and make their views
known.

The proposed Service Mark was "Jack's Fried Chicken, Where a Breast in
the Mouth is as Good As a Thigh In The Hand."

In my view, the problem (if one existed) would be self-correcting:
Either Jack would make millions or his drive-through would be burnt to
the ground. But no, we had to get the lawyers, and commissioners, and
clerks, and notaries public, and the Federal Register, and
god-knows-who involved.

Sigh.
```

I'm not a lawyer but to me, software is like a book.  You're not allowed to
copy software and claim it as your own (we are talking source only),
however, if you create a program from nothing but it's coded in a way to do
something similar to an existing program then it's ok.  Prime example :
Apple's operating GUI system followed by MicroSoft's Windows and IBM's OS/2
and WARP.  I believe that Apple was first.

Does this short example make sense or answers questions?
```

## Thread: Where can I find Microfocus COBOL tutorials? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Where can I find Microfocus COBOL tutorials?

- **From:** alfie007q@aol.com (ALFIE007Q)
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000104102726.14284.00000286@ng-fg1.aol.com>`

```
If anyone knows of a site that explains how to use Animator, Checker and Run
for MFC...PLEASE let me know!
The tutorial does NOT explain it well.
We are writing and testing newly written COBOL code then transferring to the
mainframe.... how do we code and test in the Windows version?
Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Where can I find Microfocus COBOL tutorials?

- **From:** Mike Salter <msalter@bestweb.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.BSF.4.21.0001041056500.11920-100000@monet.bestweb.net>`
- **References:** `<20000104102726.14284.00000286@ng-fg1.aol.com>`

```
On 4 Jan 2000, ALFIE007Q wrote:

A>If anyone knows of a site that explains how to use Animator, Checker and Run
A>for MFC...PLEASE let me know!
A>The tutorial does NOT explain it well.
A>We are writing and testing newly written COBOL code then transferring to the
A>mainframe.... how do we code and test in the Windows version?
A>Thanks!  

I prefer to use the character based editor, and check/animate from
there.  This editor is editor.exe. Invoke it with the cobol source file
name on the commandline. Press F2 a couple of times to set the
check/animate options.  After checking/compiling, you can run the program
at the commandline by typing 'run <progname>' where progname is either the
.int or .gnt filename.

HTH
```

#### ↳ Re: Where can I find Microfocus COBOL tutorials?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38722E70.CD09F2A8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<20000104102726.14284.00000286@ng-fg1.aol.com>`

```


ALFIE007Q wrote:
> 
> If anyone knows of a site that explains how to use Animator, Checker and Run
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> mainframe.... how do we code and test in the Windows version?

As you are specifically talking of going to mainframe, I have a book,
(my end-user thought I would like it - but I've never looked at it <G>)

"Micro Focus Workbench - Developing Mainframe COBOL Applications on the
PC" - Alida Jatich & Phil Nowak - John Wiley & Sons Inc. 
ISBN 0-471-55611-4 (It covers Version 3 and GUI Workbench).

Now I don't know how out of sync this book is with what you are trying
to do. Useless to get a book if it doesn't fit. If Gayle from Merant is
looking in, is this book of any use to 'Alfie' ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Where can I find Microfocus COBOL tutorials?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84td6m$j3p$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20000104102726.14284.00000286@ng-fg1.aol.com> <38722E70.CD09F2A8@home.com>`

```

news:38722E70.CD09F2A8@home.com...

Run
> > for MFC...PLEASE let me know!
> > The tutorial does NOT explain it well.
> > We are writing and testing newly written COBOL code then transferring to
the
> > mainframe.... how do we code and test in the Windows version?

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

I am not Gayle <G> - but I remember that book well.  It was one of the "good"
things that came out from the IBM/Micro Focus "relationship" of the late '80s
and early '90s.  It was available as an IBM "red book" - and I think is still
available from them.  As I recall, it dealt with Micro Focus Workbench V3.0
(or so). If you use "character based" (pre-Mainframe Express), then it
probably is still of some use.   It does assume some of the OLD "cics" and
"ims" emulators - but again, gives a reasonable overview of them.
```

<a id="t-c1c14e37d0"></a>
## Thread: Compiler Version — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Compiler Version

- **From:** Michael Stoll <tsc.stoll@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3871ABFB.F463E182@t-online.de>`

```
Hi,

the version of the Micro Focus Compiler II is 1.1.

It's not possible to use CURRENT-DATE.

Does anyone have an idea how to get the right DATE format.

01012000 from system gives 100010 in the DATE command.

Bye Michael
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiler Version

- **From:** PAL3000 <pauldub*REMOVETHIS*@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6TFyOLZ91tHp3QQK2k8H3hVfakgK@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3871ABFB.F463E182@t-online.de>`

```
We use logic after the ACCEPT statement:

   ACCEPT 8-DIGIT-DAT FROM DATE.

   IF 8-DIGIT-DAT-YYYY > 50
       ADD 1900 TO 8-DIGIT-DAT-YYYY
   ELSE
       ADD 2000 TO 8-DIGIT-DAT-YYYY
   END-IF



On Tue, 04 Jan 2000 09:14:51 +0100, Michael Stoll
<tsc.stoll@t-online.de> wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bye Michael

--------------------------------------
To reply by email, please take out 
*REMOVETHIS* from my email address
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiler Version

- **From:** x@wins.uva.null (X)
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84tokv$99h@adam.wins.uva.nl>`
- **References:** `<3871ABFB.F463E182@t-online.de>`

```
Michael Stoll <tsc.stoll@t-online.de> writes:

>It's not possible to use CURRENT-DATE.

is this an existing program that contains CURRENT-DATE and now does not
compile with another compiler?  If so, you should not only use the ACCEPT
DATE FROM but also take care of the fact tht the CURENT-DATE has another
format than DATE.  One solution can be found in
http://adam.wins.uva.nl/~x/con/con.html

  --XX
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiler Version

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85aefc$goo$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<3871ABFB.F463E182@t-online.de>`

```
Current-date is a reserved word in the OSVS dialect - not in ansi85 or
cobol2. Use accept from date as recommended in the thread.

Regards
Ib
Michael Stoll skrev i meddelelsen <3871ABFB.F463E182@t-online.de>...
>Hi,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bye Michael
```

<a id="t-ac33739b6f"></a>
## Thread: Y2K Woes — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Y2K Woes

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G7zc4.89$K73.7249@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```
We began readying our users for Y2K in October 1998.

We sent out our final, Y2K-checked, version of our software in August
1999.

We sent two newsletters to all our users, put notices in all
correspondence, and e-mailed those of our customers for which we had
e-mail addresses. In all these communications, we warned them that
they had been sent a Y2K-fix release of our software and must install
the it before 1/1/2000.

Fully 20% of our user base has contacted us in the last two days
regarding Y2K issues. If the whole scene was not so tragic, it would
be comical. Some of the more common laments:

"I never got a software update!"
"I didn't think it was important."
"The person who does that sort of thing has been gone for four
months."
"Everything was working fine and I didn't see the need."
"No, we have not done a backup for at least a year."
"We tried to install the update, but it didn't work. We've been
meaning to call."
"Actually, we used the diskettes you sent for something else."
"We forgot."
"We planned to do it after the Christmas rush."
"If it ain't broke, don't fix it." (honest-to-God, that's what he
said)
(and my favorite)
"When our machine boots up, it says the date is January 1, 1980. What
does that mean?"

But it has not been all bad:
We've heard from three users who (allegedly) quit using our software
as much as FIVE years ago (and who have not been remitting the annual
maintenance). They demand to know why they should pay anything for a
Y2K fix. (Answer: Because car thieves don't automatically get a full
tank of gas.) We may actually convert one or two of these to the paths
of righteousness.

Around here, it's been 'all hands on deck' in the technical support
department - we've fielded twice the number of calls we normally get
each day since the 1st. And these calls are not the kind that can be
answered with a quick "Yes," or "No." We feel like we've be "'et by a
coyote and shit off a cliff."

Just remember, folks, you can lead a horse to water but you'll get
blamed if it dies of thirst.

Best wishes for a happy and prosperous New Year to all. As for me, I'm
gonna open a pig farm.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
"I opened my Windows 95 manual and fell into a magical place."
```

Cheesle

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K Woes

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

>Jerry P wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>diskettes and backed everything up over it.

I have a customer that hired a "programmer"(I use quotes as he was an artsy
that decided to take an MS course, and thus became "certified", though
"certifiable" might be a better word). He immediately decided my backup
procedures were too onerous, and revised them with a "shareware" backup
facility that was "twice as fast and easier to use". Unfortunately, this
amazing backup facility did not come with a restore facility.  They have a
complete year of weekly backups, though.

The only thing that saved their ass is that I had done an upgrade a week
earlier, and I do a backup copy to disk as a standard procedure when I
upgrade customers.
```

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy

Backup's are good, however, you need to test the backup.  What I mean is
that you must make sure that what you had backup can be restored.  Once or
twice a year you should take any one of  your backup tapes, and try to
recover it.  You may have saved something, but if you can't read it
properly, it's no good to no one.

When I'm in Chapters I buy STAR TREK books.

Tim

Live Y2K free and prosper.
```

<a id="t-d32bcd7726"></a>
## Thread: IDMS Time Out error — 1 msgs

### IDMS Time Out error

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84ugsn01ndt@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
I've been testing some changes to some mainframe reporting programs (that 
support a PowerBuilder application).  The report 'modules' (extract and
format) are called by 'controller' programs.  I keep getting stange errors.
The MVS dumps a 2222 error but the IDMS-STATUS dump part says 0069 (run unit
time out, not bound, or bound twice).  The only database access this
particular module does is a few table calls through a handler module in the
beginning of execution.  There is also a DNS processing error
Receive_and_Wait error on IDMS.

When I run the programs where they are constantly accessing the database,
the job doesn't cancel out.

The question arose of why the programs in testing (which run at least 12
minutes when tested separately) time out.  I'm running the jobs against the
Production database (Many think I am somewhat fearless but I've never been
scared to run against prod) but still it gets run-unit timeouts.

The only thing I can think of is the CLASS of the job.  Is this possible?
When I submit a job from ROSCOE (into JES), it goes into CLASS=C.  When the
jobs are submitted by CA-7, they go into CLASS=A.  Is it possible the
different classes have an impact on IDMS run units?

I'm kinda scared to promote these.  Have a nice day.

Jeff
```

---

<a id="t-cdc38d8f52"></a>
## Thread: Hello, I Must Be Going — 2 msgs

### Hello, I Must Be Going

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pml27sc434qqa4077g7mc498tp7lflp4dh@4ax.com>`

```
Back in November, a flame war in another online forum consumed much of
my time.  It caused me to re-examine how I spend my time online, and I
decided that I needed to cut back on c.l.c.  By early December, I had
decided to drop c.l.c entirely.  I wrote a few sentences of a goodbye
to c.l.c, and saved it to finish the next day.  A few hours later, I
knocked my laptop computer off a table and cracked the screen.  I need
to plug in an old desktop monitor to use the computer.  I can't read
my email sitting on the couch, or sitting in the back yard, and the
experience has been rather depressing.  Now I find over 7000 unread
messages in Forte Agent, a third of those in comp.lang.cobol.  

So, I am writing this to tell you that I no longer read
comp.lang.cobol.  It's not that this newsgroup is bad, it's that it is
good.  I would like to reply to at least 5% of the messages posted
here.  It takes about two hours a day just to keep up with this
newsgroup when I'm not posting a lot.  I feel my COBOL time is better
spent with issues related to J4 and GNU COBOL.  Those of you who are
interested in Gnubol, the archive is at
http://www.lusars.net/maillists/gnu-cobol/threads.html.

And one final note:  The New York Times quoted me out of context.  The
article at
http://www.nytimes.com/library/tech/00/01/biztech/articles/03work.html
has me saying "I'm hoping for some big disaster somewhere to hit."  I
don't know if I said those exact words -- I recall saying "I'm hoping
for a bank somewhere to be bleeding a million dollars a day" -- but
even if the words are accurate, they are out of context.  I was
talking strictly about Y2K bugs.  I am not praying for war,
pestilence, plague, and famine, I am only expecting that accounting
data will be munged, and that I will be called upon to minimize the
losses.  I am sending an email to the NYTimes to that effect.
```

#### ↳ Re: Hello, I Must Be Going

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<84uh3j01nj7@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<pml27sc434qqa4077g7mc498tp7lflp4dh@4ax.com>`

```
That's fine, whatever makes you happy and is respectful.  We'll miss you. 
Please check back now and then.

Jeff

----------

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s871f547.061@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
>> My PC just fired up with 4th Jan 1980.  I reset it to 2000 and it is
>> all OK now. 

My husband's computer was up during the roll over and his was like this last night (first he'd used it since last week).  Mine on the other hand was shut down and I booted it the morning of the 1st and all was well.  His isn't disposable though.  Stuff crashing all over.  He did reset it to 2000 and all appears to be well.

As for work we have a few minor problems with some programs but no show stoppers on the mainframe.  The network however..... :D

Eileen - watching the network people popping pills.


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-39dd92140d"></a>
## Thread: Technical Interview Questions — 13 msgs

### Technical Interview Questions

- **From:** "Thomas J. Potenza" <tpotenza@techlink-usa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8HOc4.1755$I5.192242@typhoon1.gnilink.net>`

```
My technical staffing agency is in the process of updating our recruiting
procedures.  When we do a technical evaluation over the phone, we like to
ask between five and ten questions on the skill we are recruiting for.  If
anyone has access to some good Cobol questions that we can use for our
telephone screening, it would be greatly appreciated.  I figured this might
be a good place to ask.  We're looking for questions that range from
intermediate to advanced.  Please supply the answers as well.  Multiple
choice is probably the best format if available.

We need questions for for CICS, DB2, Visual Basic, Visual C++, C++,
PowerBuilder, SQL, Java, JavaScript, CGI, SQL Server, Cobol, RPG, IIS, etc.
Any help would be appreciated.  Thanks for your help.

Tom Potenza
tpotenza@techlink-usa.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Technical Interview Questions

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ermeY59V$GA.269@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<8HOc4.1755$I5.192242@typhoon1.gnilink.net>`

```
Look up the book "Ace the Technical Interview", which can be found at the
major online booksellers.  Heavily into the stuff you're talking about,
although the reviews say it's weak on client-server stuff.

Here's a copy of the Table of Contents
_______________________________________
Table of Contents
 Contributors
 Preface
 Acknowledgments
1 There's a New Ballgame in Town ... and It's Called the Year 2000 1
2 A Year 2000 Methodology 4
3 Managing a Year 2000 Conversion Project 21
4 Testing and Quality Control for Year 2000 Conversions 44
5 Year 2000 Conversion Tools and Reengineering Management 62
6 The Technical Interview: What to Say, What to Do, What to Know 94
7 Interviewing Frameworks 112
8 Java and the World Wide Web 123
9 The AS/400 136
10 Unix 163
11 The RS/6000 178
12 Client/Server Systems 195
13 MVS 216
14 CICS 237
15 Oracle 8 259
16 Sybase 278
17 IBM's DB2 294
18 SQL 305
19 IDMS 321
20 IMS DB-DL/I 340
21 VSAM 356
22 COBOL 365
23 C and C++ 400
24 Visual Basic 5.0 426
25 PowerBuilder 449
26 Delphi 469

                         In the 1812 campaign against Napoleon's 
armies, Russian soldiers
                         pushed all the way into the outskirts of

                         for shouting "bistro"--"hurry up"--at 
slow-moving waiters in
                         sidewalk cafes.

a word that I can guess but that will be filtered by the CDA is:

%!PS,
/Helvetica findfont 36 scalefont setfont 40 360 translate 1 -0.05
-0.05{ dup .05 le { pop 1 } if setgray 0 0 moveto (etbj) {1 add (
) dup 0 4 3 roll put show } forall -1 .5 translate } for showpage

links to hard porn sites!  But no Dutch ones.. 

Then again, the Clue that James provides is that the word is Dutch in
origin.. this makes me wonder...

Let me tell you a story and then James should tell us whether the clue is
connected to this story.

I have a Dutch colleague who lived in the UK for 3 years.  One day when he
was at London Gatwick he was daydreaming and forgot to get on his plane.

 -message for mister eff oh kay kay ay en kay: immediate boarding please
 -message for mister eff oh kay kay ay en kay: immediate boarding please
 -message for mister eff oh kay kay ay en kay: immediate boarding please
 -message for mister eff oh kay kay ay en kay: immediate boarding please
 
after the fourth message he started to compose the characters to a word
and found out that they did not dare to pronounce his name: Fokkink.

so tell me.. 

  --XX

Stop run ends your programs
      -- Lao Tse on COBOL

<a id="t-4b2ebd260f"></a>
## Thread: The next Date Issue — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Re: The next Date Issue

- **From:** Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38734F19.A8324565@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8EB0A04BEcraznarhotmailcom@139.130.250.4> <bI66+sHyQQc4Ew6Q@merlyn.demon.co.uk>`

```
Unfortunately COBOL provides no easy way of editing a numeric field to
yyyy-mm-dd because the hyphen is a sign character whereas you can code
9999/99/99 or XXXX/XX/XX as a valid PICTURE.  Given the rationale for
the use of hyphens in the date formatting, I would like to see someone
address the issue for all programming languages but have not seen even
a ripple of interest on various news groups.  I don't know what the
situation is for other languages.  I believe that VISION (DYL280) from
Sterling has the same problem.  Unfortunately standards groups act in
isolation.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, cfmtech@istar.ca

Dr John Stockton wrote:
> 
> JRS:  In article <8EB0A04BEcraznarhotmailcom@139.130.250.4> of Mon, 3
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Y2k for beginners - year2000.txt  UK mini-FAQ - y2k-mfaq.txt  Don't Mail News
```

#### ↳ Re: The next Date Issue

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38737EDB.BF1151@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8EB0A04BEcraznarhotmailcom@139.130.250.4> <bI66+sHyQQc4Ew6Q@merlyn.demon.co.uk> <38734F19.A8324565@istar.ca>`

```


Clark Morris wrote:
> 
> > >Time to standardise on yyyy/mm/dd before next years date mix up problems.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > ï¿½ John Stockton, Surrey, UK.  jrs@merlyn.demon.co.uk   Turnpike v4.00   MIME. ï¿½

Your fear is well-founded. I can only make a wild-guess that if the
analyst/programmer was Canadian-born then he uses "M D Y", a la US ;
possibly if he is an ex-Brit he will use "D M Y". Both formats appear on
bills I receive. But there is a neat feature on my telephone bill :-

						  $
	Tue Jan  4 ...........			1.67
	Wed Jan 12 ...........			2.12

the year being understood from the current billing.

The simplest solution to both date and time has to be a standarisation
on the ISO ccyymmdd (the most logical way for date storage for ascending
sequence) and the 24 hour clock. For many years I have written my
personal cheques :-

		99 Jan 23,  now of course - 2000 Jan 23

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The next Date Issue

- **From:** Dr John Stockton <jrs@merlyn.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f27bhEM3u8c4Ew5$@merlyn.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8EB0A04BEcraznarhotmailcom@139.130.250.4> <bI66+sHyQQc4Ew6Q@merlyn.demon.co.uk> <38734F19.A8324565@istar.ca> <38737EDB.BF1151@home.com>`

```
JRS:  In article <38737EDB.BF1151@home.com> of Wed, 5 Jan 2000 17:31:23
in news:comp.software.year-2000, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:
>
>The simplest solution to both date and time has to be a standarisation
>on the ISO ccyymmdd (the most logical way for date storage for ascending
>sequence) and the 24 hour clock. For many years I have written my
>personal cheques :-

Except that the ISO standard is yyyymmdd, with the separator, of any,
being "-".

I have seen it reported (comp.risks?) that a request, much more than a
week ago, for CCYY or CC YY data led to the entry of the true value of
the century for CC - 20, where 19 was then needed.  Possibly in the
Texas Highways Department?????

I have also seen a normally-reputable programmer, given a string
containing cYY, where the single digit c is actually (YYYY div 100)-19,
treating c & YY separately in recovering YYYY.

Using CC in the notation, i.e. CCYY rather than YYYY, introduces an
unnecessary hazard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The next Date Issue

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3874A74D.AECD1589@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<8EB0A04BEcraznarhotmailcom@139.130.250.4> <bI66+sHyQQc4Ew6Q@merlyn.demon.co.uk> <38734F19.A8324565@istar.ca> <38737EDB.BF1151@home.com> <f27bhEM3u8c4Ew5$@merlyn.demon.co.uk>`

```

Dr John Stockton wrote:
> 
> JRS:  In article <38737EDB.BF1151@home.com> of Wed, 5 Jan 2000 17:31:23
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Ok Ok,
Lets' fix this date thing once and for all.

01  Internal-Date-Representation.
    03 Year     PIC S9(9) comp.
    03 Month    PIC S9(4) comp.
    03 Day      PIC S9(4) comp.

External date representation is whatever you need for the job at hand.
However, the YEAR should have at least 4 digits as a minimum
requirement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The next Date Issue

- **From:** "Gary L. Smith" <gls@infinet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38741ecc$0$50322@news.voyager.net>`
- **References:** `<8EB0A04BEcraznarhotmailcom@139.130.250.4> <bI66+sHyQQc4Ew6Q@merlyn.demon.co.uk> <38734F19.A8324565@istar.ca> <38737EDB.BF1151@home.com> <f27bhEM3u8c4Ew5$@merlyn.demon.co.uk>`

```
In comp.software.year-2000 Dr John Stockton <jrs@merlyn.demon.co.uk> wrote:

: I have seen it reported (comp.risks?) that a request, much more than a
: week ago, for CCYY or CC YY data led to the entry of the true value of
: the century for CC - 20, where 19 was then needed.  Possibly in the
: Texas Highways Department?????

One case of stupidity isn't sufficient to invalidate an otherwise
reasonable practice.

: I have also seen a normally-reputable programmer, given a string
: containing cYY, where the single digit c is actually (YYYY div 100)-19,
: treating c & YY separately in recovering YYYY.

: Using CC in the notation, i.e. CCYY rather than YYYY, introduces an
: unnecessary hazard.

But what would you do when it's necessary to refer to the two high-order
digits in isolation?  Is there a symbol that's preferable to "cc"?  A
real-life example of such a situation, excepted from a remediation
document dated 19980130:  

Here YYMMDD represents a six-digit date as found in Date Entered, while CC
is the value of the century digits.  Concatenating CC with YYMMDD produces
the full eight-digit date.

Algorithm:

If YYMMDD = 000000, then CC = 00
Else if YY is less than 60, then CC = 20
Else CC = 19.

Examples:

000000  becomes  00000000
980122  becomes  19980122
000102  becomes  20000102
591231  becomes  20591231
600101  becomes  19600101
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The next Date Issue

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Dr John Stockton <jrs@merlyn.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ogF2EnMJRKd4Ew3n@merlyn.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8EB0A04BEcraznarhotmailcom@139.130.250.4> <bI66+sHyQQc4Ew6Q@merlyn.demon.co.uk> <38734F19.A8324565@istar.ca> <38737EDB.BF1151@home.com> <f27bhEM3u8c4Ew5$@merlyn.demon.co.uk> <38741ecc$0$50322@news.voyager.net>`

```
JRS:  In article <38741ecc$0$50322@news.voyager.net> of Thu, 6 Jan 2000
04:49:16 in news:comp.software.year-2000, Gary L. Smith
<gls@infinet.com> wrote:
>In comp.software.year-2000 Dr John Stockton <jrs@merlyn.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>reasonable practice.

But one example is sufficient to cast doubt on the practice, and the
practice can be avoided without difficulty.  Can one safely assume that
the THD (or whoever) are uniquely stupid?

>: I have also seen a normally-reputable programmer, given a string
>: containing cYY, where the single digit c is actually (YYYY div 100)-19,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>the full eight-digit date.

"Here YYMMDD represents a six-digit date as found in Date Entered, while
 YYYYMMDD represents a full eight-digit date."

(* The relationship is obvious. *)

>Algorithm:
>
>If YYMMDD = 000000, then CC = 00
>Else if YY is less than 60, then CC = 20
>Else CC = 19.

>If YYMMDD = 000000, then YYYY = 0000
>Else if YY is less than 60, then YYYY = 2000
>Else YYYY = 1900.

/Entia non sunt multiplicanda praeter necessitatem/.

CC of course has two problems, one nullifying the other nearly 1% of the
time : the customary century number is almost always different from Year
div 100.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The next Date Issue

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gary L. Smith" <gls@infinet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38775fbb$0$1808@news.voyager.net>`
- **References:** `<8EB0A04BEcraznarhotmailcom@139.130.250.4> <bI66+sHyQQc4Ew6Q@merlyn.demon.co.uk> <38734F19.A8324565@istar.ca> <38737EDB.BF1151@home.com> <f27bhEM3u8c4Ew5$@merlyn.demon.co.uk> <38741ecc$0$50322@news.voyager.net> <ogF2EnMJRKd4Ew3n@merlyn.demon.co.uk>`

```
In comp.software.year-2000 Dr John Stockton <jrs@merlyn.demon.co.uk> wrote:
: JRS:  In article <38741ecc$0$50322@news.voyager.net> of Thu, 6 Jan 2000
: 04:49:16 in news:comp.software.year-2000, Gary L. Smith
: <gls@infinet.com> wrote:
:>In comp.software.year-2000 Dr John Stockton <jrs@merlyn.demon.co.uk> wrote:
:>
:>: I have seen it reported (comp.risks?) that a request, much more than a
:>: week ago, for CCYY or CC YY data led to the entry of the true value of
:>: the century for CC - 20, where 19 was then needed.  Possibly in the
:>: Texas Highways Department?????
:>
:>One case of stupidity isn't sufficient to invalidate an otherwise
:>reasonable practice.

: But one example is sufficient to cast doubt on the practice, and the
: practice can be avoided without difficulty.  Can one safely assume that
: the THD (or whoever) are uniquely stupid?

I would never say anything bad about Texans (some of my relatives are
Texans) or about highway patrolmen (some of my ...), but I believe that
this particular episode, if it occurred at all, was uniquely stupid.
There's a big difference between using CC to designate the high-order
digits of the year and referring to those digits as "the century".  Tha
latter is definitely a bad move, although there seem to be very few people
these days who understand the terms "19th century" and "20th century". 

:>But what would you do when it's necessary to refer to the two high-order
:>digits in isolation?  Is there a symbol that's preferable to "cc"?  A
:>real-life example of such a situation, excepted from a remediation
:>document dated 19980130:  
:>
:>Here YYMMDD represents a six-digit date as found in Date Entered, while CC
:>is the value of the century digits.  Concatenating CC with YYMMDD produces
:>the full eight-digit date.

: "Here YYMMDD represents a six-digit date as found in Date Entered, while
:  YYYYMMDD represents a full eight-digit date."

: (* The relationship is obvious. *)

:>Algorithm:
:>
:>If YYMMDD = 000000, then CC = 00
:>Else if YY is less than 60, then CC = 20
:>Else CC = 19.

:>If YYMMDD = 000000, then YYYY = 0000
:>Else if YY is less than 60, then YYYY = 2000
:>Else YYYY = 1900.

I find my formulation easier to understand, but I suspect this is
primarily a matter od taste.  No doubt either would suffice almost all the
time.  Those who misunderstand either and fail to ask for clarification
shouldn't be allowed to write code.
```

- **References:** `<8EB0A04BEcraznarhotmailcom@139.130.250.4> <bI66+sHyQQc4Ew6Q@merlyn.demon.co.uk> <38734F19.A8324565@istar.ca>`

```
In article <38734F19.A8324565@istar.ca>, Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:
>Unfortunately COBOL provides no easy way of editing a numeric field to
>yyyy-mm-dd because the hyphen is a sign character whereas you can code
>9999/99/99 or XXXX/XX/XX as a valid PICTURE.

01 EDITED-DATE    PIC 9999B99B99.
01 NORMAL-DATE   PIC 9(8).
..
MOVE NORMAL-DATE TO EDITED-DATE
INSPECT EDITED-DATE REPLACING ALL " " BY "-"
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The next Date Issue

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8509hc$uk0$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8EB0A04BEcraznarhotmailcom@139.130.250.4> <bI66+sHyQQc4Ew6Q@merlyn.demon.co.uk> <38734F19.A8324565@istar.ca> <I2Mc4.69$Rw3.1460029@news.netdirect.net>`

```
Ask your  COBOL "vendor of choice" if they already (or soon) support the new
intrinsic functions (from the draft revision)

    LOCALE-DATE
    LOCALE-TIME

These will "format" the date or time portions of FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE
*either* to the default locale specification - or a "specified" LOCALE.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<850c60$u8$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The following looks medium "nasty" for IBM OS/390 sites (not really
COBOL-related, but it is never a nice idea to "lose your backup data").

Summary:
 DFSMShsm: Do not run the AUDIT function with the FIX parameter until further
notice

For details, see
  http://www-4.ibm.com/software/year2000/alert/20000105121859.html


Bottom-Line:
  IBM "people" - please make certain that someone at your site is continuing
to watch:

http://www-4.ibm.com/software/year2000/alert/
```

## Thread: STD: In-line comment indicator — 76 msgs

### STD: In-line comment indicator

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8536vm$ifq$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The thread about what would be the best (nicest? most user-friendly?
whatever?) indicator for starting inline comments seems to be dragging on and
on.  Is this just a thread of "wishful thinking" or something else.

The bottom-line is that "*>" is in the draft - and barring some STRONG
argument against it, I doubt that there is much (any?) chance of it changing
now.

If someone really does want it to be changed, then I strongly suggest that
you URGENTLY get your input to J4 (before their Jan 17th meeting).  If not,
can someone tell me why we are still talking about "alternatives" that would
have been nice?

P.S.  As usual, if you want to send your comments to J4, send them to the
chair at:

     doncobol <at> mediaone.net

P.P.S.  No, I am not trying to stop discussion, I just can't tell if anyone
thinks that there is any point to the current thread.
```

#### ↳ Re: STD: In-line comment indicator

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1A52F7B4C4E25404.BFAE0E46CC3B73A0.53D8D14C04D45CE4@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<8536vm$ifq$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Thu, 6 Jan 2000 17:04:41 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> enlightened us:

>The thread about what would be the best (nicest? most user-friendly?
>whatever?) indicator for starting inline comments seems to be dragging on and
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>thinks that there is any point to the current thread.

Like you said Bill, there probably isn't any way to change their
minds, but I personally don't like it.  In our company we have our own
4GL type language and a preprocessor that converts the 4GL commands to
Cobol syntax.  In our 4GL we use the /*  */ for inline comments.  Our
designers decided that  because most folks are familiar with writing
MVS JCL and it is similiar to the //* comment string it would be easy
to use.

In short, I'd like to se /*  */ for inline comments be the standard.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Should crematoriums give discounts for burn victims?


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```
