###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GEN : System Design - was Record Locks

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387A144F.D92E3CA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<852o2g$822$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38781089.3245DC23@home.com> <85cqrd$5kb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


John Chafin wrote:
> 
> Jimmy,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
John,

That's it exactly - by splitting into the three areas you are able to
'plugin' and 'unplug' just as you illustrated, giving you the advantage,
say, of using flat-files, databases, Btrieve file-handling, or whatever.
And perhaps the greater flexibility, being able to switch the
'windowing' techniques - allowing you to get closer to the concept of a
compiler independent approach.

Although I mentioned OO, because that is how I'm doing it - OO has
nothing to do with it - you have illustrated the concept/technique is
just as effective with 'structured'. OO is just a play on words; your
statement 'We wanted to be able to write something once and use it
infinitely' translates to the OO buzzword REUSABILITY.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GEN : System Design - was Record Locks

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387B1417.AED68B03@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<852o2g$822$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38781089.3245DC23@home.com> <85cqrd$5kb$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <387A144F.D92E3CA@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> Although I mentioned OO, because that is how I'm doing it - OO has
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> infinitely' translates to the OO buzzword REUSABILITY.

That buzzword predated OO.  OO was designed because of this statement.

Reuse is a powerful tool, very hard to get right but worth the effort.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GEN : System Design - was Record Locks

- **From:** Doug Bashford <spanna2000@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85g9ql$p7k$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<852o2g$822$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38781089.3245DC23@home.com>`

```
I agree 100%. In fact the design goes quite a bit further than that.

All screens are created in a text editor, then using a COBOL program,
the system generates its own SET DEFAULTS, GET data from data layer,
DISPLAY, ACCEPT, VALIDATE, PUT data to data layer and PRINT to print
layer modules for each screen.

All the business logic is handled in code modules (of which there are
standard building block modules for common tasks, e.g. a table
maintenance model, a report model etc) calling the the generated code
for user interface. One the business module is happy with the code is
calls the database layer which has open, start, read, write, delete,
read next etc etc close methods. This design has allowed us to move
from CP/M to DOS to UNIX to Windows. The database is still flat C-ISAM,
there doesn't seem to be a reason to go to a relational database. If we
need to reformat a file there's a generator to create the new layout
transfer the old to the new, create the indexes etc and execute this at
install time on user systems.

Admittedly the functionality of all of this has improved with time but
the original design had all the building blocks, three tier, generated
code, database independence and user specific database extensions (the
business objects pass them around as a memory block, only the programs
that need to know the content will manipulate the data).

All of this started in the late 70s. The bloke's a genius (and very
wealthy now). He made some mistakes - the file lock handler that we've
debated, a transaction processing system that's missing a start,commit
design but on the whole it's great a system work with.


In article <38781089.3245DC23@home.com>,
  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> foresight touch, somewhat more difficult ....?


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

- **From:** Thomas David Rivers <rivers@dignus.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<38754766.B775508F@dignus.com>`
- **References:** `<852k05$4lj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
scheiwi@my-deja.com wrote:

> I am looking for a introductory "C" programming book that would be
> best suited for a programmer that -
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.

Hi -

  As far as "C" on the mainframe; let me invite you to visit
 http://www.dignus.com to learn more about Systems/C.

 You can even use Systems/C to generate assembly language
 which is directly callable by your existing COBOL (to try
 some small things out.)

 Also, Systems/C generates commented 390 assembly source.
 If you know any 390 assembly, it might be a good way to
 learn.  You can submit small C source files on our web pages
 and take a look at the generated source.

 As far as C books - there are two ways to go.   But, since
 you already are a programmer - I'd suggest you just go
 and buy what's known as the K&R book (Kernighan
 and Ritchie) - "The C Programming Language, 2nd Edition"
 (ISBN 0131103709 ) .   This book explains the syntax
 and has info on the standard C library.

 The other top-knotch C book is "C: A Reference Manual"
 by Harbison and Steele  (people often quote Harbison and
 Steele) - ISBN  0133262243

 These are both popular books, available just about everywhere.


        - Dave Rivers -
```

#### ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

- **From:** gergo.barany@gmx.net (Gergo Barany)
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00

[Followups to comp.lang.c only]

scheiwi@my-deja.com <scheiwi@my-deja.com> wrote:
> I am looking for a introductory "C" programming book that would be
> best suited for a programmer that -
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2.  Will be programming in "C" instead of COBOL in the near future.

The C FAQ at http://www.eskimo.com/~scs/C-faq/top.html lists several
recommended books in section 18.10. Especially interesting for you might
be this paragraph:

   Many people believe that the best [book] was also the
   first: The C Programming Language, by Kernighan and Ritchie (``K&R,''
   now in its second edition). Opinions vary on K&R's suitability as an
   initial programming text: many of us did learn C from it, and learned
   it well; some, however, feel that it is a bit too clinical as a first
   tutorial for those without much programming background.

Since you have a strong programming background, you should have no
difficulties with that book.

Gergo
```

>> it

>I apologize, I was not clear.

>1. Cross-platform compatability for incorporating MQ series.
>2. Parent company, outside of the United States, is creating a
>   new product utilizing "C" and "C++".

oh, geez. client/server and messaging. the only C i know of which does
mqseries is visual age (or was it open-edition? can't remember). the
only products i could think of, with any lifetime, which last in c/c++
would be device drivers and libraries (anything else is vaporware). as
for cross-platfowm compatibility, forget it.

application porting? all your tables which are used to hold records
would have to be rewritten for lsearch. all your indexed files
rewritten for whatever index software you use, and the parent company
could end up producing vaporware.

An excellent book, very well designed, I think, is:

C for COBOL Programmers: A Business Approach

This book equates COBOL data and programming structures directly with their
equivalents in C (where possible), duplicating COBOL code with C code by way
of example.

I liked it, as the author's voice read clearly to me.  YMMV.

#### ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<852su4$qun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<852k05$4lj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In comp.lang.cobol scheiwi@my-deja.com wrote:


: I am looking for a introductory "C" programming book that would be
: best suited for a programmer that -

: 1.  Has been programming on the Mainframe utilizing COBOL for
:     several years  AND

: 2.  Will be programming in "C" instead of COBOL in the near future.

Hmmm, changing from a 40 year old language to a 30 year old one
will give you quite a culture shock, as will the fragility of
the code you will write.

There is a book 'Moving from Cobol to C' by Budlong (Sams), but
it is very lightweight and doesn't cover any of the real
requiremnts of changing tack.

But going to C is not a case of 'converting', it would be better
if you erased all memory of what you used to do and restarted
by reading K&R's 'The C Programming Language' and tried to
adopt the culture with, say, 'Software Tools in Pascal'.

Cobol programs written in the C language are poor examples of
both.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

- **From:** "Dann Corbit" <dcorbit@solutionsiq.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming
- **Message-ID:** `<9L6d4.521$Z47.1947@client>`
- **References:** `<852k05$4lj$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <852su4$qun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
news:852su4$qun$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
[snip]
> Cobol programs written in the C language are poor examples of
> both.

You make an excellent point.  Most programming languages have associated
paradigms that work very well in that language.  When trying to translate a
language, it should really incorporate the new programming paradigm to
obtain the real benefit of that language.

When teaching C at a junior college, I used to get some awful, goto laced
goo that I called "C-Tran" because it was written by old Fortran IV and
COBOL programmers.  Yuck.

Just like using C++ is not fully expressive unless you use the real
capabilities of the language (object models, templates, etc) the same thing
will be true when changing from any language base to another.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "C" book for COBOL to C programmer

- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.lang.learn.c-c++,comp.lang.c,comp.lang.cobol,comp.programming

I often say that the best language X is language X.
However, let's not put everything on the raw language itself.
You'd be surprised how many people in "the correct culture" already
are still in outer space.  IOWs, nothing like fundamentals being
taught properly too.

- Greg
```

For licensing purposes, I would like to know how I can retrieve values
from the windows registry (any Win32). I would like to build a very
simple .dll that after being called returns either the machine-id, the
user-name, a bios signature or some other useful piece of information
that can be used to distinguish between individual workstations. Yes, I
see this scheme is not waterproof. Our licensing is on a per-seat
basis, but anythinig that comes near will do, I guess.

Is there anyone out there that can supply me with an example in either
MF cobol, NetExpress, Realia (WB 3.x) or Fuji (cobol 85).

And if possible, please e-mail me direct: peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

petervz95@my-deja.com wrote:
>

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

any clues there where you could 'read from Registry'. Another

treat the latter as a separate entity ? 

Jimmy, Calgary AB

#### ↳ Re: how to read from the windows registry

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vARd4.25372$J%4.31194@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<851cva$812$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
From the SDK

Following are the functions used with the registry:

RegCloseKey
RegConnectRegistry
RegCreateKey
RegCreateKeyEx
RegDeleteKey
RegDeleteValue
RegEnumKey
RegEnumKeyEx
RegEnumValue
RegFlushKey
RegGetKeySecurity
RegLoadKey
RegNotifyChangeKeyValue
RegOpenKey
RegOpenKeyEx
RegQueryInfoKey
RegQueryValue
RegQueryValueEx
RegReplaceKey
RegRestoreKey
RegSaveKey
RegSetKeySecurity
RegSetValue
RegSetValueEx
RegUnLoadKey

If you can call 'em from COBOL, you can use 'em.

If you can translate from BASIC, Alan Earnshaw wrote a real nice article on
using the Registry in the September, 1999 issue of "BASICally Speaking"
magazine. Reprints may be ordered for $4.00 at http://www.infoms.com .
```

#### ↳ Re: how to read from the windows registry

- **From:** John Chafin <jchafin@cgwinc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85ct49$7eh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<851cva$812$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Peter,

Here's some other Windows APIs that might be of help. These are in CA-
Realia WB 3.0 format.

 01  SystemInfo.
     03  dwOemId                         PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  dwPageSize                      PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  LpMinimumApplicationAddress     POINTER.
     03  LpMaximumApplicationAddress     POINTER.
     03  dwActiveProcessorMask           PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  dwNumberOfProcessors            PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  dwProcessorType                 PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  dwAllocationGranularity         PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  dwReserved                      PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
 01  OSVERSIONINFO.
     03  dwOSVersionInfoSize             PIC 9(09)  BINARY
                                                    value 148.
     03  dwMajorVersion                  PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  dwMinorVersion                  PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  dwBuildNumber                   PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  dwPlatformId                    PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  szCSDVersion                    PIC X(128).
 01  DISKFREESPACEINFO.
     03  lpSectorsPerCluster             PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  lpBytesPerSector                PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  lpFreeClusters                  PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  lpClusters                      PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
     03  lpszRootPathName                PIC X(121).
 01  DriveType                           PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
         88  DriveType-Unknown                      value 0.
         88  DriveType-DoesNotExist                 value 1.
         88  DriveType-Floppy                       value 2.
         88  DriveType-Hard                         value 3.
         88  DriveType-Network                      value 4.
         88  DriveType-CDRom                        value 5.
         88  DriveType-RamDisk                      value 6.
 01  LogicalDrivesMask                   PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
 01  Result                              PIC 9(09)  BINARY.
 01  MEMORYSTATUS.
     03  memorystatus-length             PIC S9(09) BINARY
                                                    VALUE 32.
     03  memoryload                      PIC S9(09) BINARY.
     03  totalphysmemory                 PIC S9(09) BINARY.
     03  availphysmemory                 PIC S9(09) BINARY.
     03  totalpagefile                   PIC S9(09) BINARY.
     03  availpagefile                   PIC S9(09) BINARY.
     03  totalvirtual                    PIC S9(09) BINARY.
     03  availvirtual                    PIC S9(09) BINARY.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.

 8400-GSI.
     CALL 'S_GetSystemInfo'
         USING BY REFERENCE SystemInfo.
 8400-GSI-EXIT.
     EXIT.
 8405-GVE.
     CALL 'S_GetVersionExA'
         USING BY REFERENCE OSVERSIONINFO
         GIVING Result.
 8405-GVE-EXIT.
     EXIT.
 8410-GDFS.
     CALL 'S_GetDiskFreeSpaceA'
         USING BY REFERENCE lpszRootPathName
                            lpSectorsPerCluster
                            lpBytesPerSector
                            lpFreeClusters
                            lpClusters
         GIVING Result.
 8410-GDFS-EXIT.
     EXIT.
 8415-GDT.
     CALL 'S_GetDriveTypeA'
         USING BY REFERENCE lpszRootPathName
         GIVING DriveType.
 8415-GDT-EXIT.
     EXIT.
 8420-GLD.
     CALL 'S_GetLogicalDrives'
         GIVING LogicalDrivesMask.
 8420-GLD-EXIT.
     EXIT.
 8425-GMS.
     CALL 'S_GlobalMemoryStatus' USING
         BY REFERENCE MEMORYSTATUS.
 8425-GMS-EXIT.
     EXIT.

John

In article <851cva$812$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  petervz95@my-deja.com wrote:
> For licensing purposes, I would like to know how I can retrieve values
> from the windows registry (any Win32). I would like to build a very
> simple .dll that after being called returns either the machine-id, the
> user-name, a bios signature or some other useful piece of information
> that can be used to distinguish between individual workstations. Yes,
I
> see this scheme is not waterproof. Our licensing is on a per-seat
> basis, but anythinig that comes near will do, I guess.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

And the current list (in alphabetical order) is:

    ***

ACU: Acucobol and Acucorp products

ADMIN:  - things like this that talk about the NG itself

CA: CA-Realia, Telon, and other Computer Associates products

FUJ: Fujitsu

GUI: GUIs, Acucobol Accept/Display, etc

HOW:  How do I ...? (if not covered in other topics)

IBM: IBM - MVS, CICS, VM, VSE, JCL, VisualAge, etc

MER:  Micro Focus, MERANT, NetExpress, Mainframe Express, etc

OO: Object Orientation (Does "GUI" need to be separated out?)

OT: - Off-topic (this is fairly common across newsgroups)

RM: RM

SCH: Assistance with Homework

STD: Standards issues and question (current and proposed Standard)

      ****

Any suggestions, comments, updates?
  (upper-/lower-case use should not be significant)
```

## Thread: OO: 101 — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO: 101

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38744EDC.34DD4FA8@zip.com.au>`

```
I have been fielding some questions regarding OO.  So I thought I
would put together some mail notes on OO concepts.  Not 'the'
definition of the term or concept more a description of some of the
terms and how they are used in 'normal' programs.

This will highlight the similarities and differences of OO and
procedural programming.

OO really is not a new concept, more it is the bundling of old
concepts in a new way making them work better.

I thought that I run notes on:

Encapsulation
polymorphism
inheritance
design patterns

as a start.  If anyone wants to 'ask Horace and Winnie' please let me
know.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

locking multiple records when manual locking is in effect.  In other 
words, the lock on the previously locked record is not released.

#### ↳ Re: Please HELP me, a question.

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387629A4.4E26E2EB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<852v3g$gfv$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```


StevieB wrote:
> 
> Could anyone tell me what both these statements achieve?  I haven't got a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I've left the funny "squiggly" characters in the following; it's a pain
to try and edit them out. This is the format for Indexed, but should
give you the idea :-

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Format 5 (Indexed Files)

                                        ï¿½     ï¿½ ï¿½KEPT   ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½
READ file-name RECORD [INTO identifier] ï¿½WITH ï¿½ ï¿½NO     ï¿½ LOCK ï¿½ ï¿½
                                        ï¿½     ï¿½ ï¿½IGNORE ï¿½      ï¿½ ï¿½
                                        ï¿½     ï¿½ WAIT           ï¿½ ï¿½
    ï¿½KEY IS ï¿½data-name      ï¿½ ï¿½

    ï¿½       ï¿½split-key-name ï¿½ ï¿½

    [INVALID KEY imperative-statement-3]

    [NOT INVALID KEY imperative-statement-4]

    [END-READ]

SYNTAX RULES :

4.	The WITH LOCK phrase can be included only when single records are
being locked manually in a shareable file.

5.	The WITH NO LOCK phrase is only allowed when records are being locked
manually or automatically in a shareable file. 
6.	The WITH KEPT LOCK phrase can be included only when multiple records
are being locked manually in a shareable file. 
7.	Format 5 is used for files in random access mode or for files in
dynamic access mode when records are to be retrieved randomly.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Please HELP me, a question.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<855f08$g3p$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<852v3g$gfv$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk> <387629A4.4E26E2EB@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
: StevieB wrote:
:> Could anyone tell me what both these statements achieve?  I haven't got a
:> clue what either of them will do.
:> 
:> READ filename WITH KEPT LOCK.
:> READ filename NEXT WITH KEPT LOCK.

: [...]
: 4.	The WITH LOCK phrase can be included only when single records are
: being locked manually in a shareable file.


OTOH it may not be the syntax that he is at a loss with, but the
concept of sharing files and locking records.

Record locking is done to communicate with _other_ processes
running at the same time and accessing the same files.  It
is done to prevent the other programs (or users) from updating
the designated record(s) for a short period of time.

If a record is read WITH LOCK then that record will be locked
until it is REWRITEn or UNLOCKed and other programs trying to
read that record will be informed (via the File status) that
this is not available for them at the moment, a later read
may find that the first process has released the lock.

Normally a WITH LOCK will release any other lock on that file
so that only one record at a time is locked in each file by
each process at max.

WITH KEPT LOCK does not release previous locks on records of
this file so that a single process can hold locks on
several records at one time.

Hope this helps.
```

---

<a id="t-43477ed131"></a>
## Thread: Compressing data on 3490 tapes (OS/390) — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Compressing data on 3490 tapes (OS/390)

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<853oa70ld5@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
I have a dataset that has 3.2 million 500 byte records.  It won't fit onto
one tape, even with (LABEL=1,SL) and (BLKSIZE=0, ends up being 32,xxx).  It
really slows the job down and the run unit (IDMS) frequently times out if
there are no drives available even with (VOL=,RETAIN)

I looked into (TRTCH=COMP) but our mainframe has been running like shite
lately so I couldn't get a good test on it.  We use TMS if that makes a
difference.  I went into this online thingy called CA-1 and looked at the
dataset specs.  It had a TRTCH value of TRK32.  What does this mean?  Is
compression on?

Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: Compressing data on 3490 tapes (OS/390)

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e8OQ3mTW$GA.264@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<853oa70ld5@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
You're sure the 3490's haven't already got their hardware level compression
permanently on?  Ours was permanently on.

Do you know for sure what kind of tape is being used?  3480 or 3490E?

Since the average 3480 tape holds about 500 megabytes of data (550 feet of
tape at something like 38,000 bytes per inch and with x2 for hardware
compression).  The figures for the posted problem are 3,200,000recs x
500bytes = 1.6 gigabytes, and well, that's where the problem is.  1.6
gigabytes is above the capabilities of a 3480 tape.

A 3490E tape has twice as many data tracks across the width of the tape, and
is 1100 feet long, and so holds roughly x4 data.  However, because of poor
tape quality, the full 2 gigabytes are probably not available.

The more modern Magstar tapes start at 10 gigabytes.


However, what I don't understand is why this needs to be on "one" tape (see
the post below).  Why not just extend the dataset onto multiple tapes?  Even
without adding parameters, it should extend on up to at least 5 volumes
before taking an error.


Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:853oa70ld5@enews3.newsguy.com...
> I have a dataset that has 3.2 million 500 byte records.  It won't fit onto
> one tape, even with (LABEL=1,SL) and (BLKSIZE=0, ends up being 32,xxx).
It
> really slows the job down and the run unit (IDMS) frequently times out if
> there are no drives available even with (VOL=,RETAIN)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compressing data on 3490 tapes (OS/390)

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<upo8hrTW$GA.236@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<853oa70ld5@enews3.newsguy.com> <e8OQ3mTW$GA.264@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
Oops.  3480 tapes may be read, but not written to, by 3490E tape drives.
So, with ordinary 3490 tape drives, 3480 tapes are the norm.


ChrisOsborne <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:e8OQ3mTW$GA.264@cpmsnbbsa03...
> You're sure the 3490's haven't already got their hardware level
compression
> permanently on?  Ours was permanently on.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

and
> is 1100 feet long, and so holds roughly x4 data.  However, because of poor
> tape quality, the full 2 gigabytes are probably not available.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> However, what I don't understand is why this needs to be on "one" tape
(see
> the post below).  Why not just extend the dataset onto multiple tapes?
Even
> without adding parameters, it should extend on up to at least 5 volumes
> before taking an error.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I have a dataset that has 3.2 million 500 byte records.  It won't fit
onto
> > one tape, even with (LABEL=1,SL) and (BLKSIZE=0, ends up being 32,xxx).
> It
> > really slows the job down and the run unit (IDMS) frequently times out
if
> > there are no drives available even with (VOL=,RETAIN)
> >
> > I looked into (TRTCH=COMP) but our mainframe has been running like shite
> > lately so I couldn't get a good test on it.  We use TMS if that makes a
> > difference.  I went into this online thingy called CA-1 and looked at
the
> > dataset specs.  It had a TRTCH value of TRK32.  What does this mean?  Is
> > compression on?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Compressing data on 3490 tapes (OS/390)

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000107155000.21450.00000140@nso-fz.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<853oa70ld5@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
In article <853oa70ld5@enews3.newsguy.com>, "Jeff Baynard"
<union27@macconnect.com> writes:

>
>I have a dataset that has 3.2 million 500 byte records.  It won't fit onto
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

With 3490 there should be no delays for rewind and the like, if your operators
are stacking the carts when the initial dataset is called; you should never get

one of them timeout things.
Something else has to be wrong about your setup as we use 3490 and put
some 18M records of 3400+ bytes onto 15 carts.
It might be advisable to do some header dumps to ensure you are getting the 
largest possible blocking factor out to your carts.  If you are blocked 1, it
might
be a reason for the large volume of carts.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compressing data on 3490 tapes (OS/390)

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<856ahp01va8@enews2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<853oa70ld5@enews3.newsguy.com> <20000107155000.21450.00000140@nso-fz.aol.com>`

```
Definetly not blocked to 0.  The tape drives are slow enough at peak batch
processing time to cause an IDMS timeout.  Although, the computer has been
running like crapola lately.  I complained to my director today about space
problems and running out of spool.  I really think we need an upgrade.

For Chris, the dataset can go across 2 tapes, reallly slowing things down,
again causing the timeouts.

The solution was a matter of subtle refinement.  The main space culprit
(file) was moved to a work volume instead of PROD.  The sort sped up nearly
50%.  I reduced the time of another sort step (with the classic "Don't
duplicate work" phenomenon) from 1 hour to 8 minutes (tape to tape).  A call
to 2 instances, instead of 1, of the report format controller (print
program), put all the slow tape datasets on one controller, and all the
others on the other controller.   The job completed, from production (with
CA-7 calling) in record time, processing 6.1 million print lines in 258
minutes and this includes a Kornshell process on a UNIX box and a couple FTP
steps.   The funny thing is the night before, I kicked one of the
controllers off from ROSCOE testing it and it nearly brought production to a
halt.  Later investigation revealed the SPOOL was running at over 85%.  Afer
my changes, I wanted to give it a production test but had to wait a day
while they 'recovered'.  I called systems today, they said we were running
at 71%, go ahead and run it.  Kewl!

Anyways, the job I've been screwing with has consumed about 24 hours of my
time and almost crippling the bitch.  But it's friendly now.  No timeouts,
no space problems, faster, large volume, no JES warning messages.

Jeff



----------
In article <20000107155000.21450.00000140@nso-fz.aol.com>,
sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote:


> In article <853oa70ld5@enews3.newsguy.com>, "Jeff Baynard"
> <union27@macconnect.com> writes:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> are stacking the carts when the initial dataset is called; you should never
get
>
> one of them timeout things.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Compressing data on 3490 tapes (OS/390)

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eR$MIHOW$GA.266@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<853oa70ld5@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
1. LABEL= will not affect the number of tapes needed
2. VOL= is not needed
3.TRTCH may be needed, but COMP is usually the default

You may have more than 1 type of tape drive at your disposal, check the
UNIT= parameters available for tapes.

Check the Block Size on the CA-1 Volume report.

Check with your Systems people, and see if the Storage Management System
supports VIRTUAL TAPES.
If so, they should be able to tell you about it.

You may want to use BUFNO= to speed up the I/O.

Try writing to dasd and then copy the dataset to tape (in a followup JOB).



Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:853oa70ld5@enews3.newsguy.com...
> I have a dataset that has 3.2 million 500 byte records.  It won't fit onto
> one tape, even with (LABEL=1,SL) and (BLKSIZE=0, ends up being 32,xxx).
It
> really slows the job down and the run unit (IDMS) frequently times out if
> there are no drives available even with (VOL=,RETAIN)
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff
```

<a id="t-226dcac407"></a>
## Thread: Admin: Common "prefixes" for CLC note subjects — 8 msgs

### Admin: Common "prefixes" for CLC note subjects

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<851vea$eb0$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In response to another thread, I thought I would start a list of "common
prefixes" that we might consider using in the subject lines of CLC notes (to
help filter and "group" topics)

Please post your suggestions for changes, additions, whatever. If there is
some "general consensus," I will add it to the FAQ.  OBVIOUSLY, people
(especially newcomers) don't HAVE TO use these, but if those of us who are
"regulars" do, it may help.

Admin:  - things like this that talk about the NG itself

OT: - Off-topic (this is fairly common across newsgroups)

OO: Object Orientation (Does "GUI" need to be separated out?)

MVS: (well I certainly do NOT recommend "OS/390:" <G> - but would take IBM)
           (CICS may or may not need its own)

Acu: Acucobol and Acucorp products

Fuj: Fujitsu

MF:  Micro Focus (should be MER for MERANT, but I don't think most users
think that yet)
                               (on the other hand "MF" may get confused with
"mainframe")

RM: RM
```

<a id="t-81b9b92f3f"></a>
## Thread: Y2K and Happy New Year to all of you — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Y2K and Happy New Year to all of you

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<850n2a$mc8$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
In Denmark, where I live, we hope to see something exciting in the papers,
but in vain. We have had a 100 year old Y2K baby (because of some freakware
at a hospital), that's it.

At some time, 3 months ago, I said that I would take the on call duty
myself. We had tested a large application in the financial sector for more
than a year and I was very confident that everyting was OK.

The newspapers were announcing the end of the world. Everyone were pretty
sure that a short cut in a handmixer would start World War III. The debate
was completely out of line, so I decided to take the money to follow our
lovely world blow up.

During the night I had 4 calls - None of them had anything to do with Y2K.
4.250 jobs ran during the night, and I had one program error and 3 space
problems.

I managed to eat 2 lobsters and some scrimps, and I had a bottle of
champain. 10 minutes to midnight I started a compile, celbrated the New Year
with friends and neighbours, and went to sleep at 02.30.

Happy Y2K

Ib
```

---

<a id="t-b7fe8b37f8"></a>
## Thread: OO: 101 encapsulation — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO: 101 encapsulation

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3874595C.F4029B2@zip.com.au>`

```
Encapsulation as a word implies two things:  grouping and protection.  
We encapsulate our family in a car and move the whole car and contents
on holidays with us.  On the trip we are protected by a steel body.

Procedural encapsulation.

When we write a suite we typically will not write a single program
rather a series of programs, each program has a specific task, a
report, an update screen, etc.  Each program encapsulates a specific
function.

A lot of us extend this concept by creating modules to do functions
encapsulating our hard work so that we can reuse it many times, for
example creating a screen, hiding a screen, etc.

Sometimes these tools work really well as a module.  For example I
look up a postcode using a module, the first call checks it's status
buffers the postcodes into memory and subsequent calls use the
buffered copy of the table.  I could easily replace this with a
database call, a isam table, or even data entry.  This means that I
can totally replace the concept of postcode look up in all my programs
quickly, reliably, and not change any other part of the program.

There are times that modules require a bit more cleverness.  For
example a reporting module (now let's not discuss report writer, it's
an easily understood example).  This is a problem I solved in another
language so I can define it quickly.

Define the problem:

I want a module that allows up to 3 reports to be concurrently
written.  Each report will have standard heading data set by the
program and format standard heading lines based upon that data.  Each
report will have sub headings, which may change during the execution
of the report, these will appear on each page (where defined), and
finally report lines.  The report lines will be grouped and passed as
one entity each group will be kept on a page.

Interface:
  initialize:
     report filenames
     header info
  output:
     report number
     sub heading lines (and count)
     detail lines (and count)
  finalize:
     close up the files, etc.

OK now how do we achieve this in our system:

 a) provide three calls passing information in global space
 b) provide a single call with a code to tell us which function.

The first method allows the users of the system to tap into our data
and modify it without us knowing about it.  What happens when we need
to create 5 reports?

The second method breaks cohesion.  We send in information that
controls how the module is executed.  This is considered 'bad' by
structured programming guru's.

How does OO improve this situation.  We can wrap the report into a
class each instance of that class can be exactly one report.  The data
for each report is stored in the object's data area and can only be
updated by methods that the class designer allows.  This means:

	object - report
	        data:
                        filename and file descriptor
			page count
                        line count
			heading details
		methods:
			initialize
			write
			close

I can now create as many reports as I want.  I can also add a method
to retrieve the original filename without affecting any user of the
class.

This packaging of the data and object is the basis of OO.  The ability
to create many instances and, with care, those instances working
reliably no matter how many there.  There is no conflict between the
internal data.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

---

## Thread: Student needs help with reference modification — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Student needs help with reference modification

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rlfd4.639$i9.4083@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Hi,

Our COBOL teacher gave us work over the holiday break (sigh...).  It's a
validation program.  I'm trying to use reference modification to move part
of a field (looks like a social security type number).  I get the error
message "reserved word missing or incorrectly used".  I've got this typed in
as our book shows, but the book is sometimes wrong.  We're using MF Personal
COBOL 2.0, and at times the book has shown some compiler specific things for
RM (for example, EBCDIC vs. ASCII embedded negative signs).  I wouldn't
think this is a compiler thing, but for the life of me I can't see what I've
missed.  I'm sure it's one of those "Duh!" things (they usually are!).

Here's the code:

(working-storage)
 05  IR-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER          PIC 9(9).

10   DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER.
   15 DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-1     PIC 9(3).
   15                          PIC X(1)    VALUE "-".
   15 DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-2     PIC 9(2).
   15                          PIC X(1)    VALUE "-".
   15 DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-3     PIC 9(4).

(procedure)
MOVE IR-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER:1:3 TO DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-1
MOVE IR-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER:4:2 TO DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-2
MOVE IR-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER:6 TO DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-3

I want to move the first 3 digits of IR-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER  to
DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-1, the 4th and 5th to DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-3, and the rest
to the last.

Any help would be greatly appreciated!!

Thanks,
Pat
```

#### ↳ Re: Student needs help with reference modification

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<854lof$qho$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<rlfd4.639$i9.4083@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
You need parenthesis around your starting point and length:

Change ===>

MOVE IR-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER:1:3 TO DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-1

To ===>

MOVE IR-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER (1:3) TO DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER-1

HTH....

WOB

> Our COBOL teacher gave us work over the holiday break (sigh...).  It's a

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Re:Ref modification _ thanks!!

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<855tqs$ck98$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<rlfd4.639$i9.4083@newsfeed.slurp.net> <Y_td4.1350$Mp.1224@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```

Pat <patp@cpinternet.com> wrote in message
news:Y_td4.1350$Mp.1224@newsfeed.slurp.net...
> Hi,
>
> Thanks to all you folks who answered my post.  I did need to put ( )
around
> the numbers (IR-EMPLOYEENUMBER (1:3)) .  But the book didn't have show it
> that way - really!!!  Really really!!
>
> Anyway, I was wondering, too, about a numeric edited pic of 999-99-9999.
> Wasn't sure if it would work, our book names blank, slash, etc...as
editing
> symbols, but not a - (dash).  Other than using it as a negative sign......
> Good to know in the future, thanks so much!!
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pat

I believe you're right about 999-99-9999 not being a valid picture clause.
Unless there's been a new feature of COBOL I'm not aware of, the hyphen can
only be used as a leading, trailing, or floating minus sign.  I would
accomplish that particular result by coding the following:
01  EMPL-NO                     PIC  999B99B9999.
01  FILLER REDEFINES EMPL-NO.
       05  FILLER                                      PIC  999.
       05  DASH-1                                     PIC  X.
       05  FILLER                                      PIC  99.
       05  DASH-2                                     PIC  X.
       05  FILLER                                      PIC  9999.
MOVE 123456789                                TO EMPL-NO
MOVE '-'                                                  TO DASH-1
MOVE '-'                                                  TO DASH-2
..
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Re:Ref modification _ thanks!!

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fVOd4.789$%%6.5646@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<rlfd4.639$i9.4083@newsfeed.slurp.net> <Y_td4.1350$Mp.1224@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
>Thanks to all you folks who answered my post.  I did need to put ( ) around
>the numbers (IR-EMPLOYEENUMBER (1:3)) .  But the book didn't have show it
>that way - really!!!  Really really!!


Your BOOK you are using for your COBOL class is NOT a reference manual. It
IS a book used to present things the author thinks you need to know. In the
majority of these instances, save some of the books published by readers of
this newsgroup, these books have many errors.

When something in a manual causes an error, ALWAYS refer to the manual which
is specific to your compiler.

A REFERENCE MANUAL specifically describes the syntax used by a particular
compiler. Thus it "should" be more acurate than a book for a class, not
necessarily written for one particular compiler. Further, these books are
not always written by one author, these authors may also not be using the
same compiler.

Books for college courses are created by corporations in most cases.
Sometimes even by the professors teaching the course. In both instances this
is a conflict of interest because of the money involved. The truth about a
course is then not the most important factor in writing the book. Making
money is. Therefore to meet deadlines the books are not thoroughly revied
for errors. Thus the sad state of college books.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ref modification _ thanks!!

- **From:** georgekorz@cs.com (George K)
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00

```

George K

#### ↳ Re: Student needs help with reference modification

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Pat wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the last.

Could also be done with the ever handy STRING verb:

STRING  IR-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER (1:3) '-'
	IR-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER (4:2) '-'
	IR-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER (6:)
    DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO DL-EMPLOYEE-NUMBER
END-STRING

This approach still uses reference modification. I'm not 100% certain
it's legal to use reference modification on a numeric field, but that's
easily handled if it isn't.

Bill Lynch
```

<a id="t-7f9ce2e10a"></a>
## Thread: Pseudo conversational future — 10 this month / 14 total

### Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Mainframes have been using pseudo conversational dialogs since forever. 
PCs haven't needed to release resources while waiting for the user to
think.

COBOL dialogs have been written for these two environments.

Is this distinction going to go away from programs as operating systems
handle it?  How do people convert from one system to the other?
```

#### ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qfyd4.4727$ps.55697@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
FIrst off the Operating System DOESN'T handle it on PCs or Unix Servers w/
workstations.
The Client Apps must invoke a system API call to RELEASE/RETURN temporary
control to the OS.  Otherwise your app is a 'system hog' just as a
conversational
system is on a Mainframe.
Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
> Mainframes have been using pseudo conversational dialogs since forever.
> PCs haven't needed to release resources while waiting for the user to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> handle it?  How do people convert from one system to the other?
```

#### ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#BLefGgW$GA.248@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
 CICS is, by its nature, a pseudo-conversational environment.  Will it
change?  No!

I think that what you are asking is: "With the continual increase in storage
and CPU power is it still necessary to endure the performance degradation
inherent in managing such an environment?"

My opinion is that some of the facilities provided are no longer needed.  I
doubt that I will ever manage the allocation of storage in new development.
Another feature that I feel is obsolete is storage allocated to a terminal
device.

If you don't use a facility the system does not have to administer it.

Storage allocation was necessary in the past.  However, since storage is in
abundance it seems ludicrous to manage it.

Terminal devices, green-screens, are also on the way out.  With TCP/IP
running in CICS their is no terminal device, and therefore there is no
storage available as terminal storage.  With green-screen presentations
being replaced with GUI desktop presentations, it would seem that the path
to the GUI presentation is clearer if I do not use terminal storage.


Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
> Mainframes have been using pseudo conversational dialogs since forever.
> PCs haven't needed to release resources while waiting for the user to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> handle it?  How do people convert from one system to the other?
```

#### ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 2000-01-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000107204557.02664.00000553@ng-fr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
>Subject: Pseudo conversational future
>From: Howard Brazee brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

```

#### ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** "George McGinn" <gmcginn@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XLSd4.71$dG.1818@ha1>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
When Windows came out, programming with languages like Visual Basic allowed
for Event-Driven Software to be developed.  Resources are not allocated
until they are needed and are released when the event is completed.  So if
your terminal is waiting for your user to input data to the screen, there is
no event processing.

If you are talking about CICS emulation on a PC, you still will have a CICS
Environment running.  This is the same as on the Mainframe.  In that case,
then no, certain resources on the PC will not be released, as the CICS
Emulator needs to be active.

Best thing to do is to use a GUI like VB and link to COBOL programs when the
event is requested.  Your program runs and ends.  The event should end with
the termination of the COBOL program.

If you want to keep your legacy code and still take advantage of a GUI-style
system, use a platform like VB or some other GUI system to do all your
screen and screen edits (Numeric, Range and Acceptable Value checking) and
call your legacy COBOL programs to do your processing.  This way, when you
need to change the GUI or want your application to run on different
platforms, then your "core application" does not need to change - just your
front end interfaces.


George McGinn
Systems Engineer
GTE-TSI


Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
> Mainframes have been using pseudo conversational dialogs since forever.
> PCs haven't needed to release resources while waiting for the user to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> handle it?  How do people convert from one system to the other?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eu44kIqW$GA.274@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <XLSd4.71$dG.1818@ha1>`

```

George McGinn <gmcginn@home.com> wrote in message
news:XLSd4.71$dG.1818@ha1...
>
> If you want to keep your legacy code and still take advantage of a
GUI-style
> system, use a platform like VB or some other GUI system to do all your
> screen and screen edits (Numeric, Range and Acceptable Value checking) and
> call your legacy COBOL programs to do your processing.  This way, when you
> need to change the GUI or want your application to run on different
> platforms, then your "core application" does not need to change - just
your
> front end interfaces.
>
Be careful.  If your GUI front-end is interfacing with a mainframe, you will
probably still need to validate the data on the mainframe before applying
any updates.  While the GUI front-end can significantly reduce the number of
errors, it may be counter productive to eliminate mainframe validation.

Let's assume that you work for a bank that has acquired the credit card
portfolio of another bank.  The acquisition involves over three million
accounts.  Your assignment is to add these accounts to your system.  The
system has many complex Business Rules.  If the mainframe validation program
is out of date, your workload could be overwhelming.

The migration to a GUI front-end may require modification of the mainframe
application to access tables accessible to the mainframe and the GUI
application.  Things such as: State Codes, Ranges, and Inter-dependent Field
Values would fall into this category.  This would ensure that the systems
are in sync.

In any event, the GUI application should be able to handle rejection by the
mainframe application.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** "George McGinn" <gmcginn@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QY3e4.93$dG.2235@ha1>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <XLSd4.71$dG.1818@ha1> <eu44kIqW$GA.274@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```
You're right, and that is what I meant in having your legacy programs do the
processing.

What I was trying to explain is you can have the GUI front end validate that
a numeric field is numeric, that if a field only accepts specific values
(i.e.:  A, B and C are the only valid values for a particular field) that
validation should be done in the GUI and not on the mainframe.

I would not have the GUI edit the transaction based on business rules.
These more than likely have already been built into the legacy application.
Let the application check on the validity of the record - let the GUI edit
the fields being entered to make sure that invalid characters or values, or
to make sure that a phone number is entered in the proper edit masks.

I hope that cleared up what I was trying to explain.

-----------------------------
George McGinn
Systems Engineer
GTE-TSI
Tampa  FL

DennisHarley <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> wrote in message
news:eu44kIqW$GA.274@cpmsnbbsa04...

will
> probably still need to validate the data on the mainframe before applying
> any updates.  While the GUI front-end can significantly reduce the number
of
> errors, it may be counter productive to eliminate mainframe validation.
```

GUI-style

Let's assume that you work for a bank that has acquired the credit card

system has many complex Business Rules.  If the mainframe validation program
is out of date, your workload could be overwhelming.

The migration to a GUI front-end may require modification of the mainframe
application to access tables accessible to the mainframe and the GUI
application.  Things such as: State Codes, Ranges, and Inter-dependent Field
Values would fall into this category.  This would ensure that the systems
are in sync.

#### ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O_Td4.18204$g12.569304@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
I think Theo DP's equation is a pretty reasonable statement but leaves a bit
out in terms of an explanation ;-).

While pseudo-conversational programming may not apply directly to PC
programming, the concept of transaction based programming certainly does,
especially for client/server applications. Designing your program around a
logical unit of work is still valid.

When programming for GUI, the programming for an event model is
significantly different from the traditional state machine model that gets
used in pseudo conversational coding. However, there are similarities. You
still need to initialize stuff before passing control to a user. You still
need to respond to user actions, albeit without assuming that other
dependent actions have occurred. You still need to deal with data storage
and retrieval, business logic, etc. The differences are in how and when that
code is performed.

The approach to validation of a graphical application ought to be different
than the way it is done on the mainframe. In the 3270 world, it is
impossible to respond to every user action. Only certain keys cause a
conversation with the mainframe, namely the function keys, enter key, and a
few others. This means a heavy emphasis on correcting user's mistakes after
they have been made, via validation routines. In the Windows world every
user action flows through the message loop. Most of the messages are passed
on to Windows to handle but the few that you care about can be intercepted
and dealt with in event handling routines. This implies that you can help
prevent the user from making mistakes rather than telling them that they
have made one after the fact.

If the user must enter valid data before proceeding then disable the 'Apply'
button until they do. If they have to choose from a certain set of values
then give them a list of valid values. If some data depends on other data
then update the interface to reflect the dependency. If the data must have a
certain format (zip code, phone number, e-mail address) then intercept the
keystrokes and inhibit the entering of bad data on each keystroke, or
provide the necessary punctuation automatically .

The funny thing is, a GUI control is a lot like a 3270 terminal. It takes
some data, handles the presentation and accepts user changes. The data is
stored within the control until the program asks for it. Most COBOL GUI
tools provide a means of moving this data into WORKING-STORAGE so a COBOL
programmer doesn't need to worry about the mechanics of doing this, even
though leaving it in the control and mapping a linkage item to it is a more
efficient way to use resources, just like the way the COMMAREA is usually
handled in CICS programs. In fairness to the makers of the COBOL GUI tools
(including me), they have to cope with the conflicting requirements between
efficiency and ease-of-use.

Karl Wagner

"Howard Brazee" <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
> Mainframes have been using pseudo conversational dialogs since forever.
> PCs haven't needed to release resources while waiting for the user to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> handle it?  How do people convert from one system to the other?
```

#### ↳ Re: Pseudo conversational future

- **From:** ThaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-I44eeWdndhrR@localhost>`
- **References:** `<387642BD.256B4EAF@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
On Thu, 1 Jan 1970 01:59:59, Howard Brazee 
<brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote:

> Mainframes have been using pseudo conversational dialogs since forever. 
> PCs haven't needed to release resources while waiting for the user to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> handle it?  How do people convert from one system to the other?


Guess what.  As PC applications move "to the web" they BECOME pseudo 
conversational.  Any CGI application is pseudoconversational, up to 
and including the point of using hidden fields on the screen to hold 
place keeping data!  So we have not come as far as we appear to have 
come.

Moving from Pseudo conversational to conversational is very easy.  In 
text mode, replace the send/receive with a display/accept.  With GUI 
use something like sp2 and replace the send/receive with a panel 
converse call.  Simple. 

Now, going the OTHER way, from a field by field display/accept program
or an event driven GUI program, where there is code behind each 
object, is much much harder - but it's being done every day as people 
move applications into the browser.

-------------------------

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

Mark Blumenkrantz wrote:

> Anyone have any info on converting RM Cobol data files to dbase, access or
> other more easily accessible file types ??

I'm obviously not using a current version like you - but - if you are
doing a once only quickie then there are tools. However, if you are in a
situation where you are to-ing and frow-ing between RM and the database
- my approach would be, read the RM files sequentially, generating ascii
delimiteds which can be fed into your DB when you have set the DB record
structure. (The ascii delimiteds also give you the advantage of editing
the text files).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

OK, so we can assume that you're running the Micro Focus SCO compiler under
Linux? i don't know what kind of terminal you are using, but as i recall, i
think Micro Focus put a set of it's own terminfo entries into
$COBDIR/terminfo. if you're on a PC, try 'at386' (but then again, if you're
on a PC using telnet, then it all depends on how good your telnet software
is at emulating -- some are not very good at all; especially when you're
trying to get the right color sets).
```

first 4 numbers = PERSNUMBER
then 21 karakters = PERSNAME
then 11 karakters = PERSFIRSTN
then 22 karakters = PERSSTREET
then 20 karakters = CITY
then 7 cijfers = ABNNUM

then 6 numbers = AMOUNT

amount higher than 500000.

Cheers,
Gerda Beerda.

#### ↳ Re: Can someone help me please?

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-01-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000110105303.5437A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`
- **References:** `<858lo5$2j95$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
Generally this can be done a couple different ways.

One way is to read one file and search the other file.  This requires that
the file you are searching to have a different access say random and use
an index/record key.

Another way would be to read the file you want to search into a table and
search the table (Table must have an index to search it).  Normally the
table is loaded in the housekeeping or initialization section of the
program.  Since you are reading till end of file, the EOJ switch would
have to be switched back and reused for the other file.

If you don't understand any of this maybe you should have gone to class.

However searching files and tables requires you to read your book and find
out how to do it.  Most people will not do it for you in this newsgroup.

I will give you this hint:

select filea
 assign to file1-database
  organization is indexed
   access is random
    record key is emp-nbr

This will only work for a flat file.  Access of a VSAM file on a mainframe
is a little different.  Better read your book a little about searching a
file with an index or a record key.

A lot depends on how your instructor wants it done.  Just start writing
the program and get all the file descriptions written out and maybe you
will get a little more help.  You have to make an effort to figure it out.

Good Luck.

 Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program

On Sun, 9 Jan 2000, Gerda Beerda wrote:

> Who can help me with the following problem:
> 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Can someone help me please?

- **From:** slarsen22@aol.com (SLarsen22)
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000129152753.28337.00000318@ng-cd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000110105303.5437A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`

```
A flat file does not have a key, and thus an index either.  That is why it is
called a flat file.  To rephrase, a keyed file must have an index and is not a
flat file.  You can still search any flat file on any bytes, but then you don't
have the advantage of the index.  If you are talking databases - different
subject. 
.Pray to God but row the boat for shore....
 >http://members.aol.com/SLarsen22>
```

Try this:
if mobile-num = zeros
   then
    write file-name
            invalid key
                       display "error with key"
           not invalid-key
                      display "last record read"
                      go to aa-close-files
   end-if.

```
        When you are attempting to read an entire indexed file, you
have to avoid adding new records or changing the key field (changing prime
key is same as read, delete, write).

    The same thing can even happen in a simple "database", like
a dos directory.  Example:  Create a for loop that runs edlin, or some other
editor that uses a similar save mechanism.  You will encounter some files
more than once.  If the batch file erases BAK files as you go, you can
skip some files.

    Same problem.  As stated below, when changing prime key on an entire
file,
you should copy the file.


<SNIP>
> > > 2) I have an indexed file with the key:
> > >
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

- **References:** `<84bsiq$ume$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <386A1E5B.61C9F466@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <85a39k$9c0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <85h67p$ql8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <85qhgp$46l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


morella3253@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> In article <85h67p$ql8$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>,
…[84 more quoted lines elided]…
>  key flags.

I'm very familiar with the VMS compiler.
There are two posibilities in this case:

1. You do not have a declaratives section ("use after expection") for
the file involved. In this case the VMS compiler requires that you use
the "invalid key" phrases.

2. There is a declaratives section for the file involved. Now you have a
choice of using the "Invalid key" phrase, but it is no longer required.

It is my guess that the old programs have a declaratives section, and
the new programs don't.

With regard to indexed files on VMS the following:

1. Normally it is not possible to change the primary key, however the
file can be created in such a way that changing the primary key is
possible.

2. The records in a indexed file are physically in the same sequence as
the primary key.

3. Inserting records in a indexed file is very well possible without any
problems for performance etc. IF you reserve enough free space when
populating the file for the first time.

4. As with all other VMS languages, Cobol uses the Record Management
System (RMS file system) of VMS for its file access.

5. Creating and populating an indexed file by means of a Cobol program
is not a very good practice. The (easiest) way to do it is like this:

5.1. Let your Cobol program open a small (!) indexed file for output.
5.2. Use Analyse /RMS/FDL to create a FDL file description where you can
find all information about the keys etc.
5.3. Use Edit/FDL to make a new FDL file where you can use all the
desired parameters for free space, number of records etc. (Using global
buffers will speed up your program dramatically).
5.4. If you have data to fill the file for the first time, let a Cobol
program create a sequential (!) file with the same record layout as the
indexed file.
5.5. Use Convert/FDL to convert the sequential file to a the indexed
file.
5.6. If you already have a indexed file, you can use the same
Convert/FDL command to make the new indexed file. 

6. Read the RMS manuals !! If you want to use indexed files on VMS, you
can't do it without knowledge of RMS. Trying to do so will almost always
result in extremely poor performing Cobol programs if the files have a
size of any importance. In fact with larger files with several keys it
may even be impossible to populate a file, or to get any reasonable
performance. 
  
If you want to read a indexed file sequentially on the primary key, and
at the same time be able to change (=delete + write or rewrite if the
file has been created the right way) the primary key, you can use two
access streams to the file. The first reads the file sequentially, the
other in random mode.

It works like this:

Loop.
	read my-file-seq at end etc.
	move file-rec-seq to file-rec-random
	read my-file-random
	delete my-file-random
	(change key values in file-rec-random)
	write file-rec-random
	go to loop.

If you need more info on Cobol on VMS please let me know.

regards,

Dirk


> 
>  Thanks
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-f6e5080c85"></a>
## Thread: IBM - OS/390 Reference Material - FYI — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### IBM - OS/390 Reference Material - FYI

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OhWSMSoW$GA.224@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
Reference materials for IBM products are available in soft copy format from
IBM.

IBM has an internet site with reference manuals including: COBOL, CICS, DB2,
JCL etc.

http://www.s390.ibm.com/os390/bkserv/

The other option is a set of CD roms with more than you ever wanted to know
about software you never knew existed.
I prefer the CD collections since they can be installed on a LAN or PC.
The cost of the CD set was around $100, the last time I had a client order
them.

Order information can be found at:

http://www.s390.ibm.com/products/softcopy/softord.htm

A note on the CD rom set:
The contents of the CD rom may be overwhelming. What I have done, is to copy
the publications applicable to the current releases of the software being
used at the installation to a LAN, and set up a Bookshelf with those titles.
This makes it easier to find the applicable reference manuals when they are
needed.

It amazes me that businesses will spend millions on a computer, and then
skimp on manuals.
The same people would return a $100 VCR if it came without instructions.
I have often found obsolete 10 year old manuals still in use because "the
manuals are too expensive".
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM - OS/390 Reference Material - FYI

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OO3dkrtW$GA.70@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<OhWSMSoW$GA.224@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
DennisHarley <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> wrote in message
news:OhWSMSoW$GA.224@cpmsnbbsa02...
>
> It amazes me that businesses will spend millions on a computer, and then
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I've been in a company where not providing manuals is common practice.

I think the main reason for this attitude is that a handful of people will
literally steal every manual in sight, and so employers eventually give up
buying them.  I saw several books vanish (and a couple of laptops, to boot).
Someone stole a $60 book of mine right off my desk shelf one day (which was
the end of me leaving my books out).  Never did get it back.

I also think, regardless of how other people may laugh at me for the
thought, that each programmer should be provided with all the vendor manuals
on each subject they have any responsibility for.  This way, there won't be
any theft, because each person will have what they need.  In only a few
cases, like tech support, is this impractical on a size/space basis.

If an employer simply kept a list of what manuals had been issued to each
programmer, and made it plain that anything not turned in at the end of a
project or employment would be docked from salary, then theft or simple loss
wouldn't be much of a problem.  Employers could even provide a few more
lockable cabinents to prevent the sort of theft that might be perpetrated
just for malicious reasons.

Ah well.  End of rant.
```

The site above, presents the manuals in BookManager format - which I think is
fine, for those used to working with BookManager.  For those who would prefer
an Adobe Acrobat Reader-compaitble PDF file, check the web site for the
particular product, and look for a "library" in the site navigation window -
you will usually find the PDF and a "zipped" version suitable for downloading. 

A good starting point is the site:

http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/


Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best

You can download a single book, but you don't get the corresponding
bookshelf component if you do.
Without the bookshelf component you have to "work around" rather than "work
with" the Library Readed.
