Hello,

i have to compile a Cobol Programm with the old MS-Cobol V.5.0 compiler.
I have folowing batch-file to make the compile:

@echo off
if "%2" == "anim" goto anim
call cobol %1.cbl,%1.obj,%1.scr,%1.obl /REMOVE"FUNCTION""NUMBER"
rem if "%OS%" == "Windows_NT" goto ende
rem if not "%2" == "link" goto ende
link %1.obj,%1.exe,nul.map,coblib+cobapi,nul.def
goto ende
echo.
echo Hinweis:
echo --------   Mit dem EXE-File mï¿½ssen die Dateien
echo.
echo                    ADIS.EXE
echo                    COBLIB.DLE
echo                    XM.EXE      (bei 386er Anwendungen)
echo.
echo            mit ins Zielverzeichnis kopiert werden!
echo.
goto ende
:anim
cobol %1.cbl,%1.obj,%1.scr,%1.obl anim
echo.
echo Hinweis:
echo --------   Mit dem Befehl
echo
echo                    ANIMATE %1
echo.
echo            kann nun die Animation gestartet werden.
echo.
:ende
-------------------------------------------------

The normal compile works well, also the compile with the "animate" option.
But if i will invoke the animater with following command-line:
animate OFTP05
the animater invokes and porduces than the message:
programm does not exist: OFTP05
All modules of the program are on the current library.

Has anybody some idea. Any help would be apprciated.

Best regards

Arnim Karberg

## Thread: Question regarding WIN32 API PrintDlg — 11 msgs

### Question regarding WIN32 API PrintDlg

- **From:** Chris T. <ctaliercio@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85i7io$4qd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am using the WIN32 "PrintDlg" API call to display a print dialog box
for the user. The structure that is passed to this routine is:

01  PRINT-DLG.
    05  lStructSize             PIC  X(04)  COMP-5.
    05  hwndOwner               PIC  X(04)  COMP-5.
    05  hDevMode                PIC  X(04)  COMP-5.
    05  hDevNames               PIC  X(04)  COMP-5.
    05  hDC                     PIC  X(04)  COMP-5.
    05  Flags                   PIC  X(04)  COMP-5.
    05  nFromPage               PIC  X(02)  COMP-5.
    05  nToPage                 PIC  X(02)  COMP-5.
    05  nMinPage                PIC  X(02)  COMP-5.
    05  nMaxPage                PIC  X(02)  COMP-5.
    05  nCopies                 PIC  X(02)  COMP-5.
    05  hInstance               PIC  X(04)  COMP-5.
    05  lCustData               PIC  X(04)  COMP-5.
    05  lpfnPrintHook           POINTER.
    05  lpfnSetupHook           POINTER.
    05  lpPrintTemplateName     POINTER.
    05  lpSetupTemplateName     POINTER.
    05  hPrintTemplate          PIC  X(04)  COMP-5.
    05  hSetupTemplate          PIC  X(04)  COMP-5.

On return from the API call, the hDevMode and hDevNames contain handles
to other structures which contain all of the information the user
selected in the Print Dailog box.

I am NOT using object COBOL, so my question is this:

Is there a way to set the address of the DEVMODE and DEVNAME structures
I have defined in my program based upon the returned handle?

Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks! - Chris


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Question regarding WIN32 API PrintDlg

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85ifuc$hkh$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<85i7io$4qd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Chris,

Yes, you can do this. You need to define the DEVMODE and DEVNAMES structures
in LINKAGE SECTION and then SET ADDRESS OF DEVMODE to <pointer>. I can't
remember offhand whether you can define hDevNames as a pointer and do it
directly or whether you need to call the GlobalLock API and get a pointer
back (the latter would be safer though), but that should be all you will
need to do.

The same principle can be used for any API that returns a pointer to a
structure.

Chris T. wrote in message <85i7io$4qd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I am using the WIN32 "PrintDlg" API call to display a print dialog box
>for the user. The structure that is passed to this routine is:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Question regarding WIN32 API PrintDlg

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85j5fd$a71$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<85i7io$4qd$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <85ifuc$hkh$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
> The same principle can be used for any API that returns a pointer to a
structure.>

This is not always the case....

Ensure that you know the length of the data returned and globally allocated
by an api call...If not defined properly, you will abend...For example
getting data from the clipboard returns such a pointer to a null terminated
string...But since you do not know the length, you can only reference it one
byte at a time...treat it as an array of one byte elements, bumping the
index until you get to the null...MF cobol will abend with a 114 if it
thinks the length is more than what is actually there...

kenmullins
```

There are easier ways to deal with it than coding a loop. Try:

1) INSPECT my-var TALLYING x FOR ALL CHARACTERS BEFORE INITIAL LOW-VALUE
then using a reference modified move.

2) UNSTRING my-var DELIMITED LOW-VALUE. INTO ...

- Karl Wagner

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question regarding WIN32 API PrintDlg

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85jl3f$e3s$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<85i7io$4qd$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <85ifuc$hkh$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <85j5fd$a71$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <iyaf4.32756$g12.975855@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
How big do you define my-var to be when you don't know how big of an area
the API is going to return???...Seems like I remember defining an area that
would be more than enough for what I was concerned with...Problem is, MF
abended with a 114 when referencing my-var if it was bigger than the actual
data returned...Even though my-var was in the linkage section...The only way
I could do it was to code it as a one byte element in an array, and
reference it one byte at a time...

kenmullins

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question regarding WIN32 API PrintDlg

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vinf4.34352$g12.1035820@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<85i7io$4qd$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <85ifuc$hkh$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <85j5fd$a71$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net> <iyaf4.32756$g12.975855@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <85jl3f$e3s$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Don't reference my-var. Reference my-var(1:len), where 'len' is determined
using either an INSPECT or by length information returned by the API. When
you reference my-var there is a risk of a 114 when you get beyond the
low-value.

Another trick is to use an 'OCCURS DEPENDING ON len' in the linkage
definition. It's not too different from what you're probably already doing
byte-at-a-time but once you've established the value of 'len' you can
reference my-var safely.

Karl Wagner

"Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com> wrote in message
news:85jl3f$e3s$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net...
> How big do you define my-var to be when you don't know how big of an area
> the API is going to return???...Seems like I remember defining an area
that
> would be more than enough for what I was concerned with...Problem is, MF
> abended with a 114 when referencing my-var if it was bigger than the
actual
> data returned...Even though my-var was in the linkage section...The only
way
> I could do it was to code it as a one byte element in an array, and
> reference it one byte at a time...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > The same principle can be used for any API that returns a pointer to
a
> > > structure.>
> > >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > string...But since you do not know the length, you can only reference
it
> > one
> > > byte at a time...treat it as an array of one byte elements, bumping
the
> > > index until you get to the null...MF cobol will abend with a 114 if it
> > > thinks the length is more than what is actually there...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Question regarding WIN32 API PrintDlg

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85ifqt$hf6$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<85i7io$4qd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Chris T. <ctaliercio@my-deja.com> wrote:
>I am using the WIN32 "PrintDlg" API call to display a print dialog box
>for the user. The structure that is passed to this routine is:
[snip]
>
>On return from the API call, the hDevMode and hDevNames contain handles
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I have defined in my program based upon the returned handle?

Chris,
Try this:
Use the API "GlobalLock" to get the addresses of each structure as 
a POINTER then use SET ADDRESS of the DEVMODE/DEVNAME structures you have 
defined in _linkage_ section to those POINTERs.
When you've finished, use "GlobalUnlock"

Steve.
```

#### ↳ Re: Question regarding WIN32 API PrintDlg

- **From:** Chris T. <ctaliercio@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85nupu$cqj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<85i7io$4qd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
My thanks to everyone who submitted answers to this issue. The
functions GlobalLock and GlobalUnlock worked perfectly.

Thanks!
Chris


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

---

## Thread: Q: MicroFocus Cobol and C — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Q: MicroFocus Cobol and C

- **From:** Jacob Fried <friedy@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387C8E16.F5E673BB@ibm.net>`

```
How can I start a Cobol application from within a C program using the
load() system call ?

Using the execl("Cobol_executable",...) system call is working for the
same Cobol application but using load("Cobol_executable",...) is keep
crashing with segmentation fault.

The Cobol application was generated using Micro-Focus v4 compiler and I
have tried it on few Unix platforms (IBM-AIX, HP-UX,DEC-UNIX) with the
same results.

Thanks
Jacob
```

- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387C9C28.553446C@t-online.de>`
- **References:** `<387C8E16.F5E673BB@ibm.net>`

```
Jacob Fried wrote:

> How can I start a Cobol application from within a C program using the
> load() system call ?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jacob

  Look in MF manuals - there are examples how to call C from Cobol and
Cobol from C.
  Is the rts active?

   Vaclav
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: MicroFocus Cobol and C

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0rfq7s06fql8majuj6ntp8r08tma38tru5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<387C8E16.F5E673BB@ibm.net>`

```
Jacob Fried <friedy@ibm.net> wrote:

>How can I start a Cobol application from within a C program using the
>load() system call ?

>Using the execl("Cobol_executable",...) system call is working for the
>same Cobol application but using load("Cobol_executable",...) is keep
>crashing with segmentation fault.

segmentation refers to using a different memory model for compilation.
thus, a program which uses 64k will always run in one for 128k, but
not the reverse. the solution is to match your compiler options and
directives, i believe.


>The Cobol application was generated using Micro-Focus v4 compiler and I
>have tried it on few Unix platforms (IBM-AIX, HP-UX,DEC-UNIX) with the
>same results.


reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Q: MicroFocus Cobol and C

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000113113305.00552.00000401@nso-cm.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<0rfq7s06fql8majuj6ntp8r08tma38tru5@4ax.com>`

```
In article <0rfq7s06fql8majuj6ntp8r08tma38tru5@4ax.com>, G Moore
<gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> writes:

>
>>Using the execl("Cobol_executable",...) system call is working for the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It seems to me that the function of execl and load are totally different.
execl is more like a spawn or system() command that executes an 
external program to run concurrent to the calling program and does not
receive a return.  while the load() operation sounds more like a sub-program
call or DLL type operation.  I do not have a C language reference that tells 
me about load() and am only making a guess from the problems you are having.

When using Load(), are you trying to wait on this other process to finish
and do something with a return value or output from the called process?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Q: MicroFocus Cobol and C

- **From:** Jacob Fried <friedy@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38817A8B.2E829F6F@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<0rfq7s06fql8majuj6ntp8r08tma38tru5@4ax.com> <20000113113305.00552.00000401@nso-cm.aol.com>`

```


Sff5ky wrote:

>
> It seems to me that the function of execl and load are totally different.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

load() is Unix system call which  loads a specified module into the calling
process's address space, while execl() executes a new program in the calling
process and overlays the current program with the new image.

>
> When using Load(), are you trying to wait on this other process to finish
> and do something with a return value or output from the called process?

The load() system call aborts while I am trying to load a cobol written program,
while using load() for the same code rewritten in C is working.
```

---

<a id="t-6cee37f298"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu compiler question — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu compiler question

- **From:** "Brian" <bduke@mind~spring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DA8f4.1523$CfWc.10485985@news5.usenetserver.com>`

```
I have a COBOL routine that currently is compiled using the DEC Cobol
compiler to run on VMS. I'm trying to compile this with the Fujitsu compiler
so I can call the routine from a VC++ program running on Windows. For the
most part it seems to work ok, but as best I can tell there is a problem
with what Fujitsu thinks is 0+ (signed zero). I use this routine to convert
numbers to send to the cobol routine:

void CobolPack(char* buf, long lValue, BOOL bSigned, int iDigits, int
iPrecision)
{
 const char cSignBuf[21] = "{ABCDEFGHI}JKLMNOPQR";

 BOOL bNegative = FALSE;

 if ( bSigned && lValue < 0 )
 {
  lValue = lValue * -1;
  bNegative = TRUE;
 }

 int lBufLength = iDigits + iPrecision;

 // converts long to ascii string
 LongToString(buf, lBufLength, lValue);

 if ( bSigned )
 {
  char cLastChar = buf[lBufLength-1] - 48;
  if ( bNegative )
   cLastChar += 10;
  buf[lBufLength-1] = cSignBuf[cLastChar];
 }
}

It seems Fujitsu expects an '@' or hex 40 to represent 0+ rather than '{' or
hex 7b. Is there a way to tell the Fujitsu compiler to use '{' or hex 7b?
This is causing my calculations to be off by a small decimal amount.

Thanks,
Brian
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu compiler question

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85k441$1csi$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<DA8f4.1523$CfWc.10485985@news5.usenetserver.com>`

```
[Posted and mailed, in case it doesn't post]

The variable 'cSignBuf' contains EBCDIC sign characters.
DEC COBOL's use of EBCDIC sign characters with ASCII
numeric characters seems to be peculiar.

Try using ASCII sign characters by changing the string
for 'cSignBuf' to "0123456789pqrstuvwxy".

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith

Brian wrote in message ...
>I have a COBOL routine that currently is compiled using the DEC Cobol
>compiler to run on VMS. I'm trying to compile this with the Fujitsu
compiler
>so I can call the routine from a VC++ program running on Windows. For the
>most part it seems to work ok, but as best I can tell there is a problem
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> const char cSignBuf[21] = "{ABCDEFGHI}JKLMNOPQR";
[...]
```

- **From:** ThaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jl0PnHJ5PvPd-pn2-dqNtjeXdsvpF@localhost>`
- **References:** `<DA8f4.1523$CfWc.10485985@news5.usenetserver.com> <85k441$1csi$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
On Sun, 13 Jan 3900 07:29:41, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> 
wrote:

> [Posted and mailed, in case it doesn't post]
> 
> The variable 'cSignBuf' contains EBCDIC sign characters.
> DEC COBOL's use of EBCDIC sign characters with ASCII
> numeric characters seems to be peculiar.

very easy!

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu compiler question

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387DB993.6DEB64CD@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<DA8f4.1523$CfWc.10485985@news5.usenetserver.com>`

```
Brian wrote:
> 
> I have a COBOL routine that currently is compiled using the DEC Cobol
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Brian

This sounds like you are using packed fields.  Remove the COMP-3 or
PACKED from the PIC statements in the Cobol source and pass in the
numbers in clear text.  

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu compiler question Just Guessing

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<DA8f4.1523$CfWc.10485985@news5.usenetserver.com> <387DB993.6DEB64CD@zip.com.au>`

```

you may need to send the sign as a separate character.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compiler

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85j4au$ubu$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<85gjri$16fb$2@buty.wanadoo.nl> <387C90E2.4176F13F@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <85j3pe$2c3t$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
I'll bet you haven't marked your program as "MAIN".  This is an unusual
(unique?) issue with the Fujitsu compiler - but is included in the COBOL FAQ
and frequently in the past in this NG.

  Put
     @OPTIONS MAIN

before your ID division (of the main program) and see if the problem goes
away.
```

In article <85k45b$mf0$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>, "Gerda Beerda" <g.beerda@wanadoo.nl>
writes:

>
>Oh uh yeah ofcourse.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This looks like your program is attempting to work with a file that you 
did not setup a runtime environment variable for.

    Select My-File Assign to file1.

In the mainframe world , you would have a JCL with a DDName FILE1
followed by the full file name to be processed.   In Fujitsu, the operations
are similar.   When the program executes, a run-time environment screen
is presented where you can set environment variables to help your program
associate internal assignments to actual external files.

  FILE1=C:\TEMP\SOMEFILE.DAT
  BSORT-TMPDIR=D:\TEMP;E:\TEMP;P:\REALLYBIGSPACE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: compiler

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
That one is easy to solve.  Make your program a MAIN program.  Use the
compiler options or better yet include 
@OPTIONS MAIN 
on the first line beginning in column 8.

If you really want a leg up on using the compiler and all aspects of
Ansi 85 COBOL, check out my book: Sams Teach Yourself COBOL in 24
Hours.  Available on most online book sellers and in a Barnes And
Noble near you.  (They seem to keep it in stock, I have watched the
first, second and third printings disappear from the local store).

<g.beerda@wanadoo.nl> wrote:

>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Benn Morris wrote:
>

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

open file.
read file at end set eof to true.
perform until EOF

	read file at end ...
end-perform.
close file.

Show a sample program, tell us your compiler and platform and you will
find the answers grasshopper.

- **From:** Uwe Wahser <uwe.wahser@mlp-ag.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387C2AA1.B8132DD9@mlp-ag.com>`

```
Hello all,

we have some cobol-jobs running under UNIX which assign an output file
by ASSIGN TO '$OUTFILE' (organization sequential, access sequential).
OUTFILE has been properly assigned in the calling batch file.

For some reason the OPEN statement will produce a file status 35,
sometimes 37, for some jobs. A workaround was to read the environment
variable into a unix variable, and to do the assign via that
unix-variable. Is that a solution? or are we only hiding a problem?
which reason could be behind the problem?

thanks, uwe
```

<a id="t-7878cfb96a"></a>
## Thread: Student: vsam lookup — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student: vsam lookup

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ky%e4.536$Oe.4748@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<387c637e.16018824@news.supernews.com>`

```
I'm sure you'll figure this out soon enough, but you can only do control
breaks with sequential processing if the data are sorted. You either SORT
the data
within your program, or recreate the VSAM index and process sequentially.

If the VSAM index is not in the order you need -  the order of your control
break hierarchy - it may as well not be there.
```

---

<a id="t-270832109a"></a>
## Thread: Student: vsam lookup — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student: vsam lookup

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000112094545.06658.00001876@ng-fc1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<387c637e.16018824@news.supernews.com>`

```
You choices are something like sort the input to match one of the files key
sequences, or extract the file and sort the extract to match the data you are
doing 'control breaks on'.

The extract does not have to be the whole file. You can use the sorted extract
to go back at the VSAM file in random sequence.

At some point the apples must get into oranges order, or the oranges into
apples order, ... unless, of course, as a student you have permission to do a
BUILDX of yet another secondary index!

A variation on that is to load a new file from your extract, with keys that fit
your needs.




Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

---

<a id="t-14ebe7998f"></a>
## Thread: Student: vsam lookup — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Student: vsam lookup

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#8zp#bSX$GA.275@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<387c637e.16018824@news.supernews.com>`

```
What you are doing is the classic: Extract, Sort, Print.

There are 3 methods available:
1. Create a COBOL program with SORT INPUT and OUTPUT Sections.
2. Create Separate Extract, and Print COBOL Programs.
3. Use the SORT utility for Extract, and Sorting. Create a COBOL Print
program.

Method 1 is the least desirable. There may be times when you have to rerun
the report. If you use this method you will need to restore a backup of the
VSAM file in order to recreate the report This backup may not be available.
The data is time dependent. You may not be able to recreate the report.

Method 2 would be my choice. At times it is necessary to know where a
particular field is used in the system. The source library is my Management
Information System. If I want to see where a particular field is used, I
will scan the source library.
It is beneficial to save the extract, preferrably after the sort, as a GDG.
If the report needs to be recreated you only have to rerun the report step
adjusting the relative generation number.

Method 3 is just a variation of step 2.

Using Alternate Indexes is slow, especially when you are accessing the
entire file. Using alternate indexes causes random access. I once changed a
program that was using alternate indexes to Extract, Sort, and Print, and
run time went from 5 hours to 15 minutes.

Programming is a lot like Chess. If you play five moves ahead, you will beat
the person who doesn't.

zennmaster <zenn@master.net> wrote in message
news:387c637e.16018824@news.supernews.com...
> I'm grappling with a vsam control break project that breaks on a field
> that is neither the primary key nor alternate key.  Presently I'm
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks
```

---

<a id="t-7624bc17b0"></a>
## Thread: code to generate a unique client code — 16 msgs

### code to generate a unique client code

- **From:** Mark Fletcher <spanna2000@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
We have an old COBOL system that indexes records by a client code. The
client code is user entered Eg. John Smith = SMITH J. It has become
convention to add a numeric suffix to the code to make it unique.

Some customers have SMITH J, SMITH J1 etc
Others have SMITH J01, SMITH J02 etc
Others have SMITH J001, SMITH J099 etc

Does anyone have code to automate this that they're prepared to share
(for free)? I appreciate it's not a huge coding problem but there are
some peculiarities which have to be overcome e.g. file read sequence
SMITH J1, SMITH J10, SMITH J2.
```

#### ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Douglas Miller)
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Mark Fletcher <spanna2000@my-deja.com> wrote:
>We have an old COBOL system that indexes records by a client code. The
>client code is user entered Eg. John Smith = SMITH J. It has become
>convention to add a numeric suffix to the code to make it unique.

Assigning a sequentially incremented client *number* would be a better idea, 
for reasons that will become clear in a moment.
>
>Some customers have SMITH J, SMITH J1 etc
>Others have SMITH J01, SMITH J02 etc
>Others have SMITH J001, SMITH J099 etc

When you add another SMITHJ to the file, you have to read through *all* 
previous SMITHJs to be sure you are able to prepare a unique suffix -- which 
is why you should assign sequentially incremented client numbers. Keep the 
next sequence number in a separate file; read that file once to get the 
number; increment it; and write it back to the file.

>
>Does anyone have code to automate this that they're prepared to share
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Another reason for using incrementing client numbers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

- **From:** Mark Fletcher <spanna2000@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85o045$dp8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net>`

```
I understand the point about sequential numbers. The file has one of
those, unique and invariant. The key we refer to here is the mnemonic
that the user knows the client as.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net>`

```


Douglas Miller wrote:
> 
> In article <85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Mark Fletcher <spanna2000@my-deja.com> wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Doug,

Nothing wrong with what you are suggesting, but what Mark is after, as
he says subsequently, is a mnemonic code that users can remember. From a
design concept we are beyond the bean-counter days when everything was
expressed as numeric keys - that goes back to the 80-column punch-card
and manipulating columns of numbers to obtain different accounting or
statistical results.

From knowledgeable end-users there is now a demand for 'alphas' to
remember codes without referencing hard-copy lists. You can achieve this
with listboxes and dropdown lists, but in a heavily intensive data entry
system, such as plugging invoices into Accounts Payable, it has to be
less tedious. (I know that my son and other bean-counters in his company
had heated arguments with the systems designers before hitting on their
particular solution).

Can I offer a solution - No. It depends upon the company/type of
business. Other alternatives would be to add part of the Street/Road,
City etc - but that depends upon whether these codes are 'localized' to
a one city operation, or is this provincial/state, or national.

I can tell you one good solution that did work effectively - and we are
back to numerics - when VAT(Value Added Tax) was introduced into UK
about '73, the government issued a Mod 97 check-digited VAT # to each
business - and this had to be printed on EVERY invoice. Bingo ! A lot of
digits, ( 11, I think (?)) - but a unique way to identify a
supplier/vendor. 

And here's the real nice part - using Product X(?) which was a PDP 11 -
we shoved A/P data entry on to the bean-counters to stop their bitching
about delays in data entry - worked real good with our mainframe edits
reading, "batch accepted", "batch accepted", etc.............

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

- **From:** "keldin" <keldin_despam@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38814438_2@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net> <388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com>`

```
Well working for one of the largest finiancial services company worldwide.
I must point out that  assigning characters to the unique identifier will
slow data entry considerably.    With 16 digit account numbers Statistics
are 2300 to 3000 payments an hour keyed.  There is a couple of companies
that we also do the payment processing for and they have alpha/numerics it
can slow down to less 500 processed an hour.   102 key versus 10 keys makes
quite a difference and the cost in extra employees to key in the checks is
considerable.

Just a thought on going with 16 digit account #'s  besides the 1st 6 digits
are a bin# and can be replaced with a single letter bin code to speed things
up.

like
 4123-4500-0000-0001  same as V-00-0000-0001
 5123-4500-0000-9999  same as M-00-0000-9999

you can idenify 26 fast keyed brands like this
like visa, mastercard, gold master card, prefered, platinum etc. after
keying 25,000 of these a day / employee your data entry people will
appreciate it.

TJ


"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com...
>
>
> Douglas Miller wrote:
> >
> > In article <85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, Mark Fletcher
<spanna2000@my-deja.com> wrote:
> > >We have an old COBOL system that indexes records by a client code. The
> > >client code is user entered Eg. John Smith = SMITH J. It has become
> > >convention to add a numeric suffix to the code to make it unique.
> >
> > Assigning a sequentially incremented client *number* would be a better
idea,
> > for reasons that will become clear in a moment.
> > >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > previous SMITHJs to be sure you are able to prepare a unique suffix --
which
> > is why you should assign sequentially incremented client numbers. Keep
the
> > next sequence number in a separate file; read that file once to get the
> > number; increment it; and write it back to the file.
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<388163AE.D818328A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net> <388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com> <38814438_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```


keldin wrote:
> 
> Well working for one of the largest finiancial services company worldwide.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Wouldn't disagree one bit with what you are saying, your system being
data input intensive, and I'll take a guess, for the most part, also
check-digited; you are achieving the same results as we did with VAT
#'s. But as cited, I'm guessing Mark was looking for a user easily
recognized code for random input or selection, rather than your batch
approach(?) or our batch approach using a unique VAT #, akin to a
Mastercard/Visa #. In terms of design your comments numeric v. alpha
keyboard strokes are pivotal to an efficient performance ratio.

I'm guessing you are restricted to batch control sequence - in our case
with VAT #'s - and assuming we had 235 invoices from Aquascutum, for 60
stores, and 120 invoices from London Fog etc., then the efficiency is
increased even more if a bit of manual sorting by the operator of 'like'
documents goes on before data entry - thus allowing for 'key
duplicating' on the PrimeKey.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Mark Fletcher <spanna2000@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86ce7i$smj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net> <388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com> <38814438_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
In article <38814438_2@news1.prserv.net>,
  "keldin" <keldin_despam@ibm.net> wrote:
> Well working for one of the largest finiancial services company
worldwide.
> I must point out that  assigning characters to the unique identifier
will
> slow data entry considerably.    With 16 digit account numbers

This is undoubtably true but it doesn't work in a call centre
environment where the customer doesn't have a unique number. The
typical customer serverd by this application will make one or two
enquiries a year, even if they were allocated a number they wouldn't
use it.

The problem here is not data entry speed it's finding the record as
quickly as possible without a customer number.

Even with the post code/telephone no work that we've already discussed
the solution won't always fit. The application gets used quite a lot in
Africa where they are a long way from 1 telephone per household. post
box numbers will probably work though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "keldin" <keldin_despam@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<388bf08d_1@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net> <388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com> <38814438_2@news1.prserv.net> <86ce7i$smj$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I agree that the methods used in industrial countries, may not work in the
third world.  Not having experience in this area I do not have a solution.
I was not aware it was for africa from previous postings.

On the Americas though numbers work Best IMHO.
I worked as a rep for many years b4 transitioning over to the IT dept, a
good 95% of callers to the call centers have the account #.  People know
that it will be the first question we ask you followed by mothers maiden
name and zip-code.    Experience here, shows that if you don't know this its
usually not the card holder and a good indicator of possible fraud.  There
are the exceptions, lost card, left at home, etc. but its not the norm.
Also if you call from work or home or another number on your account, your
account pops up on our screens when you call.  If none of this works there
are always searches by zip code/last name/phone #/ss#/even the account #
from a check you paid us with.
I think people pushing for alphanumerics are just going to turn this system
into a nightmare to work with.
One more comment, you would be surprised at the number of people that have
the 16 digit account#'s memorized.

TJ

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86b8fi$64o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net> <388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

: I can tell you one good solution that did work effectively - and we are
: back to numerics - when VAT(Value Added Tax) was introduced into UK
: about '73, the government issued a Mod 97 check-digited VAT # to each
: business - and this had to be printed on EVERY invoice. Bingo ! A lot of
: digits, ( 11, I think (?)) - but a unique way to identify a
: supplier/vendor. 

Another effective mechanism is to use their phone number, including
area code if required.  If they ring you up for a query they
usually can tell you their phone, but may have trouble reciting
their VAT number.  The phone number should also be on letter heads,
faxes, etc so it is easily the better.  Also you may be lucky
enough to be able to capture the caller id into your computer
when they ring and have their details on-screen when you pick up
the handset.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38892EF4.E20AF90F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net> <388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com> <86b8fi$64o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Richard,

That's not a bad idea as an Alternate Key, but it contains a flaw if you
use it as the PrimeKey. Most major corporations don't change frequently,
but smaller companies move premises, and no doubt as here in Canada,
where possible they try to take their existing phone numbers with them -
but NOT always. If their phone number changes, so does your PrimeKey. It
works, but a government number like a VAT # is more constant. And phone
numbers for persons as opposed to corporations - think how often they
change. Maybe they don't jump from one island to the other down where
you are, but there's still a lot of 'internal migration' here in N.
America.

A further bug to telephone numbers - only last year Alberta was divided
into two area codes for Northern and Southern.

I recall first system we worked on - even before we got near to
computerization, the Sales Manager was demanding we include salesman's
number in customer account number. We resisted strongly; six months
later he had re-allocated salesmen to new territories !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "keldin" <keldin_despam@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38893afb_1@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net> <388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com> <86b8fi$64o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38892EF4.E20AF90F@home.com>`

```
This can get complex quick.

We have a DB2 on line database of our accounts with these identifiers.

Account#, Customer#, Household#

A house can have multiple customers, and a customer can have multiple
accounts.

Most people are married and most people have multiple visa/mastercards....
your household/customer is unique and is the same on all of your accounts.
We deal with millions of accounts, so this is probably more detail than you
need at a small company.
We dedupe by household/customer to save on mailings etc we only need to mail
you once regardless if you have 5 accounts with us (even though you may
think they are different card companies) IE this also generates large sorts
a different thread going here

We also can't use ss# (or VAT# canada) due to not every account holder is
from the USA.

TJ

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86c615$fhb$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net> <388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com> <86b8fi$64o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <38892EF4.E20AF90F@home.com> <38893afb_1@news1.prserv.net>`

```
And a customer can have more than one household if he is young/rich, i
beleive.

You cannot implement many-to-many relations. My suggestion is to implement a
unique hash-number as primary key in the 3 entities. Store household key in
customer as a foreign key, and customer key in account as a foreign key.

Now, if many-to-many is an exception, you might want to duplicate the
customer and account entities, but if the real world shows that many-to-many
is normal, you need to create a new table to take care of e.g.
household/customer relations. It could be that a customer is related  both
to his company and his family. The latter solution will give you an extra
select or an extra table in your union/join.

All other access plans could use alternate index. Remember to analyze
performance with DB-tools before you put 2 million card-holders in. We don't
want too many multiple index scan's, do we?

Yes, I beleive in this one.

Regards
Ib

keldin skrev i meddelelsen <38893afb_1@news1.prserv.net>...

…[56 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

- **From:** Mark Fletcher <spanna2000@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86cdeq$s2p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6lwf4.347$Rw3.6654641@news.netdirect.net> <388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com>`

```
In article <388104C9.72AA4F85@home.com>,
  "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
>
> what Mark is after, as
> he says subsequently, is a mnemonic code that users can remember.
> From a design concept we are beyond the bean-counter days when
> everything was expressed as numeric keys

The mnemonic serves us well in almost all areas even with the
limitation that we currently ask the operator to decide what the next
increment to the mnemonic should be as it's tedious when it's Smith
J579.

We introduced an invariant code for links to external systems.
Operators converting from systems which were using numeric codes often
prefer to continue to use the numeric (they the ones making most of the
fuss about the "problem" of alpha codes) but the rest of us love the
alpha mnemonics.

I've now got some code that creates the unique key by appending a
number to an operator suggested alpha when necessary. If anyone is
interested mail me. It works in almost all cases and safe fails when
there isn't space left to append a numeric, works to three numerics
where possible, reducing to 2 and then 1 if the alpha code is too long.

I'd like to offer a telephone number, post code, national insurance
(social security) number search and I plan to add these later this year
(we already do post code to address conversions).
```

#### ↳ Re: code to generate a unique client code

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85revr$8bt$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<85ksbo$3hn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Just a thought..

Add a sequential number to the key (numb-key PIC 9(04) COMP).

MOVE "SMITH" TO text-key
MOVE 65535 TO numb-key
LOCK a control file
START file KEY <= key-fields (text+numb)
READ file previous
IF text-key = "SMITH" THEN
  IF numb-key < 65535
    MOVE my-data TO my-record
    ADD 1 TO numb-key
    WRITE my-record
    UNLOCK control-file
  else
    UNLOCK control-file
    DISPLAY "To many SMITH'S"
  end
else
  MOVE my-data TO my-record
  MOVE 1 TO numb-key
  WRITE my-record
  UNLOCK control-file
end

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CzLg4.986$SM.12670@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<387DFF69.58F0CF68@gecm.com> <85r2fe$fmf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Arthur wrote:
>
>Hello, I think not all bugs were corrected.

Agreed.

> I also think the Feb 29 2000
>problem will cause lot of troubles (because nobody know about it). Just
>to say not all computers know leap-year formula is 1/4 - 1/100 + 1/400.

I do not agree.  Why would anyone have put the Year/100 test for leap
year in a system using only a 2 digit year?  Very, very few systems
using 2 digit year were concerned with February, 1900.  If they were
concerned with dates that far back, they would probably have also been
concerned with dates before 1900, and used 4 digit year.  My guess is
that there will be more programs (if any) that fail 2/29/2000 because
of faulty Y2K remediation than because they did the Y/100 test, but not
the Y/400 test, in the original code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K

- **From:** "Tim Hillock" <hillock@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5CNg4.1928$nh2.65575@cac1.rdr.news.psi.ca>`
- **References:** `<387DFF69.58F0CF68@gecm.com> <85r2fe$fmf$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <CzLg4.986$SM.12670@news4.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:CzLg4.986$SM.12670@news4.mia...
> Arthur wrote:
> >
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

2000 / 4 = 500 with no remainder.  Should be flagged as a leap year.
Programs not using the 400 yr test shouldn't fail.  it will fail in/with the
year 2100 or 1900, in the sense that it's not a leap year although it too
has zero remainder.

Most programs I've seen have only the 4 yr test and not the 400 or 100 yr.
98.9999 % of the programs should work ok in the day to day business world.
Of couse this falls apart if they still somehow use 2 digits for a date
somewhere.

Tim
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Y2K

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Thompson, William" <wthompson@okc.disa.mil>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0007BFC0B3AED3118336009027AF56DB68D85E@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
The only two gotchas we had were the default for cleared core and the
default for abend handling, both primarily with CICS.

Bill

-----Original Message-----
From: Ron Davis [mailto:rdavis@ozemail.com.au]
Sent: Sunday, January 09, 2000 10:20 PM
To: comp.lang.cobol@list.deja.com
Subject: Upgrading COBOL II R3.2 to COBOL for MVS, Hints and tips
requested...


 Message from the Deja.com forum: 
 comp.lang.cobol
 Your subscription is set to individual email delivery

Folks,
Our site will shortly be upgrading as described in the subject line.  I
would appreciate any tips from others who might have recently gone through
this exercise.
At this stage we look like our best bet will be to move first to running
our existing COBOL II under the Language Environment Run Time, then
recompiling the R3.2 Programs under COBOL for MVS, as and when they require
changes. 
All our programs are compiled with the RES compiler option and from what
limited testing I've done so far they seem to run fine with the LE runtime.
If you're aware of any "gotchas" with the upgrade I'd certainly appreciate
hearing about them!
Replies by email to rdavis@ozemail.com.au or ron.davis@dva.gov.au would be
especially appreciated.
Thanks
Ron Davis

```
Hi there :-)

we had a assembler program which would NOT save register 10 and use it in
the program (apparently some trick from  the old days....) Under Cobol 1
this worked fine - under LE we crashed 0c4.... so if you have old assembler
code you might check if they save ALL registers and restore (like they
should in the first place).

werner

Thompson, William <wthompson@okc.disa.mil> wrote in message
news:0007BFC0B3AED3118336009027AF56DB68D85E@voyager.okc.disa.mil...

## Thread: PIC syntax — 5 msgs

### PIC syntax

- **From:** mohrin@sharmahd.com (Torsten Mohrin)
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387edc28.5090079@news.aball.de>`

```
Dear collegues!

I need to convert some data files. I have a record declaration in
COBOL. It's some years ago since I wrote my last COBOL program, so I
can't remember some of PIC syntax (what's "V"? for example).

Is there an overview of the PIC syntax available online. I don't want
to buy a COBOL book.

Thank you very much.

Torsten
```

#### ↳ Re: PIC syntax

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85l0af$6qp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<387edc28.5090079@news.aball.de>`

```
In article <387edc28.5090079@news.aball.de>,
  mohrin@sharmahd.com wrote:
> Dear collegues!
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Torsten,

You can download free the IBM COBOL Language Reference in PDF format
(readable with Adobe Acrobat) at:
http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/coblib.htm
BTW: the 'V' is the decimal place: '1234567' in a PIC 9(3)V9(4) has the
meaning of 123.4567.
```

#### ↳ Re: PIC syntax

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4nnf4.150$8z5.2131@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<387edc28.5090079@news.aball.de>`

```
Well, 'V' means 'implied decimal point'.  It establishes where the
decimal point would be, but takes up no memory.

Right now on this newsgroup there is a thread on IBM OS/390 reference
books, all of which are available online.  You can check that out,
or you can go to www.adtools.com and download the documentation (and
compiler) for Fujitsu COBOL v3.
```

You can find the same sample chapter at the http://www.mcp.com site.

-------------------------

Try a better search engine: http://www.google.com

Visit my updated website at
http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/

<a id="t-0fff36fa68"></a>
## Thread: CALL X ON OVERFLOW — 6 msgs

### CALL X ON OVERFLOW

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387E23A1.5C6AD0A7@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Is the only difference between

CALL 'MYPROG' USING MYFIELDS
    ON OVERFLOW  PERFORM MYROUTINE
END-CALL
and
CALL 'MYPROG' USING MYFIELDS
    ON EXCEPTION  PERFORM MYROUTINE
END-CALL

that the second call can have a NOT ON EXCEPTION ??

If so, why?
```

#### ↳ Re: CALL X ON OVERFLOW

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85l9v4$o5m$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<387E23A1.5C6AD0A7@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
There is a "NOT ON EXCEPTION" phrase - there is not a "NOT ON OVERFLOW"
phrase.  Furthermore, "ON OVERFLOW" is being made "archaic" in the next
Standard.

As for whether or not there is any difference between "ON OVERFLOW" and "ON
EXCEPTION" - this depends on your specific COBOL.  The reason that the '85
Standard "added" the "<not> ON EXCEPTION" was that the rules for when an "ON
OVERFLOW" were pretty "obscure" and not all vendors treated it the same.  In
you case, Howard, as I recall you are an "OS/390" type person.  I believe
that IBM treats the two as identical (i.e. the same things that cause an "ON
EXCEPTION" also cause an "ON OVERFLOW" and vice versa.  However, this isn't
true with every implementation.

The bottom-line: is that you (everyone) SHOULD be moving to "ON EXCEPTION" -
and reading your manual on exactly what can cause this.  However, this
PROBABLY isn't a change that will actually change run-time behavior.

Does this answer the question?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CALL X ON OVERFLOW

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387E5968.6750A6FD@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<387E23A1.5C6AD0A7@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <85l9v4$o5m$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 

> The bottom-line: is that you (everyone) SHOULD be moving to "ON EXCEPTION" -
> and reading your manual on exactly what can cause this.  However, this
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Yep.

Thanks
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CALL X ON OVERFLOW

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000113224816.12140.00000193@ng-cr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<85l9v4$o5m$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill covered it mostly, yet let me add two very esoteric specifics (which
probably do not matter).

ON OVERFLOW can be made to behave differently under CMPR2 versus NOCMPR2. You
can not make a semantic difference for the newer ON EXCEPTION clause with the
old regression parm.

There is one other twist, and this is strange (all these comments apply to IBM
mainframe).  A _NOT_ ON EXCEPTION clause will be ignored in the absence of an
ON EXCEPTION clause!

The presence of an ON EXCEPTION clause will active the _NOT_ ON EXCEPTION
clause; the ON OVERFLOW clause will probably not do that. :-)

So if you are adding a _NOT_ clause to an old program with an ON OVERFLOW,
change the error dection to ON EXCEPTION.

I would expect diagnostics to motivate this anyway.

In a nutshell, the two clauses ON OVERFLOW and ON EXCEPTION are semantically
identical in that they both behave identically in the unusual condition in
which they behave at all. But they have a syntactic difference, in that a
successful CALL will not invoke _NOT_ ON EXCEPTION if it is only paired
syntactically with ON OVERFLOW.

I think that means, when you see this code, change the 'OVERFLOW' to
'EXCEPTION' (be careful, though, there are other OVERFLOW keywords in COBOL
(the STRING verb :-) ).

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CALL X ON OVERFLOW

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85m96c$rcb$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<85l9v4$o5m$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <20000113224816.12140.00000193@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
Robert,
  Are you saying that some release of IBM mainframe COBOL accepts (as valid
syntax - no error message):

    CALL ABC
         On overflow
              Display "1"
         Not On Exception
               Display "2"
            .

That SHOULD get a compiler error (with IBM COBOL - or any other compiler that
I know of) - but I don't have easy access to IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM to
test it out.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CALL X ON OVERFLOW

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6yKf4.1293$Lm3.13589@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<85l9v4$o5m$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <20000113224816.12140.00000193@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk wrote in message <20000113224816.12140.00000193@ng-cr1.aol.com>...
>Bill covered it mostly, yet let me add two very esoteric specifics (which
>probably do not matter).
>
>There is one other twist, and this is strange (all these comments apply to
IBM
>mainframe).  A _NOT_ ON EXCEPTION clause will be ignored in the absence of
an
>ON EXCEPTION clause!


Does the code after NOT ON EXCEPTION execute? It should, as if there _is_ an
exception, with no ON EXCEPTION clause your program is no longer running
anyway (806 (?) abend).

MCM
```

## Thread: MF - keys not detected — 4 msgs

### MF - keys not detected

- **From:** "Pete Jones" <peter@percival.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85kkms$rlb$1@news1.btconnect.com>`

```
I've just started a contract where I've gone back in time, and am using some
old MF Cobol products ! Can anyone help with this problem?

Environment - DOS box under Windows 98.

Cobol versions - MF 3.4 & 4.0

Env vars: I won't repeat these - I've been using MF s/w on various platforms
for 15 years, so I'm fairly confident I am switching these around ok.

Running: INT programs via the GUI Animator and "run.exe"

The problem I've come across is to do with the cursor direction keys (arrows
plus home, end, etc) in the separate key block and on the numeric pad.  In
all but one case, both blocks of keys work. In ALL cases, the numeric keypad
keys work.

So ......
3.4 runtime, 3.4 GUI animator - both ok
3.4 runtime, 3.4 run.exe - both ok
4.0 runtime, 4.0 GUI animator - both ok
4.0 runtime, 4.0 run.exe - separate keypad keys fails, numeric keypad
direction keys ok

I've trapped the value returned by these keys to the COBOL programs. When
working, they return x"3203" (dec 50 03), x"3204" etc. When they fail, ALL
return x"3909" (dec 57 09).

Oh, I'm using the same keyboard mapping files each time, which is backed up
by the fact that the direction keys work with the GUI animator 4.0, and the
function keys always work.

Any ideas would be welcome!

Pete Jones



3.4 under animator: separate keys work, nu
```

#### ↳ Re: MF - keys not detected

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387e2620.253579524@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<85kkms$rlb$1@news1.btconnect.com>`

```
"Pete Jones" <peter@percival.co.uk> schrieb:

 [Cursor keys]
>3.4 runtime, 3.4 GUI animator - both ok
>3.4 runtime, 3.4 run.exe - both ok
>4.0 runtime, 4.0 GUI animator - both ok
>4.0 runtime, 4.0 run.exe - separate keypad keys fails, numeric keypad
>direction keys ok

This is a bug with Win98 and MF Textmode Applications. MF says, the
problem is a bug in Win98s' CONAGENT.EXE but with NetExpress 3.0 it
works well. So whom to blame?

As a workaround you might want to use RUNW.EXE instead of RUN.EXE

Best regards


Sven
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF - keys not detected

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85mtr4$2jd$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<85kkms$rlb$1@news1.btconnect.com> <387e2620.253579524@news.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Sven,

Sven Brueggemann wrote in message <387e2620.253579524@news.btx.dtag.de>...
>
>This is a bug with Win98 and MF Textmode Applications. MF says, the
>problem is a bug in Win98s' CONAGENT.EXE but with NetExpress 3.0 it
>works well. So whom to blame?
>


Re: my previous post. I don't know who at MF told you that this is a bug in
CONAGENT but they were mistaken. I can say this with absolute certainty
because (and this could be a mistake to admit :-) ) I personally fixed the
problem for 4.0.38 and NetExpress.

The APIs that are used in console apps return slightly different values
between Win95 or NT and Win98, hence the original problem, but in this case
I would not consider the problem to be CONAGENT, borne out by the fact that
it is now fixed.
```

### Registry (continued)

- **From:** Peter van Zeeland <peter.van.zeeland@cmg.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-01-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6DE2D20B5851D2119BB300A0C9DAF698703262@NL-AMB-MAIL01>`

```

Jimmy, Michael,

Thanks for your replies. Yes: SDK has some API's to read/write from the
registry, and YES you can call'em from NetExpress quite easily. Even from
.int code, even when debugging.

One of this groups readers, Jean Villemaire, emailed me something worth
sharing: 

	Peter 

	Saw your post on the Cobol newsgroup.

	You might want to check the following link;

	http://pita.microfocus.com/prodsupp/adv/netx/index.htm

	It is the NetExpress customer support page, it
	contains
	many examples including some that you are looking for.

	The following should be interesting to you;

	http://pita.microfocus.com/prodsupp/adv/netx/getuser.zip

	http://pita.microfocus.com/prodsupp/adv/netx/getnuser.zip

	http://pita.microfocus.com/prodsupp/adv/netx/cobreg.zip

	This last one shows you how to access theWindows
	Registry.

These are ready-to-run examples.
For my original question - how to identify the machine you are running on -
I just stayed with the GetComputerName( ) API, that delivers whatever was
given in the control-panel -> network app -> identification page. Even when
not logged in to windows. This serves my purpose well enough.

Just thought I'd let you guys know.



 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

Roberto.

## Thread: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational. — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** "Bjorn Alvik" <bjorn.alvik@asp.no>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no>`

```
Hi there!

I need to convert a lot of minicomputer conversational COBOL programs to
COBOL/CICS.
Target program has to be pseudo-conversational. Not an easy issue!!!!
I believe, however, that creating a dynamic method for Working-Storage
save/reload  will be of significant value.

Does anyone know how to address and access the whole WS from within a
COBOL/CICS program?

Thank you,

Bjorn
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<enLJqvqX$GA.235@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no>`

```
Which platforms are you working on? IBM mainframe?

The areas you want to focus your attention on are: the COMMAREA, and
Temporary Storage Queues.

These areas are generally used to preserve the thread of a conversation.

Bjorn Alvik <bjorn.alvik@asp.no> wrote in message
news:0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no...
> Hi there!
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85ng7b$qu$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no>`

```
In article <0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no>,
  "Bjorn Alvik" <bjorn.alvik@asp.no> wrote:
> Hi there!
>
> I need to convert a lot of minicomputer conversational COBOL programs
to
> COBOL/CICS.
> Target program has to be pseudo-conversational. Not an easy issue!!!!
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bjorn

Maybe this will be useful...
A common implementation style for pseudo-conversational uses the 'NEXT
TRANID' and a COMMAREA. CICS sequesters the COMMAREA when you pass
control back to the typist. When the typist returns to your tran, the
COMMAREA is made available to your program. The COMMAREA could be all
of W-S but that is usually a lot more than you need.
John
>
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

Try to use EXEC CICS SEND MAP END-EXEC.
OR/AND  EXEC CICS RECEIVE MAP END-EXEC.

FOR YOUR SCREEN.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s90k3s777q5133@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no> <vk308sgh6ocu1kt93bg247i4sppcifrq1q@4ax.com>`

```
With 1000 programs, you really don't have any choice but automation.
We offer such tools and service; see
http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html.
In particular, these tools can read your sources, and carry out automated
transformations on them.

The key issue defining the exact transformations.
As you said, you have conversational programs, an implicitly expected
execution environment ("VSSUBS", plus I'm sure a lot more of Wang VS
features).    The transforms must discover the conversation
fragments, group them together into more block-like groups of I/O, and
replace all the
I/O with CICS stuff.    This is difficult but not impractical to code with
automated tools

But probably much less effort, and far more reliable than hand-porting.

Ira Baxter, Ph.D., CTO   idbaxter@semdesigns.com     512-250-1018 x140
Semantic Designs, Inc.   12636 Research Blvd #C214, Austin Texas 78759



> >  "Bjorn Alvik" bjorn.alvik@asp.no
> > Date: Fri, 14 Jan 2000 14:55:01 +0100
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> > l
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** zvi_dvir@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85qaou$vms$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no>`

```
In order to design a pseudo conversational system
you have to

1 Split your application to transactions - each transaction
should be a logical unit of work.
2 The transactions can pass data to each other via Temporary storage
Commarea etc.
3 The cics Initializes the working storage when the task begins.
If you need to keep some of it to a next session tou can save it in
Temp. storage.

In article <0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no>,
  "Bjorn Alvik" <bjorn.alvik@asp.no> wrote:

to

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** slarsen22@aol.com (SLarsen22)
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000129154624.28337.00000337@ng-cd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<85qaou$vms$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I don't know cobol and minis, however i program in mainframe cobol/cics.  I
usually use the commarea which is passes each time the task starts again (not
the first time) through the linkage section.  In the comarea I store whatever
the next screen/program/task needs.  You can't save the working-storage area
unless you take the trouble to move it, or the required portion of it, to the
commarea.  Then "SEND" with "COMMAREA", otherwise he won't include the
commarea.  As I said, it must be different for minis.   
.Pray to God but row the boat for shore....
 >http://members.aol.com/SLarsen22>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ SV: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** "Bjorn Alvik" <bjorn.alvik@asp.no>
- **Date:** 2000-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mfVk4.17357$in5.304374@news1.online.no>`
- **References:** `<85qaou$vms$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <20000129154624.28337.00000337@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
It does not nessecarily have to be different for minis, but on minis it is
convenient to program in a conversational way. Therefore, migrating
mini-programs to CICS gives you trouble. The other way is much more easy:
Moving from pseudo- to real conversational is a matter of changing a
sequence of instructions or the target for the program exit.
I certainly have no trouble with understanding and practisising the
pseudo-conversational method. But to turn conversational progs into
pseudo-conversational "withouth touching" the "business logic" is far from
simple. Especially when the programs mix any kind of processing into a
delicious spaghetti.
Saving working storage elements in Comarea or Tempstorage before each screen
display and restoring them after the receives might be part of a migration
method. But it would have to be done "automatic". Adding thousends of moves
an repective logic to more than 1000 programs really is out of the question.
Therefore I have been looking for a method to dynamically grasp the whole
working-storage, no matter how each program has defined it. And I certainly
can see no good hope for a proper solution.
However, I still will permit miracles to happen....
Bjorn

SLarsen22 <slarsen22@aol.com> skrev i
meldingsnyheter:20000129154624.28337.00000337@ng-cd1.aol.com...

I
> usually use the commarea which is passes each time the task starts again
(not

whatever
> the next screen/program/task needs.  You can't save the working-storage
area

the

#### ↳ Re: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000117111727.28428.00000478@ng-fl1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no>`

```



 "Bjorn Alvik" bjorn.alvik@asp.no 
Date: Fri, 14 Jan 2000 14:55:01 +0100
wrote
<<
Hi there!

I need to convert a lot of minicomputer conversational COBOL programs to
COBOL/CICS.
Target program has to be pseudo-conversational. Not an easy issue!!!!
I believe, however, that creating a dynamic method for Working-Storage
save/reload  will be of significant value.

Does anyone know how to address and access the whole WS from within a
COBOL/CICS program?

Thank you,

Bjorn
l
>>
Bjorn,

On the the mainframe, atleast, COBOL can not be assumed to  have a single
contiguous Working Storage.  To answer your question literally, we would have
to get into some routine that accessed the locations which hold a set of Base
Locators for numerous areas that hold working storage items. And that would be
very unworkable anyway.

Generally, each 01 (or 77) must be presumed to be placed in it's own unique
place! (although not really, its just that you can't assume any relationship of
proximity or sequence; especially in a virual environment).

CICS actually will copy certain work areas around dynamically too (particularly
if there are any mixtures of data above the line and below the line).

So I think you are going to need to deal in address lists, that being lists of
01 groups passed in USING clauses, Or else one by one  via CICS INTO() and
FROM() keywords.

Working storage is not a singularity. It is not contiguous. If you can break
your requirements down to more specific requests for help I am sure folks here
in the COBOL NG will be happy to interact with you.

Best Wishes


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ SV: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** "Bjorn Alvik" <bjorn.alvik@asp.no>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GQXg4.6365$in5.105370@news1.online.no>`
- **References:** `<0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no> <20000117111727.28428.00000478@ng-fl1.aol.com>`

```
Hi, Robert.

Thank you very much for your clarifications.
Your comment on WS non-singularity/contiguty is convincing, and I certainly
shall bother no more with the "whole-WS-grabbing" method.

Theoretically, some kind of address-list-method like you mentioned,  is
workable. The number of programs and screens, though, is high (1000/500).
Elapsed conversion time and error risk will be high, and the future
maintenance on the converted systems will not be easier than before.

Unfortunately, many programs also deal with display/read of multiple
screens. All the programs are written in an old-fashined, monolithic way,
i.e. with no COBOL SECTIONS and a lot of  GO TOs. Above all, all file access
is done with LOCK.

The source system is Wang VS. Conversion also have to deal with replacing a
lot of the so called VSSUBS, a set of predefined subroutines in the Wang VS
environment.

I know there are conversion tools out there, moving Wang to MicroFocus.
Maybe they can be useful, but i doubt that they, even with extensive
modifications, will be able to help moving the programs into the mainframe
CICS world.

So, what I need and what I want to discuss and work with, is a sound method,
preferably automated, to perform this conversion task.

See you

Bjorn



RKRayhawk <rkrayhawk@aol.com> skrev i
meldingsnyheter:20000117111727.28428.00000478@ng-fl1.aol.com...
>
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> contiguous Working Storage.  To answer your question literally, we would
have
> to get into some routine that accessed the locations which hold a set of
Base
> Locators for numerous areas that hold working storage items. And that
would be
> very unworkable anyway.
>
> Generally, each 01 (or 77) must be presumed to be placed in it's own
unique
> place! (although not really, its just that you can't assume any
relationship of
> proximity or sequence; especially in a virual environment).
>
> CICS actually will copy certain work areas around dynamically too
(particularly
> if there are any mixtures of data above the line and below the line).
>

lists of
> 01 groups passed in USING clauses, Or else one by one  via CICS INTO() and

break

here

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SV: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** chris@mullermedia.com
- **Date:** 2000-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86n8vb$hnl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no> <20000117111727.28428.00000478@ng-fl1.aol.com> <GQXg4.6365$in5.105370@news1.online.no>`

```
Noticed that you're migrating from Wang VS. Just in case you have a
substantial amount of data to convert, check out "Fast 'n Easy" at
www.mullermedia.com. It basically allows a PC to read Wang backup tapes
and translates various data formats. The result is that you don't need
the Wang system itself--can even convert it's data after it's long-gone.

Note that COBOL source code is "data", too. So tape could be an
efficient mechanism for moving the source files to PC/ASCII. (Of
course, conversion of the application is a completely separate matter--
which this package does not address.)

regards,  Chris Muller

In article <GQXg4.6365$in5.105370@news1.online.no>,

#### ↳ Re: Converting Conversational COBOL programs to pseudo-conversational.

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7bvh4.7537$SM.151957@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no>`

```
You can NOT pass more than 32K - 4bytes via
EXEC  CICS
            XCTL/RETURN [TRANSID]
          COMMAREA(some-name)
END-EXEC.

Accessing your data (WS) ina COBOL CICS pgm is just like a NON-CICS pgm.

If you need to 'retain' more than 32k - 4bytes, then you must use Transient
Data or
Temporary Storage (best).  You 'WRITEQ TS' and 'READQ TS' whenever you are
Going to XCTL, RETURN, or LINK.

Bjorn Alvik <bjorn.alvik@asp.no> wrote in message
news:0YFf4.3172$in5.52505@news1.online.no...

>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Is there anyplace on line i can get info on how COBOL and SQL speak to each
other? I Know this is done via the EXEC SQL .....END-EXEC commands, but how

ANSI spec on this?

failure.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL info please?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38807B11.C7E31B2E@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<NPHf4.10234$lN.241764@nnrp3.clara.net> <OHnbvrqX$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <2UNf4.5452$UL1.108920@nnrp4.clara.net>`

```
alan jones wrote:
> 
> Well that's kind of a long story, if i said Amiga or even someday
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> there other legetimate ways to connect to the database.

Since you are a potential linux user :-}...

SQL is an Ansi specification, use the IBM manuals online to work out
the various syntax details.  The basic problem with SQL is that there
are subtle variations in the way things actually work with the access
of data.  This means it is not as portable as originally hoped.

http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/Library

The connection to the database is generally externalised from the
program.  For example, the program is started in a database 'shell' or
there are non SQL statements that connect you to the database.

DB2 for example links the database via a 'plan' that is defined when
you start the program.  This plan allows the one program to run in
production and test without change by referencing the tables.

The other method is ODBC (microsloth) which connects to the database
and you can pass the SQL text to it.  Multiple vendors provide the
same API so that your application can hook up to many vendors. 
Gotcha's in this is that the SQL is subtly different (again).

Other platforms provide there own interface, MYSQL freeware on linux
and other platforms work this way.  You pass the SQL text in and it
returns the data by a series of calls.

Hope this helps
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```
