###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL info please?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000116144716.10160.00000178@ng-cn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3l%f4.11032$lN.267999@nnrp3.clara.net>`

```
>I wonder why it is implementors feel obliged to had their own quirkes to
>compilers, when the whole point of standards, is to have the source
>consistent for all implementations of  a given language?

Well. it usually is the result of one of the following:
1).  The standards committee was too political, or too biased, or too stupid
(sorry, WMK) to use my/our brilliant idea, so I/we will add it as an
"extension", so that at least my/our customers will be able to take advantage
of it.
2).  If I/we just have the standard's functions in my/our implementation, then
the sales folks will have no justification to say mine/ours is the best
(They'll say it any way, but it won't be justified!).
3).  If I/we just have the standard's functions in my/our implementation, then
there is absolutely nothing to stop my/our customers from using his/her/their
product instead, and I/we will have lost a customer.

See Microsoft and JAVA for examples of all of above.


Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

```
alan jones wrote:
> 
> Hi ken, your info is the best yet. My problem is i was thinking of
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> source consistent for all implementations of  a given language?

You hit the nail on the head,  I wonder why everyone does not use the
precompiler concept and keep that bit standard.

The normal way to handle this is to create a covering module that
provides the access to a data source (any data source). The second
tier of this is to provide a dynamic SQL interface routine. This can
then be replaced by a dynamic call to the real database,  this of
course adds overhead galore.

There are some C wrappers for Mysql that might be worth porting to
cobol to make it easier.  I have a reference somewhere if you are
interested, I found it too late for my project.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL info please?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Gerald Moull" <gerald@moull.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<860fi3$64k$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<NPHf4.10234$lN.241764@nnrp3.clara.net> <OHnbvrqX$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <2UNf4.5452$UL1.108920@nnrp4.clara.net> <38807B11.C7E31B2E@zip.com.au> <3l%f4.11032$lN.267999@nnrp3.clara.net> <38831112.4A9143EC@zip.com.au>`

```
I've found this thread quite interesting.

I've just helped write the SQL implementation for RM/COBOL and talk of
'standards' is an interesting (and amusing!) topic to me in many ways.

I am a firm believer in the concept and benefits of COBOL.  I write this
response therefore not as a representetive of Liant - but as someone
interested in COBOL as we all are.

I hate the 'traditional' way of COBOL EXEC SQL and preprocessors.  Why
should you have to preprocess source code?  To compile and then compile
again sounds rediculous to my simple way of thinking (obviously waiting for
the replies on the use of simple!).  I stepped back from any preconcieved
ideas of how SQL 'should' be used with COBOL and hopefully those guys back
40 years ago would be proud of us!  We've taken a look at how new 'SQL only'
languages use SQL and implemented it to COBOL ANSI-85 standards.  Obviously
there is some glue in there at execution time but any COBOL compiler could
compile the code without the need for any 'special' C or SQL datatypes and
the glue can be adapted for portability (to different databases by Liant
and, for the concept back 40 years ago, other COBOLs).

As previous postings have said, there are many different types of standards
when combining COBOL and SQL.  The talk of different vendors wanting to
change things to suit commercial results I don't think can be attached to
the COBOL industry as .  The COBOL syntax is only 1 of them (I think I'm
being impartial here).  The SQL syntax standards vary seemingly by the
vendor, package, major version and minor version (and day of the week).
They do though keep to a reasonable consistency and SQL is dynamic, hence
the need for a marriage with COBOL and also some allowance to vary.

What I believe is the real nightmare for SQL though is the way for 1 product
to talk to another so that the SQL can be passed to it and results gained.
Please do not tell anybody, but in this case I think we should thank
Microsoft for 'imposing' the ODBC standard.  Without this standard there is
a miriad of APIs which results in the impractical portability of SQL when
changing platforms and software.  If we take Oracle as an example, they
champion their OCI API and this has many incarnations dependent on version.

I havn't really helped the original question here.  I suppose that EXEC SQL
is a commonly used and documented way of using SQL from COBOL but there are
other ways of doing it to COBOL standards.

Putting my Liant hat back on for a second: Ken, why is RM/COBOL not on your
WEB site?

Gerald Moull

Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote in message
news:38831112.4A9143EC@zip.com.au...
> alan jones wrote:
> >
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL info please?

_(thread depth: 7)_

```
"Gerald Moull" <gerald@moull.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:


>As previous postings have said, there are many different types of standards
>when combining COBOL and SQL.  The talk of different vendors wanting to
>change things to suit commercial results I don't think can be attached to
>the COBOL industry as .  

i wouldn't know why anyone would want to place sql into their cobol
code. i think it would be a bad thing. if you want to run sql against
a database, and cobol against an indexed file, find an sql driver for
the cobol file. you could find a cobol extension for an sql database
as well, but it should match the indexed file data descriptions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL info please?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38846460.9A07FB4B@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<NPHf4.10234$lN.241764@nnrp3.clara.net> <OHnbvrqX$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <2UNf4.5452$UL1.108920@nnrp4.clara.net> <38807B11.C7E31B2E@zip.com.au> <3l%f4.11032$lN.267999@nnrp3.clara.net> <38831112.4A9143EC@zip.com.au> <860fi3$64k$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <5r588s00cdv4ihfal8gm53d3cfcdfrnf05@4ax.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
> i wouldn't know why anyone would want to place sql into their cobol
> code. i think it would be a bad thing. if you want to run sql against
> a database, and cobol against an indexed file, find an sql driver for
> the cobol file. you could find a cobol extension for an sql database
> as well, but it should match the indexed file data descriptions.

Driving an SQL database this way is BAD NEWS!  I worked on McCormick &
Dodge GL3 DB2 and it was 50% slower than VSAM because it used a direct
VSAM drop in like that described.

The benefit of SQL is that you can join multiple tables and return a
single record set.  It simplifies you program quite a bit.  With power
comes responsibility, you really have to watch for no matches and code
you SQL appropriately.

The second benefit is optimization:  If you code access for each table
in turn then you are forcing a path or method of extraction.  If you
code joining SQL the database can work out the optimal path based on
statistics it accumulates (called runstats in DB2).  These statistics
sometime provide a surprising access path.  It also stops reading of
data pages when you can link indexes.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL info please?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<erua8sg26oq38hursvdmits64f6ch6uic9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<NPHf4.10234$lN.241764@nnrp3.clara.net> <OHnbvrqX$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <2UNf4.5452$UL1.108920@nnrp4.clara.net> <38807B11.C7E31B2E@zip.com.au> <3l%f4.11032$lN.267999@nnrp3.clara.net> <38831112.4A9143EC@zip.com.au> <860fi3$64k$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <5r588s00cdv4ihfal8gm53d3cfcdfrnf05@4ax.com> <38846460.9A07FB4B@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:

>G Moore wrote:
>> i wouldn't know why anyone would want to place sql into their cobol
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> as well, but it should match the indexed file data descriptions.

>Driving an SQL database this way is BAD NEWS!  I worked on McCormick &
>Dodge GL3 DB2 and it was 50% slower than VSAM because it used a direct
>VSAM drop in like that described.

50% cpu time, 50% disk io, or what? it could be the driver / extension
was older technology.

>The benefit of SQL is that you can join multiple tables and return a
>single record set.  It simplifies you program quite a bit.  With power
>comes responsibility, you really have to watch for no matches and code
>you SQL appropriately.

you cannot join multiple tables in cobol, but you can search and match
keys, which is close to the same thing.

>The second benefit is optimization:  If you code access for each table
>in turn then you are forcing a path or method of extraction.  If you
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>data pages when you can link indexes.

optimization may help in large joins, but if your cobol programmer
doesn't know which is the many (in a many to one relationship), it is
unlikely your sql programmer will either. and it still requires a
count field on top of it.

i mix and matched language paradigms once. never again, i tell you.
having strings which acted as stacks, vb using sql in their
dynasets...


reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SQL info please?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PUZg4.112$hi2.4624@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<NPHf4.10234$lN.241764@nnrp3.clara.net> <OHnbvrqX$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <2UNf4.5452$UL1.108920@nnrp4.clara.net> <38807B11.C7E31B2E@zip.com.au> <3l%f4.11032$lN.267999@nnrp3.clara.net> <38831112.4A9143EC@zip.com.au> <860fi3$64k$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I think you'll find that COBOL (and other) compilers use a pre-processor on
the source code so you can use ANY SQL (or other third-party service for
that matter) within a common COBOL framework.

I, for one, would not want to have to code the stuff the IBM mainframe DB2
precompiler inserts in the COBOL source.

Besides, on mainframe you can use JCL; on 'nix, a script; on DOS/Windows, a
batch file;  and it's still one programmer command  to "precompile, compile
and linkedit my program."
```

## Thread: Cobol & COM — 5 msgs

### Cobol & COM

- **From:** fabiochi <fabiochiNOfaSPAM@hotmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<06a58763.d6a9a6c5@usw-ex0101-007.remarq.com>`

```
Hi all,
I'm trying to integrate some Cobol Routines with Windows COM object.
I'm using Microfocus NetExpress 3 (SP1) and I have to use its ODBC
feature to access to DB.

Does anyone experience about that?

In particular I'm looking information about:

- I've trouble to convert some data types from Cobol to OLE data type.
Especially my problems are about COMP and COMP-3 conversion. What are
the corresponding OLE types? What's the right way to do that? I red the
Microfocus documentation "Distributed Computing - OLE Data Types" but I
didn't find any information about these data types.

- How does Open ESQL preprocessor work to translate the embedded SQL to
ODBC API calls? If it's possible, I'd like to customize the standard
behavior to pass an opened DB connection from COM object to Cobol
routine.

Thanks in advance
Cheers
Fabio




* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol & COM

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85no0q$5eo$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<06a58763.d6a9a6c5@usw-ex0101-007.remarq.com>`

```
Hi Fabio,

I don't believe COMP or COMP-3 have any directly corresponding OLE types.
However you can easily convert them to the relevant data type:-

01    mycomp3        pic 9(5) comp-3.
01    myVT-I2           pic x(2) comp-5.

move mycomp3 to myVT-I2

COBOL handles the translation of type for you.

You can use a OpenESQL connection that you previously set up by using the
following syntax:-

EXEC SQL
    SET CONNECTION connectionname
END-EXEC

If you want to reuse connections for performance reasons you should consider
using the CONNECTIONPOOL OpenESQL directive.

Regards
David.
```

```
Hi Micael,
I think that the use of ADO is a great choice but I have a more complex
situation. I have a lot of old Cobol programs that I need to call from
a COM Environment. The calls flow is hierarchical and the child
programs are called by the CALL statement. So I'd like to modify only
the first Cobol routine for each flow path (the entry point routine of
the flow) doing something like "wrapper" and to let the other programs
"as are" (they use embedded SQL statement).
With the use Object Cobol with ADO I'm able to pass the connection from
COM client to the "wrapper" (the Cobol COM object) but I don't know how
pass the connection to the others called programs that use ODBC via
OpenESQL.
Do you know if there is a way to integrate the ADO with classic
OpenESQL ? In other way is possible to use (or specify to the OpenESQL
precompiler to use) an opened ADO connection in OpenESQL ?

Thanks
Fabio
```

<a id="t-d0e9e631a9"></a>
## Thread: Another Y2K defect — 43 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k)_

### Another Y2K defect

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com>`

```
We ran into this Y2K defect in Mainframe COBOL just this week.
(My apologies to those that have already seen this problem.)

   02  DATE1YY PIC 99.
   02  DATE2YY PIC 99.
   ...
   IF DATE1YY = DATE2YY + 1
      ... do something ...
   END-IF

We have discovered that the cobol compiler computes the expression 
"DATE2YY +1" and stores the result in a field that has more precision 
than "PIC 99", so the compare-value ends up being "100" when we expected 
the compare-value to be "00".  The value of 100 will cause this IF to 
always fail.

We have already corrected this piece of code.  I'm just posting it here 
so someone else might be able to spot this sort of defect in their 
systems.

Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K6Ff4.621$Lm3.3871@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com>`

```
Pete Wirfs wrote in message ...
>We ran into this Y2K defect in Mainframe COBOL just this week.
>(My apologies to those that have already seen this problem.)
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I think in IBM-mainframe COBOL you could "fix" (??!) this by recompiling
with TRUNC(STD).

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85n7mf$2s9$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <K6Ff4.621$Lm3.3871@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
I do NOT think that TRUNC(STD) or any other option would (should) impact the
interpretation of the arithmetic expression "DATE2YY + 1"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** Jerry Wilcox <Jerry.Wilcox@ucop.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<140120001059108759%Jerry.Wilcox@ucop.edu>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <K6Ff4.621$Lm3.3871@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <85n7mf$2s9$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <85n7mf$2s9$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>, William M. Klein
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I do NOT think that TRUNC(STD) or any other option would (should) impact the
>interpretation of the arithmetic expression "DATE2YY + 1"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>    wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com

You are absolutely correct. It will not impact the computation of the
arithmetic expression. The reason is that while the arithmetic
expression is being calculated, it is done to a greater precision, so,
assuming that DATE1YY is '00' and DATE2YY is '99', the evaluation of
the IF statement will prove to be false, because 0 is not equal to 100.
This is quite clearly explained in the section on "Intermediate
Results" in the "Programming Guide" for your platform (I'm looking at
the one for COBOL for OS/390 & VM, V2R1).

However, if, given the values above, you were to execute the statements

    ADD 1 TO DATE2YY.
    IF DATE1YY = DATE2YY
      .... do something
    END-IF

you will find that the proper (and expected) truncation rule is
followed when storing the value of DATE2YY, and the value will be '00',
causing the conditional to succeed.

This is most emphatically NOT a Y2K bug.

Jerry

>Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
>news:K6Ff4.621$Lm3.3871@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85o965$4vr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F2E14.8956AA0D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
: Pete Wirfs wrote:
:> 
:> We ran into this Y2K defect in Mainframe COBOL just this week.
:> (My apologies to those that have already seen this problem.)
:> 
:>    02  DATE1YY PIC 99.
:>    02  DATE2YY PIC 99.
:>    ...
:>    IF DATE1YY = DATE2YY + 1
:>       ... do something ...
:>    END-IF
:> 
:> We have discovered that the cobol compiler computes the expression
:> "DATE2YY +1" and stores the result in a field that has more precision

Your mistake is in thinking that the result of 'Date2YY + 1' is
stored in a field at all.  The expression just calculates a value
which is used in the comparison.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85o04k$v15$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F2E14.8956AA0D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <MPG.12e91dbabf074fc598969a@news.transport.com>`

```
I don't understand "worked fine for 26 years" .

It worked fine until the value of the expression was over 2-digits.  I can
*guarantee* that it has ALWAYS been returning more than 2-digits - it was
simply that before this, the leading digit(s) were zeroes.

Maybe we are just talking about "whose fault" is it.  I say that it is
totally an application logic (and Y2K testing problem) and NOTHING to do with
the compiler or run-time doing anything other than what the application ASKED
them to do.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Pete Wirfs wrote:
> 
> Sorry, but I got defensive.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> wrote the problem code.

I have had the same discussion with very good programmers who love that
code.  And it is attractive and fun.  But ultimately I believe it is a
bad idea to purposefully count on something which is based upon
computers instead of business rules.

I expect most of our discussion here will be about choices good
programmers make.  Poor programmers are often not too interested in this
resource.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Douglas Miller)
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rsRf4.372$Rw3.7339369@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F2E14.8956AA0D@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <MPG.12e91dbabf074fc598969a@news.transport.com>`

```
In article <MPG.12e91dbabf074fc598969a@news.transport.com>, petwir@saif.com wrote:
>This line of code was written in 1974.
>It worked fine for 26 years!
>Pete
>
Of course it did -- the calculation didn't overflow until the Y2K rollover.

The following should work, though:

01 DATE1YY PIC 99.
01 DATE2YY PIC 99.
01 DATE3YY PIC 99.
..
COMPUTE DATE3YY = DATE2YY + 1
IF DATE1YY = DATE3YY THEN ...
```

#### ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com>`

```
Pete Wirfs wrote:

> We ran into this Y2K defect in Mainframe COBOL just this week.
> (My apologies to those that have already seen this problem.)
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> We have already corrected this piece of code.

This is precisely the type of problem your Y2K section/programmer should
have been identifying and correcting for the last two years.  Anytime a date
with a two digit year was compared or computed, the code should have been
looked into and fixed.

Of course, it is difficult to catch everything;  I have a feeling minor
problems related to dates will plague COBOL programmers to some degree for
quite a few more months to come.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12e91f80d0b56a4e98969b@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net>`

```
In article <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net>, wb999port@bellsouth.net 
says...
> Pete Wirfs wrote:
> 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I was the lead-Y2K programmer for my organization during all of 1996, 
1997 and 1998.  We finished 12/1998, so all of 1999 could be used as a 
shake-down period.  But we had a consulting outfit perform our renovation 
work for us on several million lines of code.  This program was included 
in their scope, but ultimately I was responsible also.

Now I'm curious... would YOUR Y2K section/programmer have caught this 
particular defect?  Be honest now!

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85o0g2$3d1$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net> <MPG.12e91f80d0b56a4e98969b@news.transport.com>`

```
I am afraid that any site/application that hasn't TESTED any occurrence that
arithmetic was/is done on a data-field's years (when stored as two-digits)
really didn't do what they should have.  I know of lots AND LOTS of sites
that did catch and remediate exactly this type of code.

Having said that, I am not trying to say that I know of any site that caught
100% of their Y2K "problems." (What's more, I won't believe anyone who claims
they have until we get thru Feb 29, Mar 1, and Dec 31 of 2000 - at least.)

I think this also ties in with the person who asked about Y2K remediation
"jobs" after 1/1/00.  I think that lots of shops will be finding and fixing
"minor" bugs for the next year.  The good news (IMHO) is that these same
sites should now also be doing new development and enhancements after
whatever "freeze" period they went thru.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12e939c84208952398969d@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net> <MPG.12e91f80d0b56a4e98969b@news.transport.com> <85o0g2$3d1$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Our Y2K project tested every renovated program, and compared the results 
from a baseline run against a renovated run.  We compared all output 
datasets and all SYSOUT.  So where we fell down on this one, was in our 
choice of test data.

In this particular case, it is a batch system written in 1974 so we no 
longer have any in-house gurus on this particular system, thus making it 
more costly to figure out what needs to be tested.  In order to detect 
this defect, we would have had to age our masterfiles to December 1999 
data, and then apply 01/2000 transactions against it.  For this 
particular system, this particular scenario was not tested.

We didn't think it was reasonable to figure out all possible test 
scenarios to be covered for all systems.  We took a cost-savings short-
cut in that we assumed 2 or 3 tests per system should be enough.  You 
have to draw a line some where on Y2K project costs.  In fact some shops 
are now being criticized for spending too much! 

Pete


In article <85o0g2$3d1$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>, 
wmklein@nospam.netcom.com says...
> I am afraid that any site/application that hasn't TESTED any occurrence that
> arithmetic was/is done on a data-field's years (when stored as two-digits)
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > Of course, it is difficult to catch everything;  I have a feeling minor
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387F8D70.DAAE1A8A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net> <MPG.12e91f80d0b56a4e98969b@news.transport.com> <85o0g2$3d1$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Having said that, I am not trying to say that I know of any site that caught
> 100% of their Y2K "problems." (What's more, I won't believe anyone who claims
> they have until we get thru Feb 29, Mar 1, and Dec 31 of 2000 - at least.)

Do you believe people programmed for the 100 year leap year rule and
neglected the 400 year leap year rule when the only century they were
likely to see is 2000?  This seems unlikely to me.

Admittedly unlikely things happen, but why go to the work of putting in
code which doesn't apply?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net> <MPG.12e91f80d0b56a4e98969b@news.transport.com> <85o0g2$3d1$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <387F8D70.DAAE1A8A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Tim Hillock" <hillock@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<srNg4.1926$nh2.65250@cac1.rdr.news.psi.ca>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net> <MPG.12e91f80d0b56a4e98969b@news.transport.com> <85o0g2$3d1$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <387F8D70.DAAE1A8A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3880075C.4AC17999@worldnet.att.net>`

```

William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3880075C.4AC17999@worldnet.att.net...
> Howard Brazee wrote:
> >
> > "William M. Klein" wrote:
> > >
> > > Having said that, I am not trying to say that I know of any site that
caught
> > > 100% of their Y2K "problems." (What's more, I won't believe anyone who
claims
> > > they have until we get thru Feb 29, Mar 1, and Dec 31 of 2000 - at
least.)
> >
> > Do you believe people programmed for the 100 year leap year rule and
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

But Bill, even if the program did not have the 400 year rule the 4 year rule
should had flagged 2000 as a leap year.  4 into 2000 is 500 with zero
remainder.  Now 1900 or 2100 would have been flagged as leap years but we
know that it would be incorrect.  The yr 2000 is the first year that both
the 4 & 400 results are the same(in the computer age).  Of course what I
just wrote is mute if you were only using 2 digits as a date, however, it
you just add the CC to the dates and it still didn't work then it was a bad
program.

Tim
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3883B14A.FA8B1F7E@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net> <MPG.12e91f80d0b56a4e98969b@news.transport.com> <85o0g2$3d1$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <387F8D70.DAAE1A8A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3880075C.4AC17999@worldnet.att.net> <srNg4.1926$nh2.65250@cac1.rdr.news.psi.ca>`

```
Tim Hillock wrote:
> 
(snip)
> > >
> > > Do you believe people programmed for the 100 year leap year rule and
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> program.
 
No, it tested the 2-digit year for "00" and considered it not a leap
year. The old date routine (written in Assembler) handled only 2-digit
years. The new one is written in COBOL and has only 4-digit years.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38849D3F.137931CF@home.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net> <MPG.12e91f80d0b56a4e98969b@news.transport.com> <85o0g2$3d1$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <387F8D70.DAAE1A8A@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <3880075C.4AC17999@worldnet.att.net> <3884744D.15CE37D9@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8620tp$1uh$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
Not a Y2K defect - but boy, real close.

My wife prided herself in getting off a Christmas parcel to UK in
October for the first time ever. Came Xmas, "Happy Christmas" messages
across the 'phone and "Did you get my parcel ?". "What parcel ?"

As of to-day, parcel still hadn't arrived so she queried with Canada
Post who supplied an enormous official form, in triplicate of course,
which asked all sorts of questions, other than your sexual preference.
Fortunately one guy in the Post Office suggested, "Phone this 1-800
number...". 

Good lady duly did that. Reply from the 1-800, "Don't bother to fill in
that form, we've found out what happened and will follow through for
you. Britain changed postal codes and there's a whole bunch of parcels
sitting in a warehouse in Coventry, which should be delivered in a few
days".

Congratulations to Her Britannic Majesty's Post Office - like Y2K your
timing couldn't have been better.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Charles Fregeau <n5hsr@sprynet.com>

```
I had a long argument with my boss that 2000 was a leap year once.  I coded
right anyway!

Charles R Fregeau

Rick Price wrote:

> Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
> news:3884744D.15CE37D9@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3880068F.483608A8@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net> <MPG.12e91f80d0b56a4e98969b@news.transport.com>`

```
Pete Wirfs wrote:
> 
(snip)
> >
> > >    02  DATE1YY PIC 99.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> particular defect?  Be honest now!

Strictly speaking, no. My employer decided that SEEC caught enough
problems to be a valid screening tool, so everything was run thru it.
Anything that popped out of SEEC was reviewed by wetware and remedied if
needed. I suspect SEEC would have picked this up because of the "DATE"
root of the data names.

We've had a couple of hits in the past two weeks of programs which SEEC
didn't flag, but which did date calculations. So these were fixed on
failure. We also took two hits on software which SEEC found and the
wetware decided it made no difference. Both involved converting a MMDDYY
date into Julian, then using the Julian date to construct a key for
retrieval. However, the key was built with EIBDATE. Whoever looked at
this didn't understand that the the 5-char Julian date needed to be
windowed into IBM's 6-digit format. I fixed the first one approx. 3 AM 1
Jan (actually two, I corrected the routine which is called to do the
date conversion). 

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38832DBD.F8C0A9C0@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <387F4E59.6D886BE8@bellsouth.net> <MPG.12e91f80d0b56a4e98969b@news.transport.com>`

```
Pete Wirfs wrote:

> > > We ran into this Y2K defect in Mainframe COBOL just this week.
> > > (My apologies to those that have already seen this problem.)
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> particular defect?  Be honest now!

Probably yes.  One of the things our script looked for was YE*A*[RY] anywhere in a
program and we were instructed to pay close attention to any situation where dates
were compared or computed.
```

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>

```
DennisHarley <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com> wrote in message
news:Ou6fBQLY$GA.247@cpmsnbbsa03...
> It has become Standard Practice to use CC to represent the fist 2
digits of
> YYYY dates.
> Anyone who would interpret it any other way needs to be enlightened.

Wait a minute. "CC to represent the first 2 digits of YYYY"? Pardon my
(sometimes)
logical mind, but it seems "CC" ought to stand for something:

Carbon Copy?
200 (in Roman numerals)?
Cups?
Bra size?
COBOL Confabulation?
Century?

If "CC" doesn't represent Century (and I'm pretty sure it doesn't),
then
is "CC" just some pair of random characters? And if that's the case,
why replace
a perfectly unambiguous "YY" with something equivocal?

(Caution: rhetorical question follows)
So, enlighten me. How - and just as importantly, why - does YYYY get
mapped to CCYY? Is this how a blunder becomes dogma? By calling it
"Standard Practice?" Feh!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38853F55.C1CE60AA@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <lhvg4.9038$8z5.113970@news4.mia> <ekxg4.239$bd6.9053@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net> <Ou6fBQLY$GA.247@cpmsnbbsa03> <mPRg4.750$%_6.8115@nnrp2.rcsntx.swbell.net> <861qek$t1i$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <986h4.56$Zl.2791@nnrp3.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
> 
> Rick Price <rick@hpd.co.uk
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> should be coded as 20001231 in CCYYMMDD format.

I think Jerry P meant to say that 1999 was in the 20th century,
therefore 31-Dec-1999 coded in CCYYMMDD format had to be 20991231.  At
least I have seen that argument made elsewhere.

I think it's reasonable to call the two high-order digits of a
four-digit year "CC" in COBOL, simply because it allows you to work with
both 2-digit years and 4-digit years.  How else would you name the
high-order digits?

About two years ago there was a clever piece of COBOL code posted in
comp.software.year-2000 that used a 4-digit year split into CC and YY to
make a more efficient test for leap year.

01  SOME-DATE.
    05  YYYY.
        10  CC      PIC 99.
        10  YY      PIC 99.
    05  MM          PIC 99.
    05  DD          PIC 99.

IF YY = 00 THEN
    DIVIDE CC BY 4 GIVING USELESS-JUNK REMAINDER LEAP-INDICATOR
ELSE
    DIVIDE YY BY 4 GIVING USELESS-JUNK REMAINDER LEAP-INDICATOR
END-IF
IF LEAP-INDICATOR = 0 THEN
    it's a leap year, so do your leap year stuff here
ELSE
    it's a normal year
END-IF

So, 1900 is not a leap year, because 19 mod 4 does not equal zero.  But
2000 is a leap year because 20 mod 4 does equal zero.  1999 is not a
leap year because 99 mod 4 does not equal zero.  But 1996 is a leap year
because 96 mod 4 does equal zero.  I was impressed by the simplicity,
since it allows you to apply the 4-year, 100-year, and 400-year rules
while executing only one DIVIDE statement instead of three.

The person who thought of this should get some credit for creative
thinking.

I have always interpreted CC and YY to be logical subdefinitions of
YYYY.  If you need to window a 2-digit year into a 4-digit year, what
else would you call the high-order digits you're generating? 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Another Y2K defect

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tj798s0khp9gr13kgkd6psu8egf38c1gvf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.12e80dba914bfb3b989699@news.transport.com> <lhvg4.9038$8z5.113970@news4.mia> <ekxg4.239$bd6.9053@nnrp1.rcsntx.swbell.net> <Ou6fBQLY$GA.247@cpmsnbbsa03> <mPRg4.750$%_6.8115@nnrp2.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```
On Mon, 17 Jan 2000 22:17:20 -0600 "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>If "CC" doesn't represent Century (and I'm pretty sure it doesn't),
:>then
:>is "CC" just some pair of random characters? And if that's the case,
:>why replace
:>a perfectly unambiguous "YY" with something equivocal?

:>(Caution: rhetorical question follows)
:>So, enlighten me. How - and just as importantly, why - does YYYY get
:>mapped to CCYY? Is this how a blunder becomes dogma? By calling it
:>"Standard Practice?" Feh!

Historical note.

The IBM MVS macro TIME was defined as returning the date in format x'00YYDDDS'
in general register 1, where YY was the last two digits of the year, DDD was
the ordinal date number within the year and S was the positive sign. When IBM
became concerned about making the macro 4 digit year they ran into a problem.
If they changed it to x'YYYYDDDS' the results wouldn't be downwards compatible
and date arithmetic would return incorrect results. The compromise was to use
x'CCYYDDDS' where CC is defines as the century number where 19=00, 20=01, etc.
Thus the IBM code is downwards compatible.

Note that the TIME macro has new operands to get the YYYYMMDD value as well as
other forms. But the program last compiled 20 years ago will still work with
the old forms.

   [ snipped ]
```

<a id="t-45c6c85122"></a>
## Thread: Help with an Algorithm in COBOL — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with an Algorithm in COBOL

- **From:** Scott Williamson <syw@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vwaH3AAtF1f4EwWq@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>`

```
Hi,

I'm trying to write a routine in COBOL to generate a table of six digit
numbers. Each number must include all the digits from 1 to 6, and I need
to generate all 720 possible combinations from 123456 to 654321
automatically.
My attempts so far have failed, as I can't work out the appropriate
algorithm to do it neatly. So far, I've tried code like:

      01  ws-shuffle-area.
          02  ws-sh pic 9 occurs 6.

      f-shuffle.
           perform until ws-shuffle-area = "654321" 
              if sh(6) > sh(5)
                 move sh(5) to temp
                 move sh(6) to sh(5)
                 move temp to sh(6)
                 perform c-calculate            
              end-if   
              if sh(5) > sh(4)
                 move sh(4) to temp
                 move sh(5) to sh(4)
                 move temp to sh(5)
                 perform c-calculate            
              end-if   
              if sh(4) > sh(3)
                 move sh(3) to temp
          etc....
This approach, however, bubbles the high numbers backwards through the
number far too quickly.

I'm using RM-COBOL so I can't call on any intrinsic functions.

Can anyone think of a fast and compact approach?

No it's not homework, it's for fun!

Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with an Algorithm in COBOL

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387F5CAD.3F778713@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<vwaH3AAtF1f4EwWq@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>`

```
How neat do you want the code?  There are a couple of brute force
methods, including creating every possible number, and discarding those
which don't qualify.  

There are more efficient algorithms than what follows, but look at it.
PERFORM VARYING VALUE-1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL VALUE-1 > 6
       PERFORM VARYING VALUE-2 FROM BY 1 UNTIL VALUE-2 > 6
       IF VALUE-2 = VALUE-1
           CONTINUE
       ELSE
           PERFORM VARYING VALUE-3 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL VALUE-3 > 6
           IF VALUE-3 = VALUE-1
           OR VALUE-3 = VALUE-2
               CONTINUE
           ELSE
    ....
            MOVE VALUE-1 TO WS-SH(1)
            MOVE VALUE-2 TO WS-SH(2) ...

Clean up the code to make it readable with all 6 levels of performs.

Scott Williamson wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> Scott Williamson (syw@cdvdc.demon.co.uk)
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with an Algorithm in COBOL

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TELf4.2962$eO5.39867@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<vwaH3AAtF1f4EwWq@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>`

```
Scott Williamson wrote in message ...
>
>I'm trying to write a routine in COBOL to generate a table of six digit
>numbers. Each number must include all the digits from 1 to 6, and I need
>to generate all 720 possible combinations from 123456 to 654321
>automatically.

What you want is all the permutations of the digits 1-6 taken 6 at a
time.  I haven't written one of those in COBOL, and I don't have time
to do it right now.  But if you will download file PERM.ZIP from my
web site below, it is a BASIC program that prints the permutations of
an arbitrary set of letters/numbers, taken any number at a time.  Hope
that helps. :-)
```

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85p70d$s05$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<vwaH3AAtF1f4EwWq@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>`

```

"Scott Williamson" <syw@cdvdc.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:vwaH3AAtF1f4EwWq@cdvdc.demon.co.uk...
> Hi,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<SNIP>

    So far as I know, attachments can not be trusted.  So here is a program,
intended for anagram solving, that should do it,  Just use 123456 as the 6
digit
scrambled word.  Also, the output is not sorted.




000100 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000200 PROGRAM-ID.
000300     GENWORD2.
000400 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000500 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
000600 SOURCE-COMPUTER.  IBM-PC.
000700 OBJECT-COMPUTER.  IBM-PC.
000800 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000900 FILE-CONTROL.
001000     SELECT A-FILE
001100         ASSIGN TO AFILE-NAME
001200         ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL.
001300 DATA DIVISION.
001400 FILE SECTION.
001500 FD  A-FILE.
001600 01  A-REC           PIC X(10).
001700
001800 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
001900 01  GENWORD-WS.
002000     03  AFILE-SIZE      PIC 9(4) COMP-X.
002100     03  AFILE-NAME.
002200         05  FILLER      PIC X(6) VALUE "AFILE.".
002300         05  AFILE-EXT   PIC 999  VALUE ZERO.
002400     03  ACC-WORD-SIZE   PIC 99.
002500     03  WORD-SIZE       PIC 99 COMP-X.
002600     03  IN-ARRAY.
002700         05  IN-CHAR OCCURS 9 TIMES PIC X.
002800     03  IN-FLAGS.
002900         05  IN-FLAG OCCURS 9 TIMES PIC X.
003000     03  TEST-NUMBER     PIC 9(9).
003100     03  TEST-NUMBER-X REDEFINES TEST-NUMBER.
003200         05  TEST-NUM   OCCURS 9 TIMES PIC 9.
003300     03  NUM-HITS        PIC 9(8).
003400     03  THE-WORD.
003500         05  A-WORD-CHAR OCCURS 9 TIMES PIC X.
003600     03  THE-MAX         PIC 9(8).
003700     03  FLAG-SAMPLE     PIC X(9).
003800
003900
004000 01  GENWORD2-WS.
004100     03  WORD-3.
004200         05  WORD-3-ITEM OCCURS 3 TIMES PIC X.
004300     03  NUM-3 REDEFINES WORD-3 PIC 999.
004400***
004500     03  WORD-4.
004600         05  WORD-4-ITEM OCCURS 4 TIMES PIC X.
004700     03  NUM-4 REDEFINES WORD-4 PIC 9(4).
004800***
004900     03  WORD-5.
005000         05  WORD-5-ITEM OCCURS 5 TIMES PIC X.
005100     03  NUM-5 REDEFINES WORD-5 PIC 9(5).
005200***
005300     03  WORD-6.
005400         05  WORD-6-ITEM OCCURS 6 TIMES PIC X.
005500     03  NUM-6 REDEFINES WORD-6 PIC 9(6).
005600***
005700     03  WORD-7.
005800         05  WORD-7-ITEM OCCURS 7 TIMES PIC X.
005900     03  NUM-7 REDEFINES WORD-7 PIC 9(7).
006000***
006100     03  WORD-8.
006200         05  WORD-8-ITEM OCCURS 8 TIMES PIC X.
006300     03  NUM-8 REDEFINES WORD-8 PIC 9(8).
006400***
006500     03  WORD-9.
006600         05  WORD-9-ITEM OCCURS 9 TIMES PIC X.
006700     03  NUM-9 REDEFINES WORD-9 PIC 9(9).
006800***
006900     03  IDX-01          PIC 99 COMP-X.
007000     03  IDX-02          PIC 99 COMP-X.
007100     03  IDX-03          PIC 99 COMP-X.
007200     03  IDX-04          PIC 99 COMP-X.
007300
007400     03  IDX-041         PIC 99 COMP-X.
007500     03  IDX-042         PIC 99 COMP-X.
007600     03  IDX-043         PIC 99 COMP-X.
007700
007800     03  IDX-051         PIC 99 COMP-X.
007900     03  IDX-052         PIC 99 COMP-X.
008000     03  IDX-053         PIC 99 COMP-X.
008100
008200     03  IDX-061         PIC 99 COMP-X.
008300     03  IDX-062         PIC 99 COMP-X.
008400     03  IDX-063         PIC 99 COMP-X.
008500
008600     03  IDX-071         PIC 99 COMP-X.
008700     03  IDX-072         PIC 99 COMP-X.
008800     03  IDX-073         PIC 99 COMP-X.
008900
009000     03  IDX-081         PIC 99 COMP-X.
009100     03  IDX-082         PIC 99 COMP-X.
009200     03  IDX-083         PIC 99 COMP-X.
009300
009400     03  IDX-091         PIC 99 COMP-X.
009500     03  IDX-092         PIC 99 COMP-X.
009600     03  IDX-093         PIC 99 COMP-X.
009700
009800 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
009900 MAIN-SEC SECTION.
010000 START-PARA.
010100     DISPLAY SPACES AT 0101.
010200     DISPLAY
010300      "Generate all possible numbers with no repeated numbers"
010400         AT 0101.
010500
010600     DISPLAY "Enter size of word, up to 9 chars" AT 0201.
010700     MOVE 4 TO ACC-WORD-SIZE.
010800     DISPLAY ACC-WORD-SIZE AT 0301.
010900     ACCEPT ACC-WORD-SIZE AT 0301.
011000     MOVE ACC-WORD-SIZE TO WORD-SIZE.
011100     DISPLAY "Enter Scrambled word" AT 0401.
011200     ACCEPT IN-ARRAY AT 0501.
011300
011400     MOVE ZERO   TO NUM-HITS.
011500
011600     IF WORD-SIZE = 1
011700         STOP RUN.
011800     IF WORD-SIZE = 2
011900         STOP RUN.
012000     IF WORD-SIZE = 3
012100         STOP RUN.
012200     IF WORD-SIZE = 4
012300         MOVE "YYYY"     TO FLAG-SAMPLE
012400         MOVE 24         TO THE-MAX
012500         MOVE 1234       TO TEST-NUMBER.
012600     IF WORD-SIZE = 5
012700         MOVE "YYYYY"    TO FLAG-SAMPLE
012800         MOVE 120        TO THE-MAX
012900         MOVE 12345      TO TEST-NUMBER.
013000     IF WORD-SIZE = 6
013100         MOVE "YYYYYY"   TO FLAG-SAMPLE
013200         MOVE 720        TO THE-MAX
013300         MOVE 123456     TO TEST-NUMBER.
013400     IF WORD-SIZE = 7
013500         MOVE "YYYYYYY"  TO FLAG-SAMPLE
013600         MOVE 5040       TO THE-MAX
013700         MOVE 1234567    TO TEST-NUMBER.
013800     IF WORD-SIZE = 8
013900         MOVE "YYYYYYYY" TO FLAG-SAMPLE
014000         MOVE 40320      TO THE-MAX
014100         MOVE 12345678   TO TEST-NUMBER.
014200     IF WORD-SIZE = 9
014300         MOVE "YYYYYYYYY" TO FLAG-SAMPLE
014400         MOVE 362880     TO THE-MAX
014500         MOVE 123456789  TO TEST-NUMBER.
014600     IF WORD-SIZE > 9
014700         GO TO START-PARA.
014800
014900     MOVE SPACES         TO A-REC.
015000
015100     ADD 1 TO AFILE-EXT.
015200     OPEN OUTPUT A-FILE.
015300     MOVE ZERO           TO AFILE-SIZE.
015400
015500     PERFORM DOIT THRU DOIT-X.
015600     CLOSE A-FILE.
015700     DISPLAY "ALL DONE" AT 2201.
015800 THE-EXIT.
015900     EXIT PROGRAM.
016000 THE-STOP.
016100     STOP RUN.
016200***********************
016300 DOIT.
016400* START with all 6 3 digit numbers.
016500* Could generate them.  Why bother.
016600     MOVE "123"      TO WORD-3.
016700     PERFORM DO-GEN4 THRU DO-GEN4-X.
016800     MOVE "321"      TO WORD-3.
016900     PERFORM DO-GEN4 THRU DO-GEN4-X.
017000     MOVE "231"      TO WORD-3.
017100     PERFORM DO-GEN4 THRU DO-GEN4-X.
017200     MOVE "132"      TO WORD-3.
017300     PERFORM DO-GEN4 THRU DO-GEN4-X.
017400     MOVE "312"      TO WORD-3.
017500     PERFORM DO-GEN4 THRU DO-GEN4-X.
017600     MOVE "213"      TO WORD-3.
017700     PERFORM DO-GEN4 THRU DO-GEN4-X.
017800 DOIT-X.
017900     EXIT.
018000********************************************
018100********************************************
018200 DO-GEN4.
018300     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-041.
018400 DO-GEN4-LOOP1.
018500     IF IDX-041 = 4
018600         GO TO DO-GEN4-X.
018700     ADD 1 TO IDX-041.
018800     MOVE SPACES TO WORD-4.
018900     MOVE "4"    TO WORD-4-ITEM (IDX-041).
019000     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-042 IDX-043.
019100 DO-GEN4-LOOP2.
019200     IF IDX-042 = 4
019300        IF WORD-SIZE = 4
019400           MOVE NUM-4       TO TEST-NUMBER
019500           PERFORM WRITE-THE-WORD
019600           GO TO DO-GEN4-LOOP1
019700        ELSE
019800           PERFORM DO-GEN5 THRU DO-GEN5-X
019900           GO TO DO-GEN4-LOOP1.
020000
020100     ADD 1 TO IDX-042.
020200     IF WORD-4-ITEM (IDX-042) = SPACES
020300         ADD 1 TO IDX-043
020400         MOVE WORD-3-ITEM (IDX-043) TO WORD-4-ITEM (IDX-042).
020500     GO TO DO-GEN4-LOOP2.
020600 DO-GEN4-X.
020700     EXIT.
020800********************************************
020900********************************************
021000 DO-GEN5.
021100     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-051.
021200 DO-GEN5-LOOP1.
021300     IF IDX-051 = 5
021400         GO TO DO-GEN5-X.
021500     ADD 1 TO IDX-051.
021600     MOVE SPACES TO WORD-5.
021700     MOVE "5"    TO WORD-5-ITEM (IDX-051).
021800     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-052 IDX-053.
021900 DO-GEN5-LOOP2.
022000     IF IDX-052 = 5
022100        IF WORD-SIZE = 5
022200           MOVE NUM-5     TO TEST-NUMBER
022300           PERFORM WRITE-THE-WORD
022400           GO TO DO-GEN5-LOOP1
022500        ELSE
022600           PERFORM DO-GEN6 THRU DO-GEN6-X
022700           GO TO DO-GEN5-LOOP1.
022800     ADD 1 TO IDX-052.
022900     IF WORD-5-ITEM (IDX-052) = SPACES
023000         ADD 1 TO IDX-053
023100         MOVE WORD-4-ITEM (IDX-053) TO WORD-5-ITEM (IDX-052).
023200     GO TO DO-GEN5-LOOP2.
023300 DO-GEN5-X.
023400     EXIT.
023500********************************************
023600********************************************
023700 DO-GEN6.
023800     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-061.
023900 DO-GEN6-LOOP1.
024000     IF IDX-061 = 6
024100         GO TO DO-GEN6-X.
024200     ADD 1 TO IDX-061.
024300     MOVE SPACES TO WORD-6.
024400     MOVE "6"    TO WORD-6-ITEM (IDX-061).
024500     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-062 IDX-063.
024600 DO-GEN6-LOOP2.
024700     IF IDX-062 = 6
024800        IF WORD-SIZE = 6
024900           MOVE NUM-6     TO TEST-NUMBER
025000           PERFORM WRITE-THE-WORD
025100           GO TO DO-GEN6-LOOP1
025200        ELSE
025300           PERFORM DO-GEN7 THRU DO-GEN7-X
025400           GO TO DO-GEN6-LOOP1.
025500     ADD 1 TO IDX-062.
025600     IF WORD-6-ITEM (IDX-062) = SPACES
025700         ADD 1 TO IDX-063
025800         MOVE WORD-5-ITEM (IDX-063) TO WORD-6-ITEM (IDX-062).
025900     GO TO DO-GEN6-LOOP2.
026000 DO-GEN6-X.
026100     EXIT.
026200********************************************
026300********************************************
026400********************************************
026500********************************************
026600 DO-GEN7.
026700     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-071.
026800 DO-GEN7-LOOP1.
026900     IF IDX-071 = 7
027000         GO TO DO-GEN7-X.
027100     ADD 1 TO IDX-071.
027200     MOVE SPACES TO WORD-7.
027300     MOVE "7"    TO WORD-7-ITEM (IDX-071).
027400     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-072 IDX-073.
027500 DO-GEN7-LOOP2.
027600     IF IDX-072 = 7
027700        IF WORD-SIZE = 7
027800           MOVE NUM-7     TO TEST-NUMBER
027900           PERFORM WRITE-THE-WORD
028000           GO TO DO-GEN7-LOOP1
028100        ELSE
028200           PERFORM DO-GEN8 THRU DO-GEN8-X
028300           GO TO DO-GEN7-LOOP1.
028400     ADD 1 TO IDX-072.
028500     IF WORD-7-ITEM (IDX-072) = SPACES
028600         ADD 1 TO IDX-073
028700         MOVE WORD-6-ITEM (IDX-073) TO WORD-7-ITEM (IDX-072).
028800     GO TO DO-GEN7-LOOP2.
028900 DO-GEN7-X.
029000     EXIT.
029100********************************************
029200********************************************
029300 DO-GEN8.
029400     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-081.
029500 DO-GEN8-LOOP1.
029600     IF IDX-081 = 8
029700         GO TO DO-GEN8-X.
029800     ADD 1 TO IDX-081.
029900     MOVE SPACES TO WORD-8.
030000     MOVE "8"    TO WORD-8-ITEM (IDX-081).
030100     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-082 IDX-083.
030200 DO-GEN8-LOOP2.
030300     IF IDX-082 = 8
030400        IF WORD-SIZE = 8
030500           MOVE NUM-8     TO TEST-NUMBER
030600           PERFORM WRITE-THE-WORD
030700           GO TO DO-GEN8-LOOP1
030800        ELSE
030900           PERFORM DO-GEN9 THRU DO-GEN9-X
031000           GO TO DO-GEN8-LOOP1.
031100     ADD 1 TO IDX-082.
031200     IF WORD-8-ITEM (IDX-082) = SPACES
031300         ADD 1 TO IDX-083
031400         MOVE WORD-7-ITEM (IDX-083) TO WORD-8-ITEM (IDX-082).
031500     GO TO DO-GEN8-LOOP2.
031600 DO-GEN8-X.
031700     EXIT.
031800****************************************************
031900****************************************************
032000 DO-GEN9.
032100     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-091.
032200 DO-GEN9-LOOP1.
032300     IF IDX-091 = 9
032400         GO TO DO-GEN9-X.
032500     ADD 1 TO IDX-091.
032600     MOVE SPACES TO WORD-9.
032700     MOVE "9"    TO WORD-9-ITEM (IDX-091).
032800     MOVE ZERO   TO IDX-092 IDX-093.
032900 DO-GEN9-LOOP2.
033000     IF IDX-092 = 9
033100           MOVE NUM-9     TO TEST-NUMBER
033200           PERFORM WRITE-THE-WORD
033300           GO TO DO-GEN9-LOOP1.
033400     ADD 1 TO IDX-092.
033500     IF WORD-9-ITEM (IDX-092) = SPACES
033600         ADD 1 TO IDX-093
033700         MOVE WORD-8-ITEM (IDX-093) TO WORD-9-ITEM (IDX-092).
033800     GO TO DO-GEN9-LOOP2.
033900 DO-GEN9-X.
034000     EXIT.
034100****************************************************
034200****************************************************
034300****************************************************
034400 GEN-WORD.
034500     MOVE SPACES TO THE-WORD.
034600     COMPUTE IDX-04 = 9 - WORD-SIZE.
034700     PERFORM GEN-WORD-LETTER WORD-SIZE TIMES.
034800****************************************************
034900 GEN-WORD-LETTER.
035000     ADD 1 TO IDX-04.
035100     MOVE TEST-NUM (IDX-04) TO IDX-03.
035200     MOVE IN-CHAR (IDX-03) TO A-WORD-CHAR (IDX-04).
035300****************************************************
035400 WRITE-THE-WORD.
035500     PERFORM GEN-WORD.
035600     MOVE THE-WORD   TO A-REC.
035700     WRITE A-REC.
035800
035900     ADD 1 TO NUM-HITS.
036000     DISPLAY NUM-HITS AT 0801.
036100     ADD 1 TO AFILE-SIZE.
036200     IF AFILE-SIZE > 2999
036300         MOVE ZERO   TO AFILE-SIZE
036400         CLOSE A-FILE
036500         ADD 1 TO AFILE-EXT
036600         OPEN OUTPUT A-FILE.
036700****************************************************
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with an Algorithm in COBOL

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85pmjn$s9h$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<vwaH3AAtF1f4EwWq@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>`

```
01  ws-shuffle-area pic 9(6).
01  xxxx redefines ws-shuffle-area.
          02  ws-sh1 pic 9.
          ...
          02  ws-sh6 pic 9.

move 123456 to ws-shuffle-area.
set your table index to +1.
perform until ws-shuffle-area greater than 654321
  if (ws-sh1 + ws-sh2 + ws-sh3 + ws-sh4 + ws-sh5 + ws-sh6) equal 21
       move ws-shuffle-area to your table
       set your table index up by +1
  end-if
  add 1 to ws-shuffle-area
end-perform.

kenmullins
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help with an Algorithm in COBOL

- **From:** Scott Williamson <syw@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<edhbUAAo3Hg4EwLF@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<vwaH3AAtF1f4EwWq@cdvdc.demon.co.uk> <85pmjn$s9h$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Many thanks to those who responded to my plea to help.

I got Howard's reply first and amended my code to his suggestion.  It
worked first time and generated my table in a matter of minutes!

I like Ken's idea for neat coding - the sum test must have been the
algorithm in the back of my mind, so I'll bear that in mind for the
future.

Again many thanks for saving me bashing my head against the wall.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help with an Algorithm in COBOL

- **From:** dxmixxer@netdirect.net (Douglas Miller)
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d_%f4.379$Rw3.7561455@news.netdirect.net>`
- **References:** `<vwaH3AAtF1f4EwWq@cdvdc.demon.co.uk> <85pmjn$s9h$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <85pmjn$s9h$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>, "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com> wrote:
>01  ws-shuffle-area pic 9(6).
>01  xxxx redefines ws-shuffle-area.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>  if (ws-sh1 + ws-sh2 + ws-sh3 + ws-sh4 + ws-sh5 + ws-sh6) equal 21

This doesn't work. It's not even a very good try. For example, the digits in 
300099 add to 21. Even restricting the individual digits to the range 1..6 
won't work: the digits in 333336 also add to 21.

It's necessary both to restrict the individual digits to the range 1..6, *and* 
to ensure that each digit is used exactly once. This method does neither.

>       move ws-shuffle-area to your table
>       set your table index up by +1
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help with an Algorithm in COBOL

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85q5qh$740$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<vwaH3AAtF1f4EwWq@cdvdc.demon.co.uk> <85pmjn$s9h$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <d_%f4.379$Rw3.7561455@news.netdirect.net>`

```
Hey, it was early and I hadn't had my coffee...try something like this:

 01 ws-shuffle-area pic 9(6).
01  xxxx redefines ws-shuffle-area.
          02  ws-sh1 pic 9.
          ...
          02  ws-sh6 pic 9.
01  xxxx.
     02  ws-tally1       pic 9.
          ...
     02  ws-tally6       pic 9.

move 123456 to ws-shuffle-area.
set your table index to +1.
perform until ws-shuffle-area greater than 654321
  move 0 to ws-tally1 ws-tally2 ws-tally3 ws-tally ws-tally5 ws-tally6
  inspect ws-shuffle-area tallying ws-tally1 for all '1'
  inspect ws-shuffle-area tallying ws-tally2 for all '2'
  inspect ws-shuffle-area tallying ws-tally3 for all '3'
  inspect ws-shuffle-area tallying ws-tally4 for all '4'
  inspect ws-shuffle-area tallying ws-tally5 for all '5'
  inspect ws-shuffle-area tallying ws-tally6 for all '6'
  if ws-tally1 equal 1 and
     ws-tally2 equal 1 and
     ws-tally3 equal 1 and
     ws-tally4 equal 1 and
     ws-tally5 equal 1 and
     ws-tally6 equal 1
       move ws-shuffle-area to your table
       set your table index up by +1
  end-if
  add 1 to ws-shuffle-area
end-perform.
```

## Thread: about microcobol and mainframe cobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### about microcobol and mainframe cobol

- **From:** "Homin Chen" <homchen@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NoNf4.14711$o62.646651@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
is there any difference between them??
also is it possible to get free cobol compiler(85) for win98??
thanx in advance
```

#### ↳ Re: about microcobol and mainframe cobol

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000116135249.10160.00000172@ng-cn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<NoNf4.14711$o62.646651@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Several compilers that work on Win98 are mentioned in the FAQ for the group,
just reposted one or two weeks ago.  I think the back-level versions at
www.adtools.com (I think it is associated with Fujitsu) can be downloaded for
free.  But the FAQ, from Bill Klein, would be your best source.

There are what I feel are significant differences between each of the mainframe
cobol versions and all of the micro versions, mostly having to do with i/o and
data typing.  It does seem like eav\ch vendor adds a few extensions to the
standard, and those don't translate well.
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

---

#### ↳ Re: ,,..,, Warning To @Home Users - Your ISP Is S P A M C E N T R A L

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8hMf4.43286$g12.1163484@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<0F7123D4F.eqdwuytcwd@nxjxraxt.com> <85nr30$qqf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
It's quasi-legitimate. Whoever posted to CLC had no business doing so.

However, @home HAS been imposed with a UDP (Usenet Death Penalty) which goes
into effect on Jan 18 if they don't significantly reduce the amount of spam
flowing from their newgroup servers. The contents of the article are
reasonably accurate.

Hopefully this thread will now die rather than digress into a long
discussion on baking. In order to insure that it does I'll mention the word
'nazi' in order to trigger Godwin's Law.



Godwin's Law /prov./ [Usenet] "As a Usenet discussion grows longer, the
probability of a comparison involving Nazis or Hitler approaches one." There
is a tradition in many groups that, once this occurs, that thread is over,
and whoever mentioned the Nazis has automatically lost whatever argument was
in progress. Godwin's Law thus practically guarantees the existence of an
upper bound on thread length in those groups.

-- Karl Wagner


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:85nr30$qqf$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net...
> If you look at the very end of this message - or the return address - I
> suspect that this is a "scam".
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Excite@Home is hoping for a pardon from a proposed Interent newsgroup

```

```
On Thu, 1 Jan 1970 00:59:59, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

> If you look at the very end of this message - or the return address - I
> suspect that this is a "scam".
```

<a id="t-49abb00d92"></a>
## Thread: Newbie question: config file? — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Newbie question: config file?

- **From:** Bill Easley <wfe3@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<387FA6CA.83A2105@bellsouth.net>`

```
Hi,
I have just enrolled in my second COBOL course and our professor has
requested that we create a batch system to read a configuration file for
the names of our transaction, error, and audit report files. I enrolled
late and missed the lecture on how to do this and he did not have time
to explain. We use MicroFocus COBOL for PC to do this.  I am not sure
how to set this up and would appreciate any help.  My basic
understanding is that we create a text file in notepad or wordpad,
config.ini, with the file names, Write a SELECT statement for each file
as follows:
SELECT config-file ASSIGN TO "config.ini"
SELECT trans-file   ASSIGN  TO DYNAMIC WS-FILE-1, etc.
write the appropriate FD's . Beyond this, I'm not sure how to get this
to work.
Thanks for your help,
Bill
```

#### ↳ Re: Newbie question: config file?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85q24j$q5m$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<387FA6CA.83A2105@bellsouth.net>`

```
Your approach of putting the file names in the config.ini file with Notepad
(as test data) should work.  Basically, you open that file and read it as a
sequential file, placing the filenames into working storage names you used
in your select clauses. The other selects are written so that the files are
assigned to the working storage names, exactly as you specified.  IE:
open input config-file.
read config-file into WS-FILE-1 (at end is error)
read config-file into WS-FILE-2 (at end is error)
etc.
close config-file.
open other files.
Bill Easley wrote in message <387FA6CA.83A2105@bellsouth.net>...
>Hi,
>I have just enrolled in my second COBOL course and our professor has
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Looking for MULTI-USER DOS — 3 msgs

### Looking for MULTI-USER DOS

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85m0cr$k$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
I need a multi-user dos operating system that's compatible with Novell
Netware 5
& will allow me to run several copies of my compiled code. Seems such
products
have been discontinued. Any help appreciated.

Thanks in advance

Alister
```

#### ↳ Re: Looking for MULTI-USER DOS

- **From:** joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net (John McKown)
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn87tp78.1gr.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<85m0cr$k$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```

On Fri, 14 Jan 2000 02:06:00 -0000, Alister Munro
<alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:
>I need a multi-user dos operating system that's compatible with Novell
>Netware 5
>& will allow me to run several copies of my compiled code. Seems such
>products
>have been discontinued. Any help appreciated.

Have you looked at Linux? It is definately a multitasking OS (UNIX like) which
is compatable with Novell. I guess by compatable with Novell, you mean that
it has a client which can use a Novell server. Linux can act as a client to
a Novell server (file & print both). Linux also comes with a function which
is called DOSEMU. That this does is emulate an MS-DOS environment under Linux.
This is supposed to allow you to run DOS (not Windows!) programs without
any modification. However, I don't know if it supposed the "DOS Extenders"
which would run program in protected mode (Phar Lap is a name I remember
which did this).

Now for the usual disclaimer. Although I am a Linux user and lover,
I haven't used this function myself, so I am not familiar with how well it
runs. I would suggest asking that sort of on
comp.os.linux.development.system or comp.os.linux.development.apps or
comp.os.linux.misc or well, you get the idea <grin>.

I hope this was of at least some help,
John McKown
```

## Thread: Fileshare problem — 1 msgs

### Fileshare problem

- **From:** Stefan.Meyer@Triumph-international.de
- **Date:** 2000-01-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85mt60$j9d$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi all,

once again, a FS problem.

While running a bigger batch job the job stops and after a while I got
error # 9/125 (all locks cancelled due to exceeding time limit).

FS is running with following settings:
-s slowenien
-d slowenien.ref
-cm ccinampu
-cm ccitcp
-pf /etc/fscob.pwd
-t 0

so time limits are deactivated.

A look into fhneterr.log gave me the following information:
CCI: 000114 10072900 0010 07 The CCINAMPU is out of resources, try
again after when other requests have been completed.

Well I was the only one using this FS-Connection.

So tried to check if the fs-server is still running.
A self written prog (which is pretty working) told me "Yes, FS is
running".
So I tried to stop fs using a self written prog (working?!). Result: FS
is alive (using the same check proc) but communication failure while
trying to close down the server.

Than a tried to stop the fs process using fsclose. This prog never cam
back.

So I killed the process.

Next I took a look at fsscreen.lst and saw this:
FS084-I Fileshare Server is starting. (Revision 8105).
FS133-I Processing the password file, please wait.
FS136-I Fileshare Server security not active.
FS130-I Reading database reference file, please wait.
FS028-W The log file shows the last session was not shut down
successfully.
FS194-I The log file '/home2/slowenien/slowenien.log.L02' has been
backed up
        successfully to '/home2/slowenien/slowenien.log.B02'.
FS186-I The Fileshare Server is using the log file
        '/home2/slowenien/slowenien.log.L03'
FS143-W The Operating System does not allow free disk space information
to be
        obtained.
FS108-I Processing the files specified in the database reference file.
FS153-I The Fileshare Server name has been reregistered on the network.
FS153-I The Fileshare Server name has been reregistered on the network.
FS132-I The Fileshare Server is running with the following
communications
        protocol(s):
        CCITCP
CCINAMPU
FS232-I Date : 00000114
10123521 FS085-I Fileshare Server is active.
10125718 FS145-W Fileshare Client disconnected, cannot contact user:
0001 Name not 10125718         supplied

We stop fileshareserver each night and restart it again after 5 seconds.

Does anyone know what exactly happens??

When you take a look at the date format in fsscreen.lst .... what's
this?

We're running MF Cobol on Reliant Unix 5.43 C 40.

TIA - Stefan Meyer


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-bea7502659"></a>
## Thread: HOW: Learning project management skills — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: HOW: Learning project management skills

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3880F4C6.1433@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<43318s4lljgbvitvmgovi5if2vj0m2gqtp@4ax.com>`

```
Bearhair wrote:
> 
>    My employer is transitioning me from being a coder to a project "manager",
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Thanks much.

One suggestion.  Try to stay away from "percent complete" reports.  You'll find that 
every item spends 80% of its time "80% complete".
Try to arrange your work packages so that they are between a week and month in duration. 
 Nobody can fudge whether they have started something, or finished it.  This way you 
will always have a better handle since everything is "not started", "in progress" or 
"finished" and nothing can ever "run-away" too far.

(I know that sometimes you are forced to present "% complete" reports - I even provide 
for them in PlanBee - but only because nobody would buy it if I didn't).
```

#### ↳ Re: HOW: Learning project management skills

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85qvie$sn4$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<43318s4lljgbvitvmgovi5if2vj0m2gqtp@4ax.com> <3880F4C6.1433@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```
Ed Guy <ed_guy@nospamguysoftware.com> wrote:

: One suggestion.  Try to stay away from "percent complete" reports.  You'll find that 
: every item spends 80% of its time "80% complete".

Basic rule of thumb is that 80% of the work takes 80% of the time
and the reamining 20% of work takes the other 80% of time.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: HOW: Learning project management skills

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3881F69F.1236@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<43318s4lljgbvitvmgovi5if2vj0m2gqtp@4ax.com> <3880F4C6.1433@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <85qvie$sn4$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Ed Guy <ed_guy@nospamguysoftware.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and the reamining 20% of work takes the other 80% of time.

That's why it's a problem.  If you report that a 10 day job is 80% complete on day 8 
it's reasonable for the reader to assume it will be finished in two days, not thirty 
two.  
When I was doing project management reporting on multi-million dollar projects I asked 
the people to provide their progress on their units as "days from completion" and backed 
into a "% complete" from that.
```

#### ↳ Re: HOW: Learning project management skills

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iqXs8CAyq8g4Ewen@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<43318s4lljgbvitvmgovi5if2vj0m2gqtp@4ax.com> <3880F4C6.1433@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```
1. You can not deliver a baby next month by impregnating nine women
today. Which means that you will not do much to speed an ailing project
along by throwing people at it (in my experience it will slow a project
down because the guy in charge (me) spends too much time bringing extra
bodies up to speed).

2. If any thing can go wrong it will. Also means that if you are not
worrying about something then you should be worried about the fact that
you are not worried about something (MACLEAN'S LAW, before anybody
claims copyright to it!).

3. Don't trust any one, especially users. They lie. Don't trust your
programmers when they tell you they have completed their work and it
works (it will fall over in testing). I've had programmers (with
degrees) tell me that their program works, simply because the compile
did not work.

4. When some one tells you they are working flat out it usually means
that they are reading Playboy (or the local equivalent). I once watched
an experienced programmer spend a year doing that which a trainee took
two weeks to re-do. 

5. When you estimate that it would take you a wee a job, double it up
for any one else.

6. Assess risk seriously and adjust project duration accordingly. OVER-
ESTIMATE rather than be optimistic. Most projects will over-run because
we all tend to under-estimate with undue optimism. I remember one
project I worked on where I doubled my best estimate because I knew the
project manager would halve it....

In article <3880F4C6.1433@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>, Ed Guy
<ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> writes
>Bearhair wrote:
>> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
