<a id="t-ebeb4f75fc"></a>
## Thread: search — 13 msgs

### search

- **From:** "Gerda Beerda" <g.beerda@wanadoo.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-01-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
Hi,

I have 2 records.
PERSON (persnumber, name)
BANK (persnumber, amount)

Now is my question. How do I search all persnumbers in PERSON that are not
in BANK?

Cheers,
Gerda.
```

#### ↳ Re: search

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd638sg32042qakksam0jqql9ondgqqkj3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
On Sat, 15 Jan 2000 19:37:53 +0100 "Gerda Beerda" <g.beerda@wanadoo.nl> wrote:

:>I have 2 records.
:>PERSON (persnumber, name)
:>BANK (persnumber, amount)

:>Now is my question. How do I search all persnumbers in PERSON that are not
:>in BANK?

Assuming files are sequential:

1. Sort both files on person number
2. Read and match
```

#### ↳ Re: search

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
1. Choose the file having lesser number of records. Suppose it is BANK.
2. Store persnumbers from this file in a hash table (in memory).
3. Read a record from PERSON.
4. Search the table for persnumber coming from it. If it is in the table go to
3. If not, output the record and go to 3.
5. Stop.

This way, you have to sort neither file, and it will work a whole lot faster
than SEARCH ALL, even  without accounting for the time needed to sort the keys
from BANK. If you are not sure how to organize a hash table, consult Knuth,
v.3.

Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL

Gerda Beerda wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gerda.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: search

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5mLg4.962$SM.12557@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net>`

```
Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
>1. Choose the file having lesser number of records. Suppose it is BANK.
>2. Store persnumbers from this file in a hash table (in memory).
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>v.3.

It's not quite that simple.  Most hashing algorithm can and do generate
synonyms, depending on the key being hashed.  You could get a false
match because you were looking for a key but found a synonym in the
hash table.  Also, setting up *good* hashing algorithms is no trivial
task, because they tend to be very data dependent.  Much better is to
sort the smaller list of keys, possibly in a memory table.  This will
work *every* time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<388404EC.190F174A@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net> <5mLg4.962$SM.12557@news4.mia>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:

> >Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
> >>1. Choose the file having lesser number of records. Suppose it is BANK.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >It's not quite that simple.

But not too difficult, either. It is not as esoteric as it may seem. In fact, I
recall seeing it discussed (in all honesty, in the form not too suitable for
in-memory search) in the COBOL books by McCracken et al., and Brown.

>Most hashing algorithm can and do generate
>synonyms, depending on the key being hashed.
>You could get a false match because you were
>looking for a key but found a synonym in thehash table.

Not quite sure what you mean by "algorithm" and "synonyms". If "algorithm" means a
hash function used to map the keys to hash addresses, almost any hash function, no
matter how good, will inevitably hash some distinct keys to the same address in the
table. If by the "synonyms", you mean these collisions, a *good* collision
resolution policy intended to tell colliding keys apart unambiguously (and not the
hash function itself) is the heart of any hashing algorithm. If, looking for the
search key, you get a false match, it simply signifies that the program has a bug.

> >Also, setting up *good* hashing algorithms is no trivial
> >task, because they tend to be very data dependent.

Many algorithms we use routinely without any problems are data dependent. The
question is, what is the probability for the worst case scenario to materialize.
For instance, Quicksort's worst case is sorting in O(N*N) time, no better than that
of bubble sort; however, the chance of having the input arranged in such a way is
no greater than for all molecules in your cube to assemble spontaneously in one
half of it. The same is true for hashing. First of all, it is possible to choose a
hash function (that is what you refer to by "*good* hashing algorithm") called
"universal" that is *guaranteed* to spread the keys evenly across the table
addresses regardless of the input. However, it may be a bit expensive to compute.
For this reason in practice, a simpler and more rapidly calculated hash functions
are used. They result in more collisions, but it is usually faster to resolve the
latter than to spend more time computing a universal function. One of the simplest
approaches is to select a table with the number of nodes M somewhat (say 20%)
greater than the number of the keys N, let M be prime and not too close to a power
of 2, and compute a hash address as a key modulo M, i.e. the remainder of key/M. In
COBOL, this method has the advantage of computing, simultaneously with the
remainder, the quotient which can be used to resolve collisions by open addressing
with double hashing.

> >Much better is to
> >sort the smaller list of keys, possibly in a memory table.  This will
> >work *every* time.

A properly organized hash table will work *every* time as well.(As far as I can
tell, a COBOL compiler using a hash symbol table has no problem distinguishing
between "ws-item-1" and "ws-item-2" and who knows what else in the data division.)
The only difference is that if you have 2**10 (about 1000) keys to search, it takes
the binary search, on the average, 11 comparisons to find or reject the search key,
while it takes a 75% full hash table 1.3 comparisons to succeed and 0.75 (yes, less
than 1) comparisons to fail. If you have 2**20 keys, it will be 20 comparisons for
the binary search but still the same 1.3 and 0.75 for hash. As an icing on the
cake, loading a hash table requires no sorting, and duplicate keys (not "synonyms"
but exact duplicates) are unduplicated automatically.

Kind regards,
==================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL
==================

>
> --
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gerda Beerda" <g.beerda@wanadoo.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8613pp$111j$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net> <5mLg4.962$SM.12557@news4.mia> <388404EC.190F174A@mediaone.net>`

```
Oh, wow. And I thought it was not that much code.

Gerda


Paul M. Dorfman <sashole@mediaone.net> schreef in berichtnieuws
388404EC.190F174A@mediaone.net...
>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >>1. Choose the file having lesser number of records. Suppose it is
BANK.
> > >>2. Store persnumbers from this file in a hash table (in memory).
> > >>3. Read a record from PERSON.
> > >>4. Search the table for persnumber coming from it. If it is in the
table go to
> > >>3. If not, output the record and go to 3.
> > >>5. Stop.
> > >>
> > >>This way, you have to sort neither file, and it will work a whole lot
faster
> > >>than SEARCH ALL, even  without accounting for the time needed to sort
the keys
> > >>from BANK. If you are not sure how to organize a hash table, consult
Knuth,
> > >>v.3.
> >
> > >It's not quite that simple.
>
> But not too difficult, either. It is not as esoteric as it may seem. In
fact, I
> recall seeing it discussed (in all honesty, in the form not too suitable
for
> in-memory search) in the COBOL books by McCracken et al., and Brown.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Not quite sure what you mean by "algorithm" and "synonyms". If "algorithm"
means a
> hash function used to map the keys to hash addresses, almost any hash
function, no
> matter how good, will inevitably hash some distinct keys to the same
address in the
> table. If by the "synonyms", you mean these collisions, a *good* collision
> resolution policy intended to tell colliding keys apart unambiguously (and
not the
> hash function itself) is the heart of any hashing algorithm. If, looking
for the
> search key, you get a false match, it simply signifies that the program
has a bug.
>
> > >Also, setting up *good* hashing algorithms is no trivial
> > >task, because they tend to be very data dependent.
>
> Many algorithms we use routinely without any problems are data dependent.
The
> question is, what is the probability for the worst case scenario to
materialize.
> For instance, Quicksort's worst case is sorting in O(N*N) time, no better
than that
> of bubble sort; however, the chance of having the input arranged in such a
way is
> no greater than for all molecules in your cube to assemble spontaneously
in one
> half of it. The same is true for hashing. First of all, it is possible to
choose a
> hash function (that is what you refer to by "*good* hashing algorithm")
called
> "universal" that is *guaranteed* to spread the keys evenly across the
table
> addresses regardless of the input. However, it may be a bit expensive to
compute.
> For this reason in practice, a simpler and more rapidly calculated hash
functions
> are used. They result in more collisions, but it is usually faster to
resolve the
> latter than to spend more time computing a universal function. One of the
simplest
> approaches is to select a table with the number of nodes M somewhat (say
20%)
> greater than the number of the keys N, let M be prime and not too close to
a power
> of 2, and compute a hash address as a key modulo M, i.e. the remainder of
key/M. In
> COBOL, this method has the advantage of computing, simultaneously with the
> remainder, the quotient which can be used to resolve collisions by open
addressing
> with double hashing.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> A properly organized hash table will work *every* time as well.(As far as
I can
> tell, a COBOL compiler using a hash symbol table has no problem
distinguishing
> between "ws-item-1" and "ws-item-2" and who knows what else in the data
division.)
> The only difference is that if you have 2**10 (about 1000) keys to search,
it takes
> the binary search, on the average, 11 comparisons to find or reject the
search key,
> while it takes a 75% full hash table 1.3 comparisons to succeed and 0.75
(yes, less
> than 1) comparisons to fail. If you have 2**20 keys, it will be 20
comparisons for
> the binary search but still the same 1.3 and 0.75 for hash. As an icing on
the
> cake, loading a hash table requires no sorting, and duplicate keys (not
"synonyms"
> but exact duplicates) are unduplicated automatically.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hW_g4.3246$wk.43393@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net> <5mLg4.962$SM.12557@news4.mia> <388404EC.190F174A@mediaone.net>`

```
Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> >Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>But not too difficult, either. It is not as esoteric as it may seem.

Hashing isn't 'esoteric', or 'difficult', but it is 'subtle'.  Because
hashing algorithms are so sensitive to data, it is rarely, if ever,
possible to select a good hashing algorithm without some analysis,
and perhaps testing real sample data.  This is particularly true if
you want to use storage space effectively.

>In fact, I recall seeing it discussed (in all honesty, in the form
>not too suitable for in-memory search) in the COBOL books by
>McCracken et al., and Brown.

Sorry, I assumed you were making a particular mistake, but I see that
you are making a different one instead.  Hashing algorithms are very
inefficient in the use of their 'hashing address space'.  It would be
a terrible way to store a large table in computer memory, and if the
table is small, it hardly deserves a hash table, no?  Once again, the
best solution to this type of problem is either:

A. If the table is small enough to load into memory, load it into
   a memory table and sort it, perhaps by doing an insertion sort
   while loading, pass the other file and match using binary search.

B. If the table is too large for memory, then sort both files and
   do a match, or create a key index for one file, and do a match
   using the key index.

Both those solutions are simpler, and more efficient than hashing,
for this particular application. :-)

Also, you should bear in mind that many authors, and most academics,
have never designed and written applications in the 'real world', and
often have little understanding of maintaining systems over time.
Real world applications are seldom as simplistic as examples given
in books, and frequently get very messy, because of peculiar design
requirements. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<388561FB.7A70714@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net> <5mLg4.962$SM.12557@news4.mia> <388404EC.190F174A@mediaone.net> <hW_g4.3246$wk.43393@news1.mia>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:

> Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
> >Judson McClendon wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> and perhaps testing real sample data...

But of course, it is possible. It is done all the time. I have already tried to explain
that hashing is no more sensitive to the input than other popular algorithms like
quicksort widely used without ever questioning their worst-case behavior since it
practically never occurs. I doubt that your COBOL compiler gathers all the identifiers
in the working storage and tests their distribution as keys to decide whether to store
them in a symbol table (organized exactly in the form of a hash table) or not. It
simply does, and judging from the speed even very large programs compile, it does it
fairly efficiently.

> >In fact, I recall seeing it discussed (in all honesty, in the form
> >not too suitable for in-memory search) in the COBOL books by
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> you are making a different one instead.

Frankly, I fail to comprehend. Which "mistakes" you are alluding to?

>  Hashing algorithms are very
> inefficient in the use of their 'hashing address space'.

Let us see. If one wants to search 1,000,000 keys 8 byte each, an ordered table for
binary search will consume 8,000,000 bytes. It will use 20 comparisons between keys, on
the average, for either a successful or unsuccessful search. A 25% sparse hash table
with 1,250,000 elements consuming 10,000,000 bytes will need, on the average, 1
comparison to do the same work. 250,000 bytes of memory is hardly anything, by modern
standards, to pay for performance better by an order of magnitude. However, if one is
really concerned about the "inefficient use of address space", even 99% full hash table
will use 1.5  comparisons to find the search key and 0.99 - to reject it (given that
the collisions are resolved by coalesced list chaining).

> It would be
> a terrible way to store a large table in computer memory,

About 25% more terrible than in the case of binary search, with the advantage of
searching 10 times faster.

> .and if the table is small, it hardly deserves a hash table, no?

And why not? Define "small". If the number of keys is small, the memory considerations
become irrelevant altogether, and we can use a 90% sparse hash table! A successful
search would hardly occur faster (the same 1 comparison), yet only 1 comparison per 10
unsuccessful searches would be needed, and that would be quite welcome if most search
keys, as it often happens, are not in the table!

>  Once again, the
> best solution to this type of problem is either:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>    while loading, pass the other file and match using binary search.

Once again, define "small". Insertion sort is a lovely simple algorithm; however, it
sorts in O(N*N) time, and is hardly of any practical use for more that a couple of
dozen of keys, or if the input is already somewhat ordered (for instance, through a
partial partitioning quicksort scheme). As to the binary search, the faith in this
algorithm borders on religious, even though in the plain everyday activity, nobody
ventures to use it. Looking for "Aaron" in a dictionary, do you open it in the middle
or closer to the beginning? A computer algorithm paralleling it, the "interpolation
search", actually manages to search in log(log(N)) time, as opposed to log(N) time for
the binary search (i.e. 5 comparisons instead of 20 for 1,000,000 keys). However,
*that* is really input-dependent...

> B. If the table is too large for memory, then sort both files and
>    do a match, or create a key index for one file, and do a match
>    using the key index.

No doubt about that... even though with the speed hashing provides, it may be faster to
repeatedly hash in chunks writing an unmatched part for the time being and rehashing
again, than to sort both files, especially if the bigger one is burdened with a long
satellite tail.

Both those solutions are simpler, and more efficient than hashing,

> for this particular application. :-)

"Simpler" is a matter of opinion; as far as efficiency goes, let me respectfully
disagree.

> Also, you should bear in mind that many authors, and most academics,
> have never designed and written applications in the 'real world', and
> often have little understanding of maintaining systems over time.

Thank you, I will try to keep it in mind. Actually, I only mentioned the discussions of
hashing in the COBOL books to emphasize that hashing is not all that foreign to a COBOL
programmer. I am not sure whether McCracken and Gary DeWard Brown are those "academics"
who never written and designed an application in the "real world". Maybe. I simply do
not know. However, I can tell from my own experience of writing and maintaining hashing
programs in a number of tongues (including SAS, COBOL, PL/I, C, and, God forbid,
Algol-60 - circa 1972) that they are not all that horrible, and in any case, those two
or three hashing paragraphs are much more simplistic than many legacy applications'
routines I have been unfortunate to encounter at 3 o'clock in the morning.

> Real world applications are seldom as simplistic as examples given
> in books, and frequently get very messy, because of peculiar design
> requirements. :-)

Or, often times, without any requirements at all! :-)

Kind regards,
=========================
Paul M. Dorfman
AT&T Universal Card
Jacksonville, Fl
=========================
"Though this be madness, yet there is method in't".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Glenn Gordon" <ggordon@dimensional.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s7eh4.448$x3.694@wormhole.dimensional.com>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net> <5mLg4.962$SM.12557@news4.mia> <388404EC.190F174A@mediaone.net> <hW_g4.3246$wk.43393@news1.mia> <388561FB.7A70714@mediaone.net>`

```
The problem with using working storage tables of any sort with this method
is that the keys must be stored.  Most COBOL's have a limit on the size of
an 01 level and it is wasteful of system resources to code huge working
storage tables.  In an application I support we have exceeded the 01 level
size limitations several times recently.  I hate arbitrary limitations
imposed by sizes of tables.  I suppose you could code to fill the hash list
to a certain point and process the second file in stages, eliminating as
many records as possible each pass.  But then you have added a whole new
layer of complexity to the process.

If the hash list resolves collisions by quadratic probing it will degrade to
worse than a simple sequential search since each hit on an occupied node not
equal to the searched for key must continue probing until an empty node is
hit, or all nodes have been probed.  And quadratic probing is better than
secondary hashing functions.

If the hash list resolves collisions by chaining, then it will do better,
however not nearly as well as you have stated.   Each hit on an occupied
node will require a search of the full sublist chain to ensure the key does
not exist.  If the hash algorithm has a good distribution this will still
beat binary searches by at least log2(hash list size) compares, but keep in
mind the overhead of hashing a key well is very likely to be greater than
the overhead of adjusting the index for a binary search.  I once benchmarked
SEARCH vs. SEARCH ALL and the results were surprising!  Even the modest
overhead of the binary search's index adjustment moved the break even point
of the binary search from the theoretical 5 items to 32 items.  It is quite
probable the hashing overhead will eat away some of the minimum compare
advantages the hash list enjoys for lists resolving collisions through
chaining.

To use a dictionary hash list without storing the keys the best you can do
is hit an empty node and know the key does not exist.  If the node is not
empty the key may or may not exist.

In any event, hash lists are indeed sensitive to the data volume and key
cardinalities.  Without solid information about the files and the
requirements of the process being developed sorting the files and then
processing them simply is the best alternative.  These files may be quite
large or have high growth rates, or have very large keys or very high key
cardinality.  The process may be a one-shot, in which case the effort to
code and test the hash list solution is excessive, or it may be an ongoing
reconciliation which changing characteristics of the files may require the
program implementing the hashed list solution to be re-visited periodically
for tuning of the list.  It may also be that one of the files is already in
the correct sort order.

All in all the sorting and filtering solution is simpler, more stable, less
complicated, easier to test and maintain.  Its performance will not vary as
much with changing data characteristics, either.  Unless there is a pressing
need for optimal speed I do not see the hashing solution as viable other
than as an academic or ego exercise for the person coding it.  The clever
way is often not the best way in the long term, and even in the short term
the clever way is often excessively clever.

Glenn Gordon



"Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net> wrote in message
news:388561FB.7A70714@mediaone.net...
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> > Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
> > >Judson McClendon wrote:
> > >> >Paul M. Dorfman wrote:
> > >> >>1. Choose the file having lesser number of records. Suppose it is
BANK.
> > >> >>2. Store persnumbers from this file in a hash table (in memory).
> > >> >>3. Read a record from PERSON.
> > >> >>4. Search the table for persnumber coming from it. If it is in the
table go to
> > >> >>3. If not, output the record and go to 3.
> > >> >>5. Stop.
> > >> >>
> > >> >>This way, you have to sort neither file, and it will work a whole
lot faster
> > >> >>than SEARCH ALL, even  without accounting for the time needed to
sort the keys
> > >> >>from BANK. If you are not sure how to organize a hash table,
consult Knuth,
> > >> >>v.3.
...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3885C5AD.D576A6F8@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net> <5mLg4.962$SM.12557@news4.mia> <388404EC.190F174A@mediaone.net> <hW_g4.3246$wk.43393@news1.mia> <388561FB.7A70714@mediaone.net> <s7eh4.448$x3.694@wormhole.dimensional.com>`

```


Glenn Gordon wrote:
> 
> The problem with using working storage tables of any sort with this method
> is that the keys must be stored.  Most COBOL's have a limit on the size of
> an 01 level and it is wasteful of system resources to code huge working
> storage tables.  

That makes sense.  But what is the real cost of this "waste" nowadays? 
Every once in a while I need to reevaluate my old assumptions of cost. 
While the cost of maintaining a table which has new limits beyond what
analysis predicted is still high, I would like some feedback on what
currently are the costs of having a large working storage table compared
to alternatives - on mainframes and other environments.

If I stick a huge table in a COBOL program, am I costing my employer
anything significant?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ynkh4.19678$Dv1.486996@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net> <5mLg4.962$SM.12557@news4.mia> <388404EC.190F174A@mediaone.net> <hW_g4.3246$wk.43393@news1.mia> <388561FB.7A70714@mediaone.net> <s7eh4.448$x3.694@wormhole.dimensional.com> <3885C5AD.D576A6F8@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> wrote in message
news:3885C5AD.D576A6F8@NOSPAMwebaccess.net...
>
>
> Glenn Gordon wrote:
> >
> > The problem with using working storage tables of any sort with this
method
> > is that the keys must be stored.  Most COBOL's have a limit on the size
of
> > an 01 level and it is wasteful of system resources to code huge working
> > storage tables.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> anything significant?

At some point, the time taken to manage and/or search a table exceeds the
time taken to retrieve the record from an indexed file or relational table.
Since it is now possible to cache the entire data store, this point is lower
than in the past when the data store resided on disk. So, for really large
tables, consider using traditional I/O methods rather than writing your own
caching routines. As an added bonus, if the table resides in a relational
database, referential integrity rules become the problem of the database and
not your program.

Karl Wagner
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3886B7F7.3B8368CB@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net> <5mLg4.962$SM.12557@news4.mia> <388404EC.190F174A@mediaone.net> <hW_g4.3246$wk.43393@news1.mia> <388561FB.7A70714@mediaone.net> <s7eh4.448$x3.694@wormhole.dimensional.com>`

```
Glenn Gordon wrote:

> The problem with using working storage tables of any sort with this method
> is that the keys must be stored.  Most COBOL's have a limit on the size of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> layer of complexity to the process.

Right. I'd better reserve hashing (or an ordered table search, for that matter)
for the cases when a table can be organized completely in memory.

> If the hash list resolves collisions by quadratic probing it will degrade to
> worse than a simple sequential search since each hit on an occupied node not
> equal to the searched for key must continue probing until an empty node is
> hit, or all nodes have been probed.

Not *will* but rather *could*. The performance of any collision resolution
policy degrades appreciably as the table gets fuller. But even the linear
probing - the simplest and slowest scheme - performs acceptably for tables with
the load factor below 60%.

>  And quadratic probing is better than
> secondary hashing functions.

This is inaccurate. Open addressing with double hashing (if this is what you
mean by "secondary hashing function") is much faster since it randomizes probing
sequences much better than quadratic probing, the latter exhibiting secondary
clustering, and the former not. If a table is 25% sparse, double hashing, by the
very nature of the algorithm, is guaranteed - no matter  how many keys are
actually stored in the table - to use no more than 2 probes to find a search key
and no more than 4 to have it rejected if it is not in the table.

> If the hash list resolves collisions by chaining, then it will do better,
> however not nearly as well as you have stated.

What I stated is not a theoretically derived conjecture but experimental results
obtained in various languages on many platforms and with wide variety of
real-world, real-size data. Hash search coded in 3- and 4GLs has been compared
with binary search coded in the underlying software (e.g. Search All in COBOL,
formats in SAS, etc.) and always wins the bigger, the more keys are  to be
searched. It should be noted, though, that the experimental results corroborate
theoretical ones quite well, and in fact, in practice hash search runs even
slightly faster than predicted by the theory.

>   Each hit on an occupied
> node will require a search of the full sublist chain to ensure the key does
> not exist.

Right. However, how big is the sublist on the average? If there are more nodes
than keys, it is less than 1 key, so an unsuccessful search requires less than 1
comparison. Sure some addresses will contains several keys, and others none, yet
even the simplest hash function like mod(key, hashtablesize) virtually
guarantees that no sublist will be longer than several keys, and therefore can
be efficiently searched serially. Of course, to make that happen, the hash table
size should prime and not too close to the power of 2, but what is the problem
of choosing it this way?

>  If the hash algorithm has a good distribution this will still
> beat binary searches by at least log2(hash list size) compares, but keep in
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> chaining.

And how great is the hashing overhead compared to that of binary search? In each
iteration of the latter, there are two computations: one calculating a midpoint
and one addition (subtraction) to adjust the index. For an unsuccessful search,
this has to be done full log2(N) times; for a successful search, same but on the
average. On the other hand, if it is decided to hash using a modulo and resolve
collisions by chaining, hashing *always*  incorporates a single division per
given key to search for. It is all computational overhead it has, for if the
remainder points to an empty node, the key is not in the table, and the
algorithm stops. Otherwise, as you have indicated above, it has to go through
the sublist of the keys attached to the node whose average length is less than
1. I have studied distributions of real-world keys using the modulo function
with the divisor chosen according to the above simple rule, and it is quite rare
to find a mere couple of nodes per million with more than 8 keys colliding in
it, even if the table is nearly full, while Your own conclusion tells that such
short lists are best searched sequentially, plus the vast majority of the keys
occupy their seats alone. In the worst-case scenario of 8 colliding keys, 8
moves would have to be made to adjust the links, and less than 1 on the average.
However, binary search has its own data movement overhead having to move the
midpoint adjusted up or down by unity to the low/ high index, only it has to do
it, on the average, not once, log2(N) times. If double hashing were used, it
would have an extra subtraction to step through the table (2 on the average per
key searched). Seemingly, another division would be needed to compute the probe
decrement, but it is exactly in COBOL where it is not necessary, since the
quotient already obtained as a result of the first division can be utilized for
this purpose. So, all in all, whose overhead is higher?

> To use a dictionary hash list without storing the keys the best you can do
> is hit an empty node and know the key does not exist.  If the node is not
> empty the key may or may not exist.

You are referring to key-indexed search. This is the fastest existing search
scheme, indeed, and so should always be used when applicable. Unfortunately,
key-indexing requires that memory must be abundant to swallow the entire
universe of key values. This is OK if the keys are say 5-digit integers but
generally rarely possible, and certainly not if the keys are long character
strings. However, when it can be used, key-indexing is the same old hash again,
only with an infinitely sparse table and constant hash function.

> In any event, hash lists are indeed sensitive to the data volume and key
> cardinalities.  Without solid information about the files and the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> the correct sort order.

The effort of coding and testing a "hashing solution" is no greater than
anything else in data processing, and any competent programmer capable of coding
a bubble sort can implement it in a matter of an hour or two by simply literally
following one of the algorithms provided by Knuth. This is especially true if it
has already been coded and tested great many times, in which case it is a
cut-and-paste effort hardly even qualifiying for a coding effort. I routinely
run a number of such matching "solutions" in production where hash tables are
populated afresh daily with the keys from a vendor driver files, every time
different. I have never had to re-visit the programs or tune it for a particular
driver, yet they consistently run in the same specific (i.e. relative to the
number of keys in the other file) time.

> All in all the sorting and filtering solution is simpler, more stable, less
> complicated, easier to test and maintain.  Its performance will not vary as
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the clever way is often excessively clever.

A need for optimal speed is always there, and now more than ever. As data
processing is headed in the direction of data warehousing and OLAP, and business
forecasting is based more and more on time series analyses relying on literally
years of transactional data history, quick methods of data extraction,
validation, scrubbing and so on become a paramount necessity rather than an
"academic or ego exercise". Such exercises per se, by the way, are not all that
harmful. After all, "simple" and KISS methods we are taking now for granted,
were academic or ego exercises at some point. (However, hashing has hardly ever
qualified for such an exercise since it was originated, theoretically grounded,
and developed into a practical method of data searching in the depths of Big
Blue in fifties.) The saying that the most practical thing is a good theory
belongs to one of the greatest experimental physicists of all time.

Kind regards,
==================
Paul M. Dorfman,
Jacksonville, Fl
==================

> Glenn Gordon
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: search

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8643nq$1bga$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<85qet0$23hl$1@buty.wanadoo.nl> <38829D54.D3126B14@mediaone.net> <5mLg4.962$SM.12557@news4.mia> <388404EC.190F174A@mediaone.net> <hW_g4.3246$wk.43393@news1.mia> <388561FB.7A70714@mediaone.net>`

```

Paul M. Dorfman wrote in message <388561FB.7A70714@mediaone.net>...
>
>Judson McClendon wrote:
[...]
>>  Hashing algorithms are very
>> inefficient in the use of their 'hashing address space'.
>
>Let us see. If one wants to search 1,000,000 keys 8 byte each, an ordered
table for
>binary search will consume 8,000,000 bytes. It will use 20 comparisons
between keys, on
>the average, for either a successful or unsuccessful search. A 25% sparse
hash table
>with 1,250,000 elements consuming 10,000,000 bytes will need, on the
average, 1
>comparison to do the same work. 250,000 bytes of memory is hardly anything,
by modern
>standards, to pay for performance better by an order of magnitude. However,
if one is
>really concerned about the "inefficient use of address space", even 99%
full hash table
>will use 1.5  comparisons to find the search key and 0.99 - to reject it
(given that
>the collisions are resolved by coalesced list chaining).
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>About 25% more terrible than in the case of binary search, with the
advantage of
>searching 10 times faster.

10,000,000 - 8,000,000 = 2,000,000 not 250,000; but still 25%
memory overhead.

Adding a four byte field for list chaining yields

15,000,000 - 8,000,000 = 7,000,000 or 87.5% memory overhead.

I think using a hash table for the problem, as described, is
overkill.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

thru Jan 28 - for the San Diego J4 meeting (Jan 17 - Jan 28).  I may or may
not get the FAQ updated for the new "prefixes for subject lines" before I

public review periods. My stomach already "churns" at the thought of what is
in the draft, what isn't in the draft, and what is still "wacky" with the

## Thread: STD - Yet another "interesting" document — 3 this month / 90 total

### STD - Yet another "interesting" document

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85so2t$e18$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I hate to "provoke" and run <G> but,

Those who are interested in reviewing the upcoming Standard or in "open
COBOL" may be interested in reviewing a *DRAFT* document that was submitted
this last week for J4 to consider.  I expect this document to be discussed
early in the Jan 17-28 J4 meeting.  (Furthermore, due to ISO "review" rules,
I expect it to be "approved" in spirit - if not in detail.)

I do *not* see much purpose in you sending me notes - or even the J4 chair
(at doncobol <at> mediaone.net) on this paper (unless you do so very
quickly).  However, I did think that I would share with the NG the "approach"
that I am dealing with (some of which is caused by rules WELL outside the
purview of J4 or WG4)

See:

    http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/00-0177.doc
```

#### ↳ Re: STD - Yet another "interesting" document

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38823B5F.77A3643@home.com>`
- **References:** `<85so2t$e18$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> I hate to "provoke" and run <G> but, etc....

Let's take one extract from the document you referred to. Since being on
this side of the Pond I've noticed a N.American penchant for using the
word "shall" instead of "will" :-

"Certain words have significance and their correct use is necessary in
order to establish the requirements of the standard.  These words are:
shall
should
may
can

These words are defined in 5, Description techniques.   To explain them
simply:  
     -  'shall' expresses a requirement; 
     -  'should' is a recommendation (not a requirement); 
     -  'may' expresses permission (but not a requirement); 
     -  'can' expresses a capability available to the user.  ...."

It is my view that if documentors got down to writing things as "will"
rather than 'if's', 'but's' or 'maybe's' - we would have less
ambiguity in the decision making processes. If you have to use those
'qualifying' words enumerated above, either put the topic on ice for
later discussion or go firm on a ('will') defined solution now. You feel
you 'can't put it on ice'? Then beat the thing to death until you can
define it with a 'will'.

I might add, 'shall', 'should', 'may', 'can', leaves open a can of worms
for implementor defined functions - which has resulted in modules like
Report Writer being ignored by most developers. 

Let's have 'plain English' folks - not semantics.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: STD - Yet another "interesting" document

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86v1ad$5pg$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<85so2t$e18$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <38823B5F.77A3643@home.com>`

```
In my opinion, the ISO rules about "SHALL" meaning a requirement are not
related to any "real" English usage, (i.e. I know of no place where English
is the native language where it has this meaning).  Unfortunately, this is
the ISO rule - and is (now? already?) in "common usage" in ISO Standards (for
things that include non-progrmming languages).
```

<a id="t-91146dec85"></a>
## Thread: Zellers Congruence slow (long) — 17 this month / 19 total

### Zellers Congruence slow (long)

- **From:** Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<EA92F578F463D311941100A0C9EC6FBF546177@GOLD>`

```
Zeller's congruence is slow code on a mainframe.  I have a COBOL
program that will loop calculating COBOL day numbers (day 1 = January
1,
1601) for all days in a year range specified on a control record. On
an OS390 system using COBOL for MVS and VM the code below takes less
than half the time of FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE.   The calculation in
the loop is either null for getting the timing for looping through the
range, FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE or the following code which assumes
years divisible by 4000 are not leap years. The 100 and 400 year rules
are followed.  This calculation does only 3 multiplies including the
one for the subscript and no division.  The full program is available
on request.  
 

COMPUTE WS-WORK-DAY = WORK-GREG-DD OF DATEWORK
                       + WS-CUMULATIVE-DAYS (WORK-GREG-MM OF DATEWORK)
COMPUTE WS-LEAP-WORK-UNPACKED =
                  WORK-GREG-NUM-CCYY OF DATEWORK * 25
IF WORK-GREG-MM OF DATEWORK > '02'
        AND WS-LEAP-CHK = '00'
        AND (WORK-GREG-YY OF DATEWORK NOT = '00'
            OR WS-LEAP-CENTURY-CHK = '00')
     ADD 1 TO WS-WORK-DAY
END-IF
COMPUTE WS-YEAR-WORK = WORK-GREG-NUM-CCYY OF DATEWORK - 1
SUBTRACT 25 FROM WS-LEAP-WORK-UNPACKED
COMPUTE WORK-COBOL-DAY-NO OF DATEWORK =
                      (WS-YEAR-WORK * 365) + WS-LEAP-COUNT
                       - WS-CENTURY-WORK + WS-LEAP-CENTURY-CNT
                       + WS-WORK-DAY - 584388 - WS-MILLENIUM-NON-LEAP

The related working storage snippets are:

           05  WS-WORK-DAY           X-Mozilla-Status: 0009IMAL.
           05  WS-YEAR-WORK            PIC 9(4).
           05  WS-YEAR-WORK-X REDEFINES WS-YEAR-WORK.
               10  WS-CENTURY-WORK     PIC 99.
               10  WS-YY-WORK-X        PIC XX.
           05  WS-MONTH-TABLE.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE
ZERO.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 31.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'JAN'.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 31.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 28.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'FEB'.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 59.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 31.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'MAR'.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 90.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 30.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'APR'.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE
120.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 31.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'MAY'.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE
151.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 30.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'JUN'.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE
181.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 31.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'JUL'.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE
212.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 31.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'AUG'.
            X-Mozilla-Status: 0009  PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE
243.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 30.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'SEP'.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE
273.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 31.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'OCT'.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE
304.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 30.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'NOV'.
               10  FILLER           PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE
334.
               10  FILLER           PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE 31.
               10  FILLER           PIC XXX VALUE 'DEC'.
           05  FILLER REDEFINES WS-MONTH-TABLE.
               10  WS-MONTH-ENT        OCCURS 12 INDEXED WS-MONTH-IDX.
                   14  WS-CUMULATIVE-DAYS PIC S9(3) PACKED-DECIMAL.
                   14  WS-MONTH-DAY    PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL.
                   14  WS-MONTH-NAME   PIC XXX.
           05  WS-DAY                  PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL.
           05  WS-MONTH                PIC S999 PACKED-DECIMAL.
.
.
. 
           05  WS-LEAP-WORK-UNPACKED   PIC 9(7).
           05  FILLER REDEFINES WS-LEAP-WORK-UNPACKED.
               10  WS-LEAP-COUNT       PIC 9(5).
               10  WS-LEAP-CHK         PIC XX.
           05  FILLER REDEFINES WS-LEAP-WORK-UNPACKED.
               10  WS-LEAP-CENTURY-CNT PIC 9(3).
               10  WS-LEAP-CENTURY-CHK PIC XX.
               10  FILLER              PIC XX.
           05  FILLER REDEFINES WS-LEAP-WORK-UNPACKED.
               10  WS-MILLENIUM-NON-LEAP PIC 99.
               10  FILLER              PIC X(5).
           05  WS-CENTURY-CHK          PIC 99.
.
.
,
           05  WORK-GREG-DATE.
               10  WORK-GREG-CCYY.
                   15 WORK-GREG-CC    PIC 9(2).
                   15 WORK-GREG-YY    PIC 9(2).
               10  WORK-GREG-NUM-CCYY REDEFINES WORK-GREG-CCYY
                                      PIC 9(04).
               10  WORK-GREG-MMDD.
                   15  WORK-GREG-MM   PIC 9(2).
                   15  WORK-GREG-DD   PIC 9(2).
           05  WORK-GREG-NUM-DATE     REDEFINES WORK-GREG-DATE
                                      PIC 9(08).


where WS-YEAR-WORK is defined and redefined as follows:
           05  WS-YEAR-WORK            PIC 9(4).
           05  WS-YEAR-WORK-X REDEFINES WS-YEAR-WORK.
               10  WS-CENTURY-WORK     PIC 99.
               10  WS-YY-WORK-X        PIC XX.

and WS-LEAP-WORK is defined and redefined as follows:
           05  WS-LEAP-WORK-UNPACKED   PIC 9(7).
           05  FILLER REDEFINES WS-LEAP-WORK-UNPACKED.
               10  WS-LEAP-COUNT       PIC 9(5).
               10  WS-LEAP-CHK         PIC XX.
           05  FILLER REDEFINES WS-LEAP-WORK-UNPACKED.
               10  WS-LEAP-CENTURY-CNT PIC 9(3).
               10  WS-LEAP-CENTURY-CHK PIC XX.
               10  FILLER              PIC XX.
           05  FILLER REDEFINES WS-LEAP-WORK-UNPACKED.
               10X-Mozilla-Status: 0009P PIC 99.
               10  FILLER              PIC X(5).

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.
```

#### ↳ Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long)

- **From:** torek@elf.bsdi.com (Chris Torek)
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<85u3in$ovg$1@elf.bsdi.com>`
- **References:** `<EA92F578F463D311941100A0C9EC6FBF546177@GOLD> <38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca>`

```
In article <38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca>
Clark Morris  <cfmtech@istar.ca> writes:
>Zeller's congruence is slow code on a mainframe. ... the following
>code which assumes years divisible by 4000 are not leap years.

The "4000" rule is not actually part of the Gregorian calendar.
(Of course, it does not matter for another 2000 years.)

Probably a dumb question (remember, I know no COBOL), but: why not
use COMP-3 (or whatever it is that means "do this in binary instead
of packed decimal") to do arithmetic?  (How slow is packed decimal
to comp-3 conversion, both in and out?)
```

##### ↳ ↳ OT COBOL Coding (was: Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long))

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<3882A6E1.4A966026@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<EA92F578F463D311941100A0C9EC6FBF546177@GOLD> <38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca> <85u3in$ovg$1@elf.bsdi.com>`

```
Chris Torek wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> Probably a dumb question (remember, I know no COBOL), but: why not
> use COMP-3 (or whatever it is that means "do this in binary instead
> of packed decimal") to do arithmetic?  (How slow is packed decimal
> to comp-3 conversion, both in and out?)

On IBM mainframes "COMP-3" is packed decimal. From VS COBOL II on we can
specify "PACKED-DECIMAL" instead of "COMP-3" to eliminate any confusion.
While we're on the topic, "COMP" is binary and, as above, we can now
code "BINARY" instead of "COMP".

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT COBOL Coding (was: Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long))

- **From:** torek@elf.bsdi.com (Chris Torek)
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<85ucfr$p7g$1@elf.bsdi.com>`
- **References:** `<EA92F578F463D311941100A0C9EC6FBF546177@GOLD> <38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca> <85u3in$ovg$1@elf.bsdi.com> <3882A6E1.4A966026@worldnet.att.net>`

```
I asked:
>> Probably a dumb question (remember, I know no COBOL), but: why not
>> use COMP-3 (or whatever it is that means "do this in binary instead
>> of packed decimal") to do arithmetic?  (How slow is packed decimal
>> to comp-3 conversion, both in and out?)

In article <3882A6E1.4A966026@worldnet.att.net>
William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net> writes:
>On IBM mainframes "COMP-3" is packed decimal. From VS COBOL II on we can
>specify "PACKED-DECIMAL" instead of "COMP-3" to eliminate any confusion.
>While we're on the topic, "COMP" is binary and, as above, we can now
>code "BINARY" instead of "COMP".

Ah, well, that would answer the "why not convert to comp-3" question:
no sense converting to the form it already has! :-)  (Well, I *did*
say I know no COBOL.)

Given the above, let me rephrase the question in what I imagine
will be a more comprehensible form.  The languages I use generally
have no facilities for working with anything *but* internal formats
(binary and floating point), along with perhaps some extended
arithmetic and complex numbers (all still in binary and floating
point formats).  (Fixed-point, where present, is really just binary.)
Given that, my first inclination, whenever I have to deal with BCD
representations -- such as when talking to RTC chips -- is to write
code like:

	sec = frombcd(dev->sec); /* copy out the values: seconds */
	min = frombcd(dev->min); /* minutes */
	hrs = frombcd(dev->hrs); /* etc */

and then deal with everything in binary.  (Since many of the CPUs
I use lack BCD instructions, this is rarely if ever any less
efficient than working directly with the BCD representation.)

That being the case, my first impulse, on dealing with packed
decimal in COBOL, would be to do the same thing.  (Well, unless I
actually learned COBOL and changed my impulse-basis in the process.)
How (in)efficient would this be?  Obviously it depends on the
circumstances and the machine, and I presume it would be a bad
idea in the case of the code to which I posted the "dumb question"
above; but idle curosity (or something) prompts me to ask: just
how bad?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT COBOL Coding (was: Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long))

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3888D81B.62F05D6B@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<EA92F578F463D311941100A0C9EC6FBF546177@GOLD> <38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca> <85u3in$ovg$1@elf.bsdi.com> <3882A6E1.4A966026@worldnet.att.net> <85ucfr$p7g$1@elf.bsdi.com>`

```
Om mainframes, which usually have hardware packed decimal
support, packed decimal is not much slower than binary. On other
machines, which generally do not have packed decimal support in
hardware, it is around 10-100 times slower than binary.

However some PC implementations of COBOL do their binary
arithmetic in 'big-endian' format to more closely match mainframe
processing. That's slow too. 

Arithmetic using display format is slow anywhere.

Tim Josling


Chris Torek wrote:

> That being the case, my first impulse, on dealing with packed
> decimal in COBOL, would be to do the same thing.  (Well, unless I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> how bad?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long)

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<e1rqhkLY$GA.185@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<EA92F578F463D311941100A0C9EC6FBF546177@GOLD> <38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca> <85u3in$ovg$1@elf.bsdi.com>`

```
Speed is dependent on how the routine was coded.
Because one implementation was slow, does not mean that all implementations
in that environment are slow.

The internal representation of numeric values may be an issue, but the
implemention of the rules is the more likely culrpit.

Where is the coding example?

news:85u3in$ovg$1@elf.bsdi.com...

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long)

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<85vh07$3lj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<EA92F578F463D311941100A0C9EC6FBF546177@GOLD> <38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca> <85u3in$ovg$1@elf.bsdi.com>`

```
All of the COMP fields are specific to platform. If you are going to have
anything that is "standard", then it must be platform neutral.

Chris Torek wrote in message <85u3in$ovg$1@elf.bsdi.com>...
>In article <38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca>
>Clark Morris  <cfmtech@istar.ca> writes:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://claw.bsdi.com/torek/  (not always up) I report spam to abuse@.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<388481E8.5044894@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<EA92F578F463D311941100A0C9EC6FBF546177@GOLD> <38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca> <85u3in$ovg$1@elf.bsdi.com>`

```
Chris Torek wrote:
> 
> Probably a dumb question (remember, I know no COBOL), but: why not
> use COMP-3 (or whatever it is that means "do this in binary instead
> of packed decimal") to do arithmetic?  (How slow is packed decimal
> to comp-3 conversion, both in and out?)

On an IBM mainframe, COMP-3 (packed-decimal) converts quickly to binary
or to display.  Its efficiency is close to binary and it converts more
quickly to display.  Most recommendations I have seen are - use binary
for incremental loop counters, packed-decimal for arithmetic, and
display for interfacing with the outside world.
```

#### ↳ Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long)

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000117114103.28428.00000487@ng-fl1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca>`

```

I am curious about "... calculating COBOL day numbers (day 1 = January 1,
1601) for all days in a year range..." and about  the FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE.
 

The posted routine does not seem concerned about the calendar correction in a
recent century.  The way I remember the approximate time that this happened is
to remember that George Washington's first birthday was on Washington's
birthday (traditionally a holiday in the U.S.), but his last birthday was not
on Washington's birthday.

Anorher intrigue in this is that when the British corrected the calandar they
only had to correct by ten days. In America, the Johnny Come Latelies had to
correct by eleven. Now why is that? Put that into your internal format and see
what speed you can extract!

So historical time needs a locale attribute when you go back far enough. Do the
COBOL standard functions compensate for this? We may need to delay the next
standard until we get this determined.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long)

- **From:** wb3iqe@rocketmail.com
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85vriu$ogm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca> <20000117114103.28428.00000487@ng-fl1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20000117114103.28428.00000487@ng-fl1.aol.com>,
  rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk) wrote:
<snip>
> Anorher intrigue in this is that when the British corrected the
calandar they
> only had to correct by ten days. In America, the Johnny Come Latelies
had to
> correct by eleven. Now why is that?
<snip>

Ahem.

The 10 day correction was done in 1535 when Catholic countries adopted
the new Gregorian calendar. However, Henry VIII was at odds with Rome
and so England did not adopt the new calendar. England held out until
1750-something.

By that time, the Julian and Gregorian calendars had gotten an
additional day out of whack, hence the 11 day day adjustment all over
the Empire.

Sweet Old Bob


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
wb3iqe@rocketmail.com wrote:
> 
> By that time, the Julian and Gregorian calendars had gotten an
> additional day out of whack, hence the 11 day day adjustment all over
> the Empire.
> 

And in the IBM Mainframe world the LE Lilian date is 18 years away from
the COBOL Julian date (I think I have those names correct).  But since
this date varies from application to application (spreadsheets), I never
save dates with either.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long)

- **From:** Karl Kiesel <karl.kiesel@mch6.siemens.de>
- **Date:** 2000-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38882644.9A201F66@mch6.siemens.de>`
- **References:** `<38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca> <20000117114103.28428.00000487@ng-fl1.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk schrieb:
> I am curious about "... calculating COBOL day numbers (day 1 = January 1,
> 1601) for all days in a year range..." and about  the FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE.

> So historical time needs a locale attribute when you go back far enough. Do the
> COBOL standard functions compensate for this? 

FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE should have no problem, since both the function
addendum and the latest standard drafts expicitely refer to the
Gregorian calendar:
'The returned value is an integer that is the number of days the date
represented by argument-1 succeeds December 31, 1600 in the Gregorian
calendar' 
As far as I know, ther have been NO date corrections in the Gregorian
claendar after the mentioned date.

regards K Kiesel
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Zellers Congruence slow (long)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86v1me$4kh$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<38827592.D39F9F49@istar.ca> <20000117114103.28428.00000487@ng-fl1.aol.com>`

```
Interestingly enough, there was an explicit ANSI Interpretation Request on
this.  (I know, because I asked it <G>).  The answer was that although we are
an "ANSI Standard" - so you *might* think that it would be based on the dates
in what was eventually to become the US - the Standard "assumes" (requires)
that you treat all dates "as if" the Gregorian correction(s) were already
applied in the local area.  (In the US, this it is pretty "obscure" to have
to deal with dates in the 1600's - in computer programs.  However, it isn't
all that unusual in Russia - for example - to deal with "pre-revolutions"
birthrates and they didn't adopt the new calendar until "the revolution".)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PROCEDURE-POINTER: What problem was this trying to solve?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000117113052.18331.00000380@ng-fx1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<#gVRL8LY$GA.193@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
The main application for PROCEDURE-POINTER in COBOL is to support Language
Environment (LE) condition handlers.

Subsequently, you can also use it, as some have suggested, to simply hold the
address of a routine to invoke (perhaps because you need to pass this as a
parameter). You can also use a procedure-pointer as the object of a CALL
statement, so that you could be passed a parameter containing the address of a
subroutine to invoke. This means the routine you are calling doesn't even need
to be a CSECT or ENTRY point, if you are very careful.

If you are interested in more information about LE, check out our website
(http://www.trainersfriend.com), or even call us in for a class.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```

In the past, I have found people writing programs based on their need to
include a buzz word on their resume.
I hope this doen't happen with the PROCEDURE-POINTER.

I work as a consultant, I have to address the abilities of MOST of the
employees at a client site. Knowledge of the LE environment is generally
poor. This will improve in the future. Now is not the time to get fancy.

DennisHarley wrote:
>This sound like the rebirth of the ALTER Statement.

No, this is much, much worse.  GO TO's could only be ALTERed to legal
paragraph names.  Pointers can point to *anything*.

handle LE exceptions, and then pretty much decided that it is something
I hope never to see in my work environment.

- **Date:** 2000-01-25T00:00:00

>
>"Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
As well as what has been said already.

It allows you to get at MVS control blocks from COBOL without haveing to go an 
assembler routine.  You may not write the subroutine, but this gives
programmers a higher level language to use for such functions.  

Finding things like the name of the JOB or step name that you are running 
under, or if DD name xyz is present without having to do an OPEN. Or changing 
the DD dame that is pointeded to by a select.  Many other such things.  

It really is a good thing to have around.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PROCEDURE-POINTER: What problem was this trying to solve?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<388F03A0.F436E3FA@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3881f7a6_4@news1.prserv.net> <85t2fv$rfk$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <388257bc_3@news1.prserv.net> <Ppqj4.711$Wy5.22776@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Standard pointers give access to the control blocks - USAGE POINTER,
not USAGE PROCEDURE POINTER.  Usage procedure pointer is to give a
branch entry point for executing code.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc. 
cfmtech@istar.ca

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PROCEDURE-POINTER: What problem was this trying to solve?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nmsj4.1830$K26.32943@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3881f7a6_4@news1.prserv.net> <85t2fv$rfk$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <388257bc_3@news1.prserv.net> <Ppqj4.711$Wy5.22776@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Ken Leidner wrote in message ...
>>If I'm on OS/390 what does this [PROCEDURE-POINTER] buy me ?
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>It allows you to get at MVS control blocks from COBOL without haveing to go
an
>assembler routine.  You may not write the subroutine, but this gives
>programmers a higher level language to use for such functions.
>
>Finding things like the name of the JOB or step name that you are running
>under, or if DD name xyz is present without having to do an OPEN. Or
changing


You don't need PROCEDURE-POINTER for that; you need POINTER only.

In COBOL/MVS, PROCEDURE-POINTER is kind of like a sixth finger. Yeah, you
might find a way to use it every couple of years, but there are better ways.
(The only *real obvious* application for PROCEDURE-POINTER on OS/390 is to
replace ALTER. 'nuff said?)

Under Windows, though, it is vital for subclassing and/or certain system
functions (e.g., the Enumxxx functions)

MCM
```

>changing a DD ....
>

In 15 years of doing migrations and interfaces, I have always hated running
into "solutions" like those!  They almost always do not work with the new
release; they are often not intuitively obvious in function; and I can not
recall a single instance where the comments were any help.  Up until now, I've
been able to figure out what is going on, and accomplish the business task with
other tools and techniques, but I dread running into the one created by a
programmer much smarter than I am!

The most perceptive mentor I've ever had in this business once wrote me:
"Great coders strive, and often succeed, in producing elegant solutions that
amaze and mystify the rest of us.  Great programmers do not need to!"  





Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PROCEDURE-POINTER: What problem was this trying to solve?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth12.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38906712.75AB4836@bellsouth12.net>`
- **References:** `<nmsj4.1830$K26.32943@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <20000126010916.20486.00000163@ng-fh1.aol.com>`

```
Steve Newton wrote:

> >Finding things like the name of the JOB or step name that you are running
> >>under, or if DD name xyz is present without having to do an OPEN. Or
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> amaze and mystify the rest of us.  Great programmers do not need to!"

Barely three hours after reading this, I got a call from a former employer about a
program I wrote over 20 years ago.  At that time, we were charged by the
byte-second and there was a lot of emphasis on writing tight code.  In a program to
summarize employees by job type, salary range, sex, and race; I had to work around
the fact four dimensional tables were not allowed then and calculated the
displacement "by hand" so I could use a one dimensional table.  He called because
he now had six categories of race instead of two.  I told him how my formula worked
but said a rewrite is probably in order.

I think I called it "gtcode.txt" on my website.  I also have a record layout
program with source, dos, & unix executables on there as well.

http://personal.lig.bellsouth.net/~wbport/programmer
```

#### ↳ Re: PROCEDURE-POINTER: What problem was this trying to solve?

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8618h9$p86$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3881f7a6_4@news1.prserv.net>`

```
In article <3881f7a6_4@news1.prserv.net>,
  "Randy Holcomb" <randy_holcomb@attglobal.ten> wrote:
> Someone thought it was useful and/or it solved a problem: I'm trying
> to figure out what those were.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I know of one problem that AFAIK can only solved with PROCEDURE-POINTER:
Call a DLL from a non-DLL program. There is a sample in chapter '4.2.2.1
CALLs to DLLs from non-DLLs' in the 'Programming Guide' of the current
'IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM'. The non-DLL COBOL program (99.999% of all
COBOLs I guess...) calls a C which loads the DLL (written in C, C++,
PL/I or COBOL) and returns a pointer to the entry within the DLL. The
COBOL program then does:

     SET DLL-PROGRAM-PTR TO DLL-PROGRAM-HANDLE
*
*    Call the program in the DLL.
     CALL DLL-PROGRAM-PTR

Sounds very exotic, but I am afraid it isn't. The new 'VA PL/I for
OS/390 & VM' generates DLLs by default (cannot be changed), the C/C++
and the COBOL compiler can generate DLL if you set 'DLL' as a parm. At
least the C programmers will use it certainly.

So we will soon have to migrate... with PROCEDURE-POINTER...
```

<a id="t-d713536819"></a>
## Thread: rmcobol 7.00.02 Error 47,02 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### rmcobol 7.00.02 Error 47,02

- **From:** mthompson7686@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85stfc$lre$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have almost completed a unix to Windows NT4.0
conversion of our cobol programs.  The background
is the old version is RMCobol85 on the unix box
and RMCobol7.00.02 on a new NT box.  Most of the
programs with some editing have transferred
well.  However, there are several programs that
display error 47,02 (which is a read attempted on
an unopened file).  I have tried many things to
no avail.  I set the nt box to audit file and
object access. When I run this program, NT
reports success on access.  Is it possible that
somehow the index is invalid under NT and how do
I recover it.  I tried using a similar file with
no luck.  Thanks in advance for your Help,
Michael Thompson.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: rmcobol 7.00.02 Error 47,02

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lvrg4.2860$hV4.64972@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<85stfc$lre$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Did you check the file status on the OPEN? If the OPEN failed, you'd get
that error when you tried to read it or write the file.

I see this a fair amount of the time - the programmer uses the same FILE
STATUS item for all the files (which is fine), but then has something like
this:

PERFORM OPEN-THE-FILES


OPEN-THE-FILES.

  OPEN INPUT  File1  File2
             I-O       File3 File4
            OUTPUT File5.

If you get an open failure on File2, the status which can point to the
problem is gone as soon as the OPEN is attempted on File3.
(You get this with "virgin" VSAM files on IBM mainframes real easily -
whenever you re-define the VSAM file, it cannot be opened I-O until it
contains a record or has been opened output).

Better to use something like...

PERFORM OPEN-THE-FILES
  Varying File-sub from +1 by +1 until File-Sub GREATER Num-Of-Files.



OPEN-THE FILES.
  EVALUATE File-Sub
    WHEN 1
      OPEN INPUT File1
    WHEN 2
      OPEN INPUT File2
....
END-EVALUATE

   IF WS-FILE-STATUS NOT EQUAL '00' THEN
     whatever
   .....

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: stack dump help

- **From:** Michiel Erens <I.dont.want.spam@my.address.in.NL>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3881F51C.3451@my.address.in.NL>`
- **References:** `<n0ag4.5664$J9.3313@news.indigo.ie>`

```
p.h wrote:
> 
> my cobol program is now error free but i can't run it.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> kristi3001@yahoo.com


I see you run Cobol on VMS 

Invalid decimal data (S0C7 in the MVS world) means that you have
characters or spaces
in a numeric field. 

Check your program for unitialized fields or moves of spaces to a pic 9
field.
Otherwise compile, link and run with the /DEBUG option. 
```

<a id="t-90d51a587c"></a>
## Thread: Learning project management skills — 1 this month / 5 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Learning project management skills

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OlHPv$DY$GA.265@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<43318s4lljgbvitvmgovi5if2vj0m2gqtp@4ax.com>`

```

Bearhair <bearhair@enteract.com> wrote in message
news:43318s4lljgbvitvmgovi5if2vj0m2gqtp@4ax.com...
>    My employer is transitioning me from being a coder to a project
"manager",
> sometimes with direct contact with coders, other times with a technical
> "manager" in between us. My responsibilities will include managing the
processes
> of defining user requirements, writing functional specs, writing technical
> specs, coding, testing, and implementation.
>    Do you have any recommendations on educational materials that I might
find
> useful in developing my skills? Books, web sites, personal anecdotes?
>    Thanks much.
>

I would recommend reading Structured Analysis and System Specification by
Tom DeMarco.
You may find a copy at your local library.

You don't have to follow the book rigorously, but you may be able to adapt
some of the conceps to your situation.

Some Genral rules.

Hold regular meeting with ALL participants present. Do not work exclusively
thru a chain of command.
If the project falls behind schedule, you may need to cut resources. You
will have to know who the key players are.

Insist on System Design and Test Plan walk-throughs. Present the method in
one meeting, briefly discuss it, have a follow-up meeting after people have
had time to consider other options.

State your Goals and Objectives in writing.

GOAL 1: The system must be 100% accurate.
GOAL 2: The system should be easily maintainable.
GOAL 3: The system should be reasonably efficient.

Do not allow pepple to be distracted by side issues. i.e. Addressing
performance issues where no evidence of performance degradation is present.
If a Batch JOB runs in 3 minutes, don't waste time trying to get it to run
in 2 Minutes and 50 seconds.

Stay focused on your goals.

Avoid huge monolithic programs. The make maintence more difficult, and you
are stuck with 1 person doing all the coding. Modular programming can speed
up the development process.

Automate testing processes where you can. The machine does a more accurate
JOB.

Public praise, Private criticism.
```

---

- **From:** "keldin" <keldin_despam@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3882151a_2@news1.prserv.net>`

```
www.brainbench.com  does among others including CICS and the first test is
free so if you only do COBOL II its free.

This used to be techmetrics corp but they recently changed thier name to
brainbench since they are doing project management certification and not
just computer certs now.

Have fun
TJ

(below is copied from the cobol II test page)


The following outline describes the content of the COBOL IIProgrammer
Certification.  To qualify as a Certified COBOL II Programmer, you must pass
the examination with a score of 2.75 or higher.  To be certified as a Master
COBOL II Programmer, you must obtain a score of 4.00 or higher.

COBOL II Examination Content Outline

Topic
 Subtopic

Arithmetic Statements
 Add, Subtract, Multiply, Divide, Compute, Rounded

COBOL Elements
 Basic Syntax (Comments, Areas)

COBOL Elements
 Division Formats

Condition Checking
 Switches

Condition Checking
 Validating Data; Operators

Creating Reports
 Control Breaks & Totals

Creating Reports
 Headers & Footers

Creating Reports
 Page Break

Creating Reports
 Report Layout

Data Division
 Call, Linkage Section

Data Division
 Copy, Copy/Replace

Data Division
 File Section

Data Division
 Table Definition, indexing, occurs, etc.

Data Division
 Working Storage / Data Types & Definitions

Data Division
 Working Storage / Features

Decision Making
 Evaluate

Decision Making
 IF Statements

File Processing
 I/O Statements

File Processing
 Sort, Merge, Update Master File

Process Statements
 Moves, Initialize, Set, String

Process Statements
 Performs

Program Processing
 Design; Debugging; Goback

Tables
 Matching & Searching (include multiple levels)


All Brainbench Certification examinations are delivered through the web and
use computer adaptive testing (CAT), in which questions are selected based
on previous answers.  Additionally, questions are selected from large
question banks, so that it is not productive to memorize questions.

The expected time to complete the COBOL II Certification examination is 60
minutes.  In addition to registration with Brainbench, all certified
professionals receive a certificate which is suitable for framing.

#### ↳ Re: Newbie: How to clear the Screen?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ryRg4.693$%_6.8445@nnrp2.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<38838CBC.A086B1C7@wi.stud.tu-ilmenau.de>`

```
01  BLANK80    PIC X(80) VALUE SPACE.
01  MYLINE      PIC 99.


PERFORM VARYING MYLINE FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL MYLINE > 25
    DISPLAY(1:80) MYLINE
   END-PERFORM.

Lars Fischer <Lars.Fischer@wi.stud.tu-ilmenau.de> wrote in message
news:38838CBC.A086B1C7@wi.stud.tu-ilmenau.de...

>
