screen
> has to be be clear.
> I would not use the Scree Section!
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> PS. Dont laugh about my English - it's sad

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie: How to clear the Screen?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net

>
> Um, Jerry, that dog don't hunt..er, code don't work.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Of course. I was thinking of DISPLAY(MYLINE 1) BLANK90

For me, I like to use a random number generator to create row and
column numbers and blank a single byte at a time. Another trick is to
drop the random byte to the last line of the screen while blanking its
original position. Let the characters stack up in a pile. Make a
'whoop' noise for every one. Another trick is to display a box in the
center of the monitor reading 'Standby, encrypting screen,' as the
screen is blanked character-by-character.
Or do half the screen, display 'Standby, program resting' for a second
or two and continue.

There is no end to the fun one can have with just a simple screen
clear. Strange thing is, we put the following message in our program
update set:

"The installation program has done everything
it can think of to your system and grown bored."

"Press [Enter] to return to the operating system."

Not a single one of our users has commented on the message in two
years.





> But wouldn't it be easier to use...
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

Probably not for the original poster (newbie).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Newbie: How to clear the Screen?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2000-01-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000125022106.07131.00000175@ng-ck1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<BGjh4.909$72.11037@nnrp2.rcsntx.swbell.net>`

```
Concerning the evolved code for screen clearing

>
>  PERFORM VARYING MYLINE FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL MYLINE > 40
>     DISPLAY BLANK90
>   END-PERFORM.

Is there not one small (admittedly rare) issue here.  On 24 line screens, is
the screen not really twenty-five lines, in true CGA emulation? On 4x line
screens, is the screen not really forty-three lines, in true EGA emulation?

(I note one poster indicated NT, but what graphics mode should we presume).

The point is if the compiler run-time is well behaved and goes thru high level
DOS rather than BIOS, looping forever will not clear the last line!

This kind of thing was built into the graphics device to keep PFK annotations
present on the screen even while the command line interface scrolled (or user
displays scrolled). I am fairly confident that it survived into EGA.

I know that the modern VGA and SVGA (and even the Hercules stuff) emulated this
behavior. 

So, ... even though it is not especially common, someone could be executing in
an emulation mode and get either text onto the 25th (43rd) line or even
graphics artifacts; scrolling does not clear it.

((Also as an aside, sometimes when folks say that they want to clear a screen,
they may mean that they want to set up a different background color as well:
Unless you do the full job with specific functions, the last line can be left
in a different background color)).

So the easy routines may not solve all problems. You really need a clear screen
function to be sure. Well behaved programs (including well behaved compilers)
did have a DOS interface that could do that, atleast available theoretically;
that does not mean that there was any convenient syntax in the COBOL language
the compiler provided. There was a special code that you sent as text (it was
an ascii control character, perhaps Form Feed, ascii 12).  In DOS you might
have needed ANSI.SYS loaded as a device driver from CONFIG.SYS processing.

The PC hardware has 25 lines and 43 lines available in CGA and EGA emulation
modes, so even in non-DOS environments universality may require either a higher
max on the loops, or a special write to line(25) or line(43). (BASICs have line
positioning. Infact there was early weakness in the hardware that allowed a
BASIC program to position on line 25, and write excessive text and get it to
scroll up! That weakness depended upon ROM unfortunately, so behavior could
vary - that was a test of compatibility in some suites).

I am not familiar enough with VGA to know if any of this legacy was spec'ed in.
 I vaguely recall that you could do split screens in some VGA 'text' modes, so
scrolling was even more involved, screen clearing was too. 

(It might be hard to get into these VGA modes unintentionally in COBOL. But the
EGA and CGA last line behavior is built in).

In all cases it would be better to actually find a function that cleared the
screen, especially if any graphics presentation is interleaved with the text
presentation. 

Best Wishes,


Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

## Thread: Sorting with cobol — 26 this month / 27 total

### Sorting with cobol

- **From:** Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Greetings All

I am about to go out on a Cobol consulting assignment.  I will be ina
IBM mainframe environment.

When I went through our company training program, we used JCL sorting to
perform any required sorts.  Is this typical for most/all mainframe
shops or is it a mixed bag?

Seems to me the sort facility is built into cobol for platforms w/o such
handy utilities that are on a typical mainframe system.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uW99bmSY$GA.146@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
You will run into both.
Stand alone (JCL) sorting is generally done on new development. (faster
development, more flexibility)
Existing systems may use INTERNAL SORTs, depending on when the were written,
and the preference of the programmer writing the program.

Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Greetings All
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<388388FA.1DB16079@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Mark Framness wrote:
> 
> Greetings All
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> handy utilities that are on a typical mainframe system.

That would be the case if you could only sort a file in Cobol. But it is
possible to use input procedures and output procedures, and that makes
the sorting procedure in Cobol far more flexible. 

As an example you can write a input procedure that checks if field-a
contains X, field-b = 100 to 200, and field-c at the same time does not
contain Z. Only if these conditions are met, field-d is multiplied by 4,
and the record is released to sort.
 
You can't do something like this in a 'normal' Operating System sort
procedure, other than by means of temporary files. 

> 
> --
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** "keldin" <keldin_despam@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <388388FA.1DB16079@home.nl>`

```
IBM DFsort and SyncSort have come a long way.  I have have never used/seen
used the COBOL internal sort other than in a COBOL class.  Everything is
done using JCL either before or after the program.  Besides COBOL call's the
OS to sort its not really done in COBOL.   ICE is very useful utility of
dfsort w it you can sort on complex criteria like stated here.
Maybe other company's use internal sorts but I have yet to see it.

TJ

I don't know if there are any shops that encourage sorting from within 
COBOL.  My shop's style guide recomends against it just for the sake of 
simplified maintenence.  A COBOL step without a sort will be easier to 
code and easier to test.

Pete

#### ↳ SV: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** "Bjorn Alvik" <bjorn.alvik@asp.no>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8zVg4.6284$in5.104045@news1.online.no>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi, Mark.

In-line COBOL SORT is just great, and I see very few reasons why not to use
it.
COBOL SORT, however, never will remove the need for JCL utility sort, like
the JCL sort never will make COBOL SORT redundant. They both are
tools/components of great value to simplify system implementation.
When using COBOL SORT, I certainly will recommend the
INPUT-PROCEDURE/OUPUT-PROCEDURE option, in order to gain a nice sructure and
flexibility.

The reason why you don't find it in many IBM shops is pure historical: In
earlier days  COBOL didn't support the sort, and when it came along, there
could be tremendous overhead. Before that (in the punched-card era)  it was
natural to hand card decks over to the 084 sorting machine before program
processing on the 1401 and early IBM/360 systems. Then came JCL, and with it
the sort utilities. And by tradition, the "file-sorting-by-utility"-paradigm
survived.

The main reason why many IBM-shops do not utilise COBOL SORT, is lack of
competence.
That is, they never got a programmer to demonstrate that it works excellent,
because programmers also may  to stick to the "old and safe".

Today there is no overhead using COBOL SORT. It is exactly the same thing as
Utility Sort.
You should, however, consider the SELECT statement for the SD file (this is
the sort work file) by assigning more physical files  if input is big and
presequence is random. This resembles the common  JCL practice to establish
different predefined sort procedures in the JCL procedure library for
different file number and sizes.

The statement "more difficult system maintenance with COBOL SORT" is
bullshit: Any time the sort sequence should be altered, the corresponding
COBOL program also should be altered, and any time file structure alters,
both COBOL and any utility should be altered.
So, when getting down to maintenance, it's obvious that maintaining one
entity occurrence in one language is more easy than
maintaining both the COBOL and the JCL occurencies.

After having said this, though, I must comment on the advantages using
JCL-sort: If you study the DFSORT manual, you will find really easy ways to
handle tasks like inclusion/omit/reformatting of records  before/after
releasing to sort, removal of duplicates, totaling etc. In fact, the utility
could be utilized much more extensively than we normally see, in order to
eliminate many of the numerous  trivial and more or less "redundant"
"read-add-report" COBOL programs that float around.

Good luck
Bjorn



So, what is
 systems./  care  reason a
Mark Framness <framnesso@my-deja.com> skrev i
meldingsnyheter:85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Greetings All
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

How do you specify the extra sort SMS classes in the program DD's? vs the
external sort.  I have never used the internal sort for more than trivial
class projects. It easy using the external sort just add more lines like

'//sortwk01 dd dataclas=sortl
'//sortwk02 dd dataclas=sortl
'//sortwk03 dd dataclas=sortl
'//sortwk04 dd dataclas=sortl
'//sortwk05 dd dataclas=sortl
'//sortwk06 dd dataclas=sortl
'//sortwk07 dd dataclas=sortl
'//sortwk08 dd dataclas=sortl

Every month I run a job that sorts 68 million records lrecl=610,  Just
wondering how easy it is to add sort work space? (15 for this example using
JCL and DFsort)

TJ


I have never used it for more than trivial class projects.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8662p2018j0@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8zVg4.6284$in5.104045@news1.online.no> <3885319a_1@news1.prserv.net>`

```
You code:
    SELECT SORT-FILE       ASSIGN TO SORTWK1.

Then, in the JCL:

//sortwk1  dd dataclas=sortl
//         dd dataclas=sortl
//         dd dataclas=sortl
//         dd dataclas=sortl
//         dd dataclas=sortl
//         dd dataclas=sortl
//         dd dataclas=sortl
//         dd dataclas=sortl

This works with SyncSort and OS/390.

Jeff

```
I never have got deep into analyzing the decision factors for choosing
either JCL- or inline COBOL SORT. So may be this is the time to give the
topic some intuitive thoughts.
(Before I proceed, let me just remind you that the SORT discussion also
applies to the  MERGE facilities available both as JCL utility and inline
COBOL).

One big difference is intepreting vs compilation. When using COBOL, you have
to code all the nessecary COBOL code and compile in addition to JCL
preparation. Utility sort inteprets the sort statements, and is ready to
use. Therefore, never use COBOL unless there is more to the function than
just sorting.

File and data shuffling: Very often you will see jobs with a huge mix of
utility sorts and very tiny  COBOL programs (performing record reformatting,
some kind of input filtering, simple addition, building sum files for
different control levels of the input sequence etc) These jobs, or parts of
them, often can be replaced by a few (maybe only one) utility sort step. So,
if the main function for a job is to produce a reformatted/condensed/summed
file and no tough logic shall be inposed on the data, I certainly would
avoid COBOL (or any programming language)

Function consistency: Breaking main business function down to series of job
steps (programs) can reduce the understanding of what is going on, it can be
unsecure to run and recover, and it can be a burden to maintain. Framing the
function into fewer  programs can enhance maintainance, security and
turn-around.

When (multiple) sequential processing AND complex logic (more than what
utility sort can offer) is involved, it is the time for considering use of
internal sort. There are lots of examples: Preprocessors, compilers
(wouldn't use COBOL, though), production of  cross-reference listings,
assembly and processing of data elements from multiple aind different
pre-sorted registers etc etc.

Table handling: Complex internal table processing can be eliminated by
combining internal Sort and ordinary matching/break-level logic.

.....

.....

and ther certainly is much more to be mentioned

Good luck
Bjorn
Mark A. Framness <mframness@NOSPAM.lakefield.net> skrev i
meldingsnyheter:8Sd1uPikEGru-pn2-wo9r9UBPTX2H@www.framnett.net...
> On Sun, 18 Jan 3900 09:29:41, "Bjorn Alvik" <bjorn.alvik@asp.no> wrote:
>
> > Hi, Mark.
> >
> > In-line COBOL SORT is just great, and I see very few reasons why not to
use
> > it.
> > COBOL SORT, however, never will remove the need for JCL utility sort,
like
> > the JCL sort never will make COBOL SORT redundant. They both are
> > tools/components of great value to simplify system implementation.
> > When using COBOL SORT, I certainly will recommend the
> > INPUT-PROCEDURE/OUPUT-PROCEDURE option, in order to gain a nice sructure
and
> > flexibility.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Brian Framness
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SV: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3885C886.83CF3099@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8zVg4.6284$in5.104045@news1.online.no> <8Sd1uPikEGru-pn2-wo9r9UBPTX2H@www.framnett.net>`

```
"Mark A. Framness" wrote:
> 
> What do factors do you consider when deciding to use a JCL sort vs.
> internal cobol sorts?
>

#1.  Can what I want to be done be done with an external sort?  If not,
then it is internal.

#2.  I find KISS means more small steps.  This means external 90% of the
time.

#3.  Look at your environment - how difficult it is to migrate JCL vs
COBOL, how much testing will be involved, etc.

#4.  Do you have variable needs?  I have seen programs with variable
internal sorts.  On the other hand, I have seen 4 different external
sorts (with extract parameters) feeding the same COBOL program 4
different times for 4 different customers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** "George McGinn" <gmcginn@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ik_g4.109$M2.3207@ha1>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Some real old legacy code (written in the 60's and 70's) used COBOL's
Internal SORT.

If we encounter such code, we always remove it and do a SYNCSORT instead.

One of the main reasons to use it was (WAS) that you wanted to write a
report, sorted differently or to break by the sort key.  SYNCSORT allows you
to code such a report --- without writing a line of COBOL!

Mark Framness wrote:
> 
> Greetings All
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> shops or is it a mixed bag?

Very typical.  Unless you have good reason for a COBOL sort, almost
every shop around prefers doing an external sort.  Use more, simpler
steps and keep maintenance costs low.
 
> Seems to me the sort facility is built into cobol for platforms w/o such
> handy utilities that are on a typical mainframe system.

On a mainframe, the internal COBOL sort IS the external sort, but
obfuscated by being within a relatively complex program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8635p20civ@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38847EA9.99B2FC1B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Just wanted to say that I have never had a problem with an internal sort. 
Alot of the older programs use internal sorts and that says something, as
long as they've been around.  Plus, they use the FASTSRT USING and GIVING
compile option which I have yet to try.  On the contrary, I like less steps
in the JCL.  A sort step shouldn't draw too much attention from the actual
business logic, so put it in the program.  The program will always need the
sort to function properly, so why put it in the JCL?

Most people prefer the external sort.  And it works just fine for many many
jobs.  And internal sorts can cause nasty looking dumps.

Jeff

----------

>>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> obfuscated by being within a relatively complex program.
```

steps

the
> sort to function properly, so why put it in the JCL?

many

One internal sort might not be much of a problem, but where I ran across the
disadvantage was with a COBOL program that had 2 internal sorts in it.  The
output of the first was input to the second, and as I recall (this was over
15 years ago) the intermediate file was unavailable in the event I needed to
see it for debugging purposes.  It only existed for the duration of the
program.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Cmih4.222$xZ3.3414@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38847EA9.99B2FC1B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8635p20civ@enews4.newsguy.com> <863c8q$u10$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`

Jerry Heinze wrote in message
<863c8q$u10$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>...
>
>Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
>news:8635p20civ@enews4.newsguy.com...
>>...... On the contrary, I like less steps
>> in the JCL.  A sort step shouldn't draw too much attention from the
actual
>> business logic, so put it in the program.  The program will always need
>the sort to function properly, so why put it in the JCL?
>
>One internal sort might not be much of a problem, but where I ran across
the
>disadvantage was with a COBOL program that had 2 internal sorts in it.  The
>output of the first was input to the second, and as I recall (this was over
>15 years ago) the intermediate file was unavailable in the event I needed
to
>see it for debugging purposes.  It only existed for the duration of the
>program.


I, too, prefer fewer steps in JCL and like my sorts internal; I also do a
lot of development (versus maintenance) and if I need to change something,
I'd rather change a couple of SDs in my program source code than change FD's
in the program AND the sort control card in the JCL.

As far as the intermediate file being available for inspection - if you
change your sort to the USING/GIVING format and catalog the output dataset,
you got it; however, if I need to see what my sort hath wrought, I usually
just add a dataset write to my development code, as in..

RELEASE-THE-RECORD. (Or RETURN-THE-RECORD).

    WRITE DEBUG-FILE  FROM INPUT-RECORD
    RELEASE SORT-FILE FROM INPUT-RECORD.

  Remove the first line (or control it with a flag) when you create the
production version of the program.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ov3eUpjY$GA.269@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38847EA9.99B2FC1B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8635p20civ@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
The INTERNAL SORT Issue went thru several phases.

At first the machines were too small to use Internal Sorts.
It wasn't unusual to find IBM360s with 64K, for operating system, and
programs.
256K was considered a hefty machine.
You also had to code SORT Exits. If I remember correctly, the INTERNAL SORT
didn't arrive until after the IBM370 came out.

As machines got larger, I/O was a bottle neck. It was more efficient to use
an INTERNAL
SORT to reduce the I/O overhead. The Sort Utility did not support
reformatting (extracting), while the internal sort did. The ability to
eliminate writing Sort Output, and then read it was considered to be a big
plus.

Now we have larger, and faster machines, and a more functional Sort Utility.
I/O is not a problem.
Now the trend is to not use the Internal Sort because it is cheaper to code
Sort Utility statements, than it is to code the equivalent Internal Sort.

Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:8635p20civ@enews4.newsguy.com...
> Just wanted to say that I have never had a problem with an internal sort.
> Alot of the older programs use internal sorts and that says something, as
> long as they've been around.  Plus, they use the FASTSRT USING and GIVING
> compile option which I have yet to try.  On the contrary, I like less
steps
> in the JCL.  A sort step shouldn't draw too much attention from the actual
> business logic, so put it in the program.  The program will always need
the
> sort to function properly, so why put it in the JCL?
>
> Most people prefer the external sort.  And it works just fine for many
many
> jobs.  And internal sorts can cause nasty looking dumps.
>
> Jeff

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38860D14.7E9272F4@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38847EA9.99B2FC1B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8635p20civ@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> Just wanted to say that I have never had a problem with an internal sort.

They are pretty easy, and powerful.  Problems I have seen are similar to
problems with programs in general, such as dropping through to the input
procedure after the sort is finished.  Good structured code helps
considerably.  I did have a conversion from small Burroughs to large
Burroughs where it failed because it needed a minimum record size once.  

> Alot of the older programs use internal sorts and that says something, as
> long as they've been around.  Plus, they use the FASTSRT USING and GIVING
> compile option which I have yet to try.  On the contrary, I like less steps
> in the JCL.  A sort step shouldn't draw too much attention from the actual
> business logic, so put it in the program.  

A sort shouldn't draw too much attention from the actual business logic,
so remove it from the program.  That's where the complexities can be
found - simplify the program.  And when there's an abort, it will be in
the step with the problem, making maintenance easier.  The only downside
I see in external sorts (when the external sort will do) is that the
fields are not in the data dictionary or copy libraries.  I do document
the field names in the JCL though.

JCL is simple and straight forward, splitting a job into more steps is
easier to work with than making the most complicated item (the program)
more complicated.  You may prefer fewer steps, but I prefer less complex
steps, knowing that the people maintaining the program may be
inexperienced.

> The program will always need the
> sort to function properly, so why put it in the JCL?

The program will always need the JCL to function properly, so why not? 
I have also had multiple programs in a job stream using the same sorted
input file.  Why sort it multiple times?
 
> Most people prefer the external sort.  And it works just fine for many many
> jobs.  And internal sorts can cause nasty looking dumps.

Internal sorts are sometimes necessary, but I am a big believer in the
KISS principle.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38863C6B.84416A0F@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38847EA9.99B2FC1B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8635p20civ@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
I'm not familiar with the IBM environment, but I am familiar with VMS.
There we have a very good sort/merge utility, and instead of JCL we have
DCL, so no big differences in general. Cobol in VMS uses the same sort
kernel as the external sort, so the actual sorting process is no
different.

I will use a external sort if it is more convenient.
I will always use a internal sort if it prevents me building a - Cobol
program > Sort > Cobol program - sequence in one job, or if it prevents
me from making huge intermediate files. Sort has a input file, scratch
files and a output file. If the output file has to be processed again
with a Cobol program, you will save time and disk space if you use a
internal sort.
And of course I will always use a internal sort if the data has to be
processed in input and/or output procedures.

I really can't understand the argument that it would be complicated to
use a internal sort procedure. How difficult can it be to add some SD
descriptions, and a sort phrase of a few lines ? If a Cobol programmer
finds that difficult, than I wonder what kind of programming he is used
to. The debugging argument could be valid, although I doubt it because
you don't debug the actual sorting, but only input and output
procedures.   


Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> Just wanted to say that I have never had a problem with an internal sort.
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> > obfuscated by being within a relatively complex program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "keldin" <keldin_despam@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<388672cb_4@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38847EA9.99B2FC1B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8635p20civ@enews4.newsguy.com> <38863C6B.84416A0F@home.nl>`

```
Still,

There are many times where the files are just too big to sort in one step,
and not possible to do internal (not that I would use internal even if the
file was small enough).

The Cardmember master file is around 70 million records and is soon to get
36 million more records with a recent aquistion's accounts coming online.
lrecl=4000 vb (or 4004 since the first 4 bytes are the actual record length)
we have to sort visa, gold visa, master card, gold mastercard, and choice
seperately and then merge the sorted outputs together with DD's to input
them to the program.  Another benifit is several different jobs can
reference the same dataset without resorting for each job much more
effiecent use of resoures. (I usually extract what I need using inrec and
outrec so the sorts are smaller)


"Dirk Munk" <munk@home.nl> wrote in message
news:38863C6B.84416A0F@home.nl...
> I'm not familiar with the IBM environment, but I am familiar with VMS.
> There we have a very good sort/merge utility, and instead of JCL we have
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> procedures.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#bD3re3Y$GA.241@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38847EA9.99B2FC1B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8635p20civ@enews4.newsguy.com> <38863C6B.84416A0F@home.nl>`

```

Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl> wrote in message news:38863C6B.84416A0F@home.nl...
>
> I will use a external sort if it is more convenient.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> processed in input and/or output procedures.

There are a variety of factors that come into play when selecting a method
to use.

One is the size of the system. A method which is appropriate for 5,000
transactions may not be the solution I need when processing 100,000,000
transactions (This is real world).

Another is: could I possibly need historical information for problem
resolution, and system corrrection.
If I am working with financial data, I always want to have a fail-safe plan.

If I am working with a data source which is updated in place. I will keep a
copy of the extract.
Extract, Sort, Process are common to most systems I have seen.

> I really can't understand the argument that it would be complicated to
> use a internal sort procedure. How difficult can it be to add some SD
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I am used to working on programs with more that 5 input files, and up to 20
output files.
These programs are generally disigned to pass a master file once, and call
programs to process different transaction types.

I work on systems not programs.

If I have extra storage, I would rather use it for I/O buffers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting with cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12f00af4e326452b98969f@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<85vpf9$mq7$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38847EA9.99B2FC1B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <8635p20civ@enews4.newsguy.com> <38863C6B.84416A0F@home.nl>`

```
I work in a shop that uses external sorts %99 of the time.  We never 
argue that it is "...complicated to use a internal sort procedure."  

We believe that our programmers can code, test, and move to production 
their COBOL code faster if it does not have an internal sort.  It boils 
down to productivity.  

I'm sure some of you will want to respond that "I can code it internal 
faster than I can code it external", but you have to understand that shop 
standards are designed to fit "average" programmers, and you are better 
than average.

And there are always alternatives.  For instance, my shop uses CA-
EasytrievePlus for all our batch reporting needs.  It automates the 
internal-sorting of each report with a single statement.  It is a very 
powerful reporting language coded at a very high-level.

KISS = productivity

Pete



In article <38863C6B.84416A0F@home.nl>, munk@home.nl says...
> I'm not familiar with the IBM environment, but I am familiar with VMS.
> There we have a very good sort/merge utility, and instead of JCL we have
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >> IBM mainframe environment.
```

COBOL internal sorts which I see seldom now, where you have to use "SECTIONS"
with the sort/using and giving.  There are newer simpler internal sorts.  You
really need to get a newer COBOL book for your self.

## Thread: Help with another structure — 5 msgs

### Help with another structure

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0gMg4.113$bb4.315597@news1.i1.net>`

```
I'm trying to write this program that will get the local ip address of a
machine.  I'm able to get the local computer name but I'm stuck on how to
setup a particular structure.  It looks like this in the SDK:

API:
int getsockname (
  SOCKET s,
  struct sockaddr FAR*  name,
  int FAR*  namelen
);
 struct sockaddr_in {
        short   sin_family;
        u_short sin_port;
        struct  in_addr sin_addr;
        char    sin_zero[8];


This is how I have it.
working storage

       01  in-addr1 typedef.
        02 s-un.
         03  s-un.
          04 s-un-b.
           05  s-b1           uns-char.
           05  s-b2           uns-char.
           05  s-b3           uns-char.
           05  s-b4           uns-char.
         03  filler redefines s-un.
          04 s-un-w.
           05  s-w1           uns-short.
           05  s-w2           uns-short.
         03  filler redefines s-un.
          04 s-addr           uns-long.

       01  sockaddr-in.
           03 sin-family                   short.
           03 sin-port                     uns-short.
           03 sin-addr                     in-addr1.
           03 sin-zero                     pic x(8).
       01  sockaddr-in-length      usage int.

procedure division
           CALL "cob32api".
           SET MprPtr TO ENTRY "Wsock32".

           CALL WINAPI "WSAStartup" USING
                BY VALUE h"0101" SIZE 4
                BY REFERENCE WSADATA
                RETURNING STATUSCODE
           END-CALL.
           CALL WINAPI "gethostname" USING
                BY REFERENCE COMPUTERNAME
                BY value buffersizeconst
                RETURNING NETRETCODE.

        ETC........

           MOVE INVALID-SOCKET TO LISTENSOCKET.
           CALL WINAPI "socket" USING
                BY VALUE AF-INET SIZE 4
                BY VALUE SOCK-STREAM SIZE 4
                BY VALUE 0 SIZE 4
                RETURNING LISTENSOCKET
           END-CALL.

           MOVE LENGTH OF SOCKADDR-IN TO SOCKADDR-IN-LENGTH.

           CALL WINAPI "getsockname" USING
                BY VALUE LISTENSOCKET
                BY REFERENCE SOCKADDR-IN
                BY VALUE SOCKADDR-IN-LENGTH
                RETURNING NETRETCODE.

When I step thru the getsockname API I get a netretcode of failed.
Any help would be appreciated.

Tom Wright
twright@larimore.net

#### ↳ Re: Help with another structure

- **From:** G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8gh88so90o8vn5hqsicgdhr8c52ghchncb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<0gMg4.113$bb4.315597@news1.i1.net>`

```
"Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net> wrote:

>I'm trying to write this program that will get the local ip address of a
>machine.  I'm able to get the local computer name but I'm stuck on how to
>setup a particular structure.  It looks like this in the SDK:

>API:
>int getsockname (
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>);

> struct sockaddr_in {
>        short   sin_family;
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>This is how I have it.

*this needs a serious rewrite

working storage section.

01  sockaddr-in.
	03 sin-family                   pic 9999 comp-5.
	03 sin-port                     pic 9999 comp-5.
*4 9s=2 bytes, 5 to 9=4 bytes,10 to 18=8 bytes

*signeds and unsigneds don't matter until a value is moved to them.

	03 sin-addr                     in-addr1.
*this cannot be done. you need to tell us the struct of in-addr1



	03 sin-zero                     pic x(8).

*this is another thing which doesn't translate well. pic x(8) is an
*set of 8 characters in a row, not a pointer to eight characters
*you could try instead the following lines:

	03 sin-zero		usage pointer.
	03 sin-zero-data	pic x(8).

*and add the lines below in the procedure division, if the pointer
*types match


01  sockaddr-in-length      pic 9999 comp-5.


>procedure division
>           CALL "cob32api".
>           SET MprPtr TO ENTRY "Wsock32".

set sin-zero to address of sin-zero-data

>           CALL WINAPI "WSAStartup" USING
>                BY VALUE h"0101" SIZE 4
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>           END-CALL.


>           MOVE LENGTH OF SOCKADDR-IN TO SOCKADDR-IN-LENGTH.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>                RETURNING NETRETCODE.

>When I step thru the getsockname API I get a netretcode of failed.
>Any help would be appreciated.

while you step through, have you been checking the other variables to
ascertain they match when you step through your c code?

reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

<a id="t-8557d0e193"></a>
## Thread: HOW: Learning project management skills — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: HOW: Learning project management skills

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 2000-01-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EAB502EA5FFE702E.BD23936EB27202E7.D021CAE705D39271@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<43318s4lljgbvitvmgovi5if2vj0m2gqtp@4ax.com>`

```
On Sat, 15 Jan 2000 09:10:02 -0600, Bearhair <bearhair@enteract.com>
enlightened us:

>   My employer is transitioning me from being a coder to a project "manager",
>sometimes with direct contact with coders, other times with a technical
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Thanks much.

I feel for you as I've done exactly what you are about to do.  I've
since gone back to programming!

But here a few things I can pass on:

1)  Read a book by Neal Whitten called "Managing Software Development
Projects".  You can learn many good things from this book that will
help you throughout the life of your project;

2)  Lear how to manage by "deliverables".  For example, a programmer
has a program to write that has been estimated to take 16 hours.
After 8 hours the programmer says he's 50% done, or on schedule.  But
what do you have to show the 50% done and the 50% incomplete?  In
other words, the task should be broken down from 1 task taking 16
hours to maybe 4 tasks of varying lengths with something to show to
back it up. (print outs, test results, data maps etc. etc.).

3)  Ensure you have a change management process in place that is
agreed to by everyone including senior management.  The change
management process would kick in whenever someone requests a change to
the project plan that would cause a task to be lengthened by some
agreed upon amount.  I've used anything from 8-20 hours on various
projects.

4)  Be sure your project has a senior manager sponsor.  This manager
would be the final decision maker on any disputes and would be
ultimately responsible for the success or failure of the project.

There are many more things involved, but hopefully these will get you
started.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

I get enough exercise just pushing my luck. 


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: HOW: Learning project management skills

- **From:** John Grznar <jgrznar@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3887B0DB.4AC07077@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<43318s4lljgbvitvmgovi5if2vj0m2gqtp@4ax.com> <EAB502EA5FFE702E.BD23936EB27202E7.D021CAE705D39271@lp.airnews.net>`

```
  I would like to add an item 4a to Steve's excellent list.  Keep your sponser
interested in your project.  Just because your sponser is all gun-ho on your project
now does not mean that he will be there when you need him come crunch time.

John


SkippyPB wrote:

### Cobol-problem CICS -> TransActionServer Rel. 1.3

- **From:** decker <Decker@kzvk.de>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38844620.1D5CD68D@kzvk.de>`

```
Hi all,

last weekend we have upgrade CICS to TransActionServer Rel.
1.3

Now i get an ASRA-error in an old program. 

I have found the error:

There are 2 fields:

01 .....
   .....
   10  IN    pic x(12).     (contains: 12345AA89012)
   .....


77 OUT       pic xxBx(6)Bx(4).

MOVE IN TO OUT   crash with ASRA

When I remove the "B" in the OUT-Pic-clause the program
works fine.

Did anyone have had the same problem (or other!) when going
to Transactionserver 1.3?  

Please report it! Thanks!

Georg Decker 
Cologne/Germany
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol-problem CICS -> TransActionServer Rel. 1.3

- **From:** "Francois Le Maner" <flemaner@loricam.fr>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<863o8k$qmb$1@minus.oleane.net>`
- **References:** `<38844620.1D5CD68D@kzvk.de> <Ko%g4.2129$hr4.35745@newreader.ukcore.bt.net> <38856326.34F2B550@kzvk.de>`

```
Yes
For batch SCEERUN must be used , in steplib/joblib or in linklist
For CICS , SCEECICS must be added before SCEERUN in DFHRPL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol-problem CICS -> TransActionServer Rel. 1.3

- **From:** Clark Morris <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.cics-l,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3885BCC6.9A3085CC@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<38844620.1D5CD68D@kzvk.de> <Ko%g4.2129$hr4.35745@newreader.ukcore.bt.net> <38856326.34F2B550@kzvk.de>`

```
There is a separate CICS LE run-time library (SCEECICS I think) that
should be concatenated in the DFHRPL for the CICS region before
SCEERUN.  Note that neither library should be in the link-edit
concatenation for linkage editing and that only SCEELKED should be
there.  Note that I am using the DD names known to systems maintenance
and those names will be prefixed by SYS1. or some other high level
indexes.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.,
cfmtech@istar.ca

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ SV: Cobol-problem CICS -> TransActionServer Rel. 1.3

- **From:** "Bjorn Alvik" <bjorn.alvik@asp.no>
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lFhl4.18479$in5.322619@news1.online.no>`
- **References:** `<38844620.1D5CD68D@kzvk.de> <20000129153626.28337.00000325@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38959706.D2CA4E8B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>`

```
Maybe it can have something to do with alignment and/or using them (the
77-levels) as arguments in CALLs?
In former days, on IBM, most 77-levels were byte aligned, while 01 was word
(or double-word) aligned.
Hence, 77 could be a way to save storage, which was cruical up to 1970.
Nowadays it does not matter.
I also have stopped using it.
By the way, is there any use of the 66-levels (rename, I think) out there?
I've never used it, and I see no reason to.

Bjorn

Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> skrev i

> recommended"?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SV: Cobol-problem CICS -> TransActionServer Rel. 1.3

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3895D331.571774D3@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<38844620.1D5CD68D@kzvk.de> <20000129153626.28337.00000325@ng-cd1.aol.com> <38959706.D2CA4E8B@NOSPAMwebaccess.net> <lFhl4.18479$in5.322619@news1.online.no>`

```
Bjorn Alvik wrote:
> 
> Maybe it can have something to do with alignment and/or using them (the
> 77-levels) as arguments in CALLs?
> In former days, on IBM, most 77-levels were byte aligned, while 01 was word
> (or double-word) aligned.

IIRC it was the other way around (the saving storage part). Both 01's &
77's are double word boundary aligned, but the fields in an 01 are
contiguous (save any BINARY SYNC fields which might force alignment). If
you defined one 77 as PIC S9(9) COMP and another as PIC X (maybe a
switch or flag), you'd use 16 bytes of memory for only 5 bytes of data,
i.e., 4 bytes wasted in the first one and 7 wasted in the 2nd one.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SV: Cobol-problem CICS -> TransActionServer Rel. 1.3

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>

```

> IIRC it was the other way around (the saving storage part). Both 01's &
> 77's are double word boundary aligned, but the fields in an 01 are
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

Since this thread has introduced the topic of 77s, I thought it interesting
to note
that the DB2 pre-compiler still inserts 3 77s at the end of WORKING-STORAGE:
SQL-TEMP, SQL-NULL, and SQL-INIT-FLAG!
```

<a id="t-3cbaf9de48"></a>
## Thread: First time oncall... — 9 msgs

### First time oncall...

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8634ng0bik@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Well, this is my 2nd day of on-call.  I responded to 2 problems so far (is
that my phone ringing :-) ).

One was an ADS/O dialog (screen) that has a known quirk.  I passed the
problem from the help desk, to the programmer in charge of that area.  He
couldn't recreate the problem so that was the end of that.

The other was a recently modified batch COBOL IDMS program.  Well, I know
most people don't use IDMS.  IDMS is a generally a network style database.
I know they added the R for relational in v10 I think.  Anyways, a common
procedure is to obtain the first record in a set, process it, then keep
obtaining the next record in the set and processing it until you hit the
end.  Eventually you hit the END-OF-SET condition and then the code has to
deal with getting out of the loop.  Well for some reason, the person who
made the changes decided to code IF DB-REC-NOT-FOUND then go to exit or
whatever.  Duh, the program dies last night with an 0307 error (End of set,
area, or index).  This seemed like an oversight to me, so I coded OR
DB-END-OF-SET go to exit.  The code was not dropping through, and I did see
that the driver PERFORM was already correctly coded to recognize
DB-END-OF-SET. 20 minutes total, I'm compiling and submitted the library
move to production control's printer.

Fine, the job restarts and completes COND CODES=0.  Today, the lead
programmer of that team is talking to me and said they sent a whole bunch of
bad data to the accounts payable group.  He said I should have called him,
and next time, to do so.  He said he appreciated my valiant effort though
(this guy is like 40 or so and is a real smart ass like me.  He has kids so
I can understand).

Now, I understand some folks are very protective and proud of their code.
But he readily admitted that the changes were made with a style he "didn't
like".  Such as one of these:

If xxxxx
   If xxxxxx
      If xxxxxx
          iF xxxxxxxx
             if xxxxxxxxxx
                if xxxxxxxxxxx
                   if xxxxxxxxxxxxx
                      if xxxxxxxxxxxx
                         if xxxxxxxxxxx
                         end-if
                      end-if
                   end-if
                end-if
             end-if
          end-if
       end-if
    end-if
end-if.

Now, what is this, drawing class or something?  That's shite, sorry.  For
me, 4 levels is usually my limit, 5 occasionaly.  But this generally says
the data is not very streamlined running through the program.

So, anyways, I guess this whole e-mail is basically asking a question.  How
should or can I learn about this 'line' I have to draw between fellow
programmers while on-call?  What should I fix, and what shouldn't I?  What
is it, "people are like teabags; you have to put them in hot water to see
how strong they are?"   I guess I have to learn each person. I mean, missing
a DB-END-OF-SET is pretty trivial.  Maybe he was just mad not because my
'fix' was bad, but because the code sucked balls and did the wrong thing?
How possible is that?

Dunno, just asking.

Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: First time oncall...

- **From:** William Reed <adamreed@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38854A53.A8BCF6A6@bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<8634ng0bik@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```


Jeff Baynard wrote:

> Anyways, a common
> procedure is to obtain the first record in a set, process it, then keep
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff

I agree that the person who put in the change without testing it should get the
call, or at least take the blame for putting in code without testing it.

Code to process records in an IDMS set should be put in a separate paragraph,
NOT in the middle of a string of nested "IF"s.

I once saw code with an IF statement 8 pages long, but I'm sure there are others
who can tell you they saw longer ones.

Programming is no place to have a thin skin for criticism.
But there are no age boundaries on being a smart ass, 40 with kids or otherwise.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: First time oncall...

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000119011033.03564.00000194@ng-cj1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38854A53.A8BCF6A6@bellsouth.net>`

```
>How
>> should or can I learn about this 'line' I have to draw between fellow
>> programmers while on-call? 

To answer the specific question asked by Jeff, I follow these rules:
1).  If the run book (or equivalent) says to call the designee for the business
or application area, I do.
2).  If the run book does not say to call someone specified either by role or
by name, then, I am the one who needs to take the action necessary to get the
system up and running again.  If that produces unexpected results, then I
suggest that either to code needs documentation, or the run book needs update,
to prevent another untalented dofus like me from making a mess in the future.
3).  I never {well. almost never :) } criticise the coding of other
programmers, because, I probably do not know under what conditions the code was
produced, I probably do not know the business problems to be addressed in its
entirety, and I am sure that my successors could and would point to more
shameful examples on my part.  (use of ALTER is a significant exception, it is
always WRONG!).
4.  I always {well, sometimes :)} take criticism of my code as a constructive
attempt to prevent me from doing idiotic things in the future.
5.  Being in my 40's myself, I assume everybody in their 40's are being
smart-asses - after all, we're too old be whiz kids, and too young to be sages,
what else is left?

Jeff, does that help?
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

#### ↳ Re: First time oncall...

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3885ABA7.650A4BC8@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8634ng0bik@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Jeff Baynard wrote:

>
> How should or can I learn about this 'line' I have to draw
> between fellow programmers while on-call?

No programmer likes being told they can do better.  Wa all seem to
believe in ourselves too much.  Age burns this out of us when we least
need it due to experience.

>  What should I fix, and what shouldn't I?

What is broken with the least intrusive fix.  Leave a recommendation
for the code to be thoroughly reviewed with wide awake people.

Sometimes if it is really bad then the least intrusive can amount to a
rewrite but this should not happen often.

>  What is it, "people are like teabags; you have to
> put them in hot water to see how strong they are?"   I guess I have
> to learn each person. I mean, missing a DB-END-OF-SET is pretty
> trivial.

>  Maybe he was just mad not because my 'fix' was bad,

Bad fixes are bad news.  They can take an unholy amount of time to
correct the mess.  If you are not sure call in someone else.  IF you
have no one else and you are not 100% sure cancel the run.  If you do
this and it is important you should have someone to talk to very
shortly :-}

> but because the code sucked balls and did the wrong thing?
> How possible is that?

All code sucks, including the rubbish you wrote last week (if you do
not believe me take a peek at an old program you wrote and tell me
that it could not have been done better).  I hate my old code because
I could have always done better.  There is also the element that bad
code comes from work around that are no longer required.

It is dead easy to criticize, difficult to lead by example.  Make sure
you do not live in a glass house, if you do take criticism on the chin
and learn from it, otherwise shut up!

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: First time oncall...

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Oo$NjspY$GA.270@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<8634ng0bik@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```

When I was oncall for pricing, I just used straight forward judgement calls
as to whether to call in the big guns.  Say, if the day before a mass group
of new data changes had been moved into production (not code, actual new
data), and a S0C7 occurs in code that's never taken that error before, I'll
suspect the data first and call the system owner..

If the S0C7 occurs, and I can determine that this is a result of an improper
code change (compare old code versus new), and I know that the pricing
system's configuration hadn't changed, I'd fix it myself (after running a
test to see if the data itself is, in fact, all right).

This is tempered by knowing a bit about the system, and reading the purpose
of the jobstep involved, the restart instructions, and the documentation in
the code (if any).

It's a balance between what you do know, what you don't, and what you can
figure out.



Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:8634ng0bik@enews4.newsguy.com...

set,

see
> that the driver PERFORM was already correctly coded to recognize
> DB-END-OF-SET. 20 minutes total, I'm compiling and submitted the library
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

of

> (this guy is like 40 or so and is a real smart ass like me.  He has kids
so

>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…

How

missing

##### ↳ ↳ Re: First time oncall...

- **From:** "john doe" <someone@microsoft.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8661ls$r33$1@news.smartworld.net>`
- **References:** `<8634ng0bik@enews4.newsguy.com> <Oo$NjspY$GA.270@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
We do try to take it a step further in the cycle review meetings after we
get the code fixed.we relook who was in the group who reviewed and approved
the code,,Then we review the procedure they used to test,,then we fix both
and set new guidelines. Though this doesn't always works it sure helps,
especially the new programmers who can benefit most from "walkthroughs" both
their own and being in on others.
```

#### ↳ Re: First time oncall...

- **Message-ID:** `<KFNh4.265$JB.3964@nnrp2.rcsntx.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<8634ng0bik@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```

Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com>

Whoever 'owns' the busted program that needed fixing gets the
'on-call' job until the next program failure.
```

#### ↳ Re: First time oncall...

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86b3av$2uk$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8634ng0bik@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Nested IF's are nice to look at, but not at 3.15 am. Unfortunately program
structure is like religion - you cannot discuss it.

When I am on call, I insist to be 'captain on the ship' and if anyone dare
to put an untested change to production level without informing me about it,
he will hear from me. We have a member for each day, where people can tell
the person on-call what to do if a new or changed program crashes. If
nothing is stated there, I feel free to do whatever possible to correct the
error. If some child at 40 is uncomfortable with changes I made, so what?

The reason for your change was a bug, and you didn't put i there. You were
called because someone else failed to do a proper piece of work.

An other thing I don't understand. When do our nice young fellow at 40 like
to be called? I mean, if he want a call, why not call him and take a nap? I
would really like a young dedicated person to call - especially after
midnight, and preferably 3-4 times before morning breaks.

As you can see, I'm older than 40. I am taking on-call myself when something
is critical - I took the Y2K duty just for the challenge, but it was no big
deal.

The best idea I can give you is the member per day, where people can give
helpful messages and phone numbers you can call if their new/changed
programs fails. We enter the actions done during the night as well. When a
job gives bad condition codes, you can search through the members to see
what was done last time the error ocurred.

The idea removes doubt about when to call help. Further more, people are in
daytime aware of the problems you might have during the night, and it gives
a nice team spirit.

I hope this helps you.

regards
Ib

Jeff Baynard skrev i meddelelsen <8634ng0bik@enews4.newsguy.com>...
>Well, this is my 2nd day of on-call.  I responded to 2 problems so far (is

## Thread: CICS: Opening files — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### CICS: Opening files

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8620k5$9hh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Most of our VSAM files are set up in the FCT as 'Opentime  ==>
Firstref'.
I've been told this means a file will be closed until some program
tries an I/O against it.

We're having a problem with some files not opening as desired. Is there
a 'CExx' inquiry that shows whether a file has been set up as above? Or
is there some other way I can look at the FCT entry? CEMT I... doesn't
show this attribute.

The files do open when we have the operators do a CEMT S FI(...) OPE
command.

We have CICS 4.10 on a IBM mainrame.

TIA

John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS: Opening files

- **From:** Jerry@zonko.co.uk (Jerry Ozaniec)
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<memo.20000118174235.68A@zonko.cix.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8620k5$9hh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8620k5$9hh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, john_mindock@my-deja.com () 
wrote:

> Most of our VSAM files are set up in the FCT as 'Opentime  ==>
> Firstref'.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> command.

Various possibilities here. I suspect that when you do a CEMT I against 
the file(s) affected you will see that the file is "Clo Une" meaning 
Closed but Unenabled, thus preventing automatic open at first reference. 
This may be due to file definition (use CEDC to discover if this is so, or 
ask whomever is responsible for the definitions to check) or the file has 
been specifically closed (more likely) by someone to take it offline for 
some reason. If the latter is so, the problem is that the same person who 
is taking the file offline should bring it back online once it is sensible 
to do so.

Jerry Ozaniec
Zonko Enterprises Limited
http://www.zonko.co.uk/
```

#### ↳ Re: CICS: Opening files

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86319p$33d$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8620k5$9hh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8620k5$9hh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  john_mindock@my-deja.com wrote:
> Most of our VSAM files are set up in the FCT as 'Opentime  ==>
> Firstref'.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> We're having a problem with some files not opening as desired. Is
there
> a 'CExx' inquiry that shows whether a file has been set up as above?
Or
> is there some other way I can look at the FCT entry? CEMT I... doesn't
> show this attribute.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If all of the FCT's are defined as "Firstref", then instead of the
operator opening all files, just have operations do a "CEMT SET FIL
(...) ENA" and simply enable them.

You can view the FCT's via CEDC. You may need permission/authorization
to use this transaction.

Programmatically, you could issue a LINK to "DFHEMTP" and enable them.
The commarea would be the same command as the operator enters manually.

One problem you could have opening all the files is that your MVS/TIOT
may need an adjustment if you have greater than approximately 1600
files defined.

HTH....

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CICS: Opening files

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3885481C.6F76E281@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<8620k5$9hh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <86319p$33d$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
WOB Consulting wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> If all of the FCT's are defined as "Firstref", then instead of the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> The commarea would be the same command as the operator enters manually.

A more modern way is to use the SET command from the System Programming
Interface (SPI):

EXEC  CICS  SET
	FILE(xxxxxxx)
	OPEN
END-EXEC

You'll need the "SP" option for the translator. Rather than hard coding
the file names you could browse the FCT to get the file names & other
attributes. I can't remember the exact format of the start browse
command for the FCT, but it's in the SPI guide.

If the files are ENABLED, set for FirstRef and they don't open, there's
a problem. Check SDSF for your CICS region and find the error messages
for your file. It might not be defined, might be defined incorrectly,
might be open in batch, etc.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS: Opening files

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000119013112.03564.00000195@ng-cj1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3885481C.6F76E281@worldnet.att.net>`

```
>If the files are ENABLED, set for FirstRef and they don't open, there's
>a problem. 

Another cause I didn't see mentioned here is the files were closed by an
interface to a batch job (CEMTBTCH or such), and then not specifically
re-opened.  As I remember, if the CICS is brought down, and then started again,
the flags on the files still show it being "manually" closed, and they will not
open on first reference, despite the table entry.  Manually enabling the files
should "fix" the problem immediately, improving the way everybody in the shop
manipulates the files is the long-term solution.
Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

```

an

started again,
> the flags on the files still show it being "manually" closed, and
they will not

the files

the shop
> manipulates the files is the long-term solution.


Thanks to all for the responses. I think the above may be the culprit.
They run some JCL 'MACRO's that CLOSE the files before the nightly
batch. I don't think there is a 're-open' job that runs early AM after
CICS comes back up.

John



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CICS: Opening files

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<388678E2.9993F531@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3885481C.6F76E281@worldnet.att.net> <20000119013112.03564.00000195@ng-cj1.aol.com> <864kfl$7ak$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
john_mindock@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> Thanks to all for the responses. I think the above may be the culprit.
> They run some JCL 'MACRO's that CLOSE the files before the nightly
> batch. I don't think there is a 're-open' job that runs early AM after
> CICS comes back up.

My shop uses AFCS to do this. If you don't run a 2nd job to ENABLE (at a
minimum) after the CICS region is back up, your files will be closed &
disabled. Been down this road more than once. AFCS brings CICS files up
in the same state they were in when you shutdown. Of course, if you're
not bouncing the region but just running your batch stream, you have no
choice but to ENABLE whem when you're done (or code your software to
deal with the CLOSED & DISABLED status).

Bill Lynch
```

## Thread: Stored Procedures — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Stored Procedures

- **From:** Susan Allen <susan.a.allen@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38849B43.540A34BF@boeing.com>`

```
In the great tradition of large companies everywhere..

I can code in COBOL, but now  I am being asked to write a stored
procedure for a DB2 application (lucky for me someone else is writing
the GUI part of this application).

I have written exactly one program using embedded SQL, so I have that
idea down, if not firmly then at least workably.

Any advice for web-sites, training manuals or other resources ??

I have been to the IBM web site and read their documents (and managed to
stay awake while doing so).

Thanks

	Susan A
```

#### ↳ Re: Stored Procedures: HOWTO for DB2

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EG8h4.4434$SM.73902@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<38849B43.540A34BF@boeing.com>`

```
Copy my entire response into Notepad and set font to 'FixedSys'.  Then the
charts show correctly.
(COpy all between lines of @@@@@@
@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@
6.2.1 Introduction to Stored Procedures

A stored procedure is a compiled program, stored at a DB2 local or remote
server, that can execute SQL
statements. A typical stored procedure contains two or more SQL statements
and some manipulative or
logical processing in a host language. A client application program uses the
SQL statement CALL to invoke the stored procedure.

Consider using stored procedures for a client/server application that does
at least one of the following things:

   Executes many remote SQL statements.

    Remote SQL statements can create many network send and receive
operations, which results in
    increased processor costs.

    Stored procedures can encapsulate many of your application's SQL
statements into a single message
to the DB2 server, reducing network traffic to a single send and receive
operation for a series of SQL
statements.

   Accesses host variables for which you want to guarantee security and
integrity.

    Stored procedures remove SQL applications from the workstation, which
prevents workstation users
    from manipulating the contents of sensitive SQL statements and host
variables.


Figure 70 and Figure 71 illustrate the difference between using stored
procedures and not using stored
procedures.

+------------------------------------------------------------------------+
�                                                                        �
�                                                                        �
�                                                                        �
�     +------+                           +-------------------------+     �

6.2.2 An Example of a Simple Stored Procedure

Suppose that an application runs on a workstation client and calls a stored
procedure A on the DB2
server at location LOCA. Stored Procedure A performs the following
operations:

1.  Receives a set of parameters containing the data for one row of the
employee to project activity
table   (DSN8510.EMPPROJACT). These parameters are input parameters in the
SQL statement CALL:

        EMP: employee number
        PRJ: project number
        ACT: activity ID
        EMT: percent of employee's time required
        EMS: date the activity starts
        EME: date the activity is due to end

2.  Declares a cursor, C1, with the option WITH RETURN, that is used to
return a result set containing
all    rows in EMPPROJACT to the caller.

3.  Queries table EMPPROJACT to determine whether a row exists where columns
PROJNO, ACTNO,
    EMSTDATE, and EMPNO match the values of parameters PRJ, ACT, EMS, and
EMP. (The table has a
    unique index on those columns. There is at most one row with those
values.)

4.  If the row exists, executes an SQL statement UPDATE to assign the values
of parameters EMT and EME
    to columns EMPTIME and EMENDATE.

5.  If the row does not exist, executes an SQL statement INSERT to insert a
new row with all the
values in  the parameter list.

6.  Opens cursor C1. This causes the result set to be returned to the caller
when the stored procedure ends.

7.  Returns two parameters, containing these values:

       A code to identify the type of SQL statement last executed: UPDATE
or INSERT.

       The SQLCODE from that statement.



Figure 72 shows the steps involved in executing this stored procedure.
