###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You're invited to critique my new Cobol page I started

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Jerry Peters <gapeters@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<872mka$3mm$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<4Z9k4.3741$%87.112611@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3891A410.7DABF28B@neo.rr.com> <pUpk4.3121$Sa2.119928@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <#wgNZYia$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <38942B5D.E3CF898E@zip.com.au>`

```
You have not looked at much of the Linux kernel have you?
goto's are used extensively to exit various constructs for error
conditions and to improve the common code paths for performance. 
Linus has explicitely stated that _he_ uses goto's when
he thinks it's necessary. He also has rejected patches which remove
goto's, especially when he's used them for performance reasons.

I've had to fix some idiots goto'less code in Cobol. Have you ever tried
to figure out an if statement that spans several pages and nests 8 or more
levels deep? Especially when a few goto's would have greatly simplified
the code? You can design a well structured program using goto's; you can
also design a monstrosity by _not_ using goto's. It has much less to do
with goto's than with coding ability and logical thinking.
 
	Jerry

In alt.computer.consultants Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
> DennisHarley wrote:
>> 
>> Even C has a GOTO.
>> 

> I have only once seen a goto used in a C program.  It was a low level
> internal routine of Linux.  Goto's ate not required in C programming. 
> There are absolutely minimal reasons that they should appear in CObol.
> I personally never code them.

> Thanks
> Ken Foskey
> http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You're invited to critique my new Cobol page I started

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38957DFA.BBF69AED@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<4Z9k4.3741$%87.112611@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3891A410.7DABF28B@neo.rr.com> <pUpk4.3121$Sa2.119928@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <#wgNZYia$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <38942B5D.E3CF898E@zip.com.au> <872mka$3mm$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Jerry Peters wrote:
> 
> You have not looked at much of the Linux kernel have you?

Yes that right.  I code applications not rocket science.

> goto's are used extensively to exit various constructs for error
> conditions and to improve the common code paths for performance.
> Linus has explicitely stated that _he_ uses goto's when
> he thinks it's necessary. He also has rejected patches which remove
> goto's, especially when he's used them for performance reasons.

Performance is rarely an issue with application code.  Write it well
first time and then attempt to refactor for performance.  Well written
code outshines badly written code every time.

If you follow up with C++ you would use exception to escape a
sequential process that errors.  This closely follows the use case
that you would build for the system. That is another argument though.

> I've had to fix some idiots goto'less code in Cobol. Have you ever
> tried to figure out an if statement that spans several pages and
> nests 8 or more levels deep?

Yes.  I then break it up into functional groups with a named paragraph
that is called to describe what is occurring.  That is I leave
footprints of my path to the next poor sucker (err programmer).

> Especially when a few goto's would have greatly simplified the code?
> You can design a well structured program using goto's; you can
> also design a monstrosity by _not_ using goto's. It has much less to
> do with goto's than with coding ability and logical thinking.

Could not agree more.

Setting good style guidelines for new players adds to your chance of
getting a comprehensible mess rather than an incomprehensible.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You're invited to critique my new Cobol page I started

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8741m3$3af$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<4Z9k4.3741$%87.112611@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3891A410.7DABF28B@neo.rr.com> <pUpk4.3121$Sa2.119928@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <#wgNZYia$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <38942B5D.E3CF898E@zip.com.au> <872mka$3mm$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Jerry Peters wrote in message
>
>I've had to fix some idiots goto'less code in Cobol. Have you ever tried
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

While I agree that rules for the sake of rules are silly, and while I agree
that there is, indeed, a place for the odd (very odd) goto, laying the blame
for the above sort of code on the head of "structure" is ridiculous.

An eight page IF statement is the result of poor programming and is not
predicated by topdown structure. In fact, I would not consider it topdown at
all.  Topdown refers to the practice of sub-dividing a complex method into
multiple simple methods.  To equate it to simply avoiding the "GO TO"
statement reveals a profound misunderstanding of the entire methodology.

Readable code is created by trying, real hard, to write code that is easy to
read, then reading it back and changing it until it is easy to read.  With
practice, a programmer can even do it the first time. If it were possible to
just make a rule, then have the code magically become readable, then we
would not need programmers.

I could write readable code without "PERFORMS"; using *only* "GOTO's" for
flow control.  I have written thousands of lines of code in assembler that
way, simply because that is the most readable way of writing it.  I could
even write readable code using nothing but the ALTER statement if I had to.

Rules like "avoid GOTO" are training wheels for beginner programmers.
Structure is just that:  the way program parts are put together.  You can do
it logically and neatly so that it can be followed, or you can just jam it
all into a source code module and leave a mess.  Applying a label to the
various methods does not change their basic nature, nor does it affect
readability.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You're invited to critique my new Cobol page I started

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38958f9e_3@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<4Z9k4.3741$%87.112611@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3891A410.7DABF28B@neo.rr.com> <pUpk4.3121$Sa2.119928@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <#wgNZYia$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <38942B5D.E3CF898E@zip.com.au> <872mka$3mm$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <8741m3$3af$1@news.igs.net>`

```

donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote
> Jerry Peters wrote in message
> Rules like "avoid GOTO" are training wheels for beginner programmers.
> Structure is just that:  the way program parts are put together.  You can
do
> it logically and neatly so that it can be followed, or you can just jam it
> all into a source code module and leave a mess.  Applying a label to the
> various methods does not change their basic nature, nor does it affect
> readability.

It is amazing that 32 years after Dijkstra's GOTO-paper this discussion
still goes on. (just for the record, Dijkstra has also stated that the
teaching and use of COBOL ought to be a criminal offense).

As readable assembler code clearly shows, the GOTO is not the
problem. The programmer is the problem.

For COBOL there is a special problem that once you use a GOTO
you have lost the 'way back'. That is: if you perform A, that performs
B, that go to C, you can't get back to where you performed A. This
means that perform and goto cannot be mixed while retaining a
reasonable structure (except with the artifice of using sections).
This alone relegates GOTOs to error exits and abends where
a GOTO might save the day. Personally, I have never used a
GO TO in COBOL, for the simple reason that I have never
*needed* to, but different applications (and people) may have
different perceived needs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You're invited to critique my new Cobol page I started

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<874pe4$56q$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<4Z9k4.3741$%87.112611@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3891A410.7DABF28B@neo.rr.com> <pUpk4.3121$Sa2.119928@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <#wgNZYia$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <38942B5D.E3CF898E@zip.com.au> <872mka$3mm$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <8741m3$3af$1@news.igs.net> <38958f9e_3@news3.prserv.net>`

```
In comp.lang.cobol Leif Svalgaard <lsvalg@ibm.net> wrote:

: It is amazing that 32 years after Dijkstra's GOTO-paper this discussion
: still goes on. (just for the record, Dijkstra has also stated that the
: teaching and use of COBOL ought to be a criminal offense).

: As readable assembler code clearly shows, the GOTO is not the
: problem. The programmer is the problem.

It is mmy view that, in Cobol, the problem with GO TO is not the
GO TO at all.  When reading or writing code the GO TO is always
clear and explicit as to what will happen at that point (it may
be wrong but it is always clear).

This gives the impression that a GO TO may be a clearer way of
expressing the program.

The problem with GO TO is that it must have a label.  Generally
this means that the program must have more labels (paragraph and
section names).  The usual way of increasing the number of labels
to cater for GO TO is to have sections and to add paragraph  labels
within this.  In Cobol a label can be used in several different
ways: Performed, GOne TO, dropped into, and are of two types
(section and paragraph) which can be made to overlap in
complex ways.

Other languages use different label types for different purposes:
a label that may be used as a target for a goto cannot be used
for any other purpose; a procedure name cannot be used to goto.

When examining a part of the code in a Cobol program where a
mixture of PERFORM and GO TO is used it is necessary to determine
all the paths through the code by examination of the whole
program.  That is, if the part of the program being examined 
contains a label, for example:

          calculate, say, price of item.
      calc-paragraph-label.
           calculate tax on item.

If it is necessary to change the code such that the price and
tax calculations are revised then the presence of the label
_may_ indicated that it is:

      target for a goto
      terminates a perform of the paragraph above
      target for a perfrom
      drops through

So the code above and below the label may be executed separately
or together in arbitrary ways.

The only solution is to examine the whole source code to determine
every possible path involving this label and the labels above.

If, in examining some code, a GO TO is found, this is trivial to
trace and is an entirely different issue.

One way of reducing the complexity of determining program flow is 
to lay down standards which can be guaranteed.  This may be, for
example, that the only GO TOs are to the end of a performed section
with a guaranteed name.  It is thus only necessary to confirm
the guarantee.  In the above case the label must be spurious and
should be commented out (because it is not the end of a section
and is not a section).

Another way of reducing complexity is to guarantee that all labels
only have one type of use.  As PERFORM is the most useful statement
then this should eliminate all other ways that a label could be 
used.  Thus GO TO is banned, as are drop throughs and thus
THRU.

Thus when examining the example code above one can be guaranteed
that the pricing and tax are always done separately by specific
performs of the paragraphj above and the label shown and this
can be searched for without needing to understand the whole
program.

One of the skills of programming is to reduce the complexity.
There are several ways of doing this, each can work. I prefer
the way that has no sections and the only reference allowed to
a label is a perform (without thru).

Those that have used other mechanisms (such as perform section
and goto exit) usually raise objections based on applying
their styles to this different mechanism and don't see that a
completely different style is required which, once, adopted
actually produces much less complexity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: You're invited to critique my new Cobol page I started

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<875ne7$3an$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3896378c_2@news3.prserv.net> <20000131231931.24953.00000886@ng-bj1.aol.com>`

Actually, besides the GO TO/PERFORM abend-paragraph, there is another case
like this that seems similar to me.  Somewhere in this thread the topic of
(IBM mainframe) CICS programming came up. Thinking about this (and it has
been a LONG time since I actually coded CICS - so my memory may be entirely
wrong) - if I were creating a pseudo-conversational application program, I
would probably  PERFORM (not GO TO) a paragraph with an

    EXEC CICS RETURN TRANSID(xxxx)

statement.  This stands as little chance of ever "returning" from the PERFORM
as does one with an "ABEND" in it.
```

- **References:** `<4Z9k4.3741$%87.112611@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3891A410.7DABF28B@neo.rr.com> <pUpk4.3121$Sa2.119928@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <#wgNZYia$GA.307@cpmsnbbsa02> <38942B5D.E3CF898E@zip.com.au> <872mka$3mm$1@bgtnsc02.worldnet.att.net> <8741m3$3af$1@news.igs.net>`

```
In alt.computer.consultants donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
> Jerry Peters wrote in message
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>

> While I agree that rules for the sake of rules are silly, and while I agree
> that there is, indeed, a place for the odd (very odd) goto, laying the blame
> for the above sort of code on the head of "structure" is ridiculous.

I _would_ agree, except to many people, structured means no goto's.

> An eight page IF statement is the result of poor programming and is not
> predicated by topdown structure. In fact, I would not consider it topdown at
> all.  Topdown refers to the practice of sub-dividing a complex method into
> multiple simple methods.  To equate it to simply avoiding the "GO TO"
> statement reveals a profound misunderstanding of the entire methodology.

That's what I tend to do in assembler, which is my main language. I create a
stack of register save areas, then create relatively simple subroutines to do
all of the work. The problem with doing that in COBOL is that there is no
argument passing, which makes using subroutines a pain.

> Readable code is created by trying, real hard, to write code that is easy to
> read, then reading it back and changing it until it is easy to read.  With
> practice, a programmer can even do it the first time. If it were possible to
> just make a rule, then have the code magically become readable, then we
> would not need programmers.

Of course, but COBOL was designed under tha assumption that bean counters could
do the coding, if only the language were simple enough (English like, I assume).
    
> I could write readable code without "PERFORMS"; using *only* "GOTO's" for
> flow control.  I have written thousands of lines of code in assembler that
> way, simply because that is the most readable way of writing it.  I could
> even write readable code using nothing but the ALTER statement if I had to.

I once maintained a system that used alter-goto's instead of performs, because
using even one perform caused the program to become much larger (this was under
360 DOS). The alter-goto's were only used to emulate performs, not as part of
the logic. It worked and was not hard to maintain.

> Rules like "avoid GOTO" are training wheels for beginner programmers.
> Structure is just that:  the way program parts are put together.  You can do
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> readability.

The problem is that nobody seems to emphasize that the code should be readable,
certainly not in college. One of the first things I learned was to make my code
readable; in 6 months or a year even I'll need all the help I can get to understand
what the code is doing, let alone some other poor maintenance programmer who get
stuck fixing or modifying the code. Yet I'm amazed to see others who don't do that.
Especially in assembler where they code offsets from some base register rather than
using dsects. You may as well be reading a hex dump of the object code.

	Jerry
```

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

It isn't hard for someone who doesn't understand how to structure simple
GOTO-less, switch-less to mess up a program entirely by attempting to
remove GOTOs.  But someone who knew what he was doing could have done
that same program much more clear and maintainable - WITHOUT resorting
to GOTO statements.

Don't create a rule or standard for a shop without teaching the
programmers how to properly code using that rule or standard.

## Thread: A question — 3 this month / 4 total

### A question

- **From:** "Michael S." <michaelsh@amdocs.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<949079864.448181@soint7>`

```
Do somebody know any articles
about future directions in Mainframe-Cobol
field ?

Thanks a lot,
                                  Michael.
```

#### ↳ Re: A question

- **From:** stevenln@aol.comnospam (Steve Newton)
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000130201450.22423.00000590@ng-fh1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<949079864.448181@soint7>`

```
>Do somebody know any articles
>about future directions in Mainframe-Cobol
>field ?

Well, outside of Y2K, Mainframe COBOL, not being sexy, and despised by most
academics as being too pragmatic, useful, and effective, to really be worth
their time (not that I have an opinion <G>), there haven't been a lot of
articles about Mainframe COBOL recently.  

However, in past postings to this group you will see references by one of our
leading lights, William Klein, to several standards groups, such as J4, and the
various things they are doing to "improve" Cobol.  

You could also check the IBM web site, WWW.IBM.COM, where some hints as to
future direction can be gained - the trick there is to know that COBOL is in
the Visual AGE family of products.

And, you could cruise back through the threads of this newsgroup.  Lots of
folks opinions here, and some may actually be worth the time to read.

However, please, please, PLEASE, do not start a thread on the future of COBOL! 
We've had them, they get very long and tedious, and encourage many of us to say
silly things.  If you are asking if COBOL has a future, I would refer you to
the Y2K postings, where some of us worked on code originated in the 70's or
earlier, and allow you to draw your own conclusions.

Good luck


Asimov, Heinlein, and Zappa
Still the best
```

## Thread: Calling an activeX-DLL written in Delphi. — 2 msgs

### Calling an activeX-DLL written in Delphi.

- **From:** "tommy" <tommynilsen@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86ssaf$k5i$1@nntp.newmedia.no>`

```

Hi.

I am trying to call an activeX-DLL written in Delphi.

Anyone know where I can find any info on how to do this...

What is an activeX-DLL and how is it different from an "ordinary"
activeX-component ??

I use Netexpress 3.0 from Merant

Thanks

Tommy Nilsen
tommynilsen@yahoo.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling an activeX-DLL written in Delphi.

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<873t8j$h8a$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<86ssaf$k5i$1@nntp.newmedia.no>`

```
Hi Tommy,

Net Express support the calling of COM (aka ActiveX) components. It
shouldn't matter what language the component is written in as long as it
exposes an OLE Automation interface.

Check out the OLE Automation and DCOM chapter in the Distributed Computing
Online Book.

There are also some demos (supplied with Net Express) on calling Microsoft
Work/Excel which may be of some interest.

ActiveX DLLs are inprocess servers but ActiveX EXEs are out of process
servers (ie run in a seperate process from the client).

Hope this helps
David.

news:86ssaf$k5i$1@nntp.newmedia.no...

>

## Thread: COMP-3 — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### COMP-3

- **From:** rizer@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86rilr$mg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I'm having a little trouble with the COMP-3 command in the PIC clauses.
Program compiles ok, but I get an "Illegal character in numeric field"
when I try to run it. The book I am using doesn't say much about how to
use COMP-3. Thanks for any help. Here is part of the code:



WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

   01  SALES-RECORD-IN.

       05  SALES-REV-IN         PIC S9(5)V99 COMP-3.
       05  NET-INCOME-IN        PIC S9(5)V99 COMP-3.

   01  SALES-RECORD-OUT.
           05  SALES-REV-OUT    PIC -ZZZZZ.99.
           05                   PIC X.
           05  NET-INCOME-OUT   PIC -ZZZZZ.99.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       100-MAIN.
           OPEN INPUT SALES-FILE-IN
                OUTPUT SALES-REPORT-OUT.
           READ SALES-FILE-IN INTO SALES-RECORD-IN
               AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF.
           PERFORM 200-REPORT-ROUTINE
               UNTIL EOF IS EQUAL TO 'Y'.
           CLOSE SALES-FILE-IN
                 SALES-REPORT-OUT
           GOBACK.
       200-REPORT-ROUTINE.
           MOVE SALES-REV-IN  TO SALES-REV-OUT
           MOVE NET-INCOME-IN TO NET-INCOME-OUT
           MOVE SALES-RECORD-OUT TO REPORT-LINE
           WRITE REPORT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
           READ SALES-FILE-IN INTO SALES-RECORD-IN
               AT END MOVE 'Y' TO EOF.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: COMP-3

- **From:** "Glenn Gordon" <ggordon@dimensional.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6Xck4.897$x3.1499@wormhole.dimensional.com>`
- **References:** `<86rilr$mg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
COMP-3 is packed decimal.  for your s5(9)v99 field you have 7 digits and a
sign, so it is expecting 4 bytes.  If you were to look at the number in hex
for a value of 12345.67 it would look like 1234567C.  if an even number of
digits are used, the first half-byte is 0.  each 4 bits is an integer value
0-9, except for the sign nybble which is going to be A-D (if I remember
correctly, I always confuse the signs <g>).  Any compare of the COMP-3
field, or a move requiring a numeric conversion, will generate an exception
if the value in the field does not follow the rules.  SPACES, HIGH-VALUES
and LOW-VALUES in the field will cause the exception.  So, either your data
is corrupt, does not match your PIC, or whatever.  You can do a numeric
check on the field and move it to the display field when it is numeric and
move zeroes when not numeric, but you should really validate the data.
Maybe your file has a header record with field names or space records for
display purposes or something.  Figure out what in the data is causing it
and decide how to handle it.


Glenn


<rizer@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:86rilr$mg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I'm having a little trouble with the COMP-3 command in the PIC clauses.
> Program compiles ok, but I get an "Illegal character in numeric field"
> when I try to run it. The book I am using doesn't say much about how to
> use COMP-3. Thanks for any help. Here is part of the code:
...
```

#### ↳ SV: COMP-3

- **From:** "Bjorn Alvik" <bjorn.alvik@asp.no>
- **Date:** 2000-01-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PFdk4.15767$in5.277426@news1.online.no>`
- **References:** `<86rilr$mg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi, here is a little bit on numeric definition, -use and -hints: .

Definition of COMP-3 fields (IBM EBCDIC rule, though):
COMP-3 means packed decimal representation. Instead of one digit per byte,
you have two, except for the loworder byte, in which the loworder half-byte
contains the sign. This example shows how the number -1234 will be stored
using dislplay format and packed format:

PIC S9(5)               :  5 bytes   F0 F1 F2 F3 D4
Each 9 represents 1 byte. High-order part of loworder byte contains  the
sign. Value for + is hexa F or C, - is hexa D or E

PIC S9(5) COMP-3:  3 bytes  01 23 4D
Each 9 represents 1 half-byte. Sign in the rightmost halfbyte.

USE OF COMP-3 fields:
Like any numeric defined fields, it is the programmers responsibility to
assure numeric content according to the field definition (packed, decimal,
binary etc) before any references are made to it. In general you have no
guarantee that the field contains only numerics according to the format you
have specified.y.
If , by example, you initialize your record by moving spaces to the 01-level
field, the packed defined field will look like
40 40 40, and 0 is not a valid sign code. INITIALIZE would be better. Then
the fields will be set to the specified VALUE, or spaces/zeros according to
the elementary item definition.

HINT:
If you cannot know what the contents of the field really is, you should
perform the IF field NUMERIC test before further referencing the field. IF
NUMERIC is true when the content is numeric according to the field's usage
clause.

SOME OTHER HINTS RELATED TO NUMERIC FIELDS:

1. Very often a field will be used both numeric and alfanumeric. Often you
will see clumsy definitions like
02 ALFAFIELD PIC X(8).
02 NUMERICFIELD REDEFINES ALFAFIELD PIC S9(8).

Why not remove the confusing REDEFINES and specify like this?:
02 FIELD-ALFA.
03 FIELD-NUM   PIC S9(8).
In COBOL, the default USAGE for group items is alphanumeric.

2. Avoid COMP-3 if possible. The reasons for using it is not as valid as
before, and different platforms may treat it differently.

3. Avoid "numeric programming mode".
A good thumb-rule is to stribe after working with alphanumeric fields only.
This rule should be modified, of course, when the field is to be used in
arithmetics or for table indexing or report numeric editing. At least you
will reduce the 0c7 abend risk by doing so, and in maintenance you may have
less "numeric problems" bothering you .

Good liuck
Bjorn



<rizer@my-deja.com> skrev i meldingsnyheter:86rilr$mg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I'm having a little trouble with the COMP-3 command in the PIC clauses.
> Program compiles ok, but I get an "Illegal character in numeric field"
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

Hey, Howard,
I find few heavy reasons to go into a deep discussion on this redefine
topic. However, for many persons, redefine us may be more difficult to read
when large names are involved.
With redefine, you have to specify PICTURE clause for both items. After all,
this may cause compiler-hidden errors to occur.

Bjorn

Its bin a long time since i have programmed Cobol but i seem to remember
that it is not possible to move COMP-3 fields directly to DISPLAY fields
with an edit-mask. You must first move them to a numeric DISPLAY-field
like HELP PIC S9(5)V99. Then move this field to the edit-mask field. 

On the other hand, like Glenn and Bjorn mention, youre data may be
corrupted.

No, no, no. It's perfectly legitimate to move any numeric data type to an
edited field directly (at least on IBM COBOL, and I would strongly suspect for
all COBOLs). Don't go suggesting bad habits in this newsgroup, someone might
actually pick up on them.

I bet your input file doesn't contain packed numbers.  These data
contain two numeric characters per bye.  If you don't need packed,
switch your input file to display.

Alternatively, learn about packed.  Change your program to write out
SALES-RECORD-IN, passing in various hard coded values (positive and
negative).  Examine your output with a hex editor and then create test
data which agrees with what you have found.

## Thread: IF... END-IF — 15 this month / 19 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### IF... END-IF

- **From:** acimo@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86vgb1$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Okay,

How do I write an IF statement with multiple conditions followed by and
END-IF?  I attempted the following:

IF condition-1
   statement
ELSE IF condition-2
   statement
ELSE
   statement
END-IF.

My program didn't compile cleanly.  I received an error stating I was
missing an END-IF scope terminator.  I removed the END-IF and got a
clean compile.

Please Advise.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: IF... END-IF

- **From:** Bill Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38934AD6.5B419B4D@att.net>`
- **References:** `<86vgb1$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
acimo@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Okay,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Please Advise.

You'd be better off with an EVALUATE here, designed for multiple
conditions. In your example you need two END-IF's (paired with the two
IF's). I take it you took the single END-IF out but left the period
(full stop)? Lose the period & add a 2nd END-IF. I'll bet your compiler
loves that.

Bill Lynch

sample code for your example:

EVALUATE	TRUE
WHEN		CONDITION-1
	statement
WHEN		CONDITION-2
	statement
WHEN		OTHER
	yet another statement
END-EVALUATE

BTW, if you want to take the same action for multiple conditions, you
can code:

EVALUATE	TRUE
WHEN		CONDITION-3
WHEN		CONDITION-4
	statement(s)
.
.
END-EVALUATE
```

#### ↳ Re: IF... END-IF

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86vnoj$nlr$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<86vgb1$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Like this:

IF condition-1
   statement
ELSE
        IF condition-2
             statement
        END-IF
ELSE
    statement
END-IF.

Regards
Ib
acimo@my-deja.com skrev i meddelelsen <86vgb1$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Okay,
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86voli$2h5$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<86vgb1$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <86vnoj$nlr$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
    I frankly don't see any reason for a compiler to complain
about the original code (at bottom of post).  Yes, it is missing
an end-if, but it has a period, which cures all ills in a sense.

    The error message should have been an informational
in my opinion.


    The new code (directly below) looks like it has too many else's
to me.


"Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk> wrote in message
news:86vnoj$nlr$1@news.inet.tele.dk...
> Like this:
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IF... END-IF

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86vta1$2la$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<86vgb1$epl$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <86vnoj$nlr$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <86voli$2h5$1@ssauraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
I would really like to see the original code with all the "bits and pieces"
just before and after it.  I would also want to see the exact error message
that the compiler produced.  Like Russell, with the limited amount of
information that we have, it seems to me that there should have been no
compiler error message (although the code would have been better with a 2nd
END-IF and better indentation - but that is "style" only).

I can think of a couple of "things" that might have caused an error message
with the original code.

1) If one of those "statements" were actually a "NEXT SENTENCE" and not a
"real" imperative statement.  (Some compilers allow it, but a "NEXT SENTENCE"
phrase is not allowed "directly" in an IF that is terminated by an END-IF -
if ANSI conforming compilers).

2) If this code were actually "nested" in another conditional statement (such
as READ AT END - before the first "IF").  In that case, the period would end
both pending "IF" statements, but the original statement (whatever came
before the 1st IF) might have been a conditional that could NOT have a
"nested" conditional - even if the IF were terminated by a period.

  ***

Bottom-line: Show us (me) the full original code and error message, and I may
be able to explain it.  However, it is always "better" to make certain that
you have the same number of "END-IF" phrases as you have original "IF"
statements.  Better yet, do look at the EVALUATE statement, which sounds to
me like the real "logic" statement that you are trying to code (in this
case).  See other posts to show how this can/should be done.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IF... END-IF

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000129175206.15820.00000099@nso-cf.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<86vnoj$nlr$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
In article <86vnoj$nlr$1@news.inet.tele.dk>, "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
writes:

>
>Like this:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have a problem with the above proposed solution as it sets up a hanging 
ELSE that is not associated with any preceeding IF.
The way I read the original post, the second ELSE applies to the condition
where neither condition-1 or condition-2 are true.
It looks to  me like the solution would be to add another END-IF after the one
currently coded and before the period.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IF... END-IF

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n7Pk4.902$aZ4.34284@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<86vnoj$nlr$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <20000129175206.15820.00000099@nso-cf.aol.com>`

```
Aha! BASIC beats COBOL on this one, because in BASIC, you have...

IF  condition-1 THEN
    imperative-1
ELSEIF condition-2 THEN
   imperative-2
ELSEIF condition-3 THEN
   imperative-3
ELSE
   default-imperative
END IF

Easy to read, easy to understand, no?

Too bad the COBOL standards guys didn't think of adding an ELSEIF verb.
(Well, OK. I'm sure someone DID 'think' of it; it's probably that enough
people did think enough 'of' it!)

MCM
Racine WI USA

>ELSE that is not associated with any preceeding IF.....

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>

The evaluate can be used.

   EVALUATE  TRUE
       WHEN  CONDITION-1
                IMPERATIVE-1
       WHEN  CONDITION-2
                IMPERATIVE-2
       WHEN  CONDITION-3
                IMPERATIVE-3
       WHEN OTHER
                DEFAULT-IMPERATIVE
   END-EVALUATE

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IF... END-IF

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMwebaccess.net>

would read better as :

IF condition-1
    statement
ELSE
       IF condition-2
            statement
       ELSE
            statement
       END-IF
END-IF

I have no idea why you got the error, but I strongly recommend having an
END-IF for each and every IF.  This makes it more likely the compiler
and people who will maintain your code agree with you on what the scope
is for each IF.

<a id="t-47579f60be"></a>
## Thread: STD - What do you think *should* happen — 11 this month / 16 total

### STD - What do you think *should* happen

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
During the recent J4 meeting, I accepted an assignment to write a paper to
"fix" some of the  wording on what exactly the PICTURE clause symbols really
do (mean?).  I plan on creating a test-case that various people can run with
the ('74 or '85) compiler of your choice, but my question is what do you
think SHOULD happen (today - not with a future Standard) with the following
type of source code:

01 GROUP1.
     05 ALPHA-EDIT   PIC X/X.
01 ELEM1                  PIC XX.
     ...
MOVE "ABC" TO GROUP1
MOVE ALPHA-EDIT TO ELEM1


   ****

 The question is what the 2nd move "should" do.  I can think of (at least) 3
possibilities:

1) The data "AB" stored in the first two bytes of ALPHA-EDIT will be moved to
ELEM1

2) The data "A" stored in the first byte of ALPHA-EDIT will be move to the
first byte of ELEM1 - and the character "/" (which is what the picture SAYS
is in the 2nd byte) will be moved to the 2nd byte of ELEM1

3) The program will ABEND or have other "unpredictable" results because the
data in ELEM1 does not "match" what the PICTURE clause says *should* be in
that 2nd byte.

  ***

OK, folks - what do you think should happen?  (Any comments on what various
compilers do - both when running in their "ANSI-conforming" modes and in any
"full optimization" mode - will be gratefully accepted.)
```

#### ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OaTyO0na$GA.241@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I pick #1.

After the first MOVE:

MOVE "ABC" TO GROUP1
ALPHA-EDIT   contains "ABC"
The edit character is ignored because the move was to the GROUP item.

If the code was changed to:

MOVE "ABC" TO ALPHA-EDIT

ALPHA-EDIT would contain "A/B"

After the Second MOVE:

MOVE ALPHA-EDIT TO ELEM1

ELEM1 contains "AB"
The length of the receiving field causes truncation.
The Edited item is treated as PIC XXX when it is used as the sending field
of the move.

#### ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

- **From:** "Steven Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@flash.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wAHk4.12429$rI6.351885@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Well, It might be nice to "de-edit" a edited field, but I think that
truncating the "B" of ALPHA-EDIT would make more *sence*.  After all, what
would the SLASH be then?  An Imaginary Character??  Something that prints
when the report is processed but cannot be referenced with any other
statements??  I think this would lead to some confusion, and may be
problematic for some programmers who may need to edit a field, and reference
that character.  Why not just leave the de-editing for (current/new)
intrinsic functions, or other statements?

-Steve Kalemkiewicz
```

#### ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

```
    working in my head (without doing it for real):

ALPHA EDIT AND GROUP1 end up with "A/B".
MOVE GROUP1 TO ELEM1 gives "A/".
MOVE ALPHA-EDIT TO ELEM1 gives "AB".

    This would match numeric rules, except for the bit about
trunc right instead of left.

#### ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#UzHiVsa$GA.277@cpmsnbbsa04>`
- **References:** `<86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I've used code similar to this for the past 6 years and it works
consistently on all three versions of COBOL we have.  We are currently using
NetExpress 3.0 and it still works the same, so I'd like to see it stay the
same.

When you move "ABC" to GROUP1, just as Dennis Harley pointed out, the value
in GROUP1 and ALPHA-EDIT are both "ABC".  This stands to reason since GROUP1
is in essence a PIC X(3) with no edit mask.  When you move ALPHA-EDIT to
ELEM1, there still isn't any mask so the first 2 bytes get moved and the
result in ELEM1 is "AB".

If, however, you do move "ABC" into ALPHA-EDIT then the mask takes effect
and the value for both GROUP1 and ALPHA-EDIT is "A/B".  When this is moved
to ELEM1 the first 2 bytes are again moved so the value of ELEM1 is now
"A/".

If it was decided that by moving "ABC" to GROUP1 would result in a value in
GROUP1 of "A/B", then what would the following definition do?

01 GROUP1.
     05 ALPHA-EDIT   PIC X/X.
    05  ALPHA-EDIT2 REDEFINES ALPHA-EDIT
                                    PIC XX/.

bill

William M. Klein wrote in message <86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>...
>During the recent J4 meeting, I accepted an assignment to write a paper to
>"fix" some of the  wording on what exactly the PICTURE clause symbols
really
>do (mean?).  I plan on creating a test-case that various people can run
with
>the ('74 or '85) compiler of your choice, but my question is what do you
>think SHOULD happen (today - not with a future Standard) with the following
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> The question is what the 2nd move "should" do.  I can think of (at least)
3
>possibilities:
>
>1) The data "AB" stored in the first two bytes of ALPHA-EDIT will be moved
to
>ELEM1
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>compilers do - both when running in their "ANSI-conforming" modes and in
any
>"full optimization" mode - will be gratefully accepted.)
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<870832$3bh$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <#UzHiVsa$GA.277@cpmsnbbsa04>`

```
No one is thinking of changing the value of "ABC" in ALPHA-EDIT.  It has to
work that way (because a single elementary item under a group item - really
does work just like a "redefines").  I don't think there is ANY chance of
this ever changing or working differently in any other compilers.

The question, however, is whether the compiler may (is permitted) to believe
the PICTURE clause when you do the move "from" ALPHA-EDIT.  Let's look at a
similar (but slightly different) case,  if you have

  01 Group1.
       05  Num-Edit   Pic 9,9.    *> a numeric-edited item

  01 Num     Pic 999.

Move "123" to Group1
Move Num-Edit to Num

The value that will be placed in Num is either:
     13   - because you "de-edit" numeric-edited values when moved to a
numeric elementary item
            or
   "abend" - because num-edit really doesn't have contents that match what
the picture SAYS it is supposed to have.  (When I say "abend" this is similar
to a NetExpress RTS 163 error - when you have +F specified.  "ABEND" is a
typical mainframe term.)

OK,  when moving from a numeric-edited field, it is clear that the COBOL
run-time *must* look at the picture clause - not just the contents of the
field.  (what it does when the contents don't match the picture is up to the
implementor).  On the other hand, there are not the same (or at least not as
clear) rules about what happens when you move an alphanumeric-edited item to
an elementary alphanumeric item.  My guess is that (almost) all compiler do
move the real contents (i.e. the "AB" that you originally reported).
However, I think there are some "optimizing" compilers that believe that the
contents of alpha-edited will match what the picture says they will.
Therefore, the will *at compile-time* move a "/" to the 2nd byte of the
receiving field, and only move the 1st byte of alpha-edited to the 1st byte
of the receiving item (a run-time action that takes more "cycles" then the
move of the literal "/" which can be figured out at compile-time).

Does this help explain my question/issue?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

- **From:** "DennisHarley" <LegacyBlue@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#Iwn12za$GA.271@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
For Numeric-edited sending fields going to numeric or numeric-edited
receiving fields:

I think the run time shall verify the presence of the editing characters
before doing the conversion.

Given a choice between ABENDing, or having incorrect data on the system, I
would choose ABENDing.

>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

similar
> to a NetExpress RTS 163 error - when you have +F specified.  "ABEND" is a

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0t3l4.1914$rC3.56997@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <#UzHiVsa$GA.277@cpmsnbbsa04> <870832$3bh$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>
>The question, however, is whether the compiler may (is permitted) to believe
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Move Num-Edit to Num

I think the answer here is simple.  For numeric fields, it is not the
characters themselves that are important, it is the numeric value.
When moving from edited numeric fields, it therefore makes sense to
'de-edit' to get the actual numeric value, when it is being moved
to a numeric receiving field (only).  But for alphanumeric fields,
the characters themselves are the issue.  Just as when moving a
numeric edited field to an alphanumeric field, moving an alphanumeric
edited field to alphanumeric field should do no 'de-editing'.
```

#### ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38942F1E.467C65E@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> 01 GROUP1.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> moved to ELEM1

Yes.  This what my experience is with the MVS.  (untested though).

> 
> 2) The data "A" stored in the first byte of ALPHA-EDIT will be move
> to the first byte of ELEM1 - and the character "/" (which is what the
> picture SAYS is in the 2nd byte) will be moved to the 2nd byte of
> ELEM1

No:  Because the picture character is generally skipped when it is
'MOVED TO' and the content of alpha element is moved somewhere else
without interpretation.

> 3) The program will ABEND or have other "unpredictable" results
> because the data in ELEM1 does not "match" what the PICTURE clause
> says *should* be in that 2nd byte.

I hate this abend concept!  If cobol had true 'exceptions' then I
think that throwing an exception is OK.  If there is an 'invalid'
clause then that might be activated.  This checking would cause a
large amount of overhead though, I would prefer for this sort of
checking to be optional and therefore ignored in production (same as I
use SSRANGE, table overrun, in development and not in production)

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: STD - What do you think *should* happen

- **From:** shine98@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<871vmu$2ns$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
My chance to save the world!: I vote for #1 It makes more sense to keep
as much of the original data as possible. But even of the '/' were
moved, I see no reason for an abend. This bug should be sorted out in
testing.

Since you have input to the COBOL process, could we have local
variables ala 'C' or 'C++' declared after the paragraph name or section
name? As in:

 ID
 Working-Storage Div.
 Vars global to program
 01 Tbl.
     05  filler occurs 10 times.
             15  tbl-cnt pic 9(07).
             15   tbl-desc pic x(25)
 Procedure Division.
 Para1.
*vars local to Para1.
 01  I pic 999 value 0.
 Perform varying I from 1 by until I > 10
     move 0 to tbl-cnt (i)
     move spaces to tbl-desc (i)
 end-perform

 goback.


I think this would be very handy, it certainly works well in 'C'.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87230m$db0$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<86v2ur$77b$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <871vmu$2ns$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
COBOL (since the '85 Standard) has supported "local" variables - but they are
local to a nested program - not to a paragraph.  If you want data/procedure
"encapsulation" (of this type), the COBOL technique IS to use nested
programs.

If you are interested in adding data definitions in the middle of the
Procedure Division, you could suggest this to J4 (hopefully for the revision
AFTER this one).  My guess is that it would not receive a lot of support, but
it is up to you to suggest it if you think it is worth considering.  You can
communicate this suggestion (or any suggestion) directly to J4 by sending
email to the J4 chair at:
    doncobol <at> mediaone.net
```

<a id="t-c1c0559d68"></a>
## Thread: More SAP Stuff — 1 msgs

### More SAP Stuff

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yQnl4.96$VJ6.2830@news.swbell.net>`

```
From Publisher's Weekly
-------------------
Energy SAP? Random's Shift to New System Creates Delays

When the Random House-BDD (Bantam-Doubleday-Dell) merger was first
announced, one of the main advantages trumpeted by Bertelsmann brass
was SAP, the system that would integrate warehouse operations and
presumably make the ordering process go more smoothly.

A look at numbers and a random survey of some booksellers indicate
that problems have arisen, in which SAP may play a part (demand for
big titles such as movie tie-ins and weather conditions may also have
contributed).

According to figures obtained by PW Daily, the Westminster DC had a
backlog of one million units as of January 6. Of those books, nearly
half were shipped, leaving the balance unfilled. As orders continued
to come in, the backlog grew, and by January 10, even workers on
Saturday shifts couldn't stem the tide of a 2.1 million-unit backlog.
Over the next two weeks, orders picked up, and a snowstorm didn't
help. At press time, the backlog has swelled to nearly 2.3 million.
But when the process is complete, the upheaval from the SAP
implementation, say sources, is likely to be less dramatic than when
BDD undertook same.

Random explained the delays as part of the normal course of system
changes. "From where we're sitting, it's so far, so good," spokesman
Stuart Applebaum told PW Daily. "As any bookseller or publisher who
has implemented a new system will tell you, you always allow for some
hiccups along the way. Considering how mammoth and fundamental the
system's realignment has been, it sounds like they are relatively
minor in the
greater universe of what we do."
--------------------------------------------------------------------
"I opened my Windows 95 manual and fell into a magical place."
```

## Thread: Is 4000 a leap year? — 1 this month / 14 total

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### RE: Is 4000 a leap year?

- **From:** einomoto@compuserve.com (Edmond J. Inomoto)
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00000029123740.OUI26.einomoto@compuserve.com>`

```
Hi,

On January 24 2000, Kent Feiler <kfeiler@cpiusa.com> wrote:

> Interesting, I thought this was a rule as well, i.e. that dates
> divisable by 4,000 were NOT leap years.  Believe it or not, I need to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> kfeiler@cpiusa.com

According to the book "Standard C Date/Time Library" by Lance Latham,
Miller-Freeman, 1998, p. 249:

"... The alleged reform would add a fourth part to the Gregorian leap
year rule, making years evenly divisible by 4,000 common years [i.e.
non-leap years]..."

"...While this reform has been proposed occassionally, it has never been
implemented."

HTH.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
My problem is that i use aloop with a screen section for a record, when it
loops for a second time the data from the first run through is still there.
I can't remember what to do - Do i need to put in console is crt ????????  I
can't get a book till monday and i want to finish this damn thing!
PLEASE HELP!!
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen handling/console

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 2000-01-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<86vob8$oht$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<86v3gk$c84$1@brimstone.medianet.ie>`

```
You need to have at least one entry-field to accept. then something like
this will do.

Get-Data.
      Display MyScreen.
      Accept Myscreen.
      Perform Validate-Data.

Please let us see your code if this isn't helping you.

Regards
Ib

<a id="t-04f9b01993"></a>
## Thread: STD - from the San Diego J4 meeting — 4 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### STD - from the San Diego J4 meeting

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<871t1r$s91$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Two items were "resolved" at the J4 San Diego meeting.  These items are now
placed in the "controversial" category of our future agenda, because there is
(at least some) question about whether these "decisions" will stick once WG4
(the ISO-ish COBOL group) see them.   I thought that I would report them both
here (comp.lang.cobol and gnu-list) - these are both items that if you have
"strong" feelings that the group went the wrong way, you should QUICKLY
contact your "national body" and let them know (probably CC'ing the J4
chair). (Let me know by private email if you need to find out how to contact
your national body.)

1) (Possibly not real controversial)
  The "ATTRIBUTE" phrase has been entirely removed from the enhanced
Report-Writer facility.  This was the phrase that allowed an entire report to
be printed in a "proportional font" - but didn't really allow for changing -
within the same report - from fixed to proportional or vice versa (line by
line - much less field by field).  It also didn't handle "decimal point
alignment" in proportional fonts - or the ability to specify other types of
"styles" (bold, underline, color, etc).  The feeling at J4 was that it had
enough problems with sufficiently limited capability that "dropping" it now
(with the possibility of adding an extended feature in a future revision)
would be better than trying to spend the time "fixing" it in this revision.

2) The "finalizer" auto-method is being dropped from this revision (where OO
is added) and being "postponed" to the upcoming "technical report" when a
Standard Class Library will be defined.
  Almost every problem with the current specification that the committee
talked about ended up being a lengthy one - without certainty that the
committee really knew what SHOULD happen.  A lot of the underlying problem is
that this facility is "by definition" an asynchronous event - and COBOL is
(traditionally) totally "synchronous" (even in OO).  Therefore (for example)
J4 couldn't really figure out what to do if a FINALIZER included a "STOP RUN"
statement that might occur in the middle of any statement that might still be
going on in the "mainline" logic.   It was also felt (by some) that where the
entire timing of FINALIZER and all rules related to "garbage collection" were
STILL being left to the implementor (even if the auto-method were added),
that it really wouldn't have added a lot to the portability of programs.
There was also discussion of whether programmers could explicitly invoke this
auto-method and once it was decided (I was out of the room for this
discussion) that they could NOT, this also added to this being a "limited"
facility that would have taken too much time to fix before the Standard is
finished.

   ***

The bottom-line from the meeting is that there really is a lot of hope that
the revision will go out as a "final committee draft" for review during this
upcoming summer - and will actually become "official" at the end of next
year.  (I am not certain that I share this as a belief, but do certainly
sense that J4 is doing what they can now to try and make it happen.)
```

#### ↳ Re: STD - from the San Diego J4 meeting

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#NFnXj3a$GA.286@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<871t1r$s91$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I think it's good news to see they're pressing forward.
```

#### ↳ OO: Compiler vendors please note...

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<389586E3.ACED0486@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<871t1r$s91$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> 
> 2) The "finalizer" auto-method is being dropped from this revision 

Can we set an informal specification with the compiler vendors.  Net
express is using FINALIZE (yanks...).  There are some that use deep
finalize but in my opinion this is an error of inconsistency.

In net express FINALIZE must be explicitly called (yuk but I can live
with this).

If the industry agrees to a name it does make coding easier, even if
all the nuts are loose in the standard.

I hate the way that objects must be dynamically allocated.  Why can't
I create an object in working storage:

	01   an-object   my-object-type.

The compiler would then have the responsibility of allocating and
cleaning up.   Once again I suggest this to compiler vendors to take
up.  This also solve garbage collection, you know how long these
variables exist so you know when they should be purged.  It would also
be predictable and synchronous.

Finally I would vote for an explicit delete command for an object
(cancel??).  If one object creates another it should be able to force
it to disappear on demand.  Yes this is not safe (dangling pointers to
objects cause a major pain) but this is a programmer responsibility.

This asynch issue is not really an issue.   If you want threads then
you should only get them when you ask for them.  Threads are an
optimization that are rarely needed in real life.  Most multithreading
is done at a program level (multiple jobs or submitted background
processes in Unix) because of the keep it simple stupid principle. 
The only thing I have used real threads for is internet access where
responses are unreliable and sometimes incredibly slow.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO: Compiler vendors please note...

- **From:** Stephen.Biggs@merant.com (Steve Biggs)
- **Date:** 2000-01-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<874h36$jd5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<871t1r$s91$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <389586E3.ACED0486@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
>"William M. Klein" wrote:
>> 2) The "finalizer" auto-method is being dropped from this revision 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>with this).

Ken - This is not a garbage collection method and acts much more like 
something you requested below...

>Finally I would vote for an explicit delete command for an object
>(cancel??).

..except that "Finalize" is a "normal" method here, not syntax. As a 
method, rather than syntax, it does allow the object to do some cleanup.

An explicit "delete"/"finalize" can be used against an explit "new" (as 
per C/C++), but wouldn't fit well within a garbage collected 
environment - what if the object is still being used elsewhere, or is 
accidentally deleted twice? This is what leaving object lifetimes to the 
garbage collector prevents!

Existing Net Express "Finalize" methods would be unlikely to 
*automatically* become FINALIZER methods under garbage collection. A 
"Finalize" method and an "AUTO-METHOD" (or whatever syntax finally gets 
in) for finalization would almost certainly be treated differently, at 
least by default.

>If the industry agrees to a name it does make coding easier, even if
>all the nuts are loose in the standard.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>be predictable and synchronous.

Decalring an object "on the stack" as above is all very well, but doesn't 
take into account construction parameters. C/C++ uses '()' notation to 
pass the parameters to the constructor - I'm not sure what syntax COBOL 
could use?
Always declaring an object on the stack like this is also awfully wasteful 
if, say, the routine ends up using only one of the fifty objects 
declared...
(This isn't to say that it's a bad idea, just that we do need dynamically 
created objects, too.)

All these comments are mine, incidentally, not a reflection of MERANT 
past, current or future policy!

Steve.
```

---

<a id="t-4fdc47552c"></a>
## Thread: Leap Seconds and COBOL — 2 this month / 3 total

### Re: Leap Seconds and COBOL

- **From:** rkrayhawk@aol.com (RKRayhawk)
- **Date:** 2000-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000130090833.00140.00000379@ng-cm1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<hu4v8s449625angukbo3461skiki7em2ru@4ax.com>`

```

G Moore gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com 
Date: Thu, 27 Jan 2000 10:33:01 -0500
posts
<<
to hell with that. 18.2 updates is 18 transactions per account, per
second. i don't go through 18 purchases every second, and i surely
couldn't find any transaction which, written correctly, couldn't use
that 'deficiency' (outside of bad indexing engines).

>>

A simple example of the need for good resolution of transactions might be a
security system that is defending a mainframe across a firewall.  How rapidly
are the high-speed lines presenting us with traceable hacker attempts to break
in? 

Such a system must have a resolution as fast as current technology, period. The
next day the time resolution must be faster. This responsibility never eases.

Assume that the hacker is coming in on a line connected to the demand deposit
accounts of the poster. Does the poster want the financial institution to be
able to keep up with the hacker? Does the poster want the defensive system to
be as precise in its resolution of sequence of events as the assaultor is? Does
the poster care if we protect the account?

Would the poster prefer that we look at inbound hacker transactions as
duplicates?

Does the poster care about the solvancy of an institution holding that account
even if it is some other account holder(s) that get hurt? Do you want your
money in a bank where the managers are nor concerned about clock resolution on
there machines?

I assure you that hackers can present more than 18.2 assaults in a second on a
single account, and on thousands of accounts! I assure you that they realize
that the clock resolution IS a weak point.  I assure you that they realize many
folks are not up to speed on this issue. They like your approach. Please tell
them the location of your machines. Let us know the shops you worked in in the
past, too.

We like look-alike time stamps, they're fun. You can tell its Matel, its swell!

Some one in one of these notes in this thread, claimed that you can not
synchronize CPU's to anything more than  something like .047 seconds (or
hundreths of a second). I deleted that post so can't quote it exactly. I'd like
to give proper attribution because the notion is important for time critical
functions.

You actually can get devices nearly synchronized if you use triangulation with
an intermediate location, and iterate through approximations. You then hit some
threshold in the technology you are using to blip the clock ticks, but that
certainly could be a very small increment.

 The need to do that in business apps is surely rare. But you could do it.  A
more problematic issue is that the semantics of time on different devices gets
difficult if you resolve very finely. Since the capturing of an event takes
finite time, then it matters if we stamp before, during, or after the cyles for
the capture (or any other processing). 

Most applications need uniqueness either to record actual sequence, or to
impose a sequence to maintain the order of the information. Exact time to an
exreme resolution has very little meaning for a human perceiver. No one is
claiming that the exactitude is useful in itself in a single instance; it is
the sequence that matters - either the sequence as things occur, or the
sequence we want the information ordered by.


Very often for recordation of human actions, a resolution down to nothing more
than a second, with an artificial extension that just counts whatever a machine
might enumerate, is fine. But not in a multiprocessing system which permits
concurrent access to related information.  

It is especially true that large data stores connected to communication lines
need very precise unique identifiers.

The sequence in which things are done is of the essence in finite state machine
behavior. If you can not detect sequence, you are quite vulnerable.

Best Wishes,

Robert Rayhawk
RKRayhawk@aol.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Leap Seconds and COBOL

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 2000-01-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<87258m$gd6$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<hu4v8s449625angukbo3461skiki7em2ru@4ax.com> <20000130090833.00140.00000379@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
RKRayhawk <rkrayhawk@aol.com> wrote:

: Such a system must have a resolution as fast as current technology, period. The
: next day the time resolution must be faster. This responsibility never eases.

So, your resolution must be 1/100 sec ?  1/1000 ?  1/1000000 ?

: Would the poster prefer that we look at inbound hacker transactions as
: duplicates?

Using solely the time stamp would seem to be a very poor mechanism.
Relying on the timestamp to give _unique_ times would seem to be
unreliable.  What if there were two servers, what if there were
a dozen with load balancing ?

Use the time for the first part of the sequence and then add a 
uniquely generated sequence number from a single source.

: Does the poster care about the solvancy of an institution holding that account
: even if it is some other account holder(s) that get hurt? Do you want your
: money in a bank where the managers are nor concerned about clock resolution on
: there machines?

I wouldn't want it in a bank where the clock resolution would
be something they needed to be concerned about (which is an entirely
different thing).

: I assure you that hackers can present more than 18.2 assaults in a second on a
: single account, and on thousands of accounts! I assure you that they realize
: that the clock resolution IS a weak point.  I assure you that they realize many
Then the clock resolution should be removed as being a weak point
and should be replaced by a proper design.

No clock is as accurate as the master clocks, this means that to keep
as accurate as possible each clock must be adjusted to the
reference.  This adjustment could happen at any time and could mean
that seconds, or parts of seconds are skipped or repeated.

: Very often for recordation of human actions, a resolution down to nothing more
: than a second, with an artificial extension that just counts whatever a machine
: might enumerate, is fine. But not in a multiprocessing system which permits
: concurrent access to related information.  

: It is especially true that large data stores connected to communication lines
: need very precise unique identifiers.

: The sequence in which things are done is of the essence in finite state machine
: behavior. If you can not detect sequence, you are quite vulnerable.

Which is why a timestamp should not be used and a sequence generator
should be used.  The actual time of these events is usually
irrevelant, the sequence is critical.  Clock times accross several
different machines, server farms, distributed systems, multiple
comms controllers, is not a guaranteed sequence.
```
